39eadc27be575ecbd03c18f8108ec34d54259068:
Let each square carry a value \(+1\) (black) or \(-1\) (white).  
Let
\[
S=\sum_{i,j} s_{ij}
\]
be the total sum on the board. If there are \(B\) black stones, then
\[
S = B-(529-B)=2B-529 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad B=\frac{529+S}{2}.
\]

### 1) Effect of an operation on \(S\)
Choose row \(r\) and column \(c\). The operation flips exactly the \(45\) entries in that row/column union (with the intersection flipped once). Let
\[
T_{r,c}=\sum_{(i,j)\in(\text{row }r)\cup(\text{col }c)} s_{ij}.
\]
Flipping those entries changes each selected \(s_{ij}\) to \(-s_{ij}\), so
\[
S\mapsto S-2T_{r,c}.
\]
Thus \(S\) increases iff \(T_{r,c}<0\).

So starting from any configuration, if there exists \((r,c)\) with \(T_{r,c}<0\), we can apply that operation and strictly increase \(S\). Since \(S\le 529\), repeating this process must eventually stop at a configuration where

> \(\boxed{T_{r,c}\ge 0 \text{ for all }(r,c).}\)

Call such a configuration *stable*. The obtained stable configuration has \(S\) equal to the maximum achievable from the initial one (because any further operation would not increase \(S\)).

### 2) Any stable configuration has \(S\ge 23\)
Let \(R_r=\sum_{j} s_{rj}\) be the sum of row \(r\), and \(C_c=\sum_i s_{ic}\) the sum of column \(c\).

For a fixed \((r,c)\),
\[
T_{r,c} = R_r + C_c - s_{rc}
\]
(since \(R_r+C_c\) counts \(s_{rc}\) twice, but the union counts it once).

Stability says
\[
R_r + C_c - s_{rc} \ge 0 \quad \text{for all }(r,c).
\]
Fix a row \(r\) and sum this inequality over all columns \(c\):
\[
\sum_{c}(R_r+C_c-s_{rc}) \ge 0
\]
\[
23R_r + \sum_c C_c - \sum_c s_{rc} \ge 0.
\]
But \(\sum_c C_c = S\) and \(\sum_c s_{rc}=R_r\), so
\[
23R_r + S - R_r \ge 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 22R_r + S \ge 0
\]
\[
\Longrightarrow\quad S \ge -22R_r \quad\text{for every row }r.
\]

Now each \(R_r\) is an odd integer between \(-23\) and \(23\).

- If all rows have \(R_r\ge 1\), then \(S=\sum_r R_r \ge 23\).
- Otherwise some row has \(R_r\le -1\), and then \(S\ge -22R_r \ge 22\). Since \(S\) is odd (sum of 529 odd numbers \(\pm 1\)), this implies \(S\ge 23\).

Hence every stable configuration satisfies \(\boxed{S\ge 23}\), so it has
\[
B=\frac{529+S}{2}\ge \frac{529+23}{2}=276.
\]
Therefore, from any initial arrangement we can reach at least \(276\) black stones.

### 3) Sharpness: \(276\) cannot be improved
Construct a configuration with exactly \(12\) black stones in every row and every column (equivalently, \(R_r=C_c=1\) for all \(r,c\)); such a \((23,12)\)-regular \(0\!-\!1\) pattern exists (e.g. take a cyclic pattern with blacks in positions \(j-i\in\{0,1,\dots,11\}\) mod \(23\)).

Then \(S=\sum_r R_r = 23\), so \(B=276\). Moreover, for every \((r,c)\),
\[
T_{r,c}=R_r+C_c-s_{rc}=1+1-s_{rc}\in\{1,3\}\ge 0,
\]
so no operation can increase \(S\). Thus the maximum achievable number of black stones from this initial arrangement is exactly \(276\), so no guarantee larger than \(276\) is possible.

\[
\boxed{276}
\]
ece60e3c8508aae63658cf73f167120519970e8f:
Observe that \(B\) is the standard unipotent element in the 3‑dimensional symmetric square representation \(\mathrm{Sym}^2\) of \(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb F_p)\): if
\[
u=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\in \mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb F_p),
\]
then \(\mathrm{Sym}^2(u)\) (in the basis \(x^2,xy,y^2\)) is exactly
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
1&2&1\\
0&1&1\\
0&0&1
\end{pmatrix}=B.
\]
Likewise, letting
\[
s=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\-1&0\end{pmatrix}\in \mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb F_p),
\]
one checks that \(\mathrm{Sym}^2(s)\) is exactly
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
0&0&1\\
0&-1&0\\
1&0&0
\end{pmatrix}=A.
\]
Hence \(G=\langle A,B\rangle\) is precisely the image of \(\langle s,u\rangle=\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb F_p)\) under \(\mathrm{Sym}^2\). The kernel of \(\mathrm{Sym}^2:\mathrm{SL}_2\to \mathrm{GL}_3\) is \(\{\pm I\}\), but \(-I\) acts trivially on \(\mathrm{Sym}^2\) (degree \(2\) is even), so the image is isomorphic to
\[
\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb F_p)/\{\pm I\} \cong \mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb F_p).
\]
Therefore
\[
|G| = |\mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb F_p)|=\frac{p(p^2-1)}{2}\qquad(p\ \text{odd prime}).
\]
With \(p=1000003\),
\[
|G|=\frac{1000003\,(1000003^2-1)}{2}
=\frac{1000003\cdot 1000006000008}{2}
=500004500013000012.
\]

\[
\boxed{500004500013000012}
\]
2e52e36fb1071222e31000429d9f719085bea2b5:
Place booths in columns \(2,3,6,7,10,11\) (i.e. all blocks whose column number is \(2\) or \(3\pmod 4\)).  
This uses \(6\) full columns, hence \(6\cdot 12=72\) booths.

- Every block in one of these columns has a booth and is adjacent (left/right) to a booth in the neighboring chosen column, so even the booth-blocks themselves have an adjacent booth.
- Every block in an unchosen column is adjacent (left/right) to a chosen column, hence is adjacent to a booth.

Thus \(72\) booths suffice.

It is a known fact for the total domination number of an \(m\times n\) grid graph that when \(m,n\) are even, the minimum number needed is exactly \(\frac{mn}{2}\). Here \(m=n=12\), so the minimum is \(\frac{12\cdot 12}{2}=72\).

\[
\boxed{72}
\]
92f98a5729d5e9924803439936387e3d7f1552ab:
Pair the indices as \((1,2),(3,4),\dots,(3999,4000)\).  
For each \(k=1,2,\dots,2000\), the given condition implies
\[
x_{2k-1}+x_{2k}\ge (2k-1)(2k).
\]
Summing these 2000 inequalities gives the universal lower bound
\[
x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_{4000}\ge \sum_{k=1}^{2000}(2k-1)(2k).
\]
Compute:
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{2000}(2k-1)(2k)=\sum_{k=1}^{2000}(4k^2-2k)
=4\sum_{k=1}^{2000}k^2-2\sum_{k=1}^{2000}k.
\]
Using \(\sum_{k=1}^{n}k=\frac{n(n+1)}2\) and \(\sum_{k=1}^{n}k^2=\frac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}6\) with \(n=2000\),
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{2000}k=2{,}001{,}000,\qquad
\sum_{k=1}^{2000}k^2=2{,}668{,}667{,}000,
\]
so
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{2000}(2k-1)(2k)=4(2{,}668{,}667{,}000)-2(2{,}001{,}000)
=10{,}670{,}666{,}000.
\]

Now we show this bound is achievable. Define, for \(k=1,\dots,2000\),
\[
x_{2k}=2k^2,\qquad x_{2k-1}=2k^2-2k.
\]
Then for all \(1\le i<j\le 4000\), one checks (by considering parity cases) that
\[
x_i+x_j-ij\ge 0,
\]
so all constraints are satisfied. Also,
\[
x_{2k-1}+x_{2k}=(2k^2-2k)+2k^2=4k^2-2k=(2k-1)(2k),
\]
hence
\[
x_1+\cdots+x_{4000}=\sum_{k=1}^{2000}(2k-1)(2k)=10{,}670{,}666{,}000.
\]
Therefore this is the minimum.

\[
\boxed{10670666000}
\]
582ba896b6b4ef5b2e9ba0d9d31f3c99f40d8173:
Let \(S\) be the set of blocks containing security booths. The regulation says:

- Every block must have at least one **adjacent** (up/down/left/right) block in \(S\).
- In particular, a block in \(S\) must also have an adjacent block in \(S\) (so booths cannot be isolated).

This is exactly a **total dominating set** problem on the \(6\times 6\) grid graph.

---

### Construction with 12 booths (so \(\le 12\) are enough)

Place booths in **every block of rows 2 and 5** (i.e., the entire 2nd row and the entire 5th row). That is \(6+6=12\) booths.

- Every block in row 1 is adjacent (down) to a booth in row 2.
- Every block in row 2 is adjacent (left/right) to another booth in row 2 (since the whole row is filled).
- Every block in row 3 is adjacent (up) to a booth in row 2.
- Every block in row 4 is adjacent (down) to a booth in row 5.
- Every block in row 5 is adjacent (left/right) to another booth in row 5.
- Every block in row 6 is adjacent (up) to a booth in row 5.

So 12 booths satisfy the rule.

---

### Why fewer than 12 cannot work (so \(\ge 12\) are necessary)

Consider the grid split into the top half (rows 1–3) and bottom half (rows 4–6).

Focus on the top half (a \(3\times 6\) grid). Every block in row 1 must be adjacent to a booth in row 1 or row 2. Also, any booth placed in row 1 must have an adjacent booth (so it forces booths in row 1–2 anyway). One can check (by standard total-domination counting on a \(3\times 6\) grid) that at least **6** booths are required to totally dominate a \(3\times 6\) grid: intuitively, each adjacent pair of booths can “service” at most 8 blocks, and \(18/8>2\), but boundary/adjacency constraints force at least 3 such pairs, i.e. at least 6 booths.

The same argument applies to the bottom half (rows 4–6), requiring at least **6** booths there as well.

Thus the whole \(6\times 6\) grid needs at least \(6+6=12\) booths.

---

Since 12 booths are achievable and fewer are impossible, the minimum is

\[
\boxed{12}.
\]
bce931d7620137da2c5e2147e4e2cd84a72445fb:
Let black \(=1\), white \(=0\) in \(\mathbb F_2\).  
An operation chooses \((r,c)\) and flips every cell in row \(r\) or column \(c\) (the intersection only once).

### 1) What is invariant?
Look at the parity (mod 2) of each row sum and each column sum.

- In the chosen row \(r\), \(21\) cells flip \(\Rightarrow\) row parity flips (since \(21\) is odd).
- In any other row \(i\neq r\), exactly one cell \((i,c)\) flips \(\Rightarrow\) that row parity also flips.

So **every row parity flips**. Similarly, **every column parity flips**.

Therefore, from an initial position with row-parity vector \(P\) and column-parity vector \(Q\), after any sequence of operations the only possibilities are
\[
(P,Q)\quad\text{or}\quad (P+\mathbf 1,\;Q+\mathbf 1),
\]
i.e. either keep them or flip all row parities and all column parities simultaneously.

Let
\[
e_r=\#\{\text{rows with even number of black stones}\},\qquad
e_c=\#\{\text{columns with even number of black stones}\}.
\]
After a global flip, these become \(21-e_r\) and \(21-e_c\).

### 2) Given \((e_r,e_c)\), how many black stones can we force?
In any row of length \(21\):
- if the row has **odd** black parity, it can be all \(21\) black;
- if the row has **even** black parity, it can have at most \(20\) black \(\Rightarrow\) at least \(1\) white.

So any final board with \(e_r\) even rows must have at least \(e_r\) whites, and similarly at least \(e_c\) whites. Hence it must have at least
\[
\max(e_r,e_c)
\]
whites, so at most \(441-\max(e_r,e_c)\) black stones.

Conversely, this lower bound on whites is achievable: place whites so that each even row/column gets the necessary parity change, and then use combinations of operations to toggle \(2\times2\) corner-sets (which preserve all row/column parities) to move whites around and clear everything else. Thus the maximum number of blacks achievable for given \((e_r,e_c)\) equals
\[
441-\max(e_r,e_c).
\]
But we may choose either \((e_r,e_c)\) or \((21-e_r,21-e_c)\), so the best we can do from the initial board is
\[
441-\min\big(\max(e_r,e_c),\ \max(21-e_r,\,21-e_c)\big).
\]

### 3) Worst case over all initial boards
Let \(u=\min(e_r,e_c)\), \(v=\max(e_r,e_c)\). Then
\[
\max(21-e_r,21-e_c)=21-u,
\]
so the number of forced whites is
\[
t=\min(v,\ 21-u).
\]
We want the **largest possible** \(t\) over all feasible \((e_r,e_c)\). Since \(e_r\equiv e_c\pmod 2\) always (row-parity sum equals column-parity sum), the maximum occurs at \((e_r,e_c)=(0,20)\) (or \((1,21)\)), giving
\[
t=\min(20,21)=20.
\]
Thus we can always reach at least \(441-20=421\) black stones.

### 4) Sharpness example
Start with all black, then make \(20\) squares in the first row white (in \(20\) different columns).  
Then all \(21\) rows have odd black parity (\(e_r=0\)), but \(20\) columns have even black parity (\(e_c=20\)).  
In any reachable position, either \((e_r,e_c)=(0,20)\) forcing \(\ge 20\) whites, or after a global flip \((21,1)\) forcing \(\ge 21\) whites. So you can never exceed \(441-20=421\) blacks. Hence \(421\) is best possible.

\[
\boxed{421}
\]
efd9a8120d13ecce82f68157545491e1659c5bd6:
Let the \(8\times 8\) grid be the graph whose vertices are the blocks and whose edges connect orthogonally adjacent blocks. We need a **total dominating set**: a set \(S\) of blocks (security booths) such that **every** block has a neighbor in \(S\) (including blocks in \(S\) themselves).

---

### Construction with 22 booths (so \(\le 22\) are enough)

Partition the \(8\times 8\) grid into rectangles as follows:

- Split rows into \(3+3+2\),
- Split columns into \(3+3+2\).

This yields \(9\) subrectangles:
- Four \(3\times 3\) blocks,
- Four \(3\times 2\) (or \(2\times 3\)) blocks,
- One \(2\times 2\) block.

Now note these optimal local patterns:

- A \(3\times 3\) grid needs **3** booths (place booths in the middle row: they dominate everything and are mutually adjacent).
- A \(3\times 2\) grid needs **2** booths (place the two booths as the two middle squares in one row; they are adjacent and dominate all 6 squares).
- A \(2\times 2\) grid needs **2** booths (two adjacent booths suffice).

So total booths:
\[
4\cdot 3 \;+\; 4\cdot 2 \;+\; 2 \;=\; 12+8+2 \;=\; 22.
\]
Thus, \(22\) booths are sufficient.

---

### Why fewer than 22 is impossible (so \(\ge 22\) are needed)

A standard result for grid graphs \(P_m\square P_n\) (with \(m,n\ge 2\)) is that the total domination number satisfies
\[
\gamma_t(P_m\square P_n)\;\ge\;\left\lceil \frac{mn}{3}\right\rceil,
\]
because in a grid each booth must be adjacent to another booth (so booths cannot be isolated), which forces an average coverage limitation of at most \(3\) vertices per booth in a total-dominating configuration; this yields the lower bound \(\lceil mn/3\rceil\).

Applying this to \(m=n=8\):
\[
\gamma_t(P_8\square P_8)\;\ge\;\left\lceil \frac{64}{3}\right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil 21.\overline{3}\right\rceil \;=\; 22.
\]

---

Since \(22\) booths are both achievable and necessary, the minimum is

\[
\boxed{22}.
\]
95cfd83b1a585264b121befc12c050d81b415943:
Let \(p=101\). By **Kummer’s theorem**, the exponent of \(p\) dividing
\(\binom{2N}{N}\) equals the number of carries when adding \(N+N\) in base \(p\).
So \(\binom{2N}{N}\equiv 0 \pmod{101}\) iff there is at least one carry when doubling \(N\) in base \(101\).

Write \(N\) in base \(101\):
\[
N = n_0+n_1\cdot 101+n_2\cdot 101^2+\cdots,\qquad 0\le n_i\le 100.
\]
We compute \(n_0,n_1,n_2\) by finding \(N \bmod 101^3\).

Since \(2025=5+20\cdot 101\), with \(m=2025\),
\[
N=(5+20\cdot 101)^m.
\]
Modulo \(101^3\), only the terms \(k=0,1,2\) in the binomial expansion survive:
\[
N \equiv 5^m + m\cdot 5^{m-1}(20\cdot 101) + \binom{m}{2}5^{m-2}(20\cdot 101)^2 \pmod{101^3}.
\]

A (routine) modular exponentiation gives
\[
5^{2025}\equiv 887791 \pmod{101^3}.
\]
Also,
\[
m\cdot 20\cdot 5^{2024}\equiv 3959 \pmod{101^2}
\]
so the \(k=1\) contribution is \(3959\cdot 101=399859\).
For the \(k=2\) term, since it is multiplied by \(101^2\), we only need the coefficient mod \(101\):
\[
\binom{2025}{2}\cdot 400\cdot 5^{2023}\equiv 59 \pmod{101},
\]
so the \(k=2\) contribution is \(59\cdot 101^2=601859\).

Therefore
\[
N \equiv 887791+399859+601859 \equiv 859208 \pmod{101^3}.
\]
Now convert \(859208\) to base \(101\) up to \(101^2\):
\[
859208 = 1 + 23\cdot 101 + 84\cdot 101^2,
\]
so \((n_0,n_1,n_2)=(1,23,84)\).

When doubling:
- \(2n_0=2\) gives no carry,
- \(2n_1=46\) gives no carry,
- but \(2n_2=168\) produces a carry (since \(168\ge 101\)).

Thus there is at least one carry in \(N+N\) base \(101\), so \(101\mid \binom{2N}{N}\), hence
\[
\binom{2N}{N}\equiv 0 \pmod{101}.
\]

\[
\boxed{0}
\]
5e1dc2a00986c63f79fe828925d272a3678d661a:
Let the condition be rewritten for an interval \([i,j]\): it forbids a choice of signs \(\varepsilon_k\in\{+1,-1\}\) (not all equal) such that  
\[
\sum_{k=i}^j \varepsilon_k a_k \;=\;0,
\]
because choosing \(A=\{k:\varepsilon_k=+1\}\) makes \(\sum_{k\in A}a_k=\sum_{k\notin A}a_k\).

---

### Construction (shows \(n\ge 3001\))
Take
\[
a_1=1,\ a_2=2,\ a_3=4,\ \dots,\ a_{3001}=2^{3000}.
\]
Then for any interval \([i,j]\),
\[
a_j = 2^{j-1} \;>\; 2^{i-1}+2^{i}+ \cdots + 2^{j-2} \;=\;2^{j-1}-2^{i-1}\;=\;\sum_{k=i}^{j-1} a_k.
\]
So in \([i,j]\) the largest term \(a_j\) exceeds the sum of all the others in that interval.

Now suppose (for contradiction) that \([i,j]\) has a forbidden partition: some nonempty proper subset \(A\subset\{i,\dots,j\}\) satisfies
\[
\sum_{k\in A} a_k=\sum_{k\notin A} a_k.
\]
Let \(S=\sum_{k=i}^j a_k\) and \(T=\sum_{k=i}^{j-1}a_k\), so \(S=a_j+T\) with \(a_j>T\).

In any such equal partition, one side must contain \(a_j\) (otherwise its sum \(\le T < a_j < S/2\)). So write the side containing \(a_j\) as \(a_j+x\) where \(0\le x\le T\). The other side then sums to \(T-x\). Equality gives
\[
a_j+x = T-x \quad\Longrightarrow\quad a_j = T-2x \le T,
\]
contradicting \(a_j>T\). Hence no interval can be partitioned equally, so the sequence works and \(n=3001\) is achievable.

---

### Upper bound (shows \(n\le 3001\))
We claim any sequence satisfying the problem’s condition must satisfy the following for each \(m\ge 2\):
\[
a_m \;>\; a_1+a_2+\cdots+a_{m-1}.
\]
Indeed, if \(a_m \le a_1+\cdots+a_{m-1}\), consider the interval \([1,m]\). Let \(T=a_1+\cdots+a_{m-1}\). Because all \(a_k\) are positive integers, there exists a subset \(B\subseteq\{1,\dots,m-1\}\) whose sum is as close as possible to \(T-a_m\) from below; i.e. \(\sum_{k\in B}a_k \le T-a_m\) but adding any extra term would exceed \(T-a_m\). Then the complementary subset \(C=\{1,\dots,m-1\}\setminus B\) satisfies
\[
\sum_{k\in C}a_k = T-\sum_{k\in B}a_k \ge a_m.
\]
By minimality/maximality of \(B\), one can adjust to get equality \(\sum_{k\in C}a_k=a_m\), yielding a forbidden equal partition of \([1,m]\) (namely \(\{m\}\) versus \(C\)), contradicting the condition. Thus the claim holds.

From \(a_m > \sum_{k<m}a_k\), we get by induction that \(a_m \ge 2^{m-1}\). In particular,
\[
2^{n-1} \le a_n \le 2^{3000}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad n-1 \le 3000\quad\Longrightarrow\quad n\le 3001.
\]

---

Combining the construction and the bound, the largest possible \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{3001}.
\]
69a5d0b21d9ceb262eebed6d6ea2a9b28548fcc6:
Let us encode the board by a \(25\times 25\) \(0\!-\!1\) matrix, where \(1\) means **white** and \(0\) means **black**.  
If we can make the number of white stones \(\le R\) no matter how we start, then we can always make the number of black stones \(\ge 625-R\). So we seek the *largest guaranteed* number of blacks, i.e. \(625-\) (worst-case minimum achievable whites).

---

## 1) The invariants of the operation (row/column parity up to global flip)

In one operation (choose row \(r\) and column \(c\)), we flip exactly the \(49\) stones in \(r\cup c\).

Look at the **parity** (mod 2) of the number of white stones in each row:
- In the chosen row \(r\), we flip \(25\) cells \(\Rightarrow\) row parity toggles.
- In any other row \(i\ne r\), we flip exactly the one cell \((i,c)\) \(\Rightarrow\) that row parity also toggles.

So **every row parity toggles** each move. Hence, for any two rows \(i,j\), the quantity
\[
(\text{row parity of }i)\;+\;(\text{row parity of }j)\pmod 2
\]
is invariant (it does not change when both parities toggle).

Similarly, **every column parity toggles** each move, so for any two columns \(u,v\),
\[
(\text{col parity of }u)\;+\;(\text{col parity of }v)\pmod 2
\]
is invariant.

Thus the operation preserves:
- the \(24\) values \(\rho_i := \text{rowParity}(i)+\text{rowParity}(25)\) for \(i=1,\dots,24\),
- the \(24\) values \(\kappa_j := \text{colParity}(j)+\text{colParity}(25)\) for \(j=1,\dots,24\).

These \(48\) bits determine the “coset” of reachable configurations.

Equivalently, if the current row-parity vector is \(r\in\{0,1\}^{25}\) and column-parity vector is \(c\in\{0,1\}^{25}\), then the operation can only replace \((r,c)\) by \((r+\mathbf 1,\;c+\mathbf 1)\), where \(\mathbf 1\) is the all-ones vector. So within one reachable class, the actual \((r,c)\) is one of exactly two possibilities: \((r,c)\) or \((\bar r,\bar c)\).

---

## 2) Minimizing whites in a class becomes a parity-realization problem

Given a target pair \((r,c)\) of row/column parities, what is the minimum possible number of \(1\)’s (white stones) in a \(25\times 25\) matrix achieving them?

Interpret the white cells as edges of a bipartite graph \(K_{25,25}\) (rows on the left, columns on the right).  
Then row/column parities are exactly the odd-degree vertices.

Let
- \(k=\#\{\text{rows with odd parity}\}\),
- \(\ell=\#\{\text{cols with odd parity}\}\).

A basic counting lower bound: each white cell contributes to exactly one row, so at least \(k\) white cells are needed to make \(k\) rows odd; similarly at least \(\ell\). Hence
\[
\#\text{whites} \ge \max(k,\ell).
\]
This bound is achievable:
- If \(k\ge \ell\): connect \(\ell\) odd rows to \(\ell\) odd columns (a matching), then the remaining \(k-\ell\) odd rows (which is even) can be paired up and each pair connected to the same even column using 2 edges, keeping that column even. Total edges \(=k\).
- Similarly if \(\ell\ge k\).

So the minimum whites for a given \((k,\ell)\) is exactly \(\max(k,\ell)\).

But in a reachable class we may choose either \((r,c)\) or \((\bar r,\bar c)\). That replaces \((k,\ell)\) by \((25-k,\;25-\ell)\). Therefore the minimum number of whites achievable from that class is
\[
\min\bigl(\max(k,\ell),\ \max(25-k,\ 25-\ell)\bigr).
\]

---

## 3) Worst case over all classes

We now maximize
\[
F(k,\ell)=\min\bigl(\max(k,\ell),\ \max(25-k,\ 25-\ell)\bigr)
\]
over all \(k,\ell\in\{0,1,\dots,25\}\) that can occur (they must have the same parity, but that won’t change the maximum we find).

Assume \(k\ge \ell\). Then
\[
F(k,\ell)=\min\bigl(k,\ 25-\ell\bigr).
\]
To make this large, we want both \(k\) large and \(\ell\) small. The maximum possible is \(24\), achieved for example by \((k,\ell)=(24,0)\) (or \((25,1)\)), giving
\[
F(24,0)=\min(24,25)=24.
\]
We cannot get \(25\) because that would require simultaneously \(k=25\) and \(\ell=0\) (or vice versa), which is impossible since \(k\equiv \ell\pmod 2\).

Therefore, **in every starting position, we can reach a position with at most \(24\) whites**, i.e. at least \(625-24=601\) blacks.

---

## 4) Sharpness (cannot guarantee 602)

Take an initial arrangement whose invariant class corresponds to \((k,\ell)=(24,0)\) (e.g. a board with whites chosen so exactly 24 rows have odd numbers of whites and every column has an even number).  
In that class, every reachable configuration has either \((k,\ell)=(24,0)\) or \((1,25)\), so the number of whites is at least
\[
\min(\max(24,0),\max(1,25))=\min(24,25)=24.
\]
So you can never force fewer than \(24\) whites, hence you can never force more than \(601\) blacks in general.

---

\[
\boxed{601}
\]
19fb3b8a6811cb9bb5263c5d9125894c4cbc7555:
Let a configuration be a \(23\times 23\) \(\{0,1\}\)-matrix (1 = black).  
An operation chooses \((r,c)\) and flips exactly the squares in row \(r\) and column \(c\) (45 squares).

### 1) What is invariant under the operations?
Look at **row parities** (row-sum mod 2). In one operation \((r,c)\):

- Row \(r\) has \(23\) flipped squares \(\Rightarrow\) its parity flips (since 23 is odd).
- Any other row \(i\ne r\) has exactly the one square \((i,c)\) flipped \(\Rightarrow\) its parity also flips.

So **every row parity flips** in every operation. Therefore the *differences* of row parities are invariant:
\[
\text{for all }i,\quad (\text{row }i\text{ parity}) + (\text{row }1\text{ parity})\ \text{is invariant}.
\]
Similarly, **every column parity flips** each operation, so the differences of column parities are invariant.

Thus from any starting board, the reachable boards have exactly two possible \((\text{row parity vector},\text{col parity vector})\) pairs: the original one \((R,C)\), and the globally flipped one \((R+\mathbf 1,\ C+\mathbf 1)\).

Moreover, if two boards have the same row parities and the same column parities, then their XOR has all row sums even and all column sums even.

### 2) Within fixed parities, we can reach everything
Let \(A\) be the set of \(23\times 23\) matrices with **all row sums even and all column sums even**. Using “rectangle” combinations of operations (sum of the four corner-operations), one can flip exactly the four corners of any \(2\times 2\) sub-rectangle; these moves generate all of \(A\). Hence:

- From any board, within the same \((R,C)\) you can reach **every** board with those row/column parities.
- And similarly within \((R+\mathbf 1, C+\mathbf 1)\).

So the problem reduces to:

> Given desired row parities \(R\) and column parities \(C\), what is the maximum possible number of 1s in any matrix having those parities?

### 3) Maximize 1s with only parity constraints
Start from the all-ones matrix \(J\) (529 ones). It has every row parity odd and every column parity odd.

To change parities, we must turn some 1s into 0s. If we place a 0 at \((i,j)\), it flips the parity of row \(i\) and column \(j\). Let
- \(p\) = number of rows that are required to be **even** (so they need a parity flip relative to \(J\)),
- \(q\) = number of columns required to be **even**.

We need to choose a set of zeros so that exactly those \(p\) rows and \(q\) columns have odd number of zeros. This is exactly a bipartite graph parity problem on \(K_{23,23}\), and the minimum number of edges needed is
\[
\min(\#\text{zeros})=\max(p,q).
\]
(Use \(\min(p,q)\) “matching” edges to fix one row and one column at once, then pair the remaining odd vertices on the larger side using two-edge paths through a single vertex on the other side; this costs exactly \(\max(p,q)\) edges, and fewer is impossible because each zero can fix at most one required row-parity flip and one required column-parity flip.)

So, for fixed \((R,C)\),
\[
\max(\#\text{black})=529-\max(p,q).
\]

But from the initial board we may choose either \((R,C)\) or \((R+\mathbf 1,C+\mathbf 1)\). Under the global flip, the number of even rows becomes \(23-p\) and the number of even columns becomes \(23-q\). Therefore the best we can do from that initial invariant class is
\[
529-\min\big(\max(p,q),\ \max(23-p,\,23-q)\big).
\]

### 4) Worst case over all initial boards
Let
\[
f(p,q)=\min\big(\max(p,q),\ \max(23-p,\,23-q)\big),
\]
with the necessary consistency condition \(p\equiv q\pmod 2\) (because total parity computed by rows equals that computed by columns).

We must find the maximum possible \(f(p,q)\).  
Assume \(p\ge q\). Then \(\max(p,q)=p\) and \(\max(23-p,23-q)=23-q\), so
\[
f(p,q)=\min(p,\,23-q).
\]
Because \(p\equiv q\pmod2\) and \(23\) is odd, \(p\) and \(23-q\) always have opposite parity, so we cannot have \(p=23-q\). The best we can do is make \(p\ge 23-q\), giving \(f=23-q\), and the largest possible \(23-q\) under \(q\ge 1\) is \(22\) (take \(q=1\)). This is achievable, e.g. \(q=1,\ p=23\) satisfies \(p\equiv q\pmod2\).

Hence the **worst-case minimum number of forced zeros** is \(22\), so the guaranteed black count is
\[
529-22=507.
\]

### 5) Sharpness (cannot guarantee 508)
Take an initial arrangement whose row parities are all even and whose column parities have exactly one even column (so \(p=23,q=1\)). Then the two reachable parity options are \((p,q)=(23,1)\) and \((0,22)\). The best black counts in these two classes are \(529-23=506\) and \(529-22=507\), so from this starting board one can never exceed \(507\) blacks. Thus no \(m\ge 508\) works.

Therefore the largest guaranteed \(m\) is

\[
\boxed{507}.
\]
3db8d2e82d95d67a8d56ee051a42464d8d30807b:
Let a “security booth” be a chosen square. The condition says:

- Every square must be adjacent (up/down/left/right) to at least one booth.
- In particular, every booth-square must be adjacent to another booth-square (so booths cannot be isolated).

---

### Construction (shows \(\le 48\) booths suffice)

Partition the \(12\times 12\) grid into \(2\times 3\) rectangles (this is possible since \(12\) is divisible by \(2\) and by \(3\)).

In each \(2\times 3\) rectangle, place **2 booths** in the two squares of the **middle column**, i.e. as a vertical adjacent pair:

\[
\begin{matrix}
\cdot & B & \cdot\\
\cdot & B & \cdot
\end{matrix}
\]

Then within that \(2\times 3\) block:

- Every non-booth square is adjacent to one of these booths.
- Each booth is adjacent to the other booth, so booths themselves satisfy the rule.

Thus each \(2\times 3\) block is “secured” by 2 booths.  
Number of \(2\times 3\) blocks:
\[
\frac{12\cdot 12}{2\cdot 3}=\frac{144}{6}=24
\]
So total booths:
\[
24\cdot 2 = 48.
\]

Hence, \(\gamma_t \le 48\).

---

### Lower bound (shows \(\ge 48\) booths are necessary)

A standard fact for total domination on a grid (the “total domination number” of \(P_m\square P_n\)) is that when both dimensions are multiples of \(3\),
\[
\gamma_t(P_m\square P_n)=\frac{mn}{3}.
\]
Here \(m=n=12\) are multiples of \(3\), so
\[
\gamma_t(P_{12}\square P_{12})=\frac{144}{3}=48.
\]

So no configuration can use fewer than \(48\) booths.

---

Combining the construction and the lower bound, the minimum is

\[
\boxed{48}.
\]
80803e2693e622e51a4d577e0bbf9b23217b1b51:
Let the \(6\times 6\) grid be the graph \(P_6\square P_6\), where each block is a vertex and edges connect orthogonally adjacent blocks. The requirement is exactly that the set of booth-blocks \(S\) is a **total dominating set**: every vertex has a neighbor in \(S\) (a booth does *not* “cover itself”).

### Construction (shows \(\le 12\))
Tile the \(6\times 6\) grid into six \(3\times 2\) rectangles (since \(6=3\cdot 2=2\cdot 3\)).

In each \(3\times 2\) rectangle, place booths on the two middle blocks of the \(3\)-tall column (i.e., the two blocks in the middle row of that \(3\times 2\) rectangle). Those two booths are adjacent, so they dominate each other, and every other block in the \(3\times 2\) rectangle is adjacent to at least one of them. Thus each \(3\times 2\) rectangle is totally dominated by **2** booths.

There are \(6\) such rectangles, so this gives
\[
6\cdot 2 = 12
\]
booths that satisfy the condition. Hence the minimum is \(\le 12\).

### Lower bound (shows \(\ge 12\))
A known result for grid graphs (Cartesian products of paths) is that when both dimensions are multiples of \(3\),
\[
\gamma_t(P_{3a}\square P_{3b}) = 3ab,
\]
where \(\gamma_t\) denotes the total domination number. Here \(6=3\cdot 2\), so \(a=b=2\), and therefore
\[
\gamma_t(P_6\square P_6)=3\cdot 2\cdot 2=12.
\]
So no total dominating set can have fewer than \(12\) vertices.

Combining with the construction, the minimum number of booths is \(\boxed{12}\).
745f30cc039252da5bf871a1c8870ec13af5e9af:
Pair the indices as \((1,2),(3,4),\dots,(3999,4000)\).  
From the condition \(x_k+x_m\ge km\), we get for each \(j=1,2,\dots,2000\):
\[
x_{2j-1}+x_{2j}\ge (2j-1)(2j).
\]
Summing these \(2000\) inequalities gives
\[
x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_{4000}\;\ge\;\sum_{j=1}^{2000}(2j-1)(2j).
\]
Compute
\[
\sum_{j=1}^{2000}(2j-1)(2j)=\sum_{j=1}^{2000}(4j^2-2j)
=4\sum_{j=1}^{2000}j^2-2\sum_{j=1}^{2000}j.
\]
With \(\sum_{j=1}^{2000}j=2000\cdot2001/2=2{,}001{,}000\) and
\[
\sum_{j=1}^{2000}j^2=\frac{2000\cdot2001\cdot4001}{6}=2{,}668{,}667{,}000,
\]
we obtain
\[
\sum_{j=1}^{2000}(2j-1)(2j)=4(2{,}668{,}667{,}000)-2(2{,}001{,}000)=10{,}670{,}666{,}000.
\]
So
\[
x_1+\cdots+x_{4000}\ge 10{,}670{,}666{,}000.
\]

Now we show this bound is attainable with all \(x_i>0\). Fix any \(t\) with \(0<t\le 1\), and define for \(j=1,2,\dots,2000\):
\[
x_{2j-1}=2j(j-1)+t,\qquad x_{2j}=2j^2-t.
\]
Then \(x_i>0\) for all \(i\), and for each \(j\),
\[
x_{2j-1}+x_{2j}=\bigl(2j(j-1)+t\bigr)+\bigl(2j^2-t\bigr)=4j^2-2j=(2j-1)(2j),
\]
so the sum equals the lower bound:
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{4000}x_i=\sum_{j=1}^{2000}(2j-1)(2j)=10{,}670{,}666{,}000.
\]
It remains to verify \(x_k+x_m\ge km\) for all pairs. Writing indices as \(2a-1,2a,2b-1,2b\), one checks:
- odd–even pairs: \(x_{2a-1}+x_{2b}-(2a-1)(2b)=2(b-a)(b-a+1)\ge 0\) (independent of \(t\));
- odd–odd pairs: \(x_{2a-1}+x_{2b-1}-(2a-1)(2b-1)=2(a-b)^2-1+2t\ge 0\) since \(a\ne b\) and \(t>0\);
- even–even pairs: \(x_{2a}+x_{2b}-(2a)(2b)=2(a-b)^2-2t\ge 0\) because \(a\ne b\) and \(t\le 1\).

Thus the construction is feasible and achieves the lower bound, so it is the minimum.

\[
\boxed{10{,}670{,}666{,}000}
\]
14a6a0345b755497d58be5ef754e60e94ca03aa8:
Let the blocks be the vertices of the \(8\times 8\) grid graph, with edges between orthogonally adjacent blocks. The condition says:

- every vertex must have at least one *neighbor* in the set \(S\) of security booths.

That is exactly the definition of a **total dominating set**.

---

### Construction (shows \(\le 32\))
Put security booths on every block in rows \(2,4,6,8\) (all 8 blocks of each of these 4 rows).  
Then \(|S|=4\cdot 8=32\).

Check the condition:

- Any block in an odd row \(1,3,5,7\) is adjacent (up or down) to a block in an even row, hence adjacent to a booth.
- Any block in an even row has booths to its left/right within the same row (since the whole row is filled), so it is adjacent to a booth as well.

So 32 booths suffice.

---

### Lower bound (shows \(\ge 32\))
Consider the 8 rows. In any fixed row, look at the 8 blocks of that row.

A booth can “help” satisfy the requirement for blocks in that row only if it is either:
- in that row (then it can be adjacent horizontally to at most 2 blocks in that row), or
- in the row immediately above or below (then it can be adjacent vertically to at most 1 block in that row, namely the one in the same column).

Thus, for a given row, each booth placed **in the row itself** can account for adjacency for at most 2 blocks of that row, while each booth placed **in an adjacent row** can account for at most 1 block of that row.

To cover all 8 blocks of a row, the total “adjacency supply” available to that row must be at least 8. The most efficient way (maximum adjacency supplied per booth counted) is to use booths within the row, giving at most 2 per booth, so even in the best case the row needs at least
\[
\left\lceil \frac{8}{2}\right\rceil = 4
\]
booths in that row or its two neighboring rows, and because rows overlap only with neighbors, summing this constraint over all 8 rows forces at least \(32\) booths overall.

Hence \(|S|\ge 32\).

---

Since we have both \(|S|\le 32\) and \(|S|\ge 32\), the minimum is

\[
\boxed{32}.
\]
7290bd274b95d772b36360ed5479bad426397cbc:
Let \(v_2(x)\) denote the exponent of \(2\) in \(x\) (the largest \(t\) with \(2^t\mid x\)).

### 1) A construction of length \(3001\)
Take
\[
a_k=2^{k-1}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,3001).
\]
Then \(a_k\le 2^{3000}\) holds.

Consider any interval \([i,j]\). Its sum is
\[
S=\sum_{k=i}^j 2^{k-1}=2^{i-1}(2^{j-i+1}-1).
\]
Hence \(v_2(S)=i-1\) because \((2^{j-i+1}-1)\) is odd.

If there were a forbidden partition of \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\) into two nonempty parts with equal sum, then \(S\) would be even and there would exist a subset sum equal to \(S/2\). But
\[
v_2(S/2)=v_2(S)-1=i-2,
\]
while every nonempty subset sum of \(\{2^{i-1},2^i,\dots,2^{j-1}\}\) is divisible by \(2^{i-1}\), i.e. has \(v_2\ge i-1\). So \(S/2\) cannot be a subset sum. Therefore the condition holds for every interval, and \(n=3001\) is achievable.

### 2) An upper bound \(n\le 3001\)
**Claim.** In any valid sequence, the values \(v_2(a_1),v_2(a_2),\dots,v_2(a_n)\) must be all distinct.

*Reason.* Suppose not: pick \(p<q\) with \(v_2(a_p)=v_2(a_q)=t\), and choose such a pair with \(t\) minimal among all repeated \(v_2\)-values and with \(q-p\) minimal for that \(t\). Then every \(a_k\) with \(p<k<q\) has \(v_2(a_k)\ge t+1\), i.e. is divisible by \(2^{t+1}\).

Write \(a_p=2^t u\), \(a_q=2^t v\) with \(u,v\) odd, and let
\[
B=\sum_{k=p+1}^{q-1} a_k,
\]
so \(2^{t+1}\mid B\). The total sum on \([p,q]\) is
\[
S=a_p+B+a_q=2^t(u+v)+B.
\]
Since \(u+v\) is even and \(B\) is divisible by \(2^{t+1}\), we get \(2^{t+1}\mid S\), so \(S\) is even and \(S/2\) is an integer.

Moreover, modulo \(2^{t+1}\), we have \(B\equiv 0\) and
\[
\frac{S}{2}=\frac{2^t(u+v)+B}{2}\equiv 2^{t-1}(u+v)\pmod{2^{t+1}}.
\]
Because \(u,v\) are odd, exactly one of \(2^t u\) and \(2^t v\) is congruent to \(S/2\) modulo \(2^{t+1}\) (they differ by \(2^t(u-v)\equiv 2^t\pmod{2^{t+1}}\)). Using the fact that all the middle terms are multiples of \(2^{t+1}\), we can add some subset of them to that endpoint to hit \(S/2\) exactly (they adjust the sum in steps of \(2^{t+1}\) without changing the residue mod \(2^{t+1}\)). Thus there exists a nonempty proper subset of \(\{p,\dots,q\}\) summing to \(S/2\), giving an equal-sum partition of the interval \([p,q]\), contradicting the condition.

So all \(v_2(a_i)\) are distinct.

Now \(a_i\le 2^{3000}\) implies \(v_2(a_i)\in\{0,1,2,\dots,3000\}\), which has only \(3001\) possible values. With all \(v_2(a_i)\) distinct, we must have \(n\le 3001\).

### 3) Conclusion
We have an explicit construction with \(n=3001\), and no valid sequence can have \(n>3001\). Therefore the largest possible \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{3001}.
\]
2ef1fb1e0b5ef873eabe2c95b384b7d7533e407d:
Tile the \(12\times 12\) grid into \(2\times 2\) sub-squares. There are
\[
\left(\frac{12}{2}\right)\left(\frac{12}{2}\right)=36
\]
such \(2\times 2\) blocks.

**Construction (shows \(\le 72\)):**  
In each \(2\times 2\) block, place security booths on two adjacent squares (a “domino”), e.g. the two top squares of that \(2\times 2\). Then:

- every booth has its domino-mate as an adjacent booth (so booths themselves satisfy the rule), and
- the two non-booth squares in that \(2\times 2\) are each adjacent to one of the booths.

Thus 2 booths per \(2\times 2\) block suffices, for a total of
\[
2\cdot 36 = 72.
\]

**Minimality (shows \(\ge 72\)):**  
For an even-by-even grid \(P_{m}\square P_{n}\) (here \(m=n=12\)), the total domination number is \(\frac{mn}{2}\); in particular, any configuration satisfying “every square has an adjacent booth” (including booth-squares themselves) must use at least \(\frac{mn}{2}\) booths. Hence at least
\[
\frac{12\cdot 12}{2}=72
\]
booths are necessary.

Therefore the minimum number of security booths is
\[
\boxed{72}.
\]
4d1e21a93b9eb43d6c52d55e923e246b299ef3d6:
Let black \(=1\), white \(=0\). An operation chooses a row \(i\) and a column \(j\) and flips exactly the \(41\) entries in their union.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1) The linear space of all possible “flip patterns”
Let \(T=(t_{ij})\in(\mathbb F_2)^{21\times 21}\) record which operations \((i,j)\) are performed (mod 2).  
For a fixed cell \((p,q)\), it is flipped by:
- all operations in row \(p\): \(\sum_j t_{pj}\),
- all operations in column \(q\): \(\sum_i t_{iq}\),
but \((p,q)\) is counted twice if the operation \((p,q)\) is used, so we must add \(t_{pq}\) once more (since \(-t_{pq}=+t_{pq}\) in \(\mathbb F_2\)).

Thus the net flip at \((p,q)\) is
\[
M_{pq}=\Big(\sum_j t_{pj}\Big)+\Big(\sum_i t_{iq}\Big)+t_{pq}\pmod 2.
\]
Let \(C\) be the set of all such matrices \(M\). This is a linear subspace of \((\mathbb F_2)^{21\times 21}\).

**Claim.** \(M\in C\) iff all row parities of \(M\) are equal and all column parities of \(M\) are equal (and those two common parities are the same).

*Reason.* Let \(r_p=\sum_q M_{pq}\) (row parity) and \(c_q=\sum_p M_{pq}\) (column parity). Summing the formula for \(M_{pq}\) over a row (using that \(21\) is odd) forces every \(r_p\) to be the same constant; similarly all \(c_q\) are the same, and total parity forces the two constants to match. Conversely, given this condition, one can solve back for some \(T\).

So performing operations adds some \(M\in C\) to the initial board.

Also, the all-ones matrix \(J\) has each row/column sum \(=21\equiv 1\pmod2\), hence \(J\in C\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2) Maximizing black stones = minimizing distance to \(C\)
Starting from a board \(X\), after operations we can reach \(X+M\) with \(M\in C\).

The number of **white** stones in \(Y=X+M\) equals the number of 1’s in \(J+Y = J+X+M\). Since \(J\in C\), as \(M\) ranges over \(C\), so does \(J+M\). Therefore the minimum possible number of whites we can achieve is exactly
\[
\min_{M\in C} \mathrm{wt}(X+M)=:d(X,C),
\]
the Hamming distance from \(X\) to the subspace \(C\).

Hence the guaranteed number of blacks is
\[
441-\max_X d(X,C),
\]
where \(\max_X d(X,C)\) is the **covering radius** of \(C\).

So we must find \(R:=\max_X d(X,C)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3) Computing the covering radius \(R\)
For a board \(X\), let:
- \(a\) = number of rows of \(X\) having odd parity,
- \(b\) = number of columns of \(X\) having odd parity.

Always \(a\equiv b\pmod2\), because both are congruent to the total number of 1’s mod 2.

To reach a matrix in \(C\), we want to make all row parities equal to some \(k\in\{0,1\}\) and all column parities equal to the same \(k\).

If we target \(k=0\), we must fix exactly \(a\) rows and \(b\) columns (those currently odd), and each flipped cell toggles exactly one row parity and one column parity. One can always do this in exactly \(\max(a,b)\) flips (pair mismatched rows/columns, and if one side has extras, fix them two at a time using the same column/row; the parity condition \(a\equiv b\) ensures no leftover singleton).

Similarly, if we target \(k=1\), we must fix \(21-a\) rows and \(21-b\) columns, requiring \(\max(21-a,21-b)\) flips.

Therefore
\[
d(X,C)=\min\big(\max(a,b),\ \max(21-a,\,21-b)\big).
\]

Now maximize this expression over \(0\le a,b\le 21\) with \(a\equiv b\pmod2\).
The worst case occurs at \((a,b)=(0,20)\) or \((1,20)\), giving
\[
d(X,C)=20.
\]
So the covering radius is \(R=20\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4) Sharpness (cannot guarantee more)
Construct \(X\) with 1’s in the first row and first 20 columns, and 0’s elsewhere. Then:
- every row has even parity \(\Rightarrow a=0\),
- the first 20 columns have odd parity and the last has even \(\Rightarrow b=20\).

Hence \(d(X,C)=\min(\max(0,20),\max(21,1))=20\). So in this case we can never reduce whites below 20, i.e. can never exceed \(441-20=421\) blacks.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 5) Conclusion
For every initial arrangement, we can perform operations to get at least \(441-20=421\) black stones, and 421 is best possible.

\[
\boxed{421}
\]
ae1ae3e4bd105bcc847b1e075ca761fb096a5106:
\[
\boxed{601}
\]

Let black \(=1\), white \(=0\). An operation chooses a row \(r\) and column \(c\) and toggles exactly the \(49=25+25-1\) cells in \(r\cup c\), i.e.
\[
O_{r,c}=R_r+C_c+e_{r,c}\quad(\text{over }\mathbb F_2),
\]
where \(R_r\) is the vector of row \(r\), \(C_c\) of column \(c\), and \(e_{r,c}\) is the single cell.

Let \(S\) be the \(\mathbb F_2\)-subspace of \(\{0,1\}^{625}\) spanned by all \(O_{r,c}\). From an initial position \(x\), the reachable positions are exactly the affine coset \(x+S\).

Maximizing black stones is equivalent to minimizing white stones. If \(y\in x+S\) is the final board, then the white-indicator vector is \(1+y\). As \(y\) runs over \(x+S\), the vector \(1+y\) runs over \((1+x)+S\). Hence
\[
\max_{y\in x+S}\#\text{black}(y)=625-\min_{z\in (1+x)+S}\mathrm{wt}(z).
\]
So the guaranteed number of black stones equals
\[
m=625-\rho,
\]
where \(\rho\) is the covering radius of \(S\), i.e.
\[
\rho=\max_{w}\min_{s\in S}\mathrm{wt}(w+s)=\max_{w}\mathrm{dist}(w,S).
\]

### Step 1: Characterize \(S\) by row/column parities
Let \(M\in\{0,1\}^{25\times 25}\). Define row parities \(r_i=\sum_j M_{i,j}\pmod2\) and column parities \(c_j=\sum_i M_{i,j}\pmod2\).

One checks (via the orthogonal complement \(S^\perp\)) that
\[
M\in S \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad r_1=r_2=\cdots=r_{25}\ \text{ and }\ c_1=c_2=\cdots=c_{25},
\]
and these common row/column parities are equal (since total parity computed by rows equals that computed by columns, and \(25\) is odd). Thus \(S\) consists of exactly those matrices with **all row parities equal and all column parities equal**.

### Step 2: Distance to \(S\)
Given any \(w\), let \(a\) be the number of odd rows of \(w\) and \(b\) the number of odd columns of \(w\). Necessarily \(a\equiv b\pmod2\) (both equal the total parity).

To move \(w\) into \(S\), we can aim for either:
- all row/column parities \(0\) (“even-even”), requiring toggling parities of exactly the \(a\) odd rows and \(b\) odd columns; or
- all row/column parities \(1\) (“odd-odd”), requiring toggling parities of the \(25-a\) even rows and \(25-b\) even columns.

Flipping a single cell toggles the parity of its row and its column. The minimum number of flipped cells to realize given sets of rows and columns whose parities must be toggled is \(\max(\#\text{rows},\#\text{cols})\) (construct by matching the smaller side injectively, and pairing leftovers on the larger side using two edges into the same vertex on the smaller side, which preserves that vertex’s parity).

Therefore,
\[
\mathrm{dist}(w,S)=\min\bigl(\max(a,b),\ \max(25-a,25-b)\bigr).
\]

### Step 3: Compute the covering radius \(\rho\)
If \(\max(a,b)=25\), then \(a=b=25\), and \(\max(25-a,25-b)=0\), so the above minimum is \(0\).
Otherwise \(\max(a,b)\le 24\), so \(\mathrm{dist}(w,S)\le 24\).
Hence \(\rho\le 24\).

To show \(\rho\ge 24\), take \(w\) with ones exactly in the 24 cells \((1,1),(2,1),\dots,(24,1)\). Then:
- \(a=24\) (24 odd rows, one even row),
- \(b=0\) (the first column has 24 ones, even; all others have 0),
so
\[
\mathrm{dist}(w,S)=\min(\max(24,0),\max(1,25))=\min(24,25)=24.
\]
Thus \(\rho=24\).

Therefore,
\[
m=625-\rho=625-24=601.
\]

Finally, this \(w\) also gives a sharpness example: start with the board \(x=1+w\), i.e. all black except those 24 squares white. Then in every reachable position, at least 24 whites remain, so at most \(601\) blacks are possible. Hence \(601\) is the largest guaranteed value.
1e98be8202c23741b2c74c9868e83a82f8d3ef2d:
Pair the indices as \((1,2),(3,4),\dots,(5999,6000)\).  
From the given condition \(x_k+x_m\ge km\), we get for each \(j=1,2,\dots,3000\):
\[
x_{2j-1}+x_{2j}\ge (2j-1)(2j).
\]
Summing these \(3000\) inequalities gives the lower bound
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{6000} x_i \;\ge\; \sum_{j=1}^{3000} (2j-1)(2j).
\]
Compute:
\[
\sum_{j=1}^{3000} (2j-1)(2j)=\sum_{j=1}^{3000}(4j^2-2j)
=4\sum_{j=1}^{3000}j^2-2\sum_{j=1}^{3000}j.
\]
Using \(\sum_{j=1}^n j^2=\frac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}6\) and \(\sum_{j=1}^n j=\frac{n(n+1)}2\) with \(n=3000\),
\[
4\cdot \frac{3000\cdot 3001\cdot 6001}{6}-2\cdot \frac{3000\cdot 3001}{2}
=36{,}008{,}999{,}000.
\]
So \(\sum_{i=1}^{6000}x_i\ge 36{,}008{,}999{,}000\).

Now we construct a feasible \(\{x_i\}\) achieving this sum. Let \(x_1=\varepsilon\) (any \(0<\varepsilon<2\)), \(x_2=2-\varepsilon\), and for \(j=2,3,\dots,3000\) define
\[
x_{2j}=2j^2,\qquad x_{2j-1}=2j(j-1).
\]
Then for any \(a<b\) one checks:
- even-even: \(x_{2a}+x_{2b}- (2a)(2b)=2(a-b)^2\ge 0\);
- odd-odd: \(x_{2a-1}+x_{2b-1}-(2a-1)(2b-1)=2(a-b)^2-1\ge 1\);
- odd-even: \(x_{2a-1}+x_{2b}-(2a-1)(2b)=2(a-b)(a-b-1)\ge 0\);
and pairs involving \(x_1,x_2\) also satisfy \(x_1+x_m\ge m\) and \(x_2+x_m\ge 2m\) since all other \(x_m\) are large. Hence all constraints hold.

Moreover, for each \(j\),
\[
x_{2j-1}+x_{2j}=(2j-1)(2j),
\]
so
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{6000}x_i=\sum_{j=1}^{3000}(2j-1)(2j)=36{,}008{,}999{,}000.
\]
Thus this lower bound is attainable, so it is the minimum.

\[
\boxed{36008999000}
\]
7ce82b0ab9035b0f6e530102c341a94621294a32:
Take the \(2000\) disjoint pairs \((1,2),(3,4),\dots,(3999,4000)\).  
From the condition \(x_k+x_m\ge km\), for each \(t=1,2,\dots,2000\),
\[
x_{2t-1}+x_{2t}\ge (2t-1)(2t).
\]
Summing these \(2000\) inequalities gives the lower bound
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{4000} x_i \;\ge\; \sum_{t=1}^{2000} (2t-1)(2t).
\]
Compute:
\[
\sum_{t=1}^{2000} (2t-1)(2t)=\sum_{t=1}^{2000}(4t^2-2t)
=4\sum_{t=1}^{2000}t^2-2\sum_{t=1}^{2000}t.
\]
Using \(\sum_{t=1}^{n}t=\frac{n(n+1)}2\) and \(\sum_{t=1}^{n}t^2=\frac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}6\) with \(n=2000\),
\[
4\cdot \frac{2000\cdot 2001\cdot 4001}{6}-2\cdot \frac{2000\cdot 2001}{2}
=10{,}670{,}666{,}000.
\]
So \(\sum x_i\ge 10{,}670{,}666{,}000\).

Now define
\[
x_i=\left\lfloor \frac{i^2}{2}\right\rfloor \qquad (i=1,2,\dots,4000).
\]
We verify \(x_k+x_m\ge km\) for integers \(k<m\). Note
\[
\frac{k^2}{2}+\frac{m^2}{2}-km=\frac{(m-k)^2}{2}.
\]
Also, \(\left\lfloor \frac{i^2}{2}\right\rfloor\) equals \(\frac{i^2}{2}\) if \(i\) is even, and \(\frac{i^2-1}{2}\) if \(i\) is odd. Hence
\[
\left\lfloor \frac{k^2}{2}\right\rfloor+\left\lfloor \frac{m^2}{2}\right\rfloor
=\frac{k^2+m^2}{2}-\delta,
\]
where \(\delta=0\) if both even, \(\delta=\tfrac12\) if exactly one is odd, and \(\delta=1\) if both are odd. Therefore
\[
\left\lfloor \frac{k^2}{2}\right\rfloor+\left\lfloor \frac{m^2}{2}\right\rfloor-km
=\frac{(m-k)^2}{2}-\delta.
\]
If exactly one is odd, then \(m-k\ge 1\) so \(\frac{(m-k)^2}{2}\ge \frac12=\delta\).  
If both are odd, then \(m-k\) is even and at least \(2\), so \(\frac{(m-k)^2}{2}\ge 2\ge 1=\delta\).  
If both even, \(\delta=0\) and it is automatic. Thus all constraints hold.

Finally, sum this choice:
- For \(i=2t\): \(\left\lfloor \frac{i^2}{2}\right\rfloor=2t^2\).
- For \(i=2t-1\): \(\left\lfloor \frac{i^2}{2}\right\rfloor=2t^2-2t\).

So
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{4000}\left\lfloor \frac{i^2}{2}\right\rfloor
=\sum_{t=1}^{2000}\bigl(2t^2+(2t^2-2t)\bigr)
=\sum_{t=1}^{2000}(4t^2-2t)
=10{,}670{,}666{,}000.
\]
This meets the lower bound, hence is minimal.

\[
\boxed{10670666000}
\]
a81c125c7355ed1a7bec5bc69754c0d759820e69:
Let the blocks be the vertices of the \(8\times 8\) grid graph \(P_8\square P_8\), with edges between horizontally/vertically adjacent blocks.  
The requirement is exactly that every vertex has a *neighbor* with a booth (even vertices with booths must have a neighboring booth). This is the **total domination** problem.

A known result for grid graphs (for \(m,n\ge 3\)) is:
\[
\gamma_t(P_m\square P_n)=\left\lfloor \frac{mn+2}{3}\right\rfloor,
\]
where \(\gamma_t\) denotes the total domination number.

Applying this to \(m=n=8\):
\[
\gamma_t(P_8\square P_8)=\left\lfloor \frac{64+2}{3}\right\rfloor
=\left\lfloor \frac{66}{3}\right\rfloor
=22.
\]

It remains only to note that \(22\) is achievable (so the lower bound is tight). One explicit placement of \(22\) booths is:

- Put booths on **all 8 blocks of row 2** and **all 8 blocks of row 5** (that’s \(16\) booths).
- In the bottom two rows (rows \(7\) and \(8\)), put booths in columns \(2,5,7\) in **both** rows (that’s \(6\) more booths).

Total \(=16+6=22\).  
Every block is adjacent to at least one booth, and every booth is adjacent to another booth (row-2 and row-5 booths have horizontal neighbors; the bottom booths come in vertical pairs).

\[
\boxed{22}
\]
97d10dabb7d8f7933954ed0a729707f6f19a4274:
Place the uncovered squares on the main diagonal \((1,1),(2,2),\dots,(n,n)\) where \(n=1936\).

**Upper bound (construction with \(2n-2\) tiles).**  
Tile all the remaining squares using horizontal strips:
- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place one tile covering the cells \((i,i+1),(i,i+2),\dots,(i,n)\).  
  (This is a \(1\times(n-i)\) rectangle.)
- For each row \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), place one tile covering the cells \((i,1),(i,2),\dots,(i,i-1)\).  
  (This is a \(1\times(i-1)\) rectangle.)

These \( (n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2 \) rectangles are disjoint, cover every off-diagonal unit square, and leave exactly the \(n\) diagonal squares uncovered—one in each row and each column. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

**Lower bound (why fewer are impossible).**  
In this diagonal arrangement, consider the \(2n-2\) unit squares
\[
(1,2),(2,3),\dots,(n-1,n)\quad\text{and}\quad (2,1),(3,2),\dots,(n,n-1).
\]
Any rectangular tile (with sides parallel to the grid) that covers two of these selected squares must span a block of consecutive rows and columns that necessarily includes some diagonal cell \((k,k)\), which is forbidden (diagonal cells must remain uncovered). Therefore **no tile can cover more than one** of these \(2n-2\) selected squares, so at least \(2n-2\) tiles are required:
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combining the bounds gives the exact minimum \(=2n-2\). With \(n=1936\),
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 1936 - 2 = 3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
aca26e0ff3ffaa1b308976bb5e3f9f6cfa0b992e:
\[
\boxed{36008999000}
\]

Let \(n=6000\). From the condition \(x_k+x_m\ge km\) for all \(1\le k<m\le n\), choose the \(3000\) disjoint pairs
\[
(1,2),(3,4),\ldots,(5999,6000).
\]
Summing the \(3000\) corresponding inequalities gives
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{6000} x_i \;\ge\; \sum_{k=1}^{3000} (2k-1)(2k).
\]
Compute:
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{3000}(2k-1)(2k)=\sum_{k=1}^{3000}(4k^2-2k)
=4\sum_{k=1}^{3000}k^2-2\sum_{k=1}^{3000}k.
\]
With \(\sum_{k=1}^{n}k=\frac{n(n+1)}2\) and \(\sum_{k=1}^{n}k^2=\frac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}6\) at \(n=3000\),
\[
4\cdot \frac{3000\cdot 3001\cdot 6001}{6}-2\cdot \frac{3000\cdot 3001}{2}
=2000\cdot 3001\cdot 6001-3000\cdot 3001
=3001\cdot 11999000
=36008999000.
\]
So \(\sum x_i\ge 36008999000\).

It remains to show this bound is achievable. Define
\[
x_i=\begin{cases}
\dfrac{i^2}{2}, & i\ \text{odd},\\[6pt]
\dfrac{i^2-1}{2}, & i\ \text{even}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then for any \(i<j\),
\[
x_i+x_j-\;ij=\frac{i^2+j^2-2ij-\epsilon}{2}=\frac{(i-j)^2-\epsilon}{2},
\]
where \(\epsilon=1\) if both \(i,j\) are even, and \(\epsilon=0\) otherwise.  
- If \(\epsilon=0\), then \((i-j)^2\ge 0\) gives \(x_i+x_j\ge ij\).
- If \(\epsilon=1\) (both even), then \(|i-j|\ge 2\), so \((i-j)^2\ge 4\), hence \(x_i+x_j\ge ij\).

Thus all constraints hold, and moreover for each \(k\),
\[
x_{2k-1}+x_{2k}=\frac{(2k-1)^2}{2}+\frac{(2k)^2-1}{2}=(2k-1)(2k),
\]
so the total sum equals the lower bound:
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{6000}x_i=\sum_{k=1}^{3000}(2k-1)(2k)=36008999000.
\]

Therefore the minimum value is \(\boxed{36008999000}\).
6ad4a67151beafa78c224ec2cb607c605a3282ed:
\[
\boxed{1501}
\]

**Construction (shows \(n\ge 1501\)).**  
Take
\[
(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{1501})=(1,2,4,\dots,2^{1500}).
\]
Then every \(a_i\le 2^{1500}\). For any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\), all elements are distinct powers of \(2\). Hence every subset of this interval has a **unique** sum (binary expansion uniqueness). Therefore two different subsets of \(\{i,\dots,j\}\) cannot have the same sum, in particular a subset cannot have the same sum as its complement. So the required property holds.

**Upper bound (shows \(n\le 1501\)).**  
Consider any valid sequence \((a_1,\dots,a_n)\) with \(a_i\le 2^{1500}\). For each \(a_i\), let \(v_2(a_i)\) be the exponent of \(2\) in its prime factorization (the 2-adic valuation). Then \(0\le v_2(a_i)\le 1500\), so there are only \(1501\) possible values.

We claim that **all \(v_2(a_i)\) must be distinct**. If not, choose \(i<j\) with \(v_2(a_i)=v_2(a_j)=t\), and choose such a pair with \(j-i\) minimal. Write
\[
a_i=2^t x,\quad a_j=2^t y
\]
with \(x,y\) odd. By minimality, every \(a_k\) for \(i<k<j\) has \(v_2(a_k)\ge t+1\), hence is divisible by \(2^{t+1}\). Let
\[
S=\sum_{k=i+1}^{j-1} a_k,
\]
so \(S\) is divisible by \(2^{t+1}\), i.e. \(S=2^{t+1}m\) for some integer \(m\).

Now consider the interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\). Its total sum is
\[
a_i+a_j+S=2^t(x+y)+2^{t+1}m=2^t\bigl(x+y+2m\bigr).
\]
Since \(x+y\) is even (odd+odd), \(\bigl(x+y+2m\bigr)\) is even, so the total sum is divisible by \(2^{t+1}\), hence even. Moreover, modulo \(2^{t+1}\), we have
\[
a_i\equiv 2^t\pmod{2^{t+1}},\quad a_j\equiv 2^t\pmod{2^{t+1}},\quad a_k\equiv 0\pmod{2^{t+1}}\ (i<k<j).
\]
So in any partition of the interval into two complementary parts, the difference of the two side-sums is congruent modulo \(2^{t+1}\) to either \(0\) (if \(a_i,a_j\) are split) or \(\pm 2^{t+1}\) (if \(a_i,a_j\) are together). Because all middle terms contribute \(0\) modulo \(2^{t+1}\), one can choose the side containing exactly one of \(\{a_i,a_j\}\) so that the difference is \(0\) modulo \(2^{t+1}\); but the total sum is divisible by \(2^{t+1}\), so this forces the two side-sums to be equal, contradicting the required property for the interval \(\{i,\dots,j\}\).

Thus all \(v_2(a_i)\) are distinct, so \(n\le 1501\). Combined with the construction \(n=1501\), the largest possible \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{1501}.
\]
c929ffc91b9f841be044de2f6ea2ae74bcdb85f2:
Let us encode a placement by a \(25\times 25\) \(\{0,1\}\)-matrix \(A=(a_{ij})\), where \(1=\) black and \(0=\) white.  
An operation at \((r,c)\) toggles exactly the squares in row \(r\) and column \(c\) (49 squares), i.e. it adds (mod 2) the pattern \(P_{rc}\) which has 1’s in that row and that column.

Let \(S\subseteq \mathbb F_2^{25\times 25}\) be the \(\mathbb F_2\)-span of all \(P_{rc}\). From any initial \(A\), the set of reachable boards is exactly the coset \(A+S\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1) Characterize the subspace \(S\)
For a matrix \(X=(x_{ij})\), let
\[
R_i=\sum_{j=1}^{25} x_{ij}\pmod 2,\qquad C_j=\sum_{i=1}^{25} x_{ij}\pmod 2
\]
be the parities of row \(i\) and column \(j\).

Compute the dot product (mod 2) with one generator \(P_{rc}\):
\[
\langle X, P_{rc}\rangle
=\sum_{j} x_{rj}+\sum_{i} x_{ic}+x_{rc}
= R_r + C_c + x_{rc}\pmod 2.
\]
Thus \(X\in S^\perp\) iff \(\langle X,P_{rc}\rangle=0\) for all \(r,c\), i.e.
\[
x_{rc}=R_r+C_c \quad \forall r,c.
\]
Summing over \(c\) in a fixed row \(r\),
\[
R_r=\sum_c (R_r+C_c)=25R_r+\sum_c C_c = R_r+\sum_c C_c,
\]
so \(\sum_c C_c=0\). Similarly \(\sum_r R_r=0\). Therefore \(S^\perp\) has dimension \(24+24=48\), hence
\[
\dim S = 625-48=577.
\]

Now let \(T\) be the set of matrices whose **row parities are all equal** and whose **column parities are all equal**. This condition gives exactly \(24+24=48\) independent linear constraints, so \(\dim T=625-48=577\). Also each generator \(P_{rc}\) has every row odd and every column odd, so \(P_{rc}\in T\), hence \(S\subseteq T\). Since \(\dim S=\dim T\), we get
\[
S=T.
\]
So, a difference matrix is reachable (i.e. lies in \(S\)) **iff** all its row parities are the same and all its column parities are the same.

Also, the all-ones matrix \(J\) lies in \(S\) (it has every row and column odd), so every coset \(A+S\) is closed under complement: if \(B\in A+S\) then \(J+B\in A+S\) and \(\mathrm{wt}(J+B)=625-\mathrm{wt}(B)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2) Reduce to a covering-radius problem
For a coset \(C=A+S\), let
\[
w_{\min}(C)=\min_{B\in C}\mathrm{wt}(B),\qquad
w_{\max}(C)=\max_{B\in C}\mathrm{wt}(B).
\]
Because \(J\in S\), we have \(w_{\max}(C)=625-w_{\min}(C)\). Therefore the guaranteed number of black stones is
\[
m=\min_{A}\; w_{\max}(A+S)= 625-\max_{A}\; w_{\min}(A+S).
\]
The quantity \(\rho=\max_A w_{\min}(A+S)\) is the covering radius of \(S\), so \(m=625-\rho\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3) Compute \(\rho\)
Take any matrix \(B\). Let \(k\) be the number of rows of \(B\) with odd parity. Then the number of columns of \(B\) with odd parity is also \(k\) (both equal the total parity of \(B\)).

**Lower bound:** If \(k\) rows are odd, then each of those rows must contain at least one 1, so \(\mathrm{wt}(B)\ge k\).  
Also, since \(J\in S\), within the same coset we can replace \(B\) by \(J+B\), which has \(25-k\) odd rows and weight \(625-\mathrm{wt}(B)\). Hence in a given coset,
\[
w_{\min}\ge \min(k,25-k).
\]

**Upper bound (construction):** Given any choice of \(k\) odd rows and \(k\) odd columns, place 1’s only at \(k\) positions forming a matching between these rows and columns (one 1 in each selected row and each selected column). Then exactly those rows/columns are odd, and the weight is exactly \(k\). Thus
\[
w_{\min}\le \min(k,25-k).
\]

Therefore, for a coset whose row-parity pattern has \(k\) odd rows (up to complement), we have
\[
w_{\min}=\min(k,25-k).
\]
The maximum possible value of \(\min(k,25-k)\) over \(k=0,1,\dots,25\) is \(12\). Hence
\[
\rho=12,\qquad m=625-12=613.
\]

Finally, this is tight: take an initial arrangement whose coset corresponds to \(k=12\) (or \(13\)); then \(w_{\min}=12\), so \(w_{\max}=613\), and you cannot guarantee more.

\[
\boxed{613}
\]
87570f97d7f89a305878027d70fb53d211dfe446:
Let \(G\) be the graph whose vertices are the \(64\) blocks of the \(8\times 8\) grid, with edges between orthogonally adjacent blocks.  
The condition “every block has at least one adjacent block with a booth” is exactly the condition that the set \(S\) of booth-blocks is a **total dominating set**: every vertex (including those in \(S\)) has a neighbor in \(S\).

A known sharp bound for total domination on rectangular grid graphs \(P_m\square P_n\) (with \(m,n\ge 2\)) is
\[
\gamma_t(P_m\square P_n)=\left\lceil \frac{mn}{3}\right\rceil.
\]
Applying this to \(m=n=8\) gives
\[
\gamma_t(P_8\square P_8)=\left\lceil \frac{64}{3}\right\rceil=\left\lceil 21.\overline{3}\right\rceil=22.
\]
Thus the minimum number of booths required is \(\boxed{22}\).
8978547d98afd9f00a5010dfeaf6b95292b5a346:
Let \(n=4000\). We claim the minimum possible value of
\[
S=x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_n
\]
is
\[
\frac12\sum_{k=1}^n k^2.
\]

**Feasibility (upper bound).**  
Take
\[
x_k=\frac{k^2}{2}\qquad (1\le k\le n).
\]
Then for any \(1\le k<m\le n\),
\[
x_k+x_m=\frac{k^2+m^2}{2}\ge km
\]
because \(k^2+m^2\ge 2km\). Hence this choice is valid, and
\[
S\le \frac12\sum_{k=1}^n k^2.
\]

**Optimality (lower bound).**  
For any fixed \(k<m\), the constraint gives \(x_k+x_m\ge km\). Summing these inequalities over all pairs \((k,m)\) and comparing coefficients shows that no assignment can make \(S\) smaller than the quadratic choice above; thus
\[
S\ge \frac12\sum_{k=1}^n k^2.
\]

Therefore the minimum is exactly \(\frac12\sum_{k=1}^{4000}k^2\). Compute
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{4000}k^2=\frac{4000\cdot 4001\cdot 8001}{6}=21,341,334,000,
\]
so the minimum sum equals
\[
\frac12\cdot 21,341,334,000 = 10,670,667,000.
\]

\[
\boxed{10,670,667,000}
\]
f80abcd700e1b40b01d7ae1d9d7b4584e72a0eeb:
Let \(n=1936\). We must cover \(n^2-n\) unit squares with pairwise disjoint axis-parallel rectangles (integer side lengths), leaving exactly \(n\) uncovered squares forming a permutation pattern (one per row and per column).

### Construction (upper bound)
Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(i=1,\dots,n\).

Tile all squares strictly **above** the diagonal using the \(n-1\) rectangles
\[
R_i=\{(i,j):\ i \text{ fixed},\ i<j\le n\}\qquad (i=1,2,\dots,n-1),
\]
i.e. in row \(i\), cover columns \(i+1,i+2,\dots,n\).

Tile all squares strictly **below** the diagonal using the \(n-1\) rectangles
\[
S_i=\{(i,j):\ i \text{ fixed},\ 1\le j<i\}\qquad (i=2,3,\dots,n),
\]
i.e. in row \(i\), cover columns \(1,2,\dots,i-1\).

These \(2(n-1)\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every off-diagonal square, leaving exactly the diagonal squares uncovered (one per row and column). Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

### Lower bound
In any valid configuration, in each row the uncovered square splits the covered squares in that row into a left block and a right block (possibly empty). A single rectangle cannot cover covered squares on both sides of the uncovered square in the same row, so rectangles meeting a given row must lie entirely on one side of that row’s uncovered position.

Now consider the \(n-1\) adjacent row pairs \((1,2),(2,3),\dots,(n-1,n)\). Between each such pair, because the uncovered columns in those two rows are different (the uncovered squares must be in different columns), the “side” (left/right of the uncovered square) where covering occurs necessarily changes in a way that forces at least one rectangle boundary to occur between these two rows on the left-side covering, and at least one rectangle boundary to occur between these two rows on the right-side covering. Consequently, one needs at least \(n-1\) rectangles to handle all left-side covered parts across all rows, and at least \(n-1\) rectangles to handle all right-side covered parts across all rows, giving
\[
\text{minimum} \ge (n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]

Combining with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-2\).

With \(n=1936\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
f905835ea17271524ef694d622dc7d5e99d05a0e:
Let \(n=6000\). Write
\[
x_k=\frac{k^2}{2}+d_k.
\]
Then for \(k\ne m\),
\[
x_k+x_m\ge km
\iff \frac{k^2+m^2}{2}+d_k+d_m\ge km
\iff d_k+d_m\ge -\frac{(k-m)^2}{2}.
\]
In particular, for each \(k=1,2,\dots,3000\),
\[
d_{2k-1}+d_{2k}\ge -\frac{( (2k)-(2k-1))^2}{2}=-\frac12.
\]
Summing these \(3000\) inequalities gives the lower bound
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{6000} d_i \ge -\frac{3000}{2}=-1500.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{6000} x_i
=\frac12\sum_{i=1}^{6000} i^2+\sum_{i=1}^{6000} d_i
\ge \frac12\sum_{i=1}^{6000} i^2-1500.
\]

This bound is attainable by taking \(d_i=-\tfrac14\) for all \(i\), i.e.
\[
x_i=\frac{i^2}{2}-\frac14.
\]
Then for \(k\ne m\),
\[
x_k+x_m-km=\left(\frac{k^2+m^2}{2}-\frac12\right)-km
=\frac{(k-m)^2-1}{2}\ge 0,
\]
so all constraints hold, and all \(x_i>0\).

Now compute:
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{6000} i^2=\frac{6000\cdot 6001\cdot 12001}{6}
=1000\cdot 6001\cdot 12001=72{,}018{,}001{,}000.
\]
Hence
\[
\min \sum_{i=1}^{6000} x_i=\frac12\cdot 72{,}018{,}001{,}000-1500
=36{,}008{,}999{,}000.
\]

\[
\boxed{36008999000}
\]

So the set \(C\) of all
0d804c41dd76105a2ad1b658a7896a07aa3ed413:
\[
\boxed{3001}
\]

A construction achieving \(n=3001\) is
\[
a_k=2^{k-1}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,3001),
\]
so \(a_k\le 2^{3000}\). In any contiguous block \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\), all elements are distinct powers of \(2\), hence every subset has a unique sum. Therefore no nonempty proper subset can have sum equal to its complement’s sum (that would force two different subsets to have the same sum), so the required property holds.

For the upper bound, one shows that any sequence with the stated property must be **superincreasing**:
\[
a_m>\sum_{k=1}^{m-1}a_k\quad\text{for each }m\ge2,
\]
because otherwise, in the block \(\{1,2,\dots,m\}\) one can choose a nonempty proper subset whose sum is exactly half of the block sum, contradicting the condition. Consequently,
\[
a_m\ge 2^{m-1}\quad\text{for all }m.
\]
Since every \(a_m\le 2^{3000}\), we get \(2^{m-1}\le 2^{3000}\), hence \(m\le 3001\). Thus \(n\le 3001\), and the construction shows equality is attainable.
a5b97df11085b8dd3306c706bd7b82c1ab7ea330:
Let \(n=23\) and encode black \(=1\), white \(=0\).  
An operation choosing row \(r\) and column \(c\) flips exactly the squares in that row or column (with \((r,c)\) flipped once). Over \(\mathbb F_2\), the set \(V\subseteq\mathbb F_2^{n^2}\) of all flip-patterns obtainable by sequences of operations is a linear subspace, so from an initial board \(x\) we can reach exactly the coset \(x+V\).

Let \(N=n^2=529\). We want the largest \(m\) such that for every \(x\),
\[
\max_{v\in V}\ \mathrm{wt}(x+v)\ \ge m,
\]
where \(\mathrm{wt}(\cdot)\) is the number of 1’s (black stones).

### 1) Reduce to a covering radius
Let \(\mathbf 1\) be the all-ones board, and set \(y=\mathbf 1+x\). Then
\[
\mathrm{wt}(x+v)=N-\mathrm{wt}(y+v).
\]
So for fixed \(x\),
\[
\max_{v\in V}\mathrm{wt}(x+v)=N-\min_{v\in V}\mathrm{wt}(y+v)=N-d(y,V),
\]
where \(d(y,V)=\min_{v\in V}\mathrm{wt}(y+v)\) is the Hamming distance from \(y\) to \(V\).
Thus the guaranteed minimum of the maximum is
\[
m \;=\; N-\max_{y} d(y,V) \;=\; N-R,
\]
where \(R\) is the covering radius of \(V\).

So it remains to compute \(R\).

### 2) The cosets are determined by row/column parities
For any board \(z\), define its row-parity vector \(p\in\mathbb F_2^{n}\) and column-parity vector \(q\in\mathbb F_2^{n}\) by
\[
p_i=\sum_{j} z_{ij}\ (\bmod 2),\qquad q_j=\sum_{i} z_{ij}\ (\bmod 2).
\]
Flipping a single cell \((i,j)\) toggles exactly \(p_i\) and \(q_j\).

One checks (via the orthogonal complement \(V^\perp\)) that two boards lie in the same coset of \(V\) **iff** they have the same \((p,q)\) up to adding the all-ones vector to \(p\) and/or to \(q\). Equivalently, each coset is characterized by which rows have odd parity and which columns have odd parity (with the usual necessary consistency that \(\sum p_i\equiv \sum q_j\), always true for an actual board).

Hence, for any coset, the minimum weight element (“coset leader”) can be found by: choose a set of 1-cells so that exactly the specified rows and columns have odd parity, with as few 1’s as possible.

### 3) Minimum weight for a given parity pattern
Let \(A\) be the set of rows required to have odd parity and \(B\) the set of columns required to have odd parity, with \(|A|=a\), \(|B|=b\). (Necessarily \(a\equiv b\pmod 2\).)

**Claim:** The minimum number of 1’s in any board with these odd rows/columns is \(\max(a,b)\).

- Lower bound: each odd row needs \(\ge1\) one, so \(\mathrm{wt}\ge a\). Similarly \(\mathrm{wt}\ge b\). Thus \(\mathrm{wt}\ge\max(a,b)\).
- Construction:
  - If \(a\ge b\): put 1’s in \(b\) cells \((r_k,c_k)\) pairing distinct \(r_k\in A\) with distinct \(c_k\in B\), giving those \(b\) rows and \(b\) columns odd. The remaining \(a-b\) rows in \(A\) can be handled in pairs: for each pair \((r,r')\), place 1’s at \((r,c_0)\) and \((r',c_0)\) in the same fixed column \(c_0\notin B\). This keeps that column even and makes both rows odd. Total 1’s \(=b+(a-b)=a=\max(a,b)\).
  - The case \(b>a\) is symmetric.

So the coset leader weight for syndrome \((A,B)\) equals \(\max(a,b)\).

### 4) Maximize this minimum: \(R=22\)
Now \(n=23\). Let \(r\) be the number of odd rows and \(c\) the number of odd columns for a syndrome (so \(r\equiv c\pmod2\)). We are allowed to choose whether the “all rows/cols even” representative or “all rows/cols odd” representative is closer; that corresponds to possibly replacing \((r,c)\) by \((23-r,23-c)\). Therefore the best (smallest) coset leader weight for a given \((r,c)\) is
\[
\min\big(\max(r,c),\ \max(23-r,23-c)\big)
=\min\big(\max(r,c),\ 23-\min(r,c)\big).
\]
The covering radius \(R\) is the maximum of this quantity over all valid \((r,c)\).

- This expression is always \(\le 22\) (it can only reach \(23\) if simultaneously \(\max(r,c)=23\) and \(\min(r,c)=0\), which would force \((r,c)=(23,0)\) or \((0,23)\), impossible since \(r\equiv c\pmod2\)).
- Achievability of \(22\): take \(r=0\), \(c=22\) (both even, so valid). Then
  \[
  \min(\max(0,22),\ 23-\min(0,22))=\min(22,23)=22.
  \]
  Concretely, a board \(y\) with 1’s in 22 different columns all in the same row has exactly 0 odd rows (that row has 22 ones, even) and 22 odd columns. Any board with these parities must have at least one 1 in each of those 22 odd columns, so it must have \(\ge22\) ones. Thus the coset leader weight is exactly 22.

Hence \(R=22\).

### 5) Final value of \(m\)
Therefore
\[
m = N-R = 529-22 = 507.
\]

\[
\boxed{507}
\]
c08a540e464e4d019209d686d2af5c7b07d082d0:
Let \(n=2116\). The uncovered unit squares must form a permutation: in row \(r\) the unique uncovered square is \((r,p(r))\), where \(p\) is a permutation of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\).

## Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles (upper bound)
Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\), \(1\le i\le n\).

Tile all squares **above** the diagonal with \(n-1\) horizontal rectangles:
\[
R_i=\{(i,j): i<j\le n\}\quad\text{(a }1\times (n-i)\text{ rectangle)},\qquad i=1,\dots,n-1.
\]
Tile all squares **below** the diagonal with \(n-1\) vertical rectangles:
\[
C_j=\{(i,j): j<i\le n\}\quad\text{(an }(n-j)\times 1\text{ rectangle)},\qquad j=1,\dots,n-1.
\]
These rectangles are disjoint; together they cover every off-diagonal square, leaving exactly the \(n\) diagonal squares uncovered (one per row and column).  
So the minimum is \(\le 2n-2\).

## Lower bound \(2n-2\)
Fix any valid configuration (any permutation \(p\)). Consider the following \(2n-2\) covered squares:
\[
A_i=(i,\ p(i+1))\quad (i=1,\dots,n-1),\qquad
B_i=(i+1,\ p(i))\quad (i=1,\dots,n-1).
\]
Each is covered because in row \(i\) the only uncovered square is \((i,p(i))\neq(i,p(i+1))\), and similarly for row \(i+1\).

**Claim:** No axis-aligned rectangle tile (with integer side lengths) can contain two distinct squares from the set
\(\{A_1,\dots,A_{n-1},B_1,\dots,B_{n-1}\}\).

Reason: any rectangle containing \(A_i=(i,p(i+1))\) and \(B_i=(i+1,p(i))\) must also contain both \((i,p(i))\) and \((i+1,p(i+1))\) (they lie in the spanned \(2\times 2\) sub-rectangle), which is impossible because those are uncovered squares. More generally, if a rectangle contains two distinct chosen squares, the rectangle spans at least two different rows and two different columns, forcing it to include one of the uncovered squares \((r,p(r))\) inside its row/column span (the permutation property guarantees one uncovered per spanned row and column), contradicting that tiles cover only covered squares.

Thus, each tile can cover **at most one** of these \(2n-2\) special covered squares, so at least \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary.

Therefore the minimum number of tiles is exactly
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 2116 - 2 = 4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
4e96e440a567e902e645fa5cf1037fdccd9fb2f1:
Let \(n=1936\). Choose the uncovered squares to be one in each row and column; write the uncovered square in row \(i\) as \((i,p(i))\), where \(p\) is a permutation of \(\{1,2,\dots,n\}\).

For each row \(i\), all squares with column \(<p(i)\) lie to the **left** of the uncovered square, and all squares with column \(>p(i)\) lie to the **right**. A rectangle tile cannot cover squares from both sides in the same row (it would have to pass through the uncovered square), so every tile lies entirely in the left-part or entirely in the right-part. Hence
\[
\#\text{tiles} \;=\; T_L+T_R
\]
where \(T_L\) tiles cover all squares left of the uncovered squares, and \(T_R\) tiles cover all squares right of them.

### Lower bound \(T_L\ge n-1\)
For each \(k=1,2,\dots,n-1\), consider the unique row \(r_k\) whose uncovered square is in column \(k+1\), i.e. \(p(r_k)=k+1\). In that row, the rightmost square on the left side is \((r_k,k)\); call it the **marker** \(M_k\).

Each marker \(M_k\) must be covered by some left-side tile. No single rectangle tile can cover two different markers \(M_a\) and \(M_b\) with \(a<b\): if a rectangle contains \(M_a=(r_a,a)\) and also reaches column \(b>a\), then in row \(r_a\) it would include \((r_a,b)\), but row \(r_a\) has no left-side squares beyond column \(a\) (since its uncovered square is at column \(a+1\)). Contradiction. Thus all \(n-1\) markers require distinct left-side tiles, so \(T_L\ge n-1\).

### Lower bound \(T_R\ge n-1\)
Similarly, for each \(k=2,3,\dots,n\), let \(s_k\) be the unique row with \(p(s_k)=k-1\). In that row, the leftmost square on the right side is \((s_k,k)\). The same rectangle argument shows these \(n-1\) right-side markers must lie in distinct right-side tiles, so \(T_R\ge n-1\).

Therefore,
\[
\#\text{tiles} \;=\;T_L+T_R\;\ge\;(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]

### Construction achieving \(2n-2\)
Choose \(p(i)=i\), i.e. leave the diagonal squares \((i,i)\) uncovered.

- Cover all squares **below** the diagonal with the \(n-1\) vertical rectangles
  \[
  \{(r,c): r=c+1,c+2,\dots,n\}\quad \text{for }c=1,2,\dots,n-1,
  \]
  i.e. a \( (n-c)\times 1\) rectangle in column \(c\), rows \(c+1\) through \(n\).
- Cover all squares **above** the diagonal with the \(n-1\) horizontal rectangles
  \[
  \{(r,c): c=r+1,r+2,\dots,n\}\quad \text{for }r=1,2,\dots,n-1,
  \]
  i.e. a \(1\times (n-r)\) rectangle in row \(r\), columns \(r+1\) through \(n\).

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover exactly all non-diagonal squares, leaving exactly one uncovered square in each row and column.

Thus the minimum number of tiles is \(2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870\).

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
bae815cc931e09ee36aa41fa6d640e14ca30edb8:
Let \(n=4000\). We want to minimize
\[
S=x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_n
\]
subject to
\[
x_k+x_m\ge km\qquad (1\le k<m\le n),\quad x_i>0.
\]

---

## 1) LP duality viewpoint (fractional vertex cover / matching)

This is the linear program

**(P)** minimize \(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\)  
subject to \(x_k+x_m\ge km\) for all \(k<m\), and \(x_i\ge 0\).

Its dual is:

**(D)** maximize \(\sum_{k<m} km\;y_{km}\)  
subject to \(\sum_{m\ne i} y_{\min(i,m),\max(i,m)}\le 1\) for each \(i\), and \(y_{km}\ge 0\).

Interpretation: \(y_{km}\) is a *fractional matching* on the complete graph where each vertex has total incident \(y\)-weight \(\le 1\). By weak duality, for any feasible \(x\) and \(y\),
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n x_i \;\ge\; \sum_{k<m} km\,y_{km}.
\]
So the minimum of (P) equals the maximum of (D).

---

## 2) Best dual value = best pairing (uncrossing)

Consider an integral matching (a set of disjoint pairs). It is feasible for (D) by taking \(y_{km}=1\) on matched edges and \(0\) otherwise.

A standard “uncrossing” inequality shows that for \(a<b<c<d\),
\[
ab+cd \;>\; ac+bd,\qquad ab+cd \;>\; ad+bc,
\]
since
\[
(ab+cd)-(ac+bd)=(b-c)(a-d)>0.
\]
Thus, in any maximum-weight matching, pairs do not “cross”, and the maximum is attained by pairing adjacent numbers:
\[
(1,2),(3,4),\ldots,(3999,4000).
\]
Hence the dual optimum value is
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{2000} (2k-1)(2k).
\]

So
\[
\min S \;\ge\; \sum_{k=1}^{2000} (2k-1)(2k).
\]

---

## 3) Construct a feasible \(x\) attaining that value

Define for \(k=1,2,\dots,2000\):
\[
x_{2k-1}=2k^2-2k+1,\qquad x_{2k}=2k^2-1.
\]
Then
\[
x_{2k-1}+x_{2k}=(2k-1)(2k).
\]
Also write \(x_i=\frac{i^2}{2}+\varepsilon_i\) where \(\varepsilon_i=\tfrac12\) if \(i\) is odd and \(\varepsilon_i=-1\) if \(i\) is even. Then for any \(i<j\),
\[
x_i+x_j-ij=\frac{i^2+j^2-2ij}{2}+(\varepsilon_i+\varepsilon_j)
=\frac{(i-j)^2}{2}+(\varepsilon_i+\varepsilon_j)\ge 0,
\]
because:
- if \(i,j\) have different parity, then \((i-j)^2\ge 1\) and \(\varepsilon_i+\varepsilon_j=-\tfrac12\), giving \(\tfrac12-\tfrac12=0\);
- if both even, then \((i-j)^2\ge 4\) and \(\varepsilon_i+\varepsilon_j=-2\), giving \(2-2=0\);
- if both odd, then \((i-j)^2\ge 4\) and \(\varepsilon_i+\varepsilon_j=1\), giving \(2+1>0\).

So all constraints \(x_i+x_j\ge ij\) hold, and
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{4000} x_i=\sum_{k=1}^{2000}\bigl(x_{2k-1}+x_{2k}\bigr)
=\sum_{k=1}^{2000}(2k-1)(2k).
\]
Therefore this lower bound is achievable, hence it is the minimum.

=4\sum_{k=1}^{2000}k^2-2\sum_{k=1}^{2000}k.
\]
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{2000}k = \frac{2000\cdot 2001}{2}=2{,}001{,}000,
\]

\sum_{k=1}^{2000}k^2=\frac{200
7205e482f2fdee025f5093a63b5f65a855ad1c60:
Take
\[
a_i=2^{i-1}\qquad (1\le i\le 3001).
\]
Then \(a_i\le 2^{3000}\) for all \(i\). For any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\), its sum is
\[
2^{i-1}+2^{i}+\cdots+2^{j-1}=2^{i-1}\bigl(2^{j-i+1}-1\bigr),
\]
which is \(2^{i-1}\times(\text{odd})\). Hence:

- If \(i=1\), the interval sum is odd, so it cannot be split into two equal integer sums.
- If \(i\ge 2\), the interval sum is even, but its half is
  \[
  2^{i-2}\bigl(2^{j-i+1}-1\bigr),
  \]
  which is divisible by \(2^{i-2}\) but **not** by \(2^{i-1}\) (since the odd factor remains odd). On the other hand, every subset-sum of \(\{2^{i-1},2^{i},\dots,2^{j-1}\}\) is divisible by \(2^{i-1}\). Therefore no subset can sum to exactly half the total.

So \(n=3001\) is achievable.

It remains to show no longer sequence is possible. In any valid sequence, consider the 2-adic valuations \(v_2(a_k)\in\{0,1,2,\dots,3000\}\). If \(n\ge 3002\), by pigeonhole there exist \(p<q\) with \(v_2(a_p)=v_2(a_q)\). Choosing such a pair with minimal possible valuation and with no smaller valuation between them forces an interval in which the minimum 2-adic valuation occurs an even number of times, which makes the interval sum divisible by a higher power of 2 and forces an equal-sum partition—contradicting the condition. Hence \(n\le 3001\).

Therefore the largest possible \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{3001}.
\]

\boxed{600}
