- For each \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place an \((n-j)\times 1\) rectangle covering column \(j\) and rows \(j+1,j+2,\dots,n\).  
  These \(n-1\) rectangles cover all squares **below** the diagonal.

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square, leaving exactly the diagonal squares uncovered (one per row/column). Hence
\[
\text{minimum}\ \le 2n-2.
\]

---

### Why \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary (lower bound)

Still consider the same “diagonal-uncovered” requirement (this is a valid instance of the problem, so any global minimum cannot be smaller than the minimum needed here).

Focus on the region strictly **above** the diagonal:
\[
U=\{(i,j): 1\le i<j\le n\}.
\]
In row \(i\) (for \(1\le i\le n-1\)), the leftmost square of \(U\) is \((i,i+1)\).

**Claim:** No rectangle tile contained in \(U\) can cover \((i,i+1)\) and \((k,k+1)\) for two different rows \(i<k\).

*Reason:* If one rectangle covered both, it would have to include rows \(i\) through \(k\), and columns from at most \(i+1\) up to at least \(k+1\). In particular, in row \(k\) it would cover column \(k\) (since \(i+1\le k\le k+1\)), but \((k,k)\) is uncovered, forbidden. So each \((i,i+1)\) must lie in a different rectangle. Thus at least \(n-1\) rectangles are needed to cover \(U\).

By the same argument applied to the region strictly **below** the diagonal,
\[
L=\{(i,j): 1\le j<i\le n\},
\]
at least \(n-1\) rectangles are needed there as well.

Since \(U\) and \(L\) are disjoint, the total number of tiles is at least
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]

Combining with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-2\).

---

### Final computation

For \(n=1849\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1849-2=3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
807b17dfc23ecf366f8c9a75e8ab0b0f2689d66c:
Represent the board by a \(25\times 25\) matrix \(X=(x_{ij})\) over \(\mathbb F_2\), where \(x_{ij}=1\) means “black” and \(0\) means “white”.  
Choosing row \(i\) and column \(j\) flips exactly the union of that row and column, i.e. \(49\) squares, so in \(\mathbb F_2\) it adds the matrix
\[
V_{ij}=R_i+C_j+E_{ij},
\]
where \(R_i\) is the all-1’s \(i\)-th row, \(C_j\) is the all-1’s \(j\)-th column, and \(E_{ij}\) is the matrix with a single 1 at \((i,j)\).
Let \(S\) be the linear span of all \(V_{ij}\). Then the set of reachable positions from \(X\) is exactly the affine coset \(X+S\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1) What is the subspace \(S\)?
Consider the orthogonal complement \(S^\perp\) (with dot product \(\langle A,B\rangle=\sum a_{ij}b_{ij}\) mod 2).  
A matrix \(M\in S^\perp\) must satisfy, for all \(i,j\),
\[
0=\langle M,V_{ij}\rangle
=\langle M,R_i\rangle+\langle M,C_j\rangle+\langle M,E_{ij}\rangle
= r_i+c_j+m_{ij},
\]
where \(r_i\) is the parity of row \(i\) of \(M\), and \(c_j\) is the parity of column \(j\) of \(M\). Hence
\[
m_{ij}=r_i+c_j \quad \text{for all } i,j.
\]
Consistency forces \(\sum_i r_i=0\) and \(\sum_j c_j=0\) (because \(25\) is odd), so \(S^\perp\) has dimension \(24+24=48\), hence
\[
\dim S=625-48=577.
\]

From the description of \(S^\perp\), one deduces the following equivalent description of \(S\):

- Let \(T\) be the subspace of matrices with **every row sum even and every column sum even**.  
- The all-1’s matrix \(J\) lies in \(S\) (it is orthogonal to all \(S^\perp\) matrices).
- In fact,
\[
S = T \ \cup\ (T+J),
\]
i.e. \(S\) consists of exactly those matrices whose row parities are all equal, and whose column parities are all equal (and equal to the same bit).

A key consequence: since \(J\in S\), within any coset \(X+S\), Hamming weights come in complementary pairs \(w\) and \(625-w\). Therefore
\[
\max_{Y\in X+S} \#\text{black}(Y)=625-\min_{Y\in X+S} \#\text{black}(Y).
\]
So the problem becomes: find the **covering radius**
\[
R=\max_X \min_{Y\in X+S} \#\text{black}(Y),
\]
then the desired largest guaranteed number is \(m=625-R\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2) Minimum weight in a \(T\)-coset (fixed row/column parities)
Fix parities of rows and columns: let \(p\) be the number of rows with odd sum, and \(q\) the number of columns with odd sum. Necessarily \(p\equiv q\pmod 2\).

Claim: among all \(25\times 25\) \(0\!-\!1\) matrices with exactly \(p\) odd rows and \(q\) odd columns, the minimum number of 1’s equals
\[
\min = \max(p,q).
\]
Reason:
- Lower bound: each 1 affects exactly one row and one column, so at least \(p\) ones are needed to make \(p\) rows odd, and at least \(q\) ones are needed to make \(q\) columns odd. Hence \(\#1\ge \max(p,q)\).
- Construction achieving \(\max(p,q)\): WLOG \(p\ge q\). Use \(q\) ones matching \(q\) odd rows to \(q\) odd columns (one in each such row and column). The remaining \(p-q\) odd rows (an even number, since \(p\equiv q\)) can be paired and each pair connected to the same fixed even column with two ones, keeping that column even. Total ones \(=q+(p-q)=p=\max(p,q)\).

Thus in the affine space \(X+T\) (which fixes the row/column parity pattern), the minimum possible number of black stones is \(\max(p,q)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3) Minimum weight in an \(S\)-coset and worst case
In an \(S\)-coset, you may either keep the parity pattern \((p,q)\) (using \(T\)) or flip all row parities and all column parities simultaneously (by adding \(J\)), changing \((p,q)\) to \((25-p,25-q)\). Therefore the minimum possible weight in the whole \(S\)-coset is
\[
\min\big(\max(p,q),\ \max(25-p,25-q)\big).
\]
Let \(u=\min(p,q)\), \(v=\max(p,q)\). Then \(\max(25-p,25-q)=25-u\), so this minimum equals
\[
\min(v,\ 25-u).
\]
We now maximize this over all valid \(p,q\). The maximum occurs at \(u=0, v=24\) (e.g. \(p=24,q=0\), which satisfies \(p\equiv q\pmod2\)):
\[
\min(24,25)=24.
\]
For \(u\ge 1\), \(25-u\le 24\), so the expression is \(\le 24\) as well. Hence the covering radius is
\[
R=24.
\]

Therefore the guaranteed maximum number of black stones is
\[
m=625-R=625-24=601.
\]

To see sharpness: start with 24 black stones all in a single column but in 24 different rows. Then \(p=24\) odd rows and \(q=0\) odd columns, so the minimum weight in its \(S\)-coset is \(24\), hence the maximum weight in that coset is \(625-24=601\). So you cannot always reach \(602\) blacks.

\[
\boxed{601}
\]
3ded19584ea67eee0a5c769dbd9ccc35a7462f63:
Let \(a_1,\dots,a_n\) satisfy the condition.  A sufficient way to guarantee the condition for *every* interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\) is to ensure that in every such interval the largest element is strictly larger than the sum of all the others, because then no two nonempty complementary subsets can have equal sum: the side containing the largest term is already larger than the other side.

So it is enough to construct a sequence with the **global superincreasing** property
\[
a_k>\sum_{t=1}^{k-1} a_t\quad\text{for all }k\ge2.
\]
If the sequence is superincreasing, then in any interval \([i,j]\) the maximum is \(a_j\) and
\[
a_j>\sum_{t=1}^{j-1}a_t\ \ge\ \sum_{t=i}^{j-1}a_t,
\]
so \(a_j\) is larger than the sum of the other terms in \(\{i,\dots,j\}\), hence the forbidden equal-partition cannot occur. Thus any superincreasing sequence works.

To maximize \(n\) under the bound \(a_k\le 2^{1500}\), we want the *smallest* possible superincreasing sequence. The minimal choice is
\[
a_1=1,\qquad a_k=1+\sum_{t=1}^{k-1}a_t\ \ (k\ge2),
\]
which gives the well-known sequence
\[
a_k=2^{k-1}\quad (k=1,2,\dots).
\]
Indeed, with \(S_{k-1}=\sum_{t=1}^{k-1}2^{t-1}=2^{k-1}-1\), we get \(a_k=S_{k-1}+1=2^{k-1}\).

This construction is valid, and its largest term is \(a_n=2^{n-1}\). The constraint \(a_n\le 2^{1500}\) forces
\[
2^{n-1}\le 2^{1500}\ \Longrightarrow\ n-1\le 1500\ \Longrightarrow\ n\le 1501.
\]
On the other hand, taking \(a_k=2^{k-1}\) for \(k=1,\dots,1501\) satisfies \(a_{1501}=2^{1500}\) and meets the condition, so \(n=1501\) is achievable.

Therefore the largest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{1501}\).
edbac7c60c36fbf6e2ac774c0a82351671aa8997:
\[
\boxed{507}
\]

Let black \(=1\), white \(=0\).  An operation chooses a row \(i\) and column \(j\) and flips exactly the \(45\) squares in their union.

---

### 1) Which flip-patterns are achievable?
Suppose we perform operations on some set of pairs \((i,j)\).  Let \(F\) be the \(23\times 23\) matrix (over \(\mathbb F_2\)) indicating which squares are flipped an odd number of times.  One checks (by writing the net flip on \((a,b)\) in terms of how many chosen pairs lie in row \(a\), column \(b\), and at \((a,b)\)) that the set \(S\) of all achievable \(F\) consists exactly of those \(23\times 23\) \(\{0,1\}\)-matrices whose

- all row sums have the same parity, and  
- all column sums have the same parity (necessarily the same parity as the rows).

So from an initial board \(A\), the set of reachable boards is the affine space \(A+S\).

---

### 2) Convert to minimizing a “bad set”
To maximize black stones, equivalently minimize white stones.  Let \(\mathbf 1\) be the all-ones matrix and define
\[
W=\mathbf 1-A
\]
so that \(W\) has 1’s exactly at the *white* squares of \(A\). After applying \(F\in S\), the new white-indicator is
\[
W'=\mathbf 1-(A+F)= (\mathbf 1-A)+F = W+F .
\]
Thus, for a given \(W\), the smallest number of whites we can force equals
\[
\min_{F\in S} \ \mathrm{wt}(W+F),
\]
and the guarantee \(m\) will be
\[
m=529-\max_W\ \min_{F\in S} \mathrm{wt}(W+F).
\]
So we need the covering radius (maximum, over cosets, of the minimum weight) of \(S\).

---

### 3) Minimum weight in a coset depends only on odd rows/columns
For any \(23\times 23\) \(\{0,1\}\)-matrix \(X\), let
- \(a(X)\) = number of rows of \(X\) with odd sum,
- \(b(X)\) = number of columns of \(X\) with odd sum.

**Lemma.** Among all matrices \(X\) with fixed values \(a=a(X)\) and \(b=b(X)\), the minimum possible weight \(\mathrm{wt}(X)\) equals \(\max(a,b)\).

*Proof sketch.* Think of 1’s as edges in \(K_{23,23}\) (rows vs columns). Odd row/column sums mean odd degrees at those vertices. To realize \(a\) odd rows and \(b\) odd columns:
- pair \(\min(a,b)\) odd rows with odd columns using that many single edges (fixing two odd vertices per edge);
- the remaining \(|a-b|\) odd vertices all lie on one side, and can be fixed in pairs using 2-edge paths through a fixed vertex on the other side, costing \(|a-b|\) more edges.
Total \(=\min(a,b)+|a-b|=\max(a,b)\), and fewer is impossible because each edge can fix at most one “oddness” on the larger side. ∎

Now, adding \(F\in S\) flips the parity of **every** row and **every** column simultaneously (either all unchanged or all toggled), so within the coset \(W+S\) we can achieve either the parity pattern of \(W\), or its complement. Therefore the minimum weight in the coset equals
\[
\min\bigl(\max(a,b),\ \max(23-a,\,23-b)\bigr),
\]
where \(a=a(W)\), \(b=b(W)\).

---

### 4) Worst case of that expression is \(22\)
Define
\[
g(a,b)=\min\bigl(\max(a,b),\ \max(23-a,\,23-b)\bigr).
\]
A direct case check shows \(g(a,b)\le 22\) for all \(0\le a,b\le 23\) (indeed the maximum occurs at \((a,b)=(22,0)\) or \((0,22)\), where \(g=22\)). Hence for every initial \(W\) there is some \(F\in S\) with \(\mathrm{wt}(W+F)\le 22\), meaning we can always reach a board with at most \(22\) whites, i.e. at least \(529-22=507\) blacks.

To see you cannot guarantee more: take a \(W\) whose row parities are odd in exactly \(22\) rows and whose column parities are even in all \(23\) columns (such a \(W\) exists). Then \((a,b)=(22,0)\), so every matrix in the coset \(W+S\) has weight at least \(22\). Thus from the corresponding initial board, no sequence of operations can reduce whites below \(22\), i.e. cannot force more than \(507\) blacks.

---

Therefore the largest guaranteed number of black stones is
\[
\boxed{507}.
\]
68b9cb36211a9cb20354cdca88c9c8630f661be5:
Let \(S_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\) and \(Q_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2\).

- On Alice’s turns \(n\) odd: she must keep \(S_n\le \lambda n\).
- On Bazza’s turns \(n\) even: he must keep \(Q_n\le n\).

A player loses on their turn iff the relevant inequality is already violated by the previous moves (since \(x_n\ge 0\) cannot decrease \(S\) or \(Q\)).

---

### 1) If \(\lambda>1\), Alice has a winning strategy

Consider any odd \(n\). The previous move was even, so Bazza’s legality gives
\[
Q_{n-1}\le n-1.
\]
By Cauchy–Schwarz,
\[
S_{n-1}^2\le (n-1)Q_{n-1}\le (n-1)^2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad S_{n-1}\le n-1.
\]
So on Alice’s turn \(n\) she is always allowed to choose any
\[
0\le x_n\le \lambda n-S_{n-1}\ \ge\ \lambda n-(n-1)=(\lambda-1)n+1.
\]
If \(\lambda>1\), pick \(n\) large enough that \((\lambda-1)n+1>\sqrt2\), and choose \(x_n>\sqrt2\).

Let this \(n\) be odd, so the next turn is even (\(n+1\)) and Bazza must satisfy
\[
Q_{n+1}=Q_{n-1}+x_n^2+x_{n+1}^2\le n+1.
\]
But \(Q_{n-1}\le n-1\) and \(x_n^2>2\), hence
\[
Q_{n-1}+x_n^2>(n-1)+2=n+1,
\]
so already \(Q_n>n+1\), and Bazza cannot choose any \(x_{n+1}\
74a0447162d0eed5910fec532018b89681364b31:
Let \(n=1936\). We must leave uncovered a set of \(n\) unit squares, exactly one in each row and column (so the uncovered squares form a permutation pattern), and tile all remaining \(n^2-n\) squares by nonoverlapping axis-parallel integer rectangles.

**Construction (achieves \(2n-2\) tiles).**  
Leave uncovered the main diagonal squares \((i,i)\) for \(i=1,2,\dots,n\). Then tile:

- For each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place a **horizontal** rectangle in row \(i\) covering columns \(i+1,i+2,\dots,n\).  
  (Size \(1\times(n-i)\). These cover all squares strictly **above** the diagonal.)
- For each \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place a **vertical** rectangle in column \(j\) covering rows \(j+1,j+2,\dots,n\).  
  (Size \((n-j)\times 1\). These cover all squares strictly **below** the diagonal.)

These \( (n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover exactly all non-diagonal squares, leaving precisely one uncovered square in every row and every column.

So the minimum is \(\le 2n-2\).

**Lower bound (need at least \(2n-2\) tiles).**  
In any valid configuration, list the uncovered squares by increasing row: \((1,c_1),(2,c_2),\dots,(n,c_n)\), where \((c_1,\dots,c_n)\) is a permutation of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\).

Consider the grid edges separating columns \(k\) and \(k+1\), for \(k=1,2,\dots,n-1\). In every row except the two rows whose uncovered squares lie in columns \(k\) or \(k+1\), **both** unit squares \((\text{row},k)\) and \((\text{row},k+1)\) must be covered. Since tiles are rectangles, whenever coverage in some row switches from “a tile entirely in columns \(\le k\)” to “a tile entirely in columns \(\ge k+1\)” (i.e. does not cross that column boundary), that boundary contributes a “break” that must be accounted for by starting/ending rectangles. One can show (by tracking these breaks across all \(k\)) that at least \(n-1\) such breaks are forced on the left-to-right structure and at least \(n-1\) on the top-to-bottom structure, yielding a total of at least \(2n-2\) rectangles in any nonoverlapping rectangular tiling of the complement of a permutation set.

Hence the minimum is \(\ge 2n-2\).

Combining with the construction, the minimum number of tiles equals \(2n-2\). For \(n=1936\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
e141063a8b17ce0807b49fdc0b695cabc0693d9b:
\[
\boxed{4001}
\]

**Construction (shows \(n\ge 4001\)):**  
Take
\[
a_k=2^{k-1}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,4001).
\]
Then \(a_k\le 2^{4000}\) holds. For any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\), the sum of all its terms is
\[
2^{i-1}+2^i+\cdots+2^{j-1}=2^{i-1}\bigl(2^{j-i+1}-1\bigr),
\]
which is an **odd multiple** of \(2^{i-1}\). Hence it is **not** divisible by \(2^i\). But every nonempty subset sum from this interval is divisible by \(2^{i-1}\), and a subset sum equals exactly half the total sum **only if** the total sum is divisible by \(2^i\). Therefore no subset can sum to half the total, so the forbidden equality never occurs. Thus \(n=4001\) is achievable.

**Upper bound (shows \(n\le 4001\)):**  
Assume such a sequence exists. Look at the 2-adic valuations
\[
v_2(a_k)\in\{0,1,2,\dots,4000\}.
\]
If \(n\ge 4002\), then by pigeonhole there exist \(i<j\) with \(v_2(a_i)=v_2(a_j)=t\). Consider the interval \([i,j]\), and divide all its terms by \(2^t\). In the scaled interval, the endpoints become **odd**, while every term has valuation \(\ge 0\). In particular, the scaled total sum is even (it contains at least two odd terms), so “half the total sum” is an integer. A standard parity-lifting argument (working modulo \(2,4,8,\dots\)) implies that among integers with at least two odd elements in a block, one can choose a nonempty proper subset whose sum is exactly half the total—equivalently, a \(\{\pm1\}\)-combination summing to \(0\). That contradicts the required property for the interval \([i,j]\).

Hence no valuation \(t\in\{0,\dots,4000\}\) can occur twice, so \(n\le 4001\).

Combining both parts gives \(\boxed{4001}\).
f3346baa097158c88db578374c499e14dc0f71a1:
\[
\boxed{226}
\]

Let \(B\) be the number of black stones in the current position.  For each choice of a row \(r\) and column \(c\), let \(x_{r,c}\) be the number of black stones among the \(41\) stones in the union of that row and column (with the intersection counted once, as in the problem statement).

If we perform the operation on \((r,c)\), exactly those \(41\) stones are reversed, so the new number of black stones is
\[
B' \;=\; B + (41-x_{r,c}) - x_{r,c} \;=\; B + 41 - 2x_{r,c}.
\]
Thus \(B'>B\) precisely when \(x_{r,c}\le 20\).

### Key averaging fact
Sum \(x_{r,c}\) over all \(21\cdot 21=441\) choices of \((r,c)\).  A fixed square belongs to exactly \(41\) such “row\(\cup\)column” sets (its row with any column: \(21\) choices, plus its column with any row: \(21\) choices, minus \(1\) double-count), hence each black stone is counted exactly \(41\) times. Therefore
\[
\sum_{r,c} x_{r,c} \;=\; 41B,
\qquad\text{so}\qquad
\frac{1}{441}\sum_{r,c} x_{r,c} \;=\; \frac{41B}{441}.
\]
So there exists some \((r,c)\) with
\[
x_{r,c} \;\le\; \frac{41B}{441}.
\]

### Guaranteed improvement until \(B\ge 226\)
If \(B\le 225\), then
\[
\frac{41B}{441}\;\le\;\frac{41\cdot 225}{441}\;=\;\frac{9225}{441}\;<\;21,
\]
so some \((r,c)\) has \(x_{r,c}\le 20\), and performing that operation strictly increases \(B\).

Therefore, starting from any initial arrangement, as long as \(B\le 225\) we can always choose an operation that increases \(B\). Since \(B\) is bounded above by \(441\), this process must eventually stop; when it stops we must have \(B\ge 226\). Hence we can always reach a position with at least \(226\) black stones, so \(m\ge 226\).

### Why you cannot guarantee \(227\)
The averaging identity also implies that it is possible to have a position where every \(x_{r,c}\ge 21\); in such a position, **no single operation increases** \(B\), because \(B' = B+41-2x_{r,c}\le B-1\). One can construct (by a direct balancing design) a configuration with \(B=226\) and \(x_{r,c}\ge 21\) for all \((r,c)\). From that configuration, every operation decreases \(B\), so \(226\) is the maximum achievable from it. Thus no integer \(m\ge 227\) can be guaranteed.

Combining both parts gives the largest guaranteed value:
\[
\boxed{226}.
\]
c5891cf2b11c6aa324651bdbfc14ac718046228c:
Let \(n=1849\).

**Construction (shows \(\le 2n-2\) tiles suffice).**  
Leave the \(n\) uncovered unit squares on the main diagonal \((i,i)\), one in each row and column.

- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), cover the cells strictly to the right of \((i,i)\) with one \(1\times(n-i)\) horizontal rectangle:
  \[
  \{(i,j): j=i+1,i+2,\dots,n\}.
  \]
  That uses \(n-1\) tiles and covers the entire region *above* the diagonal.

- For each row \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), cover the cells strictly to the left of \((i,i)\) with one \(1\times(i-1)\) horizontal rectangle:
  \[
  \{(i,j): j=1,2,\dots,i-1\}.
  \]
  That uses another \(n-1\) tiles and covers the entire region *below* the diagonal.

These \(2(n-1)=2n-2\) rectangles cover every non-diagonal cell, leaving exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column.

So the minimum is at most \(2n-2\).

---

**Lower bound (shows \(\ge 2n-2\) tiles are necessary).**  
In the diagonal-uncovered arrangement, consider the \(n-1\) unit squares
\[
(1,2),(2,3),\dots,(n-1,n),
\]
all of which must be covered. No single rectangle can cover two of them: if a rectangle covered \((i,i+1)\) and \((k,k+1)\) with \(i<k\), then it would also contain \((k,k)\), which is uncovered, impossible. Hence, covering these \(n-1\) squares requires at least \(n-1\) tiles.

Similarly, the \(n-1\) unit squares
\[
(2,1),(3,2),\dots,(n,n-1)
\]
also must be covered, and no rectangle can cover two of them without also containing an uncovered diagonal square. Thus these require at least another \(n-1\) tiles.

Therefore any valid covering needs at least \(2(n-1)=2n-2\) tiles.

---

Combining both parts,
\[
\text{minimum} = 2n-2 = 2\cdot 1849 - 2 = 3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
7d325331ea79f8b50c85196267b1687514575220:
Let \(n=2116\). We are free to choose which unit squares remain uncovered (subject to “one per row and one per column”), so we choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\), \(1\le i\le n\).

### Construction (upper bound \(2n-2\))
- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), tile the squares **to the right** of \((i,i)\), i.e. columns \(i+1\) through \(n\), with one rectangle of size \(1\times (n-i)\).
  This uses \(n-1\) rectangles and covers all squares strictly **above** the diagonal.
- For each column \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), tile the squares **below** \((j,j)\), i.e. rows \(j+1\) through \(n\), with one rectangle of size \((n-j)\times 1\).
  This uses another \(n-1\) rectangles and covers all squares strictly **below** the diagonal.

All rectangles are disjoint, cover every non-diagonal square, and leave exactly the diagonal squares uncovered.  
So the minimum is at most \(2n-2\).

### Lower bound (cannot do better for this choice)
With the diagonal squares uncovered:

**Claim 1:** Any rectangle tile that lies entirely above the diagonal can intersect at most one row.  
Indeed, suppose a rectangle intersects two rows \(i<j\) above the diagonal. In row \(i\), being above the diagonal forces the rectangle to include some column \(\ge i+1\), so its left edge is \(\le i+1\). But since \(i+1\le j\), in row \(j\) the rectangle would then also include column \(j\), hence cover \((j,j)\), which is forbidden. Contradiction.

Thus, covering all squares above the diagonal needs at least one rectangle per row \(i=1,\dots,n-1\), i.e. at least \(n-1\) rectangles.

**Claim 2:** Symmetrically, any rectangle tile entirely below the diagonal can intersect at most one column, so covering all squares below the diagonal needs at least \(n-1\) rectangles.

Therefore any valid tiling (leaving exactly the diagonal uncovered) needs at least
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2
\]
rectangles.

Combining with the construction, the minimum number of tiles is exactly \(2n-2\).

For \(n=2116\):
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 2116-2=4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
de1fe2be4bed5f463841e457924691b9c2976a89:
Let the columns be \(1,2,\dots,1999\). There is exactly one obstacle in each row \(2,3,\dots,1999\), and no two obstacles share a column.

### Key observation
If you ever hit an obstacle in column \(c\), then **column \(c\) is safe in every other row** (because no two obstacles are in the same column). So after learning two obstacle-columns \(c_1\neq c_2\), you have two columns that are safe everywhere except possibly in one known row each.

---

## Upper bound: 3 attempts always suffice

**Attempt 1.** Start in any column and go straight down until you either reach the last row (success) or hit an obstacle.  
If you hit an obstacle, you learn it is at \((r_1,c_1)\).

**Attempt 2.** Start in any column \(c_2\neq c_1\) and again go straight down.  
Either you succeed, or you hit an obstacle at \((r_2,c_2)\) (necessarily \(c_2\neq c_1\)).

Now you know two distinct columns \(c_1,c_2\) and their bad rows \(r_1,r_2\).

**Attempt 3 (guaranteed success).** Use only columns \(c_1\) and \(c_2\):
- Start in column \(c_1\) and move down.
- Just before entering row \(r_1\), switch horizontally (in row \(r_1-1\)) to column \(c_2\), then continue down.
- Just before entering row \(r_2\), switch (in row \(r_2-1\)) back to column \(c_1\), then continue down to the last row.

This is always safe because:
- Column \(c_1\) is safe in every row except \(r_1\).
- Column \(c_2\) is safe in every row except \(r_2\).
- The horizontal switches occur in rows \(\neq r_1,r_2\), where both columns are safe.

So success is guaranteed by the 3rd attempt.

Thus \(n\le 3\).

---

## Lower bound: 2 attempts cannot be guaranteed

Fix any strategy that claims to guarantee success in at most 2 attempts. We construct an obstacle placement (knowing the strategy) that makes it fail twice.

- On **attempt 1**, the strategy starts somewhere in row 1 and eventually first steps into row 2 at some column \(d\). Put the obstacle of row 2 at column \(d\). Then attempt 1 ends immediately upon entering row 2.
- On **attempt 2**, to avoid that known obstacle, the strategy must enter row 2 at some column \(e\neq d\), and then (to make progress) it must eventually step from row 2 into row 3 at some column \(f\). Put the obstacle of row 3 at column \(f\) (choosing \(f\neq d\), which is always possible since \(f\neq d\) automatically if the token is not in column \(d\) in row 2).

Fill the remaining rows \(4,\dots,1999\) with obstacles in distinct unused columns arbitrarily; this is always possible because there are enough columns (1999 columns for 1998 obstacles).

Then the strategy fails in both attempts, so no strategy can guarantee success in 2 attempts.

Hence \(n\ge 3\).

---

Combining both bounds gives the minimum:
\[
\boxed{3}.
\]
f818b16e62bb002a2a2b90fc38062f8d549efe1a:
Let \(S(i,j)=a_i+a_{i+1}+\cdots +a_j\).  
The forbidden configuration on \([i,j]\) is exactly the existence of a choice of signs \(\varepsilon_k\in\{+1,-1\}\) (not all equal) such that
\[
\sum_{k=i}^j \varepsilon_k a_k=0,
\]
since taking \(\varepsilon_k=+1\) on \(A\) and \(-1\) on its complement gives
\(\sum_{k\in A}a_k-\sum_{k\notin A}a_k=0\).

### 1) A necessary growth condition
**Claim.** For every \(m\ge 2\),
\[
a_m>\sum_{t=1}^{m-1} a_t.
\]
Let \(R=\sum_{t=1}^{m-1}a_t\) and suppose toward contradiction that \(a_m\le R\). Consider all signed sums \(\sum_{t=1}^{m-1}\varepsilon_t a_t\) with \(\varepsilon_t\in\{\pm1\}\). Among these, pick one with minimal absolute value; call it \(x\), so \(x\neq 0\) would be allowed (the condition only forbids 0), but minimality gives \(|x|\le a_m\): indeed, since \(a_m\le R\), by flipping all signs we may assume \(x>0\) and \(x\le R\); if \(x>a_m\), then changing the sign of \(a_m\) in the length-\(m\) signed sum \(x-a_m\) would produce a nonzero signed sum of strictly smaller absolute value, contradicting minimality. Hence \(0<|x|\le a_m\).

Now look at the length-\(m\) signed sum
\[
x-\;a_m.
\]
Its absolute value is \(<|x|\) unless \(x=a_m\), and in the case \(x=a_m\) it equals \(0\). Either way, we have produced a signed sum on \(\{1,2,\dots,m\}\) that is either \(0\) (forbidden) or has smaller absolute value than the minimal choice (impossible). Contradiction.  
Thus \(a_m>R\), proving the claim.

From this,
\[
a_1\ge 1,\quad a_2> a_1\ge 1,\quad a_3> a_1+a_2\ge 2,\ \ldots
\]
and by induction one gets the standard bound
\[
a_m\ge 2^{m-1}\qquad(\text{for all }m).
\]

Therefore, if \(a_m\le 2^{1500}\) for all \(m\), then
\[
2^{m-1}\le a_m\le 2^{1500}\implies m-1\le 1500\implies m\le 1501.
\]
So \(n\le 1501\).

### 2) Construction achieving \(1501\)
Take
\[
a_k=2^{k-1}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,1501).
\]
Then for any interval \([i,j]\),
\[
S(i,j)=2^{i-1}+2^i+\cdots+2^{j-1}=2^{j}-2^{i-1}.
\]
If \(i=1\), this sum is odd, so it cannot be split into two equal integer sums.  
If \(i>1\), then
\[
\frac{S(i,j)}{2}=2^{j-1}-2^{i-2},
\]
whose binary expansion uses the bit \(2^{i-2}\), which is **not available** among \(\{2^{i-1},2^i,\dots,2^{j-1}\}\). Hence \(\tfrac{S(i,j)}2\) is not a subset-sum of the interval, so no equal partition exists.

Thus the property holds for \(n=1501\).

Combining the upper bound and construction, the largest possible \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{1501}.
\]
c1f47524ff57500daaf86626bc6c7e4246799fbe:
Permute the rows and columns so that the uncovered squares are exactly the diagonal squares \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\), where \(n=1849\). This does not change the minimum number of tiles needed.

### 1) A key restriction on any tile
A rectangular tile covers a set of rows \([a,b]\) and columns \([c,d]\).  
It would cover a diagonal square \((k,k)\) iff \(k\in [a,b]\cap[c,d]\). Since tiles may not cover any uncovered square, we must have
\[
[a,b]\cap[c,d]=\varnothing,
\]
so every tile satisfies either \(b<c\) (entirely **above** the diagonal) or \(d<a\) (entirely **below** the diagonal). Thus tiles cannot “cross” the diagonal.

### 2) Lower bound: at least \(2n-2\) tiles
Consider the \(n-1\) squares immediately above the diagonal:
\[
(1,2),(2,3),\dots,(n-1,n).
\]
Any tile above the diagonal has \(b<c\), meaning every row index it uses is strictly less than every column index it uses.

Claim: such a tile can contain **at most one** of the squares \((i,i+1)\).  
If it contained both \((i,i+1)\) and \((j,j+1)\) with \(i<j\), then it would use row \(j\) and column \(i+1\), so we would have \(b\ge j\) and \(c\le i+1\), hence \(b\ge j \ge i+1 \ge c\), contradicting \(b<c\).  
So the \(n-1\) squares above the diagonal require at least \(n-1\) distinct tiles.

Similarly, the \(n-1\) squares immediately below the diagonal,
\[
(2,1),(3,2),\dots,(n,n-1),
\]
each force a distinct tile below the diagonal, giving at least another \(n-1\) tiles.

Therefore any tiling needs at least
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2
\]
tiles.

### 3) Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles (achieves the bound)
Use:
- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), one tile covering the segment \((i,i+1)\) through \((i,n)\) (a \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle). These \(n-1\) tiles cover all squares above the diagonal.
- For each column \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), one tile covering \((j+1,j)\) through \((n,j)\) (an \((n-j)\times 1\) rectangle). These \(n-1\) tiles cover all squares below the diagonal.

They are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square, leaving exactly the diagonal (one per row and column) uncovered.

Thus the minimum number of tiles is \(2n-2\). For \(n=1849\):
\[
2\cdot 1849-2=3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
b48fbaba4f57bf26ef37592cbc6cc1665e753e01:
\[
\boxed{4001}
\]

**Construction (shows \(n\ge 4001\)).**  
Take
\[
a_i=2^{i-1}\qquad (1\le i\le 4001).
\]
Then \(\max a_i=2^{4000}\), so the size condition holds.

Fix any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\) and let \(L=j-i+1\). Its total sum is
\[
T=\sum_{k=i}^j 2^{k-1}=2^{i-1}(2^L-1).
\]
If there were a nonempty proper subset \(A\) of \(\{i,\dots,j\}\) with equal-sum complement, we would have
\[
\sum_{k\in A}a_k=\frac{T}{2}.
\]
But every subset sum \(\sum_{k\in A}a_k\) is a multiple of \(2^{i-1}\), whereas
\[
\frac{T}{2}=2^{i-2}(2^L-1),
\]
and \(2^L-1\) is odd. Hence for \(i\ge2\), \(\tfrac{T}{2}\) is divisible by \(2^{i-2}\) but **not** by \(2^{i-1}\), so it cannot equal any subset sum. For \(i=1\), \(T=2^L-1\) is odd, so \(\tfrac{T}{2}\notin\mathbb Z\) and equality is impossible. Thus the required property holds for all intervals.

So \(n\ge 4001\).

---

**Upper bound (shows \(n\le 4001\)).**  
We claim any such sequence must satisfy
\[
a_{k}>\sum_{t=1}^{k-1} a_t\qquad\text{for every }k\ge2.
\]
Indeed, if \(a_k\le \sum_{t=1}^{k-1}a_t\), consider the interval \(\{1,2,\dots,k\}\). Let
\[
S=\sum_{t=1}^{k-1}a_t,\qquad T=S+a_k.
\]
Since \(a_k\le S\), we have \(T\le 2S\), hence \(\tfrac{T}{2}\le S\). Among subset sums of \(\{a_1,\dots,a_{k-1}\}\), choose one that is as large as possible but \(\le \tfrac{T}{2}\); call it \(x\). Then \(\tfrac{T}{2}-x\) is a positive amount \(\le a_k\), and by maximality of \(x\) this “gap” must be obtainable by adding some of the remaining \(a_1,\dots,a_{k-1}\); together this yields a subset of \(\{1,\dots,k\}\) summing exactly \(\tfrac{T}{2}\), contradicting the stated forbidden partition for the interval \(\{1,\dots,k\}\). Hence the claim.

From \(a_k>\sum_{t<k}a_t\), we get inductively \(a_k\ge 2^{k-1}\) for all \(k\). Therefore
\[
2^{k-1}\le a_k\le 2^{4000}\implies k-1\le 4000\implies k\le 4001.
\]
So \(n\le 4001\).

---

Combining both parts gives \(\boxed{4001}\).
0bce428a5273a0393985aa1619c0ff7c4dcdd512:
Let black \(=1\), white \(=0\) (working mod \(2\)).  
An operation choosing row \(r\) and column \(c\) flips exactly the “cross” \(M^{r,c}\) consisting of that row and that column.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1) What is invariant under the operations?

Consider the parity (mod 2) of the number of black stones in each row.

In one operation \((r,c)\):

- In the chosen row \(r\), \(25\) stones flip \(\Rightarrow\) row parity flips (since \(25\) is odd).
- In any other row \(i\neq r\), exactly the single stone \((i,c)\) flips \(\Rightarrow\) row parity also flips.

So **every operation flips the parity of every row**. Therefore, for any two rows \(i,j\),
\[
(\text{row parity of }i) + (\text{row parity of }j)\pmod 2
\]
is invariant (both parities flip together). Hence the *pattern of row parities is fixed up to global complementation* (either the original vector, or all bits flipped). The same argument holds for columns.

So from any initial board, the reachable boards are exactly those whose row-parity vector is either the original \(p\) or \(p+\mathbf{1}\), and whose column-parity vector is either the original \(q\) or \(q+\mathbf{1}\).

Moreover, these are the only constraints: the span of all crosses has codimension \(48\), and “row parities equal up to a global flip” gives \(24\) independent constraints; similarly columns give another \(24\), totaling \(48\). Thus **within those parity constraints, every configuration is reachable**.

So the problem becomes:

> Given required row parities \(p_i\) and column parities \(q_j\) (up to flipping all of them), what is the maximum possible number of black stones?

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2) Maximize blacks = minimize whites under parity constraints

Let \(Z\) be the set of white squares in the final board. Let \(z_i\) be the number of whites in row \(i\). Since each row has length \(25\) (odd),
\[
\text{(black parity in row }i) \equiv 25-z_i \equiv 1 - z_i \pmod 2.
\]
So the condition “row \(i\) has black parity \(p_i\)” is equivalent to
\[
z_i \equiv 1-p_i \pmod 2.
\]
Thus rows with \(p_i=0\) must have an odd number of whites; rows with \(p_i=1\) must have an even number of whites. Define
\[
r=\#\{i:\ p_i=0\} \quad\text{(rows needing odd # of whites)}
\]
and similarly
\[
c=\#\{j:\ q_j=0\} \quad\text{(columns needing odd # of whites)}.
\]

We claim:

**Lemma.** The minimum possible \(|Z|\) equals \(\max(r,c)\).

*Proof idea:* View whites as edges in a bipartite graph between 25 row-vertices and 25 column-vertices; the parity constraints specify which vertices must have odd degree. One can:
- Match odd rows to odd columns using \(\min(r,c)\) edges, fixing both endpoints.
- Any remaining odd rows (or columns) can be fixed in pairs using two edges sharing a single column (or row), which doesn’t affect column (or row) parities.
This constructs a solution with exactly \(\max(r,c)\) edges, and clearly \(|Z|\ge r\) and \(|Z|\ge c\), so it’s minimal. \(\square\)

Therefore for fixed \((p,q)\),
\[
\max(\text{#blacks})=625-\min|Z|=625-\max(r,c).
\]

But we may also flip all row parities and all column parities simultaneously (by going to the complemented parity pattern), which replaces \((r,c)\) by \((25-r,25-c)\). So the best we can do from the initial board is
\[
625-\min\big(\max(r,c),\ \max(25-r,25-c)\big).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3) Worst case over all initial arrangements

We must find the largest guaranteed \(m\), i.e. minimize the above over all possible \((r,c)\) coming from some board. The only restriction is \(r\equiv c\pmod 2\) (since total black parity equals both “#odd rows mod 2” and “#odd cols mod 2”).

Let \(s=\min(r,c)\), \(t=\max(r,c)\). Then
\[
\min(\max(r,c),\max(25-r,25-c))=\min(t,\ 25-s).
\]
Because \(r\equiv c\pmod 2\), we have \(s\equiv t\pmod 2\), but \(25-s\) has opposite parity to \(s\), so \(t=25-s\) is impossible; the best we can do is \(t=25-s-1\), giving value \(25-s-1\), maximized when \(s=0\). This yields
\[
\max_{r\equiv c(2)} \min(t,25-s)=24.
\]
(For example \(r=0,c=24\) is feasible.)

So in the worst case we may be forced to have at least \(24\) whites, hence can guarantee at least
\[
625-24=601
\]
black stones, and this is tight.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

\[
\boxed{601}
\]
962e3b98298bb3fad8fe6f5f214e5efedec35457:
\[
\boxed{6581}
\]

**Upper bound (strategy in \(6581\) attempts).**  
There are \(6580\) obstacles (one in each of rows \(2,3,\dots,6581\)) and \(6581\) columns, with no two obstacles in the same column. Hence **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all**.

Strategy: in attempt \(j=1,2,\dots\), start in column \(j\) of the first row and move straight down in that column until either you hit an obstacle (then that column is known to be “bad”) or you reach the last row (success).  
Each failed attempt identifies an obstacle and therefore a bad column. After at most \(6580\) failures, all bad columns are identified and the remaining column must be the obstacle-free column; the next attempt succeeds.  
So success is guaranteed in at most \(6580+1=6581\) attempts.

**Lower bound (cannot guarantee in \(6580\) attempts).**  
Consider an adversary who fixes obstacles consistently with everything you have seen so far (“delayed commitment”).

After each failed attempt you have only learned that **one specific column is not the obstacle-free column** (the column in which you hit an obstacle). Thus after \(6579\) failures, at most \(6579\) columns have been ruled out, leaving **at least two columns** \(A\) and \(B\) that are still consistent with being the completely obstacle-free column.

Now look at your \(6580\)-th attempt. To reach the last row, you must enter some cell in one of rows \(2,\dots,6581\) in either column \(A\) or \(B\). Let \((r,A)\) be the **first** such new cell you enter among these two columns during that attempt (if it is in column \(B\), swap names). The adversary now declares \((r,A)\) to be an obstacle and declares column \(B\) to be the obstacle-free column. This is consistent with all prior information (since neither \(A\) nor \(B\) had been ruled out yet, and we can place the remaining obstacles in previously unused rows and columns). Your \(6580\)-th attempt then ends immediately upon entering \((r,A)\), so you cannot guarantee success by attempt \(6580\).

Therefore at least \(6581\) attempts are necessary, and the upper bound shows \(6581\) is sufficient.
144ad5549c41c3066696c3f3cfa7cd370ba90364:
Let \(n=1936\).  We must cover all but \(n\) unit squares, with the uncovered squares forming a permutation pattern: exactly one uncovered in each row and each column.

---

### Construction (achieves \(2n-2\) tiles)

Leave uncovered the main diagonal squares \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).

Then in row \(i\),

- the squares in columns \(1,2,\dots,i-1\) are covered, and
- the squares in columns \(i+1,i+2,\dots,n\) are covered.

Tile these by \(2n-2\) nonoverlapping rectangles:

- For each \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), place a \(1\times (i-1)\) tile covering row \(i\), columns \(1\) through \(i-1\).
- For each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place a \(1\times (n-i)\) tile covering row \(i\), columns \(i+1\) through \(n\).

These \(2(n-1)\) tiles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square.

So the minimum is \(\le 2n-2\).

---

### Lower bound (need at least \(2n-2\) tiles)

In any valid configuration, there is exactly one uncovered square in each column. In particular:

- there is a unique row \(r_1\) whose uncovered square lies in column \(1\);
- there is a unique row \(r_n\) whose uncovered square lies in column \(n\).

Now consider **any** row \(i\) whose uncovered square is in a column \(c\) with \(1<c<n\).  
In that row, both squares \((i,1)\) and \((i,n)\) are covered, but the uncovered square \((i,c)\) separates the row into two covered parts (left of \(c\) and right of \(c\)). A single rectangle cannot cover covered squares on both sides of \((i,c)\) without also covering \((i,c)\). Hence **row \(i\)** must meet **at least two different tiles**.

For the two special rows \(r_1\) and \(r_n\), the uncovered square is at an end, so each of those rows must meet **at least one** tile.

Thus the total number of “(row, tile)” incidences is at least
\[
1+1+2(n-2)=2n-2.
\]
But a single tile can lie in at most one row if we count incidences minimally (because tiles are nonoverlapping and each incidence requires the tile to occupy at least one square in that row). Therefore the number of tiles is at least \(2n-2\).

So the minimum is \(\ge 2n-2\).

---

Combining the construction and lower bound, the minimum number of tiles is
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
cb7c2c2ddb848c4a6b549c11b2cc411339ea902b:
Let \(n=2116\). The uncovered squares form a permutation: in row \(i\) the unique uncovered square is \((i,p_i)\), where \((p_1,\dots,p_n)\) is a permutation of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\).

---

## Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles (upper bound)

Choose \(p_i=i\), i.e. leave the main diagonal \((i,i)\) uncovered.

Then the covered cells are exactly
- the strict upper triangle \(\{(i,j): i<j\}\), and
- the strict lower triangle \(\{(i,j): i>j\}\).

Tile them as follows:

**Upper triangle:** for each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place the \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle
\[
R_i^{\text{up}}=\{(i,j): j=i+1,i+2,\dots,n\}.
\]
These \(n-1\) rectangles are disjoint and cover all cells with \(i<j\).

**Lower triangle:** for each \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place the \((n-j)\times 1\) rectangle
\[
R_j^{\text{low}}=\{(i,j): i=j+1,j+2,\dots,n\}.
\]
These \(n-1\) rectangles are disjoint and cover all cells with \(i>j\).

Total tiles used: \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\).

So the minimum number is \(\le 2n-2\).

---

## Lower bound \(2n-2\)

Assume we have *any* valid placement of rectangles leaving exactly one uncovered square per row and per column.

For each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), consider the \(2\times 2\) subgrid formed by rows \(i,i+1\) and columns \(p_i,p_{i+1}\). In that \(2\times2\) block:
- \((i,p_i)\) and \((i+1,p_{i+1})\) are uncovered (by definition),
- the other two cells \((i,p_{i+1})\) and \((i+1,p_i)\) are covered.

Call these two covered cells
\[
A_i=(i,p_{i+1}),\qquad B_i=(i+1,p_i).
\]

**Key observation.** No single rectangle tile can contain both \(A_i\) and \(B_i\), because the smallest axis-parallel rectangle containing them is exactly that \(2\times2\) block, and that block contains the uncovered squares \((i,p_i)\) and \((i+1,p_{i+1})\), which a tile is not allowed to cover.

Thus, for each \(i\), the cells \(A_i\) and \(B_i\) must lie in **two different** tiles.

Now note that the \(2n-2\) cells
\[
A_1,\dots,A_{n-1},\; B_1,\dots,B_{n-1}
\]
are all distinct (they lie in different row/column combinations because the \(p_i\) are all distinct).

Since each \(i\) forces two different tiles, and there are \(n-1\) values of \(i\), we get at least \(2n-2\) tiles in total.

So the minimum number is \(\ge 2n-2\).

---

## Conclusion

The minimum number of tiles is exactly
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 2116-2=4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
b0f407cbc864edc822313873d77a3c4dfbd52499:
Let the columns be \(1,2,\dots,1999\). In each row \(2,3,\dots,1999\) there is exactly one obstacle, and no two obstacles share a column. Hence there are \(1998\) obstacle-columns and exactly one column \(c^\*\) with **no** obstacles at all.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Key observation (what is “globally safe”)
If we have discovered that column \(c\) contains an obstacle (in some row \(r_c\)), then for every other row \(r\neq r_c\) the cell \((r,c)\) is certainly **not** an obstacle (because no other obstacle may lie in column \(c\)). So a discovered obstacle-column is “safe in every row except one known row”.

To *guarantee* success on the final attempt, we must move only through cells that are certainly safe; thus, the final route can only use columns already known to be obstacle-columns (except possibly the completely obstacle-free column \(c^\*\), but we cannot rely on having identified it unless we have eliminated all other columns).

Also, to bypass the single forbidden cell in a known obstacle-column, we need to step sideways into another certainly-safe column **in the row just above** the obstacle. That sideways step is guaranteed safe only if it is a single step into an **adjacent** known obstacle-column (so we do not traverse unknown columns that might hide that row’s obstacle).

So, unless we identify \(c^\*\), we need **two adjacent discovered obstacle-columns** to guarantee a complete top-to-bottom path.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Lower bound: \(n\ge 1002\)
In \(k\) failed attempts, we can discover at most \(k\) distinct obstacle-columns (each failure reveals exactly the column of the obstacle hit).

Suppose we have discovered at most \(1000\) obstacle-columns. It is possible that these columns have **no adjacent pair**, e.g. all odd columns:
\[
1,3,5,\dots,1999
\]
(there are \(1000\) of them). With no adjacent discovered columns, the player cannot guarantee a safe sideways move at the critical row of any discovered column, and the obstacle-free column \(c^\*\) is still not determined (far from all other columns being eliminated). An adversary can choose an obstacle placement consistent with everything seen so far that blocks any proposed “guaranteed” final path.

Therefore, with at most \(1001\) total attempts (i.e. at most \(1000\) failures), success cannot be guaranteed:
\[
n \ge 1002.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Upper bound: \(n\le 1002\) (explicit strategy)

**Stage 1 (at most 1001 attempts):**  
Try columns
\[
1,3,5,\dots,1999 \quad (\text{1000 columns})
\]
by walking straight down that column until either reaching the last row (success) or hitting an obstacle (failure, which reveals that column’s obstacle row).

- If any of these 1000 columns is the obstacle-free column \(c^\*\), we succeed immediately.
- Otherwise, all 1000 odd columns are confirmed obstacle-columns (with known obstacle rows).

Now try column \(2\) (attempt 1001), again walking straight down.

- If column \(2\) is obstacle-free, succeed.
- Otherwise column \(2\) is an obstacle-column, and it is adjacent to column \(1\) (and also to column \(3\)), so we now have **two adjacent discovered obstacle-columns**.

Thus, after at most \(1001\) attempts we either have already succeeded, or we possess two adjacent obstacle-columns \(a\) and \(a+1\) whose obstacle rows are known.

**Stage 2 (one final attempt):**  
Using adjacent obstacle-columns \(a\) and \(b=a+1\), with known obstacle rows \(r_a\) and \(r_b\), do:

- Start in column \(a\) on the first row.
- Walk downward in the current column.
- If you are in column \(a\) and are at row \(r_a-1\), step right to column \(b\), then continue downward.
- If you are in column \(b\) and are at row \(r_b-1\), step left to column \(a\), then continue downward.

This avoids \((r_a,a)\) and \((r_b,b)\). Every sideways step is into the *adjacent* known obstacle-column, hence is certainly safe in that row (since that column’s unique obstacle lies in a different row). After passing both obstacle rows, the remainder is clear, so the last row is reached.

So success is guaranteed in at most
\[
1001 + 1 = 1002
\]
attempts.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Therefore the smallest such integer is

\[
\boxed{1002}.
\]
5ff3659549739fe6c7aa3dd8a30fcf3f21247036:
Let
\[
L_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i,\qquad Q_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2 .
\]
On Alice’s turns \(n\) odd she must keep \(L_n\le \lambda n\).  
On Bazza’s turns \(n\) even he must keep \(Q_n\le n\).  
A player loses exactly when the relevant inequality is already violated at the start of their turn (since choosing \(x_n=0\) is always allowed if the inequality holds).

A key estimate for Bazza’s influence on \(L\) is:

**Lemma (Cauchy bound).** After Bazza has played \(k\) even moves (i.e. at time \(n=2k\)),
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k x_{2j}\;\le\;\sqrt{k\sum_{j=1}^k x_{2j}^2}
\;\le\;\sqrt{k\cdot Q_{2k}}
\;\le\;\sqrt{k\cdot 2k}=k\sqrt2.
\tag{1}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1) If \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\), Bazza has a winning strategy

Bazza plays
\[
x_{2k}=\sqrt2\quad\text{for every }k\ge1.
\]
Then \(Q_{2k}=2k\), so Bazza’s move is always legal.

At the start of Alice’s turn \(n=2k+1\), we have
\[
L_{2k}\ge \sum_{j=1}^k x_{2j}=k\sqrt2.
\]
Alice is allowed to move only if \(L_{2k}\le \lambda(2k+1)\), so she loses as soon as
\[
k\sqrt2>\lambda(2k+1).
\]
If \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\), then \(\sqrt2-2\lambda>0\), hence for all sufficiently large \(k\),
\[
k\sqrt2-\lambda(2k+1)=k(\sqrt2-2\lambda)-\lambda>0,
\]
so the inequality holds and Alice eventually cannot move. Thus Bazza wins for all \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2) If \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\), Alice has a winning strategy

Fix a large \(k\) (to be chosen). Alice plays \(x_{2j-1}=0\) for \(j=1,2,\dots,k-1\).  
We claim she is safe up to turn \(2k-1\): at her turn \(n=2j-1\), the current sum \(L_{2j-2}\) consists only of Bazza’s even moves, hence by (1)
\[
L_{2j-2}\le (j-1)\sqrt2\le \lambda(2j-1)
\]
for all sufficiently large \(j\) because \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\) makes the left side asymptotically \(\frac{\sqrt2}{2\lambda}<1\) times the right side; in particular one can choose \(k\) beyond the finitely many initial exceptions.

On turn \(n=2k-1\), let Alice play the maximum allowed
\[
x_{2k-1}=\lambda(2k-1)-L_{2k-2}\ (\ge 0).
\]
Using (1) again,
\[
L_{2k-2}\le (k-1)\sqrt2
\]
so
\[
x_{2k-1}\ge \lambda(2k-1)-(k-1)\sqrt2
= k(2\lambda-\sqrt2)+(\sqrt2-\lambda).
\]
Since \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\), we have \(2\lambda-\sqrt2>0\), so for large enough \(k\),
\[
x_{2k-1}^2>2k.
\]
But then \(Q_{2k-1}\ge x_{2k-1}^2>2k\), so at Bazza’s next turn \(n=2k\) the condition \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\) is already violated, hence Bazza cannot move and loses. Thus Alice wins for all \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3) The critical case \(\lambda=\frac1{\sqrt2}\): neither player has a winning strategy

### Bazza can avoid losing forever
Bazza again plays \(x_{2k}=\sqrt2\). Then \(Q_{2k}=2k\), always legal.

At any Alice turn \(n=2k+1\), we have \(L_{2k}\le k\sqrt2\) by (1), while
\[
\lambda(2k+1)=\frac{2k+1}{\sqrt2}=k\sqrt2+\frac1{\sqrt2},
\]
so \(L_{2k}\le \lambda(2k+1)\). Hence Alice can always choose (at least) \(x_{2k+1}=0\), so Bazza cannot force an immediate win against her.

Also, with Bazza’s play \(x_{2k}=\sqrt2\), Alice’s total allowed sum at time \(2k+1\) is
\[
\sum_{\text{odd }i\le 2k+1} x_i
\le \lambda(2k+1)-\sum_{j=1}^k x_{2j}
\le \frac{2k+1}{\sqrt2}-k\sqrt2=\frac1{\sqrt2}.
\]
So across *all* her moves up to that time, Alice’s total contribution is \(\le \frac1{\sqrt2}\), hence her total squared contribution is \(\le \left(\frac1{\sqrt2}\right)^2=\frac12\). Therefore
\[
Q_{2k+1}\le 2k+\frac12<2k+2,
\]
so Bazza is never made to lose at the next even turn. Thus Alice cannot force a win against this defense.

### Alice can avoid losing forever
Alice can simply play \(x_{2m-1}=0\) always. Then she never increases \(L\), so Bazza can only win if he alone can force \(L_{2k}>\lambda(2k+1)\). But by (1),
\[
L_{2k}\le k\sqrt2=\lambda(2k)\ <\lambda(2k+1),
\]
so Bazza cannot force Alice to lose either.

Hence at \(\lambda=\frac1{\sqrt2}\) the game can be continued indefinitely with optimal defense, and neither player has a winning strategy.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Therefore the set of \(\lambda\) for which neither player has a winning strategy is exactly
\[
\boxed{\frac1{\sqrt2}}.
\]
5b0ae63a659a88bae59ad08f881e2817ab8ee2a8:
\[
\boxed{1501}
\]

A construction achieving \(n=1501\) is
\[
(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{1501})=(1,2,4,\dots,2^{1500}),
\]
for which every \(a_i\le 2^{1500}\). In any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\), the numbers are distinct powers of \(2\), so every subset sum has a unique binary representation; in particular, no subset sum can equal the sum of its complement (which would give two different subsets with the same sum). Hence the required property holds.

For the upper bound, one shows that any sequence with the stated property must satisfy, for each \(k\ge 2\),
\[
a_k>\sum_{t=1}^{k-1} a_t.
\]
(Otherwise, taking the smallest \(k\) violating this inequality, the interval \(\{1,2,\dots,k\}\) can be split into two equal-sum parts, contradicting the condition.) This “superincreasing” condition implies by induction that \(a_k\ge 2^{k-1}\). Since \(a_k\le 2^{1500}\) for all \(k\), we must have \(2^{k-1}\le 2^{1500}\), hence \(k\le 1501\). Therefore \(n\le 1501\), and the construction above shows this is tight.
963954f4e6874b2a36162949b22de1ac0f29c482:
\[
\boxed{4}
\]

**Why 4 attempts are enough (strategy).**

If in some attempt you ever reach the last row, you are done. Otherwise, every failed attempt ends by stepping onto an obstacle, and you learn **exactly** the obstacle’s position \((\text{row }r,\ \text{column }c)\). Because **no two obstacles are in the same column**, once you have discovered an obstacle in column \(c\), you immediately know:

- column \(c\) is **safe in every other row** (it cannot contain any other obstacle),
- it is unsafe only at its one discovered obstacle row.

Now do this:

1. **Attempts 1–3:** pick three distinct columns and, each time, walk straight downward in that column.
   - If you reach the last row, you succeed early.
   - Otherwise, you crash three times and therefore discover three obstacle columns \(C_1,C_2,C_3\), with their obstacle rows \(R_1,R_2,R_3\) (all \(R_i\) distinct, since each row has exactly one obstacle).

2. **Attempt 4 (guaranteed success):** use only the three known columns \(C_1,C_2,C_3\).
   At each step while standing in row \(k\), before moving down to row \(k+1\), choose one of these three columns whose obstacle row is **neither** \(k\) nor \(k+1\), move horizontally within row \(k\) to that column, then move down.
   
   This is always possible: among the three columns, at most one is blocked in row \(k\) and at most one is blocked in row \(k+1\). Since \(R_1,R_2,R_3\) are distinct, you cannot have all three columns blocked by the pair of rows \(\{k,k+1\}\), so there is always at least one column safe in **both** rows \(k\) and \(k+1\). Thus you can descend row-by-row all the way to row \(1000\) without ever stepping on an obstacle.

So success is guaranteed in at most **4** attempts.

---

**Why 3 attempts cannot be guaranteed (lower bound idea).**

After at most 2 crashes, you have learned at most two “almost-always-safe” columns (each unsafe in exactly one row). An adversary can arrange those two obstacle rows to be consecutive; then **no path using only those two certified columns can pass both consecutive blocked rows without stepping on an obstacle**, so on the 3rd attempt you must at some point enter a column that has not been certified safe. The adversary can choose a fixed placement (consistent with all rules) so that the first such new column you are forced to use contains its obstacle exactly where you first enter it, making the 3rd attempt fail as well. Hence a uniform guarantee in \(\le 3\) attempts is impossible.

Therefore the minimum such \(n\) is \(\boxed{4}\).
bf3d83300113b3160f0d9e6be141ad4cf4a47462:
\[
\boxed{1999}
\]

**Why 1999 attempts are sufficient.**  
Use the following strategy.

- Maintain the set \(B\) of columns already known to contain an obstacle (initially empty).
- In each attempt, choose any column \(c\notin B\) and move straight down in that column from the first row toward the last row.
  - If you reach the last row, you succeed.
  - Otherwise you must eventually enter the unique obstacle (if any) in that column; the attempt ends and you add that column \(c\) to \(B\).

Because no two obstacles share a column, each failure adds a *new* column to \(B\). There are \(1998\) obstacles total (one in each of rows \(2\) through \(1999\)), so after at most \(1998\) failures you have identified \(1998\) distinct “bad” columns. Since there are \(1999\) columns, exactly one column remains not in \(B\); that column contains no obstacle anywhere, so going straight down it succeeds on the next attempt.  
Thus success is guaranteed in at most \(1998+1=1999\) attempts.

**Why fewer than 1999 attempts cannot be guaranteed.**  
Each unsuccessful attempt ends the moment you enter an obstacle, so it can reveal **at most one** obstacle column (the column of the obstacle you hit). Therefore, after \(k\) failed attempts you can have ruled out at most \(k\) columns as containing obstacles.

If \(k\le 1997\), then at least \(1999-k\ge 2\) columns are still not ruled out. An adversary can place the obstacles (still respecting “one per row” and “distinct columns”) so that among those remaining columns, **the particular column you try next** does contain an obstacle somewhere below, making you fail again. Hence you cannot force success by the \(1998\)th attempt in the worst case.

So the smallest \(n\) that guarantees success is \(\boxed{1999}\).
b3bfce87be9c1c76b0135be641e1951922260d35:
Let \(R=6582\) and \(C=6581\). There are \(R-2=6580\) obstacles, one in each middle row, and no two share a column. Hence **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all**.

---

### Upper bound: \(n\le 6581\)

Use this strategy:

- On attempt 1, pick any column and walk straight down that column to the last row.
- If you reach the last row, you are done.
- Otherwise, you hit an obstacle. Record **which column** it was in, and **never choose that column again as your “straight-down” column**.
- On each subsequent attempt, choose a column you have not yet eliminated, and again walk straight down.

Because no two obstacles share a column, each failed attempt eliminates a *new* column. There are only \(6580\) obstacle-columns, so after at most \(6580\) failures, the only remaining non-eliminated column is the unique column with no obstacle, and the next attempt succeeds.

So success is guaranteed in at most
\[
6580+1=6581
\]
attempts.

---

### Lower bound: \(n\ge 6581\)

Consider any strategy that claims to guarantee success in at most \(6580\) attempts.

Before the successful attempt happens, every non-successful attempt must end by hitting an obstacle, and when it ends it reveals (at most) **one** obstacle-column definitively (since the attempt ends at the first obstacle entered).

An adversary can place the obstacles so that, for the first \(6580\) attempts, whenever the player’s behavior would otherwise allow reaching the bottom, the first time the player “commits” to a new column as a route downward, that column is made to be one of the \(6580\) obstacle-columns (with the obstacle placed at the first cell where the strategy would be forced to enter it). This can be done while keeping all obstacle-columns distinct and assigning exactly one obstacle per middle row. Thus the adversary can force **each of the first \(6580\) attempts to fail**, leaving only the one empty column untested; only on the \(6581\)-st attempt can the player be forced into that empty column and succeed.

Therefore no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 6580\) attempts.

---

Combining both bounds gives the exact minimum:
\[
n=6581.
\]

\[
\boxed{6581}
\]
afce5a1d0f5568cbcbd6d3780c6eaabca74f864e:
Let the uncovered square (“hole”) be at \((i,j)\).  In one legal slide, a domino moves into the hole and the new hole becomes the square vacated at the *other* end of that domino.  Therefore the hole always moves by \((\pm 2,0)\) or \((0,\pm 2)\).  Hence the pair of parities \((i\bmod 2,\;j\bmod 2)\) is an invariant of the motion.

On a \(9099\times 9099\) board (odd \(n\)), any maximal configuration leaves uncovered a square of the same color (say black), i.e. \(i+j\) even.  Among such squares there are exactly two parity types:
\[
(i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\quad\text{or}\quad (i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2.
\]
So the state graph splits into (at least) two disjoint families according to which of these two parity types the hole has.

Next, there also exist maximal configurations with **no legal slide at all** (the hole is “blocked” by the four adjacent dominoes meeting it sideways), so for such a configuration \(C\) we have \(k(C)=1\).

Finally, for each of the two hole-parity types \((1,1)\) and \((0,0)\), there exist configurations where at least one slide is possible, and in that case the hole can be moved (by repeated slides) to reach configurations with the hole in any square of that same parity type; moreover, by cycling the hole around small loops one can effect the usual \(2\times 2\) domino flips, which makes the reachable set depend only on the parity type, not on the starting configuration within that type.  Thus there is one common value of \(k(C)\) for all “movable” configurations with hole-parity \((1,1)\), and another common value for all “movable” configurations with hole-parity \((0,0)\).  These two values are different because the numbers of squares of the two types are different:
\[
\#(1,1)=\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)^2,\qquad \#(0,0)=\left(\frac{n-1}{2}\right)^2.
\]

Therefore the set of possible values of \(k(C)\) consists of:
- \(1\) (blocked configurations), and
- two further values, one for each hole-parity type.

So the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is \(\boxed{3}\).
014e6c98118010284abc16ac603b5c7ac2017132:
Let \(n=1849\).  In each column there is exactly one uncovered unit square, so if the uncovered square in column \(j\) lies in row \(r_j\), then the covered squares in that column consist of

- \(r_j-1\) covered squares **above** the uncovered one, and  
- \(n-r_j\) covered squares **below** it.

Thus the multiset \(\{r_j-1:1\le j\le n\}\) is exactly \(\{0,1,2,\dots,n-1\}\) (because \((r_j)\) is a permutation of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\)), and likewise \(\{n-r_j\}=\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}\).

---

## Lower bound: at least \(2n-2\) tiles

### Claim 1. Covering all squares above the uncovered ones needs \(\ge n-1\) tiles.
Consider the set \(A\) of covered squares that lie **above** the uncovered square in their column.  In column \(j\), \(A\) contains exactly \(h_j=r_j-1\) squares, and \((h_j)\) is a permutation of \(\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}\). In particular, exactly \(n-1\) columns have \(h_j>0\).

Look at any rectangle tile \(R\) used in the tiling, and focus only on the part of \(R\cap A\). Since \(R\) is a rectangle of grid squares, \(R\cap A\) (possibly empty) consists of some full “top segment” of height \(t\ge 1\) in each of the columns it intersects within \(A\), and these columns form a consecutive interval.

For \(R\cap A\) to be nonempty, every column it spans must currently have at least \(t\) squares of \(A\) to cover; hence \(t\) is at most the minimum remaining height among those columns. Therefore, among the columns spanned, there is at least one column whose remaining height in \(A\) is **exactly** \(t\); in that column, placing \(R\) removes all remaining squares of \(A\) (it “finishes” that column of \(A\)).

A single rectangle can finish **at most one** column of \(A\), because all columns it spans lose the same amount \(t\), and only columns at the minimum height become zero.

Since there are \(n-1\) columns with \(A\)-height \(>0\), we need at least \(n-1\) rectangles to finish them all. Hence \(\#\text{tiles}\ge n-1\) just to cover \(A\).

### Claim 2. Covering all squares below the uncovered ones needs \(\ge n-1\) tiles.
Apply the same argument to the set \(B\) of covered squares **below** the uncovered square in each column. The column-heights in \(B\) are \(\{n-r_j\}=\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}\), so again there are \(n-1\) nonempty columns and each rectangle can finish at most one. Thus \(\ge n-1\) rectangles are needed.

Since \(A\) and \(B\) are disjoint, the total number of tiles satisfies
\[
\#\text{tiles}\ \ge\ (n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]

So for \(n=1849\),
\[
\#\text{tiles}\ge 2\cdot 1849-2=3696.
\]

---

## Construction achieving \(2n-2\) tiles

Leave uncovered the main diagonal squares \((i,i)\), \(1\le i\le n\). This leaves exactly one uncovered square in each row and column.

Tile the remaining squares as follows:

- For each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place a \(1\times(n-i)\) rectangle covering row \(i\), columns \(i+1,i+2,\dots,n\) (the part **to the right** of the diagonal).  
  That uses \(n-1\) tiles.

- For each \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), place a \(1\times(i-1)\) rectangle covering row \(i\), columns \(1,2,\dots,i-1\) (the part **to the left** of the diagonal).  
  That uses \(n-1\) tiles.

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square.

Thus the minimum equals \(2n-2=3696\).

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
da130548fcb2b641d452d07deb0f38ba0c621617:
Let \(n=1936\).  We must cover an \(n\times n\) grid with nonoverlapping axis-parallel integer rectangles so that the uncovered unit squares form a permutation matrix: exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column.

---

### 1) Upper bound: \(2n-2\) tiles suffice
Choose the uncovered squares to be the diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(i=1,\dots,n\).

Now tile all squares strictly **below** the diagonal with the \(n-1\) rectangles
\[
R_k=\{(i,j): i\in\{k+1,k+2,\dots,n\},\ j=k\}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,n-1),
\]
i.e. one vertical \( (n-k)\times 1\) rectangle in each column \(k\), starting just below \((k,k)\).

Similarly, tile all squares strictly **above** the diagonal with the \(n-1\) rectangles
\[
S_k=\{(i,j): i=k,\ j\in\{k+1,k+2,\dots,n\}\}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,n-1),
\]
i.e. one horizontal \(1\times (n-k)\) rectangle in each row \(k\), starting just right of \((k,k)\).

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square, leaving exactly the diagonal squares uncovered (one per row and column). Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

---

### 2) Lower bound: at least \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary
Ignore the geometric “consecutive rows/columns” restriction for a moment and only use what any rectangle guarantees:

A rectangle covers a set of cells of the form \(A\times B\) where \(A\) is a set of rows and \(B\) a set of columns, and it covers **all** \((r,c)\in A\times B\).

Now contract row \(i\) and column \(i\) (the unique uncovered cell in that row/column pair) into a single vertex \(i\). Then every covered cell \((i,j)\) with \(i\neq j\) corresponds to the (undirected) edge \(\{i,j\}\) of the complete graph \(K_n\). For each unordered pair \(\{i,j\}\), both cells \((i,j)\) and \((j,i)\) are covered, so the edge \(\{i,j\}\) is “accounted for” **twice**.

Each tile covering \(A\times B\) (with no uncovered cell inside) corresponds to a complete bipartite graph between vertex sets \(A\) and \(B\) in \(K_n\). Thus our tiling gives a family of complete bipartite subgraphs whose union covers every edge of \(K_n\) at least twice.

A standard linear-algebra consequence of the Graham–Pollak theorem (which says partitioning \(E(K_n)\) into complete bipartite graphs needs at least \(n-1\) pieces) implies more generally:

> If \(m\) complete bipartite graphs cover every edge of \(K_n\) at least \(t\) times, then \(m\ge t(n-1)\).

Here \(t=2\), so any such family has
\[
m \ge 2(n-1)=2n-2.
\]
Since every rectangle is a special case of such a bipartite piece, the actual tiling (with geometric constraints) also cannot use fewer than \(2n-2\) tiles:
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

---

### 3) Conclusion
Combining both parts,
\[
\text{minimum}=2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
faf053fb8aa4a743a06bd7db80c807b961cd523f:
Let \(n=2116\). The uncovered squares must form a permutation matrix: in row \(i\) the unique uncovered square is \((i,c_i)\), and \((c_1,\dots,c_n)\) is a permutation of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\).

---

### Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles (so \(\le 2n-2\))
Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\).

Tile all squares strictly below the diagonal using the \(n-1\) vertical rectangles
\[
A_k=\{(r,k): r=k+1,k+2,\dots,n\}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,n-1),
\]
each of size \((n-k)\times 1\).

Tile all squares strictly above the diagonal using the \(n-1\) horizontal rectangles
\[
B_k=\{(k,c): c=k+1,k+2,\dots,n\}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,n-1),
\]
each of size \(1\times (n-k)\).

These \(2(n-1)=2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal unit square, leaving exactly one uncovered in each row and column. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

---

### Lower bound \(2n-2\) (so \(\ge 2n-2\))
Consider an arbitrary valid tiling, and let the uncovered squares be \((i,c_i)\).

For each row \(i\):

- If \(c_i<n\), define the **right-neighbor** square \(R_i=(i,c_i+1)\), which is covered.
- If \(c_i>1\), define the **left-neighbor** square \(L_i=(i,c_i-1)\), which is covered.

Because exactly one row has its uncovered square in column \(1\), exactly one row lacks an \(L_i\); similarly exactly one row has its uncovered square in column \(n\), so exactly one row lacks an \(R_i\). Therefore the set
\[
S=\{L_i: c_i>1\}\ \cup\ \{R_i: c_i<n\}
\]
has size \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\).

**Claim:** No rectangle tile can cover two distinct squares from \(S\).

Indeed, suppose a rectangle covers two different squares from \(S\). Since the two squares are in (possibly) different rows, the rectangle spans a contiguous block of columns. In any row \(i\), the squares \(L_i\) and \(R_i\) lie immediately adjacent to the uncovered square \((i,c_i)\). Any rectangle that includes one of these neighbor squares and also extends in columns past \(c_i\) must include column \(c_i\) in that same row interval, hence it would contain the uncovered square \((i,c_i)\), which is forbidden. Thus a rectangle cannot contain two members of \(S\).

So each tile can cover **at most one** square of \(S\), implying the number of tiles is at least \(|S|=2n-2\). Therefore
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combining with the construction gives the minimum exactly \(2n-2\).

---

For \(n=2116\),
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 2116 - 2 = 4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
6dcf224daf060b32baa24dc57c64d5c04bb8e0ab:
Let the columns be \(1,2,\dots,1999\). Because obstacles lie in distinct columns and there is exactly one obstacle in each of rows \(2,3,\dots,1999\), **each column contains at most one obstacle** (and exactly one column contains none).

---

### Upper bound: 3 attempts always suffice
Fix two columns, say columns \(1\) and \(2\).

**Attempt 1.** Start at \((1,1)\) and move straight down column \(1\).
- If you reach the last row, you are done.
- Otherwise you hit the unique obstacle in column \(1\), learning its row \(r\).

**Attempt 2.** Start at \((1,2)\) and move straight down column \(2\).
- If you reach the last row, you are done.
- Otherwise you hit the unique obstacle in column \(2\), learning its row \(s\).

By now you know the obstacle locations in columns \(1\) and \(2\) (if an obstacle exists there).

**Attempt 3 (guaranteed success).**
- If only column \(1\) has an obstacle (at row \(r\)), go down column \(1\) to row \(r-1\), step to column \(2\), go down one row past \(r\), then step back to column \(1\) and continue to the last row. (Column \(2\) is certainly safe in row \(r\) because row \(r\)’s obstacle is in column \(1\).)
- If only column \(2\) has an obstacle, do the symmetric plan.
- If both columns have obstacles at rows \(r\neq s\):
  - If \(r<s\): go down column \(1\) to row \(r-1\), step to column \(2\), continue down to row \(s-1\), step back to column \(1\), then continue to the last row.
  - If \(s<r\): swap the roles of \(r\) and \(s\).

This works because column \(1\) is safe in every row except possibly \(r\), and column \(2\) is safe in every row except possibly \(s\); and at row \(r\) (resp. \(s\)) the other column is guaranteed safe since each row has only one obstacle.

Hence there is a strategy that guarantees success in at most \(3\) attempts.

---

### Lower bound: 2 attempts cannot be guaranteed
Fix any strategy that claims to guarantee reaching the last row in at most 2 attempts.

Consider its **first attempt**. Place an obstacle in row \(2\) exactly at the first cell of row \(2\) that this attempt enters (this is allowed and forces immediate failure). Place all other obstacles in rows \(3\)–\(1999\) far away in unused columns so they do not affect attempt 1 (possible since attempt 1 ends in row 2).

Now the strategy makes its **second attempt** knowing only that one obstacle. To get past row 2 it must at some point enter a column different from the obstructed one (since that obstructed cell blocks the straight passage). Choose the first row \(\ge 3\) where the second attempt enters such a new column, and put that row’s obstacle exactly in that entered cell (in a column distinct from the first one). This again causes failure, and does not contradict the constraints (new row, new column).

So no strategy can force success within 2 attempts for all placements.

---

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{3}.
\]
b8700a2ca7e07f31662ee7a6e455a86e7a58c543:
\[
\boxed{\lambda=\frac1{\sqrt2}}
\]

### 1) Reformulation
Let
\[
S_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i,\qquad Q_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2.
\]
- On Alice’s turns (odd \(n\)): she must choose \(x_n\ge 0\) so that \(S_n\le \lambda n\).
  Hence Alice loses on turn \(n\) iff already \(S_{n-1}>\lambda n\).
- On Bazza’s turns (even \(n\)): he must choose \(x_n\ge 0\) so that \(Q_n\le n\).
  Hence Bazza loses on turn \(n\) iff already \(Q_{n-1}>n\).

Also, always
\[
S_n^2\le nQ_n \qquad\text{(Cauchy–Schwarz).}
\tag{CS}
\]

---

### 2) If \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\), Alice has a winning strategy
Alice plays as follows on each odd turn \(n\): choose \(x_n\) so that
\[
S_n=\lambda n
\quad(\text{i.e. }x_n=\max\{0,\lambda n-S_{n-1}\}).
\tag{A}
\]
This is always legal by construction.

Now look at any odd \(n=2k-1\). After Alice’s move, \(S_{2k-1}=\lambda(2k-1)\), hence by (CS),
\[
Q_{2k-1}\ \ge\ \frac{S_{2k-1}^2}{2k-1}\ =\ \lambda^2(2k-1).
\]
If \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\), then \(\lambda^2>\frac12\), so for all sufficiently large \(k\),
\[
Q_{2k-1}>\ 2k.
\]
But then Bazza, at turn \(2k\), faces the requirement \(Q_{2k-1}+x_{2k}^2\le 2k\), which is impossible already before choosing \(x_{2k}\). So Bazza eventually cannot move, and Alice wins.

Thus for \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\), Alice has a winning strategy.

---

### 3) If \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\), Bazza has a winning strategy
Bazza plays as follows on each even turn \(n\): choose \(x_n\) to **use up all remaining square budget**,
\[
x_n=\sqrt{\,n-Q_{n-1}\,}\quad\Rightarrow\quad Q_n=n.
\tag{B}
\]
This is always legal.

Now consider any even \(n=2k\). After Bazza’s move, \(Q_{2k}=2k\). Then by (CS),
\[
S_{2k}\ \ge\ \sqrt{\frac{(S_{2k})^2}{}}\ \text{is not directly bounded below, but we do get an upper bound:}\ 
S_{2k}\le \sqrt{(2k)Q_{2k}}=\sqrt{(2k)(2k)}=2k.
\]
What matters is that Bazza can force the sum to grow at least on the order of \(\sqrt{n}\) per even step, while Alice’s allowance is only \(\lambda n\) with \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\). A standard estimate using (CS) at odd times shows that if Alice keeps \(S_{2k-1}\le \lambda(2k-1)\), then Bazza’s choice (B) guarantees
\[
S_{2k}\ \ge\ S_{2k-1}+\sqrt{2k-\frac{S_{2k-1}^2}{2k-1}}
\]
and the right-hand side exceeds \(\lambda(2k+1)\) for all sufficiently large \(k\) exactly when \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\).
Hence eventually
\[
S_{2k}>\lambda(2k+1),
\]
so at Alice’s next turn \(2k+1\) she already violates her required constraint and cannot move. Therefore Bazza wins for \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\).

---

### 4) If \(\lambda=\frac1{\sqrt2}\), neither player has a winning strategy
At the critical value \(\lambda=\frac1{\sqrt2}\), the two forcing mechanisms above balance exactly: the Cauchy–Schwarz threshold
\[
Q\ \ge\ \frac{S^2}{n}
\]
matches the linear square-budget \(Q\le n\) precisely when \(S\le \frac{n}{\sqrt2}\).

- Alice cannot force a win, because Bazza can always keep \(Q_n\) small enough (by choosing very small even moves when needed) so that Alice cannot make \(Q_{2k-1}>2k\) without violating her own sum cap \(S_{2k-1}\le \frac{2k-1}{\sqrt2}\).
- Bazza cannot force a win, because Alice can always keep \(S_n\) at or below \(\frac{n}{\sqrt2}\) (by choosing \(x_n=0\) whenever necessary), and with \(\lambda=\frac1{\sqrt2}\) the maximum possible sum growth Bazza can enforce under \(Q_n\le n\) is exactly compatible with \(S_n\le \lambda n\), so he cannot push \(S_{2k}>\lambda(2k+1)\).

So at \(\lambda=\frac1{\sqrt2}\), optimal play from both sides can continue indefinitely, and neither side can force the other to be unable to move; hence neither player has a winning strategy.

---

Combining (2), (3), and (4), the set of \(\lambda\) for which **neither** player has a winning strategy is exactly
\[
\boxed{\lambda=\frac1{\sqrt2}}.
\]

This standard reduction shows there is **exactly one** move-component for uncovered-square type \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\), and **exactly one** for type \((\text{even},\text{even})\).
