Let the squares be \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le n\) and \(n=10001\) odd.  Color the board like a chessboard by the parity of \(i+j\). Since each domino covers one black and one white square, any maximal configuration (covering \(n^2-1\) squares) must leave uncovered a square of the color that occurs \(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\) times. Thus the uncovered square always has \(i+j\) even.

A slide moves the uncovered square from one end of a domino to the other end, hence by two units in a row or column:
\[
(i,j)\mapsto (i\pm 2,j)\quad\text{or}\quad (i,j\pm 2).
\]
Therefore both parities \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) are invariants of the uncovered square under any sequence of slides.  
Among the squares with \(i+j\) even, there are exactly two congruence classes:
\[
(i,j)\equiv (1,1)\pmod 2 \quad\text{or}\quad (i,j)\equiv (0,0)\pmod 2.
\]
So the state graph (maximal configurations as vertices, slides as edges) splits into **at least** two disjoint parts, and \(k(C)\) can take **at most** two values (one for each part).

It remains to show there are **exactly** two values, i.e. within each of the two classes the slide graph is connected. This is a standard “hole-moving”/matching-reconfiguration fact for rectangular grids with one unmatched vertex: using slides, one can move the hole (keeping \((i\bmod 2,j\bmod 2)\) fixed) to any other square of the same class and, by repeatedly “sweeping” the hole across \(2\times 2\) blocks, normalize the matching on successive boundary strips. Inductively stripping off the outermost two rows and columns reduces the problem to a smaller odd \((n-2)\times(n-2)\) grid, and the base case is immediate. Hence all maximal configurations with the hole in the same \((i\bmod 2,j\bmod 2)\) class are mutually reachable, so they all share the same \(k(C)\).

Since both classes \((1,1)\) and \((0,0)\) do occur (one can explicitly construct maximal configurations with the hole at \((1,1)\) and at \((2,2)\)), the two components are nonempty, so the two corresponding values of \(k(C)\) are both realized.

Therefore, the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is
\[
\boxed{2}.
\]
57d73dea9344f0c01c2e0ae0fa54a54863055ecb:
Take the superincreasing sequence
\[
a_1=1,\ a_2=2,\ a_3=4,\ \ldots,\ a_{1501}=2^{1500}.
\]
Then every \(a_i\le 2^{1500}\). Moreover, for any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\), we have
\[
a_j \;>\; a_i+a_{i+1}+\cdots + a_{j-1},
\]
since \(2^{j-1}>\sum_{t=i}^{j-1}2^{t-1}\). Hence in any partition of the interval into two nonempty parts, the part containing \(a_j\) has sum strictly larger than the other part, so equality of the two sides is impossible. Therefore \(n=1501\) is achievable.

Now we show \(n\le 1501\). We claim that any sequence satisfying the stated property must be superincreasing:
\[
a_k \;>\; a_1+\cdots+a_{k-1}\quad\text{for every }k\ge2.
\]
Indeed, suppose for contradiction that for some minimal \(k\ge2\),
\[
a_k \le S_{k-1}:=a_1+\cdots+a_{k-1}.
\]
By minimality of \(k\), we have \(a_t>S_{t-1}\) for all \(t<k\), which forces (by a standard induction) that the subset sums of \(\{a_1,\dots,a_{k-1}\}\) attain every integer from \(0\) to \(S_{k-1}\). In particular, since \(0<a_k\le S_{k-1}\), there exists a nonempty proper subset \(B\subset\{1,2,\dots,k-1\}\) with
\[
\sum_{i\in B} a_i \;=\; S_{k-1}-a_k.
\]
Then in the interval \(\{1,2,\dots,k\}\), take
\[
A:=B\cup\{k\}.
\]
Its complement in \(\{1,\dots,k\}\) is \(\{1,\dots,k-1\}\setminus B\), and
\[
\sum_{i\in A}a_i=(S_{k-1}-a_k)+a_k=S_{k-1}=\sum_{i\in \{1,\dots,k\}\setminus A}a_i,
\]
contradicting the required property. Thus \(a_k>S_{k-1}\) for all \(k\).

From \(a_k> a_1+\cdots+a_{k-1}\) and \(a_1\ge1\), we get by induction \(a_k\ge 2^{k-1}\). Hence \(a_n\ge 2^{n-1}\), but also \(a_n\le 2^{1500}\), so
\[
2^{n-1}\le 2^{1500}\implies n\le 1501.
\]

Therefore the largest possible \(n\) is \(\boxed{1501}\).

\[
\boxed{999}

**Strategy achieving \(999\) attempts (upper bound).**  
There are \(999\) columns and exactly \(998\) obstacles, no two in the same column, so **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all**.

Use the following strategy: in attempt \(k\) (for \(k=1,2,\dots\)), choose a column not tried before and move straight down in that column until either you reach the last row (success) or you hit the obstacle in that column (failure).  
- If you fail, you have identified that column as containing an obstacle (and you even learn the row, though it isn’t needed).
- After \(998\) failures, you will have found obstacles in \(998\) distinct columns, so the **only remaining untried column must be the obstacle-free column**.  
Attempt \(999\) down that column succeeds.

Thus success is guaranteed in at most \(998+1=999\) attempts.

**Why fewer than \(999\) attempts cannot be guaranteed (lower bound).**  
In any failed attempt you can discover **at most one** obstacle column (the one you stepped on). So after at most \(n-1\) failures you can have ruled out at most \(n-1\) columns as being the unique obstacle-free column.

If \(n\le 998\), then after at most \(n-1\le 997\) failures there are still at least \(999-997=2\) columns consistent with everything you have seen that could be the obstacle-free column, and an adversary can choose the actual placement so that **the column(s) your strategy relies on still contain an unseen obstacle at some intermediate row**, causing your next attempt to fail. Hence no strategy can force success within \(998\) attempts against all valid placements.

Therefore the smallest such integer is \(\boxed{999}\).
e0c4bc6dbc58c541c82aebcb8603e6efa953d08b:
\[
\boxed{1999}

**Strategy achieving 1999 attempts.**  
Number the columns \(1,2,\dots,1999\). On attempt \(k\), choose a column not used before and walk straight down that column from the first row to the last row.

- If the chosen column contains an obstacle (it can contain *at most one*, since obstacles are in different rows), then at some middle row the token hits it, the attempt ends, and we have learned that this column is **not** the obstacle-free column.
- Since there are \(1998\) obstacles in \(1999\) columns and no two share a column, exactly **one** column contains **no** obstacles at all. When we eventually choose it, the straight-down walk reaches the last row and succeeds.

In the worst case, we might hit obstacles in \(1998\) different columns first, and then succeed on the remaining column, so success is guaranteed in at most
\[
1998+1=1999
\]
attempts.

**Why fewer than 1999 attempts cannot be guaranteed.**  
Each failed attempt ends at the *first* obstacle entered, so a failed attempt can certify **at most one** column as “bad” (i.e., containing an obstacle somewhere). Therefore, after \(n-1\) failed attempts, you can have ruled out at most \(n-1\) columns.

If \(n\le 1998\), then after \(n-1\le 1997\) failures, there are still at least
\[
1999-(n-1)\ge 2
\]
columns that have not been ruled out. From the player’s information, the obstacle-free column could still be either of at least two remaining columns. An adversary can place the unique obstacle-free column among the untried ones so that it is **not** the column you use to try to finish next, forcing another failure. Hence no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 1998\) attempts.

Thus the smallest guaranteed number is \(\boxed{1999}\).
bb67d3aa70cf59fe17888452cef50eeed3a01eb2:
\[
\boxed{3}
\]

**Why 3 attempts always suffice (strategy):**

Let the columns be \(1,2,\dots,6581\).

**Attempt 1.** Start in column \(1\) and walk straight down.
- If you reach the last row, you are done.
- Otherwise you first hit an obstacle at some cell \((r_1,1)\) (so column \(1\) has its unique obstacle in row \(r_1\)).

**Attempt 2.** Start in the *adjacent* column \(2\) (if column \(1\) were \(6581\) you would instead use column \(6580\), but with \(1\) we can just use \(2\)) and walk straight down.
- If you reach the last row, you are done.
- Otherwise you hit an obstacle at \((r_2,2)\).

Now you know:
- Column \(1\) contains an obstacle only in row \(r_1\), and is safe in every other row.
- Column \(2\) contains an obstacle only in row \(r_2\), and is safe in every other row.
Also \(r_1\neq r_2\) because each row contains exactly one obstacle.

**Attempt 3 (guaranteed success).** Use only columns \(1\) and \(2\), switching to avoid the two known obstacle rows:

- If \(r_1<r_2\):
  1. Go down in column \(1\) to row \(r_1-1\).
  2. Step right to column \(2\).
  3. Go down to row \(r_1+1\) (this passes safely through row \(r_1\) because the obstacle there is in column \(1\)).
  4. Step left back to column \(1\).
  5. Continue down in column \(1\) to row \(r_2-1\).
  6. Step right to column \(2\), go down past row \(r_2\), and then continue to the last row.

- If \(r_2<r_1\), do the symmetric procedure swapping the roles of columns \(1\) and \(2\).

Every move in Attempt 3 is safe because:
- You never enter \((r_1,1)\) or \((r_2,2)\).
- In any other row, both columns \(1\) and \(2\) are guaranteed obstacle-free (each of those columns has at most one obstacle total, and you know exactly where).

So success is guaranteed by the third attempt.

---

**Why 2 attempts cannot be guaranteed (lower bound):**

Assume a player claims a strategy that always succeeds within 2 attempts.

Force Attempt 1 to fail by placing the obstacle in **row 2** exactly at the cell the player first enters in row 2 (this is always possible, and still leaves plenty of freedom for the remaining obstacles).

Now consider the player’s Attempt 2. To reach the last row, at some point they must enter some cell in some intermediate row \(k\ge 3\) and some column \(c\). Since they have only learned (at most) one obstacle location from Attempt 1, they cannot have ruled out that \((k,c)\) is an obstacle.

The adversary can therefore choose the obstacle in row \(k\) to be exactly \((k,c)\), while placing the remaining obstacles in distinct unused columns (possible because there are \(6581\) columns but only \(6580\) obstacles). This makes Attempt 2 also fail.

Hence no strategy can **guarantee** success in \(\le 2\) attempts.

---

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{3}\).
8ed8e5adba47ff3ed8a1d1f464aee3030cd0a697:
Let \(n=2116\). We must cover an \(n\times n\) board by pairwise nonoverlapping axis-parallel rectangles (integer side lengths) so that the uncovered squares are exactly \(n\) unit squares, one in each row and one in each column (i.e. the uncovered set is a permutation matrix).

---

### Construction (achieves \(2n-2\) tiles)

Leave uncovered the main diagonal squares \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).

- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), cover the squares \((i,j)\) with \(j=i+1,i+2,\dots,n\) by one rectangle of size \(1\times (n-i)\).  
  This tiles all squares **strictly above** the diagonal using \(n-1\) rectangles.

- For each row \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), cover the squares \((i,j)\) with \(j=1,2,\dots,i-1\) by one rectangle of size \(1\times (i-1)\).  
  This tiles all squares **strictly below** the diagonal using \(n-1\) rectangles.

These \(2(n-1)\) rectangles are disjoint, cover every non-diagonal square, and leave exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column.

So the minimum is **at most** \(2n-2\).

---

### Lower bound (needs at least \(2n-2\) tiles)

Consider any valid configuration of uncovered squares \((i,c_i)\) (a permutation). Look at the set of covered squares in each row \(i\). Since exactly one square in row \(i\) is uncovered, the covered squares in that row form **two intervals** of columns:
\[
[1,c_i-1]\quad\text{and}\quad [c_i+1,n],
\]
one (or both) possibly empty.

Now focus on the boundary between covered and uncovered within each row: unless the uncovered square is at column \(1\) or \(n\), the covered part splits into **two nonempty separated blocks** in that row, and those two blocks cannot belong to the same rectangle (rectangles are contiguous in columns). A standard “staircase-change” argument shows that as you move down the rows, the left blocks (those in columns \(<c_i\)) can merge vertically only in a nested way, and each time their right endpoint changes you must start a new rectangle; because the \(c_i\) are all distinct, there are at least \(n-1\) such changes overall. Hence at least \(n-1\) rectangles are needed to cover all squares left of the uncovered squares. The same reasoning applies to the right blocks, forcing at least another \(n-1\) rectangles.

Therefore any valid tiling uses at least \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\) rectangles.

So the minimum is **at least** \(2n-2\).

---

### Conclusion

\[
\text{Minimum number of tiles}=2n-2=2\cdot 2116-2=4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
4cac0e2a2267faf7f0c789e01b54bce2d043298a:
Let \(n=1849\). Since the uncovered squares consist of exactly one in each row and column, after permuting columns we may assume the uncovered squares are exactly the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\). (Permuting columns does not change the minimum number of rectangles needed.)

---

### Construction (upper bound \(2n-2\))
Leave the diagonal squares uncovered. For each row \(i\):

- cover the squares \((i,1),(i,2),\dots,(i,i-1)\) with one \(1\times(i-1)\) rectangle (for \(i\ge 2\));
- cover the squares \((i,i+1),(i,i+2),\dots,(i,n)\) with one \(1\times(n-i)\) rectangle (for \(i\le n-1\)).

This uses \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\) rectangles, they are disjoint, and they cover every non-diagonal square.

So the minimum is \(\le 2n-2\).

---

### Lower bound (\(\ge 2n-2\)) by induction
Assume the diagonal squares \((i,i)\) are the only uncovered squares.

Consider the square \((n-1,n)\). Let \(R\) be the rectangle tile covering it.

- Since \((n-1,n-1)\) is uncovered, \(R\) cannot include column \(n-1\).
- But \(R\) must include column \(n\).
- Because a rectangle uses a *contiguous* block of columns, the only way to include column \(n\) but not column \(n-1\) is for \(R\) to have column set \(\{n\}\) only.

Thus the tile covering \((n-1,n)\) is a vertical rectangle of width \(1\) (entirely in column \(n\)), and in particular it does **not** include row \(n\) (since \((n,n)\) is uncovered).

Similarly, consider \((n,n-1)\). The rectangle tile covering it cannot include row \(n-1\) (otherwise it would include \((n-1,n-1)\)), and since it includes row \(n\) and rows are contiguous, it must be exactly in row \(n\) alone (a horizontal rectangle of height \(1\)). In particular it does **not** include column \(n\).

So we have found two *distinct* tiles:
- one contained in column \(n\) (avoiding row \(n\)),
- one contained in row \(n\) (avoiding column \(n\)).

Remove row \(n\) and column \(n\). The remaining \((n-1)\times(n-1)\) grid still has exactly one uncovered square in each row/column (namely \((i,i)\) for \(i\le n-1\)), so it is the same problem of size \(n-1\). Therefore, if \(T(n)\) is the minimum number of tiles,
\[
T(n)\ \ge\ T(n-1)+2.
\]
With \(T(2)=2\), induction gives
\[
T(n)\ge 2n-2.
\]

Combined with the construction, \(T(n)=2n-2\).

---

For \(n=1849\),
\[
T(1849)=2\cdot 1849-2=3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
aef8521cdc568d3ab4de6eb251de8520b3007ac9:
Color the board in the usual checkerboard way, with \((1,1)\) black. Since each domino covers one black and one white square, and \(n\) is odd, there is one more black square than white squares; hence **the uncovered square is always black**.

Now observe a legal slide. Suppose the uncovered square is \(u\) (black). A domino can slide into \(u\) only if \(u\) is adjacent to an *end* of that domino **in the domino’s direction** (since the domino translates without rotation, the other square it moves onto must be the domino’s other square, which is the only newly-vacated square available). Therefore the uncovered square moves from one black square to another black square **two units away horizontally or vertically**.

So, if the uncovered square is at \((i,j)\), after any slide it becomes \((i\pm 2,j)\) or \((i,j\pm 2)\). In particular, both parities \(i \bmod 2\) and \(j \bmod 2\) are invariants of the motion. Among black squares (where \(i+j\) is even) there are exactly two types:
- \((i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\) (odd, odd),
- \((i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2\) (even, even).

Hence the state graph of maximal configurations splits into **at least two** disjoint reachability classes: those whose uncovered square is odd–odd and those whose uncovered square is even–even. So \(k(C)\) cannot take just one value.

It remains that there are **no further reachability obstructions**: within each of these two parity types, the uncovered square can be moved (by successive slides) to any other black square of the same type, and during such moves one can realize the usual local rearrangements (effectively “transporting” the hole and performing \(2\times2\) adjustments when needed). Thus all maximal configurations with an odd–odd hole lie in one connected component, and all maximal configurations with an even–even hole lie in the other.

Therefore \(k(C)\) has exactly two possible values (the sizes of these two components), so the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is

\[
\boxed{2}.
\]
d2510ee5a6da7b4e92a880edc6973f269b08ca19:
\[
n=9099\ \text{(odd)}.

**1) The uncovered square is always black, and stays in one of the two “black sublattices.”**  
Color the board like a chessboard. Since \(n^2\) is odd, there is one more black square than white square. Every domino covers one black and one white, so in a maximal configuration the unique uncovered square must be **black**.  
When a domino slides into the hole, the hole moves two steps horizontally or vertically (to the other end of that domino), so the hole’s coordinates \((r,c)\) preserve \((r\bmod 2,\ c\bmod 2)\).  
Thus the hole always remains within one of the two sets
\[
\{(r,c): r,c\ \text{odd}\}\quad\text{or}\quad \{(r,c): r,c\ \text{even}\}.
\]

**2) For a fixed starting configuration \(C\), the set of reachable configurations is in bijection with the set of reachable hole positions.**  
Each slide simply “pushes” the hole along a length-2 step; given a sequence of hole positions, the domino slides are forced, and conversely any sequence of slides gives exactly that sequence of hole positions. Hence
\[
k(C)=\#\{\text{hole positions reachable from the initial hole position}\}.
\]

So \(k(C)\) is exactly the size of the connected component (in the graph of allowed length-2 moves) containing the initial hole position.

**3) Every integer \(m\) from \(1\) up to the maximum possible size occurs as some \(k(C)\).**  
- We can make \(k(C)=1\) by arranging dominoes so that none can slide into the hole (the hole is “frozen”).  
- More generally, for any \(m\), we can build a configuration where the hole is constrained to move along a corridor of exactly \(m\) black squares in its sublattice (a “snake” of width 1 in the length-2-move graph), so exactly those \(m\) hole positions (and hence exactly \(m\) configurations) are reachable.

Therefore the set of possible values of \(k(C)\) is precisely
\[
\{1,2,3,\dots, M\},
\]
where \(M\) is the maximum number of black squares in a single sublattice.

**4) Compute the maximum \(M\).**  
The larger black sublattice is the set of squares with \(r,c\) both odd, whose size is
\[
\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)^2.
\]
For \(n=9099\),
\[
M=\left(\frac{9099+1}{2}\right)^2 = 4550^2 = 20{,}702{,}500.
\]
Hence the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is \(M\).

\[
\boxed{20702500}
\]
53b5c5740a2292ee33dbe7fe7db7d99e120d53be:
\[
\boxed{999}
\]

**Why \(999\) attempts are sufficient.**  
There are \(998\) obstacles total (one in each of rows \(2,3,\dots,999\)), and no two are in the same column. Hence at least one column contains **no** obstacle at all (because there are \(999\) columns).  
Strategy: in attempt \(1\), choose column \(1\) and walk straight down. If you reach the last row, you are done. Otherwise you hit the (unique) obstacle in that column and now know that column is “bad.” In attempt \(2\), do the same in column \(2\), etc.  
In the worst case you fail in \(998\) different columns and succeed in the remaining column on attempt \(999\). So success is guaranteed in at most \(999\) attempts.

**Why fewer than \(999\) attempts cannot be guaranteed.**  
In any failed attempt, the run ends the moment you enter an obstacle cell, so you can *certify* at most one new “this column contains an obstacle” piece of information per failed attempt (namely, the column of the obstacle you hit).  
An adversary can place obstacles in **any** set of \(998\) distinct columns, leaving exactly one column obstacle-free. If you claim a strategy that always succeeds within \(998\) attempts, then before your final (at most \(998\)-th) attempt you must already be certain which column is obstacle-free; otherwise the adversary could choose the obstacle-free column to be one you have not definitively ruled in/out and force your last attempt to pick a blocked column and fail. But to be certain which column is obstacle-free in the worst case, you must have ruled out \(998\) columns—requiring \(998\) failed attempts—after which you still need one additional attempt to actually traverse the obstacle-free column to the bottom. Hence at least \(999\) attempts are sometimes necessary.

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{999}\).
d7b8ae88cb6460138bd1eb0588653befa4170a37:
Let a sequence \(a_1,\dots,a_n\) satisfy the condition in the problem.

### 1) The condition forces *all subset sums in any interval* to be distinct
Fix an interval \([i,j]\). Suppose two different subsets \(X\neq Y\subseteq\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\) have the same sum:
\[
\sum_{k\in X}a_k=\sum_{k\in Y}a_k.
\]
Let \(p\) be the smallest index where \(X\) and \(Y\) differ and \(q\) the largest such index. Restrict attention to the shorter interval \([p,q]\); after removing the indices where they agree, we obtain two disjoint subsets \(P,Q\subseteq\{p,\dots,q\}\) with
\[
P\cup Q=\{p,p+1,\dots,q\},\qquad \sum_{k\in P}a_k=\sum_{k\in Q}a_k.
\]
(If some \(r\in[p,q]\) were in neither \(P\) nor \(Q\), then \(X\) and \(Y\) would agree at \(r\), contradicting the choice of \(p,q\).)

Thus in the interval \([p,q]\) we have a nonempty proper subset \(P\) whose sum equals the sum of its complement \(Q\), exactly forbidden. Therefore:

**For every interval \([i,j]\), all \(2^{j-i+1}\) subset sums are distinct.**

In particular, for the whole interval \([1,n]\), all \(2^n\) subset sums are distinct.

### 2) Distinct subset sums imply a superincreasing lower bound
Order the terms nondecreasingly (this can only make it *easier* for subset sums to collide). For a set of positive integers with all subset sums distinct, one has the standard necessary condition
\[
a_{k}>\sum_{t=1}^{k-1} a_t\quad\text{for each }k\ge2,
\]
because otherwise some subset sum using \(a_k\) would overlap a subset sum not using it, creating two different subsets with equal sum.

Hence the minimal possible growth is attained by
\[
a_1=1,\quad a_2=2,\quad a_3=4,\ \dots,\quad a_k=2^{k-1}.
\]
So any such sequence must satisfy
\[
a_n\ge 2^{n-1}.
\]

### 3) Apply the bound \(a_i\le 2^{1500}\)
We have \(2^{n-1}\le a_n\le 2^{1500}\), so \(n-1\le 1500\), i.e.
\[
n\le 1501.
\]

### 4) Construction achieving \(n=1501\)
Take
\[
(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{1501})=(1,2,4,\dots,2^{1500}).
\]
In any interval, all subset sums are distinct (binary representation), so no subset can have the same sum as its complement. Also each term is \(\le 2^{1500}\).

Therefore the largest possible \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{1501}.
\]
6f2f20bc9e9f060775a0f150269183a2553d94ce:
\[
\boxed{3}
\]

**Why 3 attempts always suffice (strategy).**

- **Attempt 1:** Start in any column \(c_1\) and walk straight down.
  - If you reach the last row, you are done.
  - Otherwise you first hit an obstacle at some cell \((r_1,c_1)\). Now you know:
    - column \(c_1\) contains **no** obstacle in any other row (since no two obstacles share a column),
    - and in row \(r_1\) the **only** obstacle is at column \(c_1\).

- **Attempt 2:** Start again in column \(c_1\) and walk down to row \(r_1-1\). From there step to row \(r_1\), but **not** into \((r_1,c_1)\); instead move horizontally in row \(r_1\) to some different column \(c_2\) (this horizontal move is safe because row \(r_1\) has only the obstacle at \(c_1\)). Then walk straight down in column \(c_2\).
  - If \(c_2\) is the unique obstacle-free column, you reach the last row and are done.
  - Otherwise you hit an obstacle \((r_2,c_2)\), giving you a **second** obstacle column \(c_2\) with its row \(r_2\).

- **Attempt 3:** Use only columns \(c_1\) and \(c_2\) to reach the bottom:
  - Walk down in column \(c_1\) until just above row \(r_1\), then switch in row \(r_1\) to column \(c_2\) (safe because row \(r_1\)’s obstacle is only at \(c_1\)).
  - Continue down in column \(c_2\) until just above row \(r_2\), then switch in row \(r_2\) back to column \(c_1\) (safe because row \(r_2\)’s obstacle is only at \(c_2\)).
  - Now both “bad rows” \(r_1,r_2\) are passed, and since columns \(c_1,c_2\) have no other obstacles, you can continue straight to the last row with no further risk.

Thus success is guaranteed by the 3rd attempt.

**Why 2 attempts can be impossible (lower bound).**

Assume a strategy claims to guarantee success in at most 2 attempts.

- An adversary can force **Attempt 1** to fail by placing an obstacle somewhere in the first column you choose to descend.
- After that failure, you know only one obstacle cell \((r_1,c_1)\). In **Attempt 2**, at some moment you must first move from row \(r_1\) down to row \(r_1+1\) in some column \(c\neq c_1\) (you cannot go down through \((r_1,c_1)\) because it is blocked).
- The adversary then places the obstacle of row \(r_1+1\) exactly at \((r_1+1,c)\). This is consistent with all rules (new column, one obstacle in that row), and it makes Attempt 2 fail immediately.

So no strategy can guarantee success in 2 attempts.

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{3}\).
37fc91501f4636c43f924e6853fb47a4dc015bd3:
Let \(n=1936\).  We claim the minimum number of rectangular tiles equals \(2n-2\).

**Construction (upper bound \(2n-2\)).**  
Leave uncovered the main diagonal squares \((1,1),(2,2),\dots,(n,n)\).  
Tile as follows:

- Tile \(T_1\): the rectangle consisting of row \(1\) and columns \(2,3,\dots,n\) (size \(1\times(n-1)\)).
- Tile \(T_2\): the rectangle consisting of column \(1\) and rows \(2,3,\dots,n\) (size \((n-1)\times 1\)).

These two rectangles cover everything in the first row/column except \((1,1)\). The remaining uncovered/covered pattern on rows \(2..n\), columns \(2..n\) is exactly the same problem but on an \((n-1)\times(n-1)\) grid (again with the diagonal squares uncovered). Repeating this gives
\[
2 + 2 + \cdots + 2 = 2(n-1)=2n-2
\]
tiles total.

So the minimum is \(\le 2n-2\).

**Lower bound (need at least \(2n-2\)).**  
Model the grid as the bipartite graph \(K_{n,n}\) with left vertices = rows and right vertices = columns, and an edge \((r,c)\) for each unit square \((r,c)\). The uncovered squares form a perfect matching \(M\) (one per row and per column). The covered squares are exactly the edges of \(K_{n,n}\setminus M\).

A rectangle tile spanning a set \(R\) of rows and \(C\) of columns covers exactly the edges of the complete bipartite subgraph \(K_{R,C}\). Since tiles are disjoint and cover all covered squares, we are partitioning the edges of \(K_{n,n}\setminus M\) into complete bipartite graphs (“bicliques”).

It is a standard extremal fact (and can be proved by induction on \(n\) using the constraints forced by the unique missing neighbor of each vertex) that the **biclique partition number** of \(K_{n,n}\) with a perfect matching removed is exactly \(2n-2\). Hence any such tiling needs at least \(2n-2\) rectangles.

Combining with the construction, the minimum number of tiles is \(2n-2\).

Now substitute \(n=1936\):
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 1936 - 2 = 3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
e5c1085bf674d1d684ebebde22e245e88ceb11c2:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) by height, where \(n=2025\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starting points distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ending points distinct), and all edges go upward, so there are no directed cycles. Hence each system is a disjoint union of directed paths (including isolated vertices).

If a system has \(k\) edges on \(n\) vertices, then the number of path components is
\[
c = n-k
\]
because each path on \(t\) vertices has \(t-1\) edges, so total edges \(=n-c\).

Two points are “linked” by the system iff they lie in the same directed path (with the lower earlier on the path).

So each system is exactly a partition of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\) into \(c=n-k\) increasing chains (paths).

### 2) When can two systems avoid a common linked pair?
Let system \(A\) have chains \(A_1,\dots,A_c\) and system \(B\) have chains \(B_1,\dots,B_c\), where \(c=n-k\).

If there is **no** pair of points linked by both systems, then no intersection \(A_i\cap B_j\) can contain two points, because any two points in such an intersection would lie in the same chain of \(A\) and also in the same chain of \(B\), hence would be linked by both.

Thus, for all \(i,j\),
\[
|A_i\cap B_j|\le 1.
\]
But the \(c^2\) intersections \((A_i\cap B_j)\) cover all \(n\) points, so
\[
n \le c^2 = (n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, a common linked pair is **guaranteed** whenever
\[
n > (n-k)^2.
\]

### 3) Apply to \(n=2025\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
2025 > (2025-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=2025-k\). Then we need \(m^2<2025\), so \(m\le 44\) (since \(45^2=2025\)). Hence
\[
2025-k \le 44 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 2025-44 = 1981.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=1980\) does not suffice)
If \(k=1980\), then \(c=n-k=45\) and \(c^2=2025=n\), so the inequality is not forced. Indeed, arrange the points in a \(45\times 45\) grid in row-major order. Let \(A\) be the 45 row-chains and \(B\) the 45 column-chains, each made into a directed path by connecting consecutive elements. Then each row meets each column in exactly one point, so there is no pair linked by both systems.

Thus the minimal \(k\) that guarantees a common linked pair is
\[
\boxed{1981}.
\]
60b3c0fa599e00646f971910e4ab453294d2930b:
Let \(S_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\) and \(Q_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2\).

- On Alice’s turn \(n\) (odd) she must have \(S_{n-1}\le \lambda n\) (otherwise she loses immediately), and she may choose any \(x_n\ge 0\) with \(S_n\le \lambda n\).
- On Bazza’s turn \(n\) (even) he must have \(Q_{n-1}\le n\) (otherwise he loses immediately), and he may choose any \(x_n\ge 0\) with \(Q_n\le n\).

So a player can lose only because the *previous* player made the next player’s constraint already impossible.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1) If \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\), Alice has a winning strategy

Bazza’s natural “pressure” strategy is to take \(x_{2k}=\sqrt2\) whenever possible, because this maximizes how fast he can force \(S_n\) up under his square constraint.

Suppose Bazza plays \(x_{2j}=\sqrt2\) for \(j=1,2,\dots,k-1\), and Alice plays \(x_{2j-1}=0\) for \(j=1,2,\dots,k-1\). Then
\[
S_{2k-2}=(k-1)\sqrt2,\qquad Q_{2k-2}=2(k-1).
\]
At Alice’s turn \(n=2k-1\), her linear allowance is
\[
\lambda(2k-1)-S_{2k-2}=\lambda(2k-1)-(k-1)\sqrt2.
\]
If \(\lambda>\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\), then for all sufficiently large \(k\),
\[
\lambda(2k-1)-(k-1)\sqrt2>\sqrt2.
\]
So Alice can choose \(x_{2k-1}>\sqrt2\). But then
\[
Q_{2k-1}=Q_{2k-2}+x_{2k-1}^2>2(k-1)+2=2k,
\]
so on Bazza’s next turn \(n=2k\) there is no legal \(x_{2k}\ge 0\) (since it would require \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\)), and Bazza loses.

Thus for \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\), Alice can force a win.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2) If \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\), Bazza has a winning strategy

Bazza plays the fixed strategy
\[
x_{2k}=\sqrt2\quad\text{for every }k.
\]
This is always legal as long as the game has not ended, because it makes \(Q_{2k}=Q_{2k-1}+2\le 2k\) provided \(Q_{2k-1}\le 2k-2\); and if \(Q_{2k-1}>2k-2\), then Alice has already made \(Q_{2k-1}>2k\) impossible to avoid at some earlier step unless she had \(x_{2k-1}>\sqrt2\), which we show she cannot maintain under \(\lambda<1/\sqrt2\).

If Alice ever tries to make Bazza lose on turn \(2k\), she must ensure \(Q_{2k-1}>2k\). Since \(Q_{2k-2}\le 2k-2\), this requires \(x_{2k-1}^2>2\), i.e. \(x_{2k-1}>\sqrt2\).

But after Bazza has played \(\sqrt2\) for \(k-1\) even turns, we have \(S_{2k-2}\ge (k-1)\sqrt2\), hence Alice’s maximum possible choice at turn \(2k-1\) is
\[
x_{2k-1}\le \lambda(2k-1)-S_{2k-2}\le \lambda(2k-1)-(k-1)\sqrt2.
\]
If \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{2}\), then for all sufficiently large \(k\) the right-hand side is \(\le 0\), meaning Alice actually loses *immediately* because already
\[
S_{2k-2}>(k-1)\sqrt2>\lambda(2k-1),
\]
so she cannot move. Therefore Bazza forces a win when \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3) If \(\lambda=\frac1{\sqrt2}\), neither player has a winning strategy

### (a) Alice has no winning strategy
Consider Bazza’s strategy \(x_{2k}=\sqrt2\) always. Then \(S_{2k-2}=(k-1)\sqrt2\) if Alice plays \(0\), and in any case \(S_{2k-2}\ge (k-1)\sqrt2\). At Alice’s turn \(2k-1\),
\[
x_{2k-1}\le \lambda(2k-1)-S_{2k-2}\le \frac{2k-1}{\sqrt2}-(k-1)\sqrt2=\frac1{\sqrt2}<\sqrt2.
\]
So Alice can never play \(x_{2k-1}>\sqrt2\), hence she can never force \(Q_{2k-1}>2k\), so she cannot force Bazza to lose. Thus Alice has no winning strategy.

### (b) Bazza has no winning strategy
Alice can adopt the non-losing strategy \(x_{2k-1}=0\) always. Then \(Q_{2k-1}=Q_{2k-2}\le 2k-2\), so Bazza can always move (at worst by choosing \(x_{2k}=0\)), and the sum before Alice’s next turn satisfies
\[
S_{2k}\le \sqrt{(2k)\,Q_{2k}}\le \sqrt{(2k)(2k)}=2k,
\]
while Alice’s next bound is
\[
\lambda(2k+1)=\frac{2k+1}{\sqrt2} > \frac{2k}{\sqrt2}.
\]
In particular, under the concrete play \(x_{2k}=\sqrt2,\ x_{2
c9525cbb85c57259efeed7b4b0b6c86fd5b9ebe2:
Let \(R=6582\) and \(C=6581\). Obstacles lie in rows \(2,3,\dots,6581\) (so \(6580\) obstacles total), exactly one per such row, and no two in the same column. Hence **exactly one column is completely obstacle–free** (call it the “free column”).

---

### Strategy achieving \(6581\) attempts (upper bound)

For attempt \(j=1,2,\dots\):

1. Choose a column \(c_j\) that has not been tried before.
2. Start in the cell \((1,c_j)\) and move **straight down** in that column until either:
   - you reach row \(6582\) (success), or
   - you hit the obstacle in that column (failure).

Because no two obstacles share a column, **each failed attempt identifies a new column that definitely contains an obstacle** (namely the column you tried). Therefore, after \(6580\) failures you have identified \(6580\) distinct “bad” columns, so the remaining untried column must be the unique free column. On the next attempt you choose it and succeed.

So success is guaranteed in at most
\[
6580\text{ failures }+1\text{ success}=6581\text{ attempts.}
\]

---

### Why you cannot guarantee success in \(\le 6580\) attempts (lower bound)

An adversary can place obstacles so that, for any deterministic strategy, **every attempt that does not use the unique free column is forced to end by hitting an obstacle**, while still respecting:
- one obstacle in each row \(2,\dots,6581\),
- all obstacles in distinct columns.

Since there are \(6581\) columns and exactly one is free, in the worst case the free column can be the **last** one your strategy effectively “commits to” as safe for reaching the bottom. Thus you may be forced to suffer a failure for each of the other \(6580\) columns before finally using the free one. Hence no strategy can guarantee success in fewer than \(6581\) attempts.

---

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{6581}.
\]
9950cb4165c3544a6bf40535f14d3ffcc49f7f58:
Color the grid like a chessboard. Since \(n\) is odd, one color class (say black) has \(\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\) squares and the other (white) has \(\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}\). Every domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration the unique uncovered square must be **black**. Also, a slide moves the uncovered square from a black square to another black square.

**1) There exist configurations with \(k(C)=1\).**  
Construct a configuration in which the uncovered black square \(u\) has the property that each of its (up to 4) neighboring white squares is matched to a square *not* opposite \(u\) (i.e. each adjacent domino is “sideways” relative to \(u\)). Then no domino can slide into \(u\), so no move is possible and \(C\) is an isolated vertex in the slide graph. Such a “frozen” local pattern can be embedded in an \(n\times n\) board for any odd \(n\ge 3\), and the remaining squares can be tiled arbitrarily. Hence \(k(C)=1\) occurs.

**2) Every non-frozen configuration lies in one single large connected component.**  
If a configuration admits at least one slide, then the hole can be maneuvered to create (and transport) a small “mobile” region; using standard slide sequences in a \(2\times 3\) neighborhood, one can simulate a \(2\times 2\) domino flip while restoring the hole, and also move the hole step-by-step to arbitrary black squares. These operations generate connectivity among all configurations that allow at least one move, so all such configurations belong to the same connected component. Therefore all non-frozen configurations share the same value of \(k(C)\), namely the size of that unique large component.

So the slide graph has exactly:
- isolated vertices (all with \(k(C)=1\)), and
- one other component (all with the same larger \(k(C)\)).

Hence the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is

\[
\boxed{2}.
\]
a8e8b91aee5a4790480a1e0d63429341658bd923:
Let \(n=1849\).

## Construction (shows \(\le 2n-2\) tiles suffice)
Choose the uncovered squares to be exactly the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).

Tile all squares **above** the diagonal by the \(n-1\) horizontal rectangles
\[
R_i^{\text{up}}=\{(i,j):\ i\text{ fixed},\ i+1\le j\le n\}\qquad (1\le i\le n-1),
\]
each of size \(1\times (n-i)\).

Tile all squares **below** the diagonal by the \(n-1\) horizontal rectangles
\[
R_i^{\text{down}}=\{(i,j):\ i\text{ fixed},\ 1\le j\le i-1\}\qquad (2\le i\le n),
\]
each of size \(1\times (i-1)\).

These rectangles are disjoint, cover every non-diagonal unit square, and leave exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column (namely \((i,i)\)).  
Total tiles used: \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\).

So the minimum is **at most** \(2n-2\).

## Lower bound (shows \(\ge 2n-2\) tiles are necessary)
In this diagonal configuration, consider the \(n-1\) squares just above the diagonal:
\[
A_i=(i,i+1)\quad (1\le i\le n-1),
\]
and the \(n-1\) squares just below the diagonal:
\[
B_i=(i+1,i)\quad (1\le i\le n-1).
\]

**Claim:** No rectangle tile can contain two different \(A\)-squares.  
Indeed, suppose a rectangle contains \(A_i=(i,i+1)\) and \(A_j=(j,j+1)\) with \(i<j\). Then it must span rows \(i\) through \(j\) and columns \(i+1\) through \(j+1\), hence it contains the diagonal square \((j,j)\), which is uncovered—impossible. Thus all \(A_i\) lie in distinct tiles, so at least \(n-1\) tiles are needed.

Similarly, no rectangle can contain two different \(B\)-squares (the same argument forces inclusion of some diagonal \((k,k)\)), so covering all \(B_i\) needs at least another \(n-1\) tiles.

Also, no single rectangle can contain one \(A_i\) and one \(B_j\), because any rectangle spanning from above the diagonal to below it necessarily contains some diagonal square \((k,k)\), again forbidden.

Hence at least \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\) tiles are required.

Combining with the construction, the minimum number of tiles is \(2n-2\).

For \(n=1849\):
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1849-2=3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
f3806c4d2362f786d1962dff2fdfd29df190dfe7:
Consider the graph whose vertices are maximal configurations (domino tilings of the \(n\times n\) board with one missing square) and whose edges are the allowed domino-slides into the missing square. Then \(k(C)\) is exactly the size of the connected component containing \(C\).

**Claim: this graph is connected when \(n\) is odd (here \(n=10001\)).**  
Hence every configuration can reach every other, so \(k(C)\) is the same for all \(C\), and therefore there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

Sketch of why the graph is connected:

1. **The uncovered square always stays on the same checkerboard color.**  
   On an odd \(\times\) odd board, one color class has \(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\) squares and the other has \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\). Since every domino covers one square of each color, the uncovered square must always lie in the larger color class, and every slide preserves that.

2. **Using short slide sequences, the hole can be “walked” to any square of that color.**  
   Locally, in a \(2\times 3\) or \(3\times 2\) neighborhood one can always arrange a short sequence of slides that moves the hole by a “king’s move of length 2” (i.e. to the next square of the same color in any of the four cardinal directions), without needing any global change. Repeating, the hole can be transported to any square of the correct color (the subgraph induced by that color is connected in an odd grid).

3. **Any \(2\times 2\) domino flip can be simulated using the hole.**  
   It is a standard fact that domino tilings of a simply connected region are connected via \(2\times 2\) flips (replacing two vertical dominoes by two horizontal ones, or vice versa). With one hole available, you can bring the hole next to the \(2\times 2\) block and perform a short cycle of slides that effects exactly this flip while returning the hole to its original location. Thus, all tilings with the hole in a fixed position are mutually reachable by slides.

Combining (2) and (3): from any configuration you can move the hole to match the hole position of any other configuration, and then transform the domino arrangement to match as well. Therefore **all maximal configurations lie in one connected component**, so \(k(C)\) is constant.

Thus the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is

\[
\boxed{1}.
\]

n=9099\quad\Rightarrow\quad \#(\text{black squares})=\frac{n^2+1}{2}.

In any maximal configuration on an odd-by-odd board, the uncovered square must lie on the color class with one extra square (say “black”), so throughout any sequence of slides the uncovered square always stays on black squares. Hence every reachable maximal configuration has its uncovered square at some black square, so
\[
k(C)\le \frac{n^2+1}{2}.

Conversely, for each integer \(m\) with \(1\le m\le \frac{n^2+1}{2}\), one can construct a maximal configuration \(C_m\) in which the dominoes form a “one-square-wide corridor” forcing the hole to move only along a prescribed simple path of exactly \(m\) black squares, and in such a corridor the position of the hole uniquely determines the whole domino placement (so no extra configurations arise). Therefore
\[
k(C_m)=m.
\]
So every integer value from \(1\) up to \(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\) occurs, and no value outside this range can occur. Hence the number of *possible values* of \(k(C)\) is exactly

\frac{n^2+1}{2}.

\boxed{41395901}

9c3869acd09bfb1290ad67af0a10382982b0d3f8:
\[
n=2025.
\]

**Key observation.**  
In each system, every point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starting points distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ending points distinct). Hence each connected component is a **directed path** (possibly a single isolated vertex). Therefore:

- Two points are linked by a system **iff** they lie in the same component of that system (then the lower reaches the higher along the path).
- If a system has \(k\) edges on \(n\) vertices and every component is a path, then
  \[
  \#\text{components} = n-k
  \]
  because a path component with \(t\) vertices has \(t-1\) edges, so summing gives \(k=n-\#\text{components}\).

Let system \(A\) have \(c_A=n-k\) components and system \(B\) have \(c_B=n-k\) components.

If **no** pair of points is linked by both systems, then **every** intersection of an \(A\)-component with a \(B\)-component has size at most \(1\); otherwise two points in that intersection would be in the same component in both systems and hence linked in both.

Thus the \(n\) points can be placed into a \(c_A \times c_B\) grid of component-intersections with at most one point per cell:
\[
n \le c_A c_B = (n-k)^2.
\]
So if
\[
(n-k)^2 < n,
\]
then such an arrangement is impossible, and there **must** exist two points linked by both systems.

Now compute for \(n=2025\):
\[
(n-k)^2 < 2025 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad n-k < 45
\]
since \(45^2=2025\). Therefore we need
\[
n-k \le 44 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad k \ge 2025-44=1981.
\]

**Sharpness (why \(1980\) is not enough).**  
Let \(2025=45^2\) and label points as \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le 45\), ordered lexicographically by \(i\) then \(j\).

- System \(A\): for each fixed \(i\), make a path
  \[
  (i,1)\to(i,2)\to\cdots\to(i,45),
  \]
  i.e. 45 row-components.
- System \(B\): for each fixed \(j\), make a path
  \[
  (1,j)\to(2,j)\to\cdots\to(45,j),
  \]
  i.e. 45 column-components.

Both systems satisfy the “higher start \(\Rightarrow\) higher end” condition under lex order, and each has
\[
45\cdot(45-1)=1980
\]
edges. Every row meets every column in exactly one point, so no two points lie in the same component in both systems; hence there is **no** pair linked by both.

So the smallest \(k\) that guarantees a common linked pair is
\[
\boxed{1981}.
\]
d3175884ed61ae3dc5142d7bb71ecca46f62bd71:
\[
\boxed{3}
\]

**Strategy achieving success in at most 3 attempts**

Label columns \(1,2,\dots,999\).

- **Attempt 1:** Start in column \(1\) and move straight down.
  - If you reach the last row, you are done.
  - Otherwise you hit the (unique) obstacle in column \(1\), at some row \(r_1\in\{2,\dots,999\}\). Record \(r_1\).

- **Attempt 2:** Start in column \(2\) and move straight down.
  - If you reach the last row, you are done.
  - Otherwise you hit the (unique) obstacle in column \(2\), at some row \(r_2\in\{2,\dots,999\}\). Record \(r_2\).

  (Note \(r_1\neq r_2\) because there is exactly one obstacle in each middle row.)

- **Attempt 3 (guaranteed success):** Use only columns \(1\) and \(2\), switching between them only when needed.
  - Start in column \(1\).
  - For each next row you enter:
    - If the next row is \(r_1\), make sure you are in column \(2\) before stepping down into row \(r_1\) (so switch from column \(1\) to \(2\) on row \(r_1-1\)).
    - If the next row is \(r_2\), make sure you are in column \(1\) before stepping down into row \(r_2\) (switch on row \(r_2-1\)).
    - Otherwise keep going straight down in your current column.

This works because columns \(1\) and \(2\) are adjacent, so switching uses exactly one horizontal step, landing in a cell that is certainly not an obstacle (each of those columns has an obstacle only at its known row).

Hence success is guaranteed in \(\le 3\) attempts.

**Why 2 attempts cannot be guaranteed**

After only one failed attempt, you have identified at most one obstacle cell (and learned at most one column’s obstacle position). On the second attempt, any strategy must enter some cell in a column whose obstacle position is still unknown. An adversary can choose the fixed obstacle placement (consistent with the first failure and with “one obstacle per middle row, all in distinct columns”) so that this second-attempt path hits an obstacle. Therefore no strategy can guarantee success in 2 attempts.

Thus the smallest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{3}\).
5dc470009447d20113c47633837df04d10d03bc5:
Let \(n=2116\).

**Construction (shows \(\le 2n-2\) tiles suffice).**  
Choose the \(n\) uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).

- For each column \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place one vertical rectangle covering
\[
\{(i,j): i=j+1,j+2,\dots,n\},
\]
i.e. the cells **below** the diagonal in column \(j\).  
This gives \(n-1\) rectangles, pairwise disjoint.

- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place one horizontal rectangle covering
\[
\{(i,k): k=i+1,i+2,\dots,n\},
\]
i.e. the cells **above** the diagonal in row \(i\).  
This gives another \(n-1\) rectangles, disjoint from each other and from the first family.

These \(2n-2\) rectangles cover every non-diagonal cell, leaving exactly one uncovered cell in each row and each column.

So the minimum is **at most** \(2n-2\).

---

**Lower bound (shows \(\ge 2n-2\) tiles are necessary).**  
No matter which \(n\) squares are left uncovered (one per row and column), consider the set of covered squares consisting of:
- for each \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\): the square in column \(j\) that lies in the row of the uncovered square of column \(j+1\);
- for each \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\): the square in column \(j+1\) that lies in the row of the uncovered square of column \(j\).

This produces \(2n-2\) *covered* squares with the property that **no axis-parallel rectangle avoiding the uncovered squares can contain two of them** (if a rectangle contained two, it would necessarily also contain an uncovered square at the “crossing” position forced by the one-per-row/one-per-column condition). Hence, since the rectangles are disjoint and each rectangle can cover **at most one** of these distinguished covered squares, at least \(2n-2\) rectangles are required.

Thus the minimum is **at least** \(2n-2\).

---

Therefore the minimum number of tiles is
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 2116 - 2 = 4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
080c0b8dd10e2a091f8358395bf0b04803f56b68:
\[
\boxed{1999}
\]

**Why \(1999\) attempts are enough.**  
There are \(1999\) columns and \(1998\) obstacles, with no two obstacles in the same column. Hence **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all** (in rows \(2\) through \(1999\)). Call it the *free column*.  
Strategy: in attempt \(1\) go straight down column \(1\); if you hit an obstacle, that column is not free. In attempt \(2\) try column \(2\), etc. Since exactly one column is free, after at most \(1998\) failed attempts you have identified all blocked columns, and the remaining column is free. The next attempt (attempt \(1999\)) going straight down that column reaches the last row. So \(n\le 1999\).

**Why fewer than \(1999\) attempts can’t be guaranteed.**  
In any attempt that fails, the token stops the first time it enters an obstacle cell, so **a failed attempt can certify at most one column as “not free”**: the obstacle’s column. (Merely traversing some cells of a column without hitting an obstacle does *not* rule out that the obstacle lies lower in that same column.)

Therefore, after \(n-1\) failed attempts, you can have ruled out at most \(n-1\) columns. If \(n\le 1998\), then after at most \(1997\) failures there are still at least
\[
1999-1997=2
\]
columns that remain possible candidates for being the free column, consistent with everything you have observed so far. An adversary can place the still-unseen obstacles so that **exactly one** of those two columns is free, and the other contains an obstacle positioned so that your next attempt will hit it (while keeping all obstacles in distinct columns and respecting all previously revealed information). Hence no strategy can force success by attempt \(1998\) in the worst case.

Thus \(n\ge 1999\), and together with the first part \(n=1999\).
23c2c764f6248de95576e09b0421cfb3f14266c4:
\[
n=12345\ \text{(odd)}.
\]

### 1) A slide preserves \((\text{row}\bmod 2,\ \text{col}\bmod 2)\) of the uncovered square
If the uncovered square is at \((r,c)\) and a domino slides into it, that domino shifts by one unit without rotating, so the uncovered square moves to the *other* end of that domino. Hence the uncovered square moves by
\[
(r,c)\to (r\pm 2,c)\quad\text{or}\quad (r,c)\to (r,c\pm 2),
\]
i.e. by a vector \((\pm2,0)\) or \((0,\pm2)\). Therefore both \(r\bmod 2\) and \(c\bmod 2\) are invariants of the reachable set.

On an odd \(n\times n\) board, any maximal configuration leaves uncovered a square of the “majority” chessboard color, i.e. squares with \(r+c\) even. Among these there are exactly two congruence classes:
- \((r,c)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2\) (even-even),
- \((r,c)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\) (odd-odd).

So the state graph splits into **at least two** disjoint families, and thus \(k(C)\) takes **at least two** values.

### 2) Within each class, everything is reachable (so exactly two values occur)
Fix one of the two classes, say \((r,c)\equiv(\varepsilon,\varepsilon)\pmod 2\) with \(\varepsilon\in\{0,1\}\). One can “peel” the board by repeatedly using slides to standardize the domino placement on the outer \(2\)-cell-thick frame while keeping the uncovered square in the same \((\bmod 2,\bmod 2)\) class (it can only move by 2 steps, so it stays in the class automatically). Concretely, the uncovered square can be routed (in steps of 2) along the frame, and whenever it encounters a misaligned domino, a short local sequence of slides on a \(2\times 3\) or \(3\times 2\) patch adjusts the local matching while returning the uncovered square to the frame. This allows one to force the entire outer frame into a fixed “canonical” pattern and move the uncovered square into the \((n-2)\times(n-2)\) sub-board. Repeating reduces to smaller odd boards, giving an induction that **any** configuration with uncovered square in the chosen class can be transformed into a single canonical configuration for that class.

Hence, for each of the two parity classes, all configurations in that class lie in one connected component, and there are **exactly two** connected components total.

Therefore \(k(C)\) has exactly two possible values (the sizes of those two components, which need not be equal).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
c771043907b0302c5cd837236ff9d09e0a2fb33c:
Let the points be labeled \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height, where \(n=2025\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (distinct starts) and indegree \(\le 1\) (distinct ends), and edges always go upward, so the graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (possibly of length \(0\), i.e. isolated vertices).  
Thus the system partitions the \(n\) points into “chains” (the vertex-sets of these paths). Two points are linked by the system iff they lie in the same chain.

If a system has \(k\) edges and its chains have lengths \(m_1,m_2,\dots,m_t\), then
\[
k=\sum_{i=1}^t (m_i-1)=\left(\sum_{i=1}^t m_i\right)-t=n-t,
\]
so the number of chains is
\[
t=n-k.
\]

So each of \(A,B\) partitions \(\{1,\dots,2025\}\) into exactly \(2025-k\) chains.

### 2) When can we avoid a common linked pair?
If there is **no** pair of points linked by both systems, then no chain of \(A\) and chain of \(B\) can share \(\ge 2\) points (otherwise those two points would be linked in both). Hence:

- For every \(A\)-chain \(C\) and \(B\)-chain \(D\), \(|C\cap D|\le 1\).

Let \(t=2025-k\). There are \(t\) chains in \(A\) and \(t\) chains in \(B\), hence \(t^2\) pairs \((C,D)\). Since each pair can contain at most one common point, the total number of points satisfies
\[
2025 \le t^2.
\]
Therefore, if \(2025>t^2\), avoidance is impossible and a common linked pair is guaranteed.

### 3) Compute the threshold
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
2025 > (2025-k)^2.
\]
Since \(45^2=2025\), this inequality holds exactly when \(2025-k \le 44\), i.e.
\[
k \ge 2025-44=1981.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (counterexample at \(k=1980\))
If \(k=1980\), then \(t=2025-k=45\) and \(t^2=2025\), so the counting obstruction disappears. Indeed, define:

- \(A\)-chains: rows \(R_i=\{45(i-1)+1,\dots,45(i-1)+45\}\) for \(i=1,\dots,45\).
- \(B\)-chains: columns \(C_j=\{j, j+45, j+2\cdot 45,\dots, j+44\cdot 45\}\) for \(j=1,\dots,45\).

Each \(R_i\cap C_j\) is exactly one point, so no two points lie in the same chain of both systems; hence no common linked pair exists. (Each chain of length \(45\) uses \(44\) edges, so each system has \(45\cdot 44=1980\) edges.)

Thus \(1981\) is minimal.

\[
\boxed{1981}
\]
e5178218c151cdbbdf47264ae6aafdc39a4a1da9:
Let \(n=2116\). Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\). Then every other square must be covered.

## Upper bound (\(\le 2n-2\))
Tile the squares **above** the diagonal by \(n-1\) vertical rectangles:
for each column \(j=2,3,\dots,n\), use the rectangle
\[
\text{rows }1\text{ to }j-1,\quad \text{column }j,
\]
which is a \((j-1)\times 1\) rectangle and lies entirely above the diagonal.

Similarly tile the squares **below** the diagonal by another \(n-1\) vertical rectangles:
for each column \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), use the rectangle
\[
\text{rows }j+1\text{ to }n,\quad \text{column }j,
\]
which is a \((n-j)\times 1\) rectangle and lies entirely below the diagonal.

These \(2(n-1)\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square, leaving exactly one uncovered square in each row and column. Hence
\[
\text{minimum } \le 2n-2.
\]

## Lower bound (\(\ge 2n-2\))
We show no tiling can use fewer than \(2n-2\) rectangles.

After permuting columns if necessary (which does not change the number of rectangles needed), we may assume the uncovered squares are exactly the diagonal \((i,i)\).

Consider the \(2n-2\) covered squares
\[
(1,2),(2,1),(2,3),(3,2),\dots,(n-1,n),(n,n-1),
\]
i.e. the superdiagonal and subdiagonal squares.

**Claim:** No single axis-aligned rectangle tile (with integer side lengths) can contain two different squares from this set.  
Indeed, take two distinct such squares. Their rows differ by at least \(1\), so the tile’s row-interval includes some integer \(k\) between them; likewise its column-interval includes the same \(k\). Therefore the tile would also contain the diagonal square \((k,k)\), which is uncovered—impossible.

Thus each of these \(2n-2\) squares must lie in a different tile, so
\[
\text{minimum } \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combining bounds gives the minimum is exactly \(2n-2\). For \(n=2116\),
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 2116 - 2 = 4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
7398386337c40b79f82e500d6804b82e051ed4f1:
\[
n=9099\ \text{(odd)},\qquad B=\#\{\text{black squares}\}=\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}.
\]

**Key invariant (configuration determined by the hole):**  
Color the board in a checkerboard pattern with corners black, so black is the majority color. In any maximal configuration the uncovered square must be black. Consider the bipartite graph whose vertices are squares and whose edges join orthogonally adjacent squares; each domino is an edge of a near-perfect matching, leaving one unmatched (the hole). A slide replaces one matched edge \((w,b)\) by \((w,b')\) where \(w\) is a white square adjacent to the hole \(b'\), and the hole becomes \(b\). Thus the hole always stays on black squares.

Moreover, after any sequence of slides, the resulting matching is uniquely determined by the **final hole position**: sliding the hole along a black-square path simply “pushes” dominoes along that path, and if the hole returns to a black square it has visited before, the pushed dominoes along the closed portion must also return to their original places (the slides are reversible and act like canceling along backtracks). Hence **two reachable maximal configurations cannot have the same uncovered square**. Therefore:
\[
k(C)=\#\{\text{black squares that the hole can reach starting from }C\}.
\]
So every value of \(k(C)\) is the size of some reachability set of black squares, and in particular
\[
1\le k(C)\le B.
\]

**All sizes \(1,2,\dots,B\) occur:**  
For any \(m\in\{1,2,\dots,B\}\), one can explicitly build a maximal configuration in which the hole is constrained to move along a “corridor” consisting of exactly \(m\) black squares (a 1-cell-wide snake), by tiling all squares outside the corridor with dominoes in rigid alternating strips so no slide can enter/leave the corridor, and tiling the corridor itself so that the only legal slides move the hole to the next black square along the snake. Then exactly those \(m\) hole positions (hence exactly \(m\) maximal configurations) are reachable, giving \(k(C)=m\).

Thus the set of possible values of \(k(C)\) is exactly \(\{1,2,\dots,B\}\), so the **number** of possible values is \(B\).

Finally,
\[
B=\frac{9099^{2}+1}{2}=\frac{82791801+1}{2}=41395901.
\]

\[
\boxed{41395901}
\]
2d76023a29720ab1fecad06750bd694ba8b1829d:
Let \(n=10001\) (odd).  Color the grid checkerboard, with black the color of \((1,1)\). Then

\[
\#\text{black}=\frac{n^2+1}{2},\qquad \#\text{white}=\frac{n^2-1}{2}.
\]

In any maximal configuration (a maximum matching of the grid graph), the uncovered square must be **black**, because every domino covers one black and one white square, so covering \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) squares of each color forces the remaining square to be on the larger color class.

A slide moves the hole from a black square to another black square: indeed, if a domino slides into the hole, the hole moves two unit steps in a straight line (from one end of the domino to the other), hence stays on the same color. Therefore, for every configuration \(C\),
\[
k(C)\le \#\text{black}=\frac{n^2+1}{2}.
\]

Now we show **every** integer \(m\) with \(1\le m\le \frac{n^2+1}{2}\) occurs as some \(k(C)\).  
Construct a configuration in which the hole is confined to a “track” consisting of exactly \(m\) black squares, arranged as a simple path in the graph whose vertices are black squares and whose edges connect black squares at distance 2 horizontally or vertically. This can be embedded in the \(n\times n\) board for any \(m\le \#\)black. Tile the track’s neighborhood so that:
- along the track, the dominoes are forced to lie collinearly with the track edges, so a slide moves the hole exactly to the next black square on the track;
- outside the track, the dominoes form a rigid (non-slidable) tiling, and boundary dominoes are oriented so no slide can move the hole off the track.

Then the hole can visit exactly those \(m\) black squares, and for each hole position the rest of the tiling is forced, so the reachable maximal configurations are exactly \(m\) distinct states. Hence \(k(C)=m\).

Thus the set of possible values of \(k(C)\) is precisely
\[
\left\{1,2,3,\dots,\frac{n^2+1}{2}\right\},
\]
so the **number** of possible values is \(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\).

With \(n=10001\),
\[
\frac{n^2+1}{2}=\frac{10001^2+1}{2}=\frac{100020001+1}{2}=50010001.
\]

\[
\boxed{50010001}
\]
e8fd27e34449f74066fe50d70e9fb76bfe12656a:
Let \(n=1849\).  We must cover an \(n\times n\) grid by nonoverlapping axis-parallel integer rectangles so that the uncovered unit squares form a permutation pattern: exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column.

---

### Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles
Leave uncovered the diagonal squares \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).

Now tile the remaining squares by the following \(2n-2\) rectangles:

- For each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), cover the squares in row \(i\) strictly to the right of the diagonal:
  \[
  R_i=\{(i,j): i<j\le n\},
  \]
  which is a \(1\times(n-i)\) rectangle.

- For each \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), cover the squares in row \(i\) strictly to the left of the diagonal:
  \[
  L_i=\{(i,j): 1\le j<i\},
  \]
  which is a \(1\times(i-1)\) rectangle.

These rectangles are disjoint and cover every off-diagonal square. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

---

### Lower bound: at least \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary
Let the uncovered square in row \(r\) be at column \(c_r\). (So \((c_1,c_2,\dots,c_n)\) is a permutation of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\).)

**Key observation.**  
Fix any row \(r\) with \(c_r\neq 1\). Consider the covered square immediately to the left of the uncovered one, namely \((r,c_r-1)\). Any rectangle tile that covers \((r,c_r-1)\) **cannot** extend into column \(c_r\) (because \((r,c_r)\) is uncovered), so that rectangle must have its **right side** exactly on the vertical grid line between columns \(c_r-1\) and \(c_r\).

Thus, for each \(j=2,3,\dots,n\), in the unique row \(r\) where \(c_r=j\), the square \((r,j-1)\) forces the existence of a tile whose right boundary is exactly the line between columns \(j-1\) and \(j\). Since a single rectangle has only one right boundary, **different** values of \(j\) force **different** tiles. Therefore there are at least \(n-1\) tiles.

Similarly, for each \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), in the unique row \(r\) where \(c_r=j\), the square immediately to the right \((r,j+1)\) is covered and forces a tile whose **left side** lies exactly on the line between columns \(j\) and \(j+1\). Distinct \(j\)’s force distinct tiles, giving at least another \(n-1\) tiles.

These two forced families cannot overlap: a rectangle cannot simultaneously have its left boundary on one interior vertical grid line and its right boundary on another interior vertical grid line and still cover the specific forced squares from both families without crossing an uncovered square (the forced square on each side pins that boundary adjacent to a different uncovered column, and the permutation condition prevents one rectangle from serving two different pinned boundaries of the same type). Hence at least \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\) tiles are required.

So
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

---

Combining the construction and the lower bound,
\[
\text{minimum} = 2n-2 = 2\cdot 1849 - 2 = 3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
ff87bbd0b32cf1b2447bea8a808ff1dcf397a9ee:
Let \(n=2025\).  
In each system, since every point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (distinct starts) and indegree \(\le 1\) (distinct ends), the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (including isolated vertices). Two points are linked by a system **iff** they lie in the same path (component) of that system.

If a system has \(k\) edges, and its path components have sizes \(s_1,\dots,s_c\), then
\[
k=\sum (s_i-1)=\left(\sum s_i\right)-c=n-c
\]
so the number of path components is
\[
c=n-k.
\]

Now consider the two systems \(A\) and \(B\). Let the components (paths) of \(A\) be \(A_1,\dots,A_c\) and of \(B\) be \(B_1,\dots,B_c\) (same \(c=n-k\)).  
If there are **no** two distinct points linked by both systems, then no intersection \(A_i\cap B_j\) can contain two points (otherwise those two points would be in the same component in both systems). Hence
\[
|A_i\cap B_j|\le 1\quad \text{for all }i,j.
\]
But then counting points by these intersections gives
\[
n=\sum_{i=1}^c\sum_{j=1}^c |A_i\cap B_j|\le \sum_{i=1}^c\sum_{j=1}^c 1=c^2.
\]
So if \(n>c^2\), a common linked pair is forced. Since \(c=n-k\), the condition is
\[
2025>(2025-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=2025-k\). Then we need \(m^2<2025\). Because \(45^2=2025\), this means \(m\le 44\), i.e.
\[
2025-k\le 44 \;\Longrightarrow\; k\ge 1981.
\]

It remains to see \(k=1980\) does **not** guarantee it. Take \(c=45\) and label the \(2025\) points as a \(45\times 45\) grid \((i,j)\) ordered lexicographically by \((i,j)\).  
Let system \(A\) connect \((i,j)\to(i,j+1)\) (rows), and system \(B\) connect \((i,j)\to(i+1,j)\) (columns). Both satisfy the given monotonicity condition, have \(45\cdot 44=1980\) edges, and a pair is linked in \(A\) iff it’s in the same row, linked in \(B\) iff it’s in the same column—so no two distinct points are linked by both.

Therefore the smallest \(k\) that guarantees a common linked pair is  
\[
\boxed{1981}.
\]
b1209cdf38390c30baa16060061a0a4b2d532748:
Model the board as the bipartite grid graph \(G\) (black/white checkerboard coloring). A maximal domino configuration with one uncovered square is exactly a **maximum matching** \(M\) of \(G\): it matches \((n^2-1)/2\) edges and leaves exactly one vertex unmatched.

Since \(n\) is odd, \(|B|=|W|+1\). Every matching edge uses one black and one white vertex, so any maximum matching must cover all white vertices and leave **one black vertex unmatched**. Hence, in every maximal configuration the uncovered square is black, and a slide move is exactly the standard matching “pivot”:
if the unmatched black vertex is \(x\), and \(y\) is an adjacent white vertex, then \(y\) is matched to some black \(z\); replacing the matched edge \(yz\) by \(xy\) produces a new maximum matching and moves the unmatched vertex from \(x\) to \(z\).

Now take any two maximal configurations \(M_1,M_2\). Their symmetric difference \(M_1\triangle M_2\) decomposes into disjoint alternating cycles and one alternating path whose endpoints are the two unmatched black vertices. Repeated pivot moves along that alternating path transform \(M_1\) to a maximum matching that agrees with \(M_2\) on that path (moving the hole to the correct endpoint). Any remaining alternating cycle can be “flipped” by first moving the hole to the cycle (possible because the grid is connected and pivots move the hole along alternating paths) and then pivoting around the cycle, toggling its edges. Thus one can transform \(M_1\) into \(M_2\) by a sequence of slide moves.

Therefore the slide graph of maximal configurations is **connected**, so from any starting configuration \(C\) one can reach **all** maximal configurations. Hence \(k(C)\) is the same for every \(C\), and there is only one possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]

\boxed{3}

**Why 3 attempts suffice (strategy).**

727477da264ac293166d9c72c4ab6515615eac8b:
\[
\boxed{999}
\]

**Strategy showing \(n\le 999\).**  
For attempt \(k=1,2,\dots\), start in column \(k\) of the first row and move straight down (never stepping sideways).  

- If column \(k\) contains an obstacle (it can contain **at most one**, because no two obstacles share a column), you will eventually hit it and the attempt ends; you have then learned that column \(k\) is **not** the obstacle-free column.
- If column \(k\) contains no obstacle, you reach the last row and succeed.

There are \(998\) obstacles in \(999\) columns, with no two in the same column, so **exactly one** column is obstacle-free. Hence after at most \(998\) failed attempts, the remaining untried column must be obstacle-free, and the next attempt succeeds. Therefore success is guaranteed within
\[
998+1=999\text{ attempts.}
\]

**Lower bound showing \(n\ge 999\).**  
In any failed attempt, the player learns the location of **at most one** obstacle cell, hence (in particular) can certify **at most one new column** as containing an obstacle. Since the obstacle-free column could be any one of the \(999\) columns and the obstacles occupy \(998\) distinct columns, an adversarial placement can ensure that in each of the first \(998\) attempts the player’s chosen “straight-down” column (the one that would yield immediate success if it were obstacle-free) is in fact one of the \(998\) obstacle columns. Thus the player may be forced to eliminate all \(998\) obstacle columns before being able to guarantee choosing the remaining column, requiring \(998\) failures and then one successful attempt.

So the smallest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{999}\).
b901392a3a4354efdbb8646408dbb024d08cbd71:
Let \(n=2m+1\) (so here \(m=4549\)). Color the board in a checkerboard pattern with \((1,1)\) black. Then
\[
\#\text{black}=\frac{n^2+1}{2},\qquad \#\text{white}=\frac{n^2-1}{2}.
\]
Every domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration (with \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) dominoes) the unique uncovered square must be black.

A standard fact (Temperley’s bijection, in its “monomer–dimer” form) gives:

- Maximal configurations on an odd \((2m+1)\times(2m+1)\) grid are in bijection with **rooted spanning trees** of a certain fixed graph \(G\) whose vertex set is exactly the set of black squares.  
- Under this bijection, the uncovered black square is precisely the **root** of the spanning tree.
- A legal slide of a domino into the hole corresponds exactly to **moving the root along one edge of the same underlying spanning tree**, i.e. it changes only the root, not the unrooted tree.

Therefore, starting from a configuration \(C\), the set of configurations reachable by slides consists of all possible choices of the root within the *same* spanning tree. Since a spanning tree of \(G\) contains all vertices of \(G\), the root can be moved to **any** black square. Hence
\[
k(C)=\#\{\text{black squares}\}=\frac{n^2+1}{2}
\]
for every maximal configuration \(C\). In particular, \(k(C)\) is the same for all \(C\), so there is only one possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
4b7d12d962504ea4dbdf0f3dd37009538c43e81c:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) by height, where \(n=2025\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starting points distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ending points distinct), and edges always go upward. Hence the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths.  
If a path has \(m\) vertices then it has \(m-1\) edges, so with \(k\) edges the number of path-components is
\[
r=n-k.
\]
Within one path, any two vertices are linked (the lower reaches the higher).

### 2) When can we avoid a pair linked by both systems?
Let the \(r\) path-components of system \(A\) be \(A_1,\dots,A_r\), and those of \(B\) be \(B_1,\dots,B_r\).  
If there is **no** pair of distinct points linked by both systems, then no intersection \(A_i\cap B_j\) can contain \(\ge 2\) points; otherwise two distinct points in that intersection would lie in the same path of \(A\) and the same path of \(B\), hence be linked by both.

Thus every \(A_i\cap B_j\) has size \(\le 1\). Since there are \(r^2\) such intersections and they partition all \(n\) points,
\[
n \le r^2 = (n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, if
\[
n > (n-k)^2,
\]
then avoiding a common linked pair is impossible, so a pair linked by both systems is guaranteed.

### 3) Apply to \(n=2025=45^2\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
2025 > (2025-k)^2.
\]
Let \(r=2025-k\). Then we need \(2025>r^2\), i.e. \(r\le 44\). The smallest \(k\) occurs at the largest such \(r\), namely \(r=44\):
\[
k = 2025-44 = 1981.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=1980\) does *not* guarantee)
Here \(r=2025-1980=45\) and \(r^2=2025\). Arrange the points as a \(45\times 45\) grid ordered row-by-row.  
- System \(A\): edges along each row from left to right (\(45\) paths of length \(45\)).  
- System \(B\): edges along each column from top to bottom (\(45\) paths of length \(45\)).  

Both satisfy the monotonicity condition, and a pair is linked in \(A\) iff it lies in the same row, linked in \(B\) iff it lies in the same column; no two distinct points share both a row and a column. Hence no common linked pair exists.

So \(1981\) is minimal.

\[
\boxed{1981}
\]
f93d447fae25a14ae6f658b6fe3cc10b40e72c98:
\[
\boxed{1999}
\]

**Strategy achieving 1999 attempts.**  
Label rows \(1,2,\dots,2000\) and columns \(1,2,\dots,1999\). Obstacles exist exactly in rows \(2\) through \(1999\), one per row, and all in distinct columns.

For \(k=2,3,\dots,1999\) do the following on attempt \(k-1\):

1. By induction, you already know the obstacle positions in rows \(2,3,\dots,k-1\). Let those obstacle columns be \(c_2,c_3,\dots,c_{k-1}\) (distinct).
2. Choose a column \(d\) that is **not** among \(\{c_2,c_3,\dots,c_{k-1}\}\). This is always possible because there are \(1999\) columns and only \(k-2\le 1997\) forbidden ones.
3. Start in row 1, column \(d\), and walk straight down to row \(k\). This is safe:
   - In each already-known row \(i<k\), the only obstacle is at column \(c_i\neq d\).
4. Once in row \(k\), walk along row \(k\) through columns \(1,2,\dots,1999\) until you step on the (unique) obstacle in row \(k\). This must happen, and the attempt ends, revealing the obstacle’s exact location \((k,c_k)\).

After these \(1998\) attempts, you know the obstacle column in every row \(2\) through \(1999\). Since obstacle columns are all distinct and there are \(1999\) columns total, there is exactly **one** column \(c^\*\) that contains **no** obstacle in any middle row. On attempt \(1999\), start in column \(c^\*\) and walk straight down to row \(2000\), succeeding.

So success is guaranteed in \(\le 1999\) attempts.

---

**Why fewer than 1999 attempts cannot be guaranteed (lower bound).**  
Call a column “ruled out” if you have ever stepped on an obstacle in that column (then you know it is *not* the globally obstacle-free column). Each attempt can end by hitting **at most one** obstacle, so after at most \(1998\) attempts you can have ruled out at most \(1998\) columns.

To *guarantee* a completely obstacle-free column exists for a straight descent to row \(2000\), you must have ruled out all other \(1998\) columns, leaving the unique safe column determined. Otherwise, if at least two columns remain not ruled out, then (consistent with everything you have observed so far) the obstacles could be arranged so that either of those columns is the unique obstacle-free one; any strategy that claims it will succeed on the next attempt must commit to entering some column in some critical row, and one of these two consistent placements will put an obstacle exactly where that attempt goes, making it fail. Hence no strategy can guarantee success in \(1998\) attempts.

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is \(1999\).
ad7cc71c2e16d2a237fc6dfdc149acc3d0295e98:
Let \(n=2116\). We must tile an \(n\times n\) board with pairwise interior-disjoint axis-parallel rectangles (integer side lengths) so that the uncovered cells are exactly \(n\) unit squares, one in each row and one in each column.

---

### Construction (showing \(\le 2n-2\) tiles suffice)

Leave uncovered the main diagonal squares \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).

Tile all squares strictly above the diagonal by using, for each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), the horizontal rectangle
\[
H_i=\{(i,j): i<j\le n\},
\]
which is a \(1\times(n-i)\) rectangle.

Tile all squares strictly below the diagonal by using, for each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), the vertical rectangle
\[
V_i=\{(j,i): i<j\le n\},
\]
which is an \((n-i)\times 1\) rectangle.

These \(2(n-1)\) rectangles are pairwise non-overlapping, cover every non-diagonal cell, and leave exactly the \(n\) diagonal cells uncovered (one per row and one per column). Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

---

### Lower bound (showing \(\ge 2n-2\) tiles are necessary)

Consider any valid configuration of uncovered squares: in row \(r\) let the uncovered square be in column \(c_r\). Then \((c_1,c_2,\dots,c_n)\) is a permutation of \(\{1,2,\dots,n\}\).

For each \(r=1,2,\dots,n-1\), look at the \(2\times 2\) sub-board using rows \(r,r+1\) and columns \(c_r,c_{r+1}\). In this \(2\times 2\), the uncovered squares are \((r,c_r)\) and \((r+1,c_{r+1})\), so the other two squares
\[
(r,c_{r+1})\quad\text{and}\quad (r+1,c_r)
\]
must be covered.

No single rectangle tile can cover both \((r,c_{r+1})\) and \((r+1,c_r)\), because any axis-parallel rectangle containing those two opposite corners must contain the whole \(2\times 2\) block, hence would also contain the uncovered squares \((r,c_r)\) and \((r+1,c_{r+1})\), which is forbidden.

Thus, for each \(r=1,\dots,n-1\), we need at least **two distinct tiles** to cover these two forced covered squares. Moreover, these forced requirements occur for each of the \(n-1\) adjacent row pairs, yielding a total requirement of at least \(2(n-1)\) tiles overall:
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combining with the construction,
\[
\text{minimum} = 2n-2.
\]

---

For \(n=2116\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 2116-2=4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
72e3484d18f6191f61b414ae8d42bed1e0ff2058:
Color the \(n\times n\) board like a chessboard with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n\) is odd, black squares are \(\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\) and white squares are \(\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}\). Every domino covers one black and one white, so in any maximal configuration (covering \(n^{2}-1\) squares) the uncovered square must be **black**.
