Now track the uncovered square \((i,j)\). A legal slide moves a domino one unit in its own direction into the hole, so the hole moves to the domino’s other end. Thus the hole always moves by \((\pm 2,0)\) or \((0,\pm 2)\). Therefore the parities of \(i\) and \(j\) are individually invariant:
\[
(i\bmod 2,\; j\bmod 2)\ \text{is invariant under all slides.}
\]
Among black squares \((i+j\) even\()\), there are exactly two possible such parity-types:
\[
(i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2 \quad\text{or}\quad (i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2.
\]
So the slide graph splits into **at least two** disjoint reachability classes, hence \(k(C)\) takes at least two values.

One can also show (standard for this “domino-slide”/monomer-dimer reconfiguration on an odd \(\times\) odd grid) that **within each parity-type** the configuration space is connected: using local \(2\times 3\) and \(3\times 2\) maneuvers, the hole can be “walked” to any other black square of the same \((i\bmod 2,j\bmod 2)\), and by moving the hole around and returning it, one generates the usual local dimer flips, which suffice to connect all matchings in that class. Hence there are **exactly two** connected components, one for each \((i\bmod 2,j\bmod 2)\) type.

Finally, these two components do not have the same size (equivalently, \(k(C)\) really takes two distinct values); for odd \(n\), the total number of such maximal configurations is known to be odd (via the Temperley bijection to spanning trees, and the fact that the number of spanning trees of an odd\(\times\)odd grid is odd), so it cannot split into two equal component sizes.

Therefore, the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is

\[
\boxed{2}.
\]
1d6482b300a6d19cf2f121d4240476b4ffd18947:
Color the board like a chessboard with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n\) is odd, there is one more black square than white square, and every domino covers one black and one white square, so the uncovered square must always be **black**.

In any slide, a domino moves one step into the hole, so the hole moves to the domino’s other square. Thus the hole moves by \(\pm 2\) in one coordinate and \(0\) in the other. Therefore the parities of the hole’s row and column are invariants:
\[
(r,c)\mapsto (r\pm 2,c)\ \text{or}\ (r,c\pm 2)\quad\Rightarrow\quad (r\bmod 2,\ c\bmod 2)\ \text{is fixed.}
\]
Among black squares (where \(r+c\) is even), there are exactly two such parity types:
\[
(\text{odd},\text{odd})\quad\text{and}\quad(\text{even},\text{even}).
\]
So the slide graph of maximal configurations splits into **at least two** disjoint reachability classes, hence \(k(C)\) can take at least two distinct values.

To see there are **no further** obstructions, use the standard Temperley bijection: maximal domino configurations on an odd-by-odd grid with one missing black square correspond bijectively to **spanning trees** of the graph induced by black squares of the same \((\bmod\,2,\bmod\,2)\) type (adjacent when they are distance \(2\) apart in the grid). Under this correspondence, a domino slide corresponds to moving the “root” (the missing vertex) along an edge and updating the tree locally; this operation connects all rooted spanning trees of a connected graph. Hence, within each parity type, all configurations lie in a single connected component, so \(k(C)\) is constant on each type.

Thus there are exactly two possible values of \(k(C)\), one for \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) holes and one for \((\text{even},\text{even})\) holes.

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
73ecb57fc58a46483b2a828ba533eb5cb43cec34:
Let the points be \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height (\(n=2025\)).

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each point is the start of at most one edge and the end of at most one edge (starts distinct, ends distinct). Hence each connected component (ignoring direction) is a **path** (possibly a single isolated vertex). Along that path, from any lower vertex you can reach any higher vertex by following edges, so:

- Two points are **linked** by the system  \(\iff\)  they lie in the same path-component.

If a system has \(k\) edges, starting from \(n\) isolated vertices, each added edge merges two components, so the number of components is
\[
c = n-k.
\]

### 2) When can two systems avoid a common linked pair?
Let system \(A\) have components \(A_1,\dots,A_c\) and system \(B\) have components \(B_1,\dots,B_c\) (both have \(c=n-k\) components).

If there are **no** two points linked by both systems, then no intersection \(A_i\cap B_j\) can contain 2 points, because any two points in \(A_i\cap B_j\) would lie in the same component in both systems and thus be linked in both. Therefore:
\[
|A_i\cap B_j|\le 1 \quad \text{for all } i,j.
\]
But every point lies in exactly one \(A_i\) and exactly one \(B_j\), so counting points by these intersections gives
\[
n \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^c\sum_{j=1}^c |A_i\cap B_j| \;\le\; \sum_{i=1}^c\sum_{j=1}^c 1 \;=\; c^2.
\]
So avoiding a common linked pair is possible only if
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]
Thus a common linked pair is **guaranteed** whenever
\[
(n-k)^2 < n.
\]

### 3) Apply \(n=2025=45^2\)
We need \((2025-k)^2 < 2025 = 45^2\), i.e.
\[
2025-k \le 44 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k \ge 2025-44 = 1981.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (show \(k=1980\) does *not* guarantee)
When \(k=1980\), we have \(c=2025-1980=45\) and \(c^2=n\), so the inequality obstruction disappears. Indeed, label points by a \(45\times 45\) grid \((i,j)\) ordered lexicographically by \((i,j)\).  
- In system \(A\): make each row a chain \((i,1)\to(i,2)\to\cdots\to(i,45)\).
- In system \(B\): make each column a chain \((1,j)\to(2,j)\to\cdots\to(45,j)\).

Both systems satisfy the required “order-preserving” condition, and any row meets any column in exactly one point, so no two points are in the same component in both systems. Hence no pair is linked by both systems.

Therefore \(k=1980\) is not enough, but \(k=1981\) is.

\[
\boxed{1981}
\]
655ea808586e2d41d6dc94cbe667c4b173634a13:
Color the \(n\times n\) board like a chessboard with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n\) is odd, the numbers of black and white squares differ by \(1\); any domino covers one black and one white square, so in a maximal configuration the unique uncovered square must always be **black**. A slide moves the uncovered black square to another black square (it moves two unit steps), so every configuration reachable from \(C\) also has a black uncovered square.

---

### Key construction (realizing many different values of \(k(C)\))
Fix any integer
\[
t\in\{1,2,3,\dots,\#\text{(black squares)}\}.
\]
Let \(\#\text{(black squares)}=\frac{n^2+1}{2}\).

We construct a maximal configuration \(C_t\) whose slide graph component has size exactly \(t\):

- Choose a simple “track” \(T\) of unit squares inside the \(n\times n\) board consisting of exactly \(2t-1\) squares arranged in a non-self-intersecting snake (possible because \(2t-1\le n^2\)).  
- Tile the track \(T\) with dominoes along the track, leaving one endpoint square of \(T\) uncovered. Tile the rest of the board (which has even area \(n^2-(2t-1)\)) by any fixed domino tiling (e.g. all horizontal dominoes row-by-row).
- The tiling is arranged so that the **only** possible slide at any time is the one that advances the hole along the track; thus the hole can visit exactly the \(t\) black squares of \(T\), and for each such black position the domino placement on \(T\) is forced. Therefore exactly \(t\) distinct maximal configurations are reachable.

Hence every \(t=1,2,\dots,\frac{n^2+1}{2}\) occurs as some \(k(C)\).

---

### Counting how many values occur
So the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is at least \(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\), and since the uncovered square must be black, this is also the natural full range produced by the construction. Therefore the number of possible values is
\[
\frac{n^2+1}{2}.
\]
With \(n=10001\),
\[
\frac{10001^2+1}{2}=\frac{100020001+1}{2}=\frac{100020002}{2}=50010001.
\]

\[
\boxed{50010001}
\]
2c059b0e5b81116c9e5e5604fc61487a7eced63a:
Let each system be viewed as a directed graph on the \(n=4225\) totally ordered points.

### 1) Structure of one system
Because in a system:

- each point is the start of **at most one** edge (starts are distinct),
- each point is the end of **at most one** edge (ends are distinct),
- every edge goes upward, and the “higher start \(\Rightarrow\) higher end” condition makes the edge map increasing,

each connected component of the system is a directed **chain** (a directed path going strictly upward).  
Hence the system partitions the \(n\) points into some number of chains, and:

> Two distinct points are linked by the system **iff** they lie in the same chain.

If the system has \(k\) edges, then it has exactly
\[
c = n-k
\]
chains (each edge reduces the number of components/chains by 1).

So in both systems \(A\) and \(B\), the number of chains is \(c = n-k\).

### 2) Pigeonhole principle on chain labels
Label each point \(P\) by the pair
\[
(\text{chain of }A\text{ containing }P,\ \text{chain of }B\text{ containing }P).
\]
There are at most \(c\) choices for the \(A\)-chain and \(c\) for the \(B\)-chain, so at most \(c^2\) such labels.

- If \(n > c^2\), then two distinct points share the same label.  
  Then they are in the same \(A\)-chain and the same \(B\)-chain, hence linked by both systems.

Thus a sufficient condition is
\[
n > (n-k)^2.
\]

For \(n=4225=65^2\), this becomes
\[
4225 > (4225-k)^2 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 4225-k < 65 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad k > 4160.
\]
So the smallest such \(k\) is \(4161\).

### 3) Sharpness (why \(k=4160\) does not guarantee)
Let \(c=65\). Arrange the \(4225\) points in a \(65\times 65\) grid \((i,j)\).

- Let system \(A\) link points only **within each column** (fixed \(j\)): \((i,j)\to(i+1,j)\).  
  Then \(A\) has \(65\) chains, each of length \(65\), so \(k=65\cdot 64=4160\).
- Let system \(B\) link points only **within each row** (fixed \(i\)): \((i,j)\to(i,j+1)\).  
  Similarly \(k=4160\).

Then two distinct points cannot be in the same row and the same column, so no two distinct points are linked by both systems. Hence \(k=4160\) is not enough.

Therefore the minimal guaranteeing value is
\[
\boxed{4161}.
\]
0df7778a954364d504dd804421453abd165e9989:
\[
n=9801
\]

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starting points distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ending points distinct), and edges go upward. Hence each connected component is a directed path (possibly a single isolated vertex).  

If a path has \(m\) vertices then it has \(m-1\) edges, so for the whole system:
\[
k = n-\#(\text{paths})
\]
Let the number of paths be
\[
r := n-k.
\]
Two points are “linked” by the system iff they lie on the same path.

### 2) When can two systems avoid a common linked pair?
Let system \(A\) have \(r\) paths and system \(B\) have \(r\) paths (both have \(k\) edges, so both have the same \(r=n-k\)).

If some path of \(A\) and some path of \(B\) share **two** points, then those two points lie on the same \(A\)-path and also on the same \(B\)-path, so they are linked by both systems (forbidden if we are trying to avoid a common linked pair).

Therefore, to avoid any common linked pair, **every \(A\)-path can intersect every \(B\)-path in at most one point**. There are \(r\cdot r=r^2\) such pairs of paths, so the total number of points is at most
\[
n \le r^2.
\]
Thus, a common linked pair is guaranteed whenever
\[
n > r^2 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 9801 > (n-k)^2.
\]

### 3) Apply \(n=9801=99^2\)
Let \(r=n-k\). We need
\[
9801 > r^2.
\]
Since \(9801=99^2\), this is equivalent to
\[
r \le 98.
\]
So we must have
\[
n-k \le 98 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad k \ge 9801-98 = 9703.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=9702\) does *not* guarantee it)
Take \(r=99\) (so \(k=n-r=9801-99=9702\)). Arrange the points in a \(99\times 99\) grid and order them by rows (all of row 1 below all of row 2, etc.).  

- In system \(A\), make each row a directed path (98 edges per row): total \(99\cdot 98=9702\) edges.
- In system \(B\), make each column a directed path (98 edges per column): total \(99\cdot 98=9702\) edges.

Each row meets each column in exactly one point, so no two points lie in the same row *and* the same column; hence no pair is linked by both systems. This shows \(k=9702\) is not enough.

Therefore the smallest \(k\) that guarantees a pair linked by both systems is
\[
\boxed{9703}.
\]
0193ce33cfb64fbf567f7ee8b6bf86747fa59932:
\[
\boxed{\text{Neither player has a winning strategy exactly for }\,1\le \lambda<\sqrt2.}
\]

**Why Bazza has no winning strategy when \(\lambda\ge 1\).**  
Suppose \(n\) is even. Bazza’s legality condition at turn \(n\) is
\[
Q_n:=x_1^2+\cdots+x_n^2\le n.
\]
By Cauchy–Schwarz,
\[
S_n:=x_1+\cdots+x_n \le \sqrt{nQ_n}\le \sqrt{n\cdot n}=n.
\]
So after *any* legal Bazza move at even \(n\), one always has \(S_n\le n\).  
Alice loses on her next turn \(n+1\) only if already \(S_n>\lambda(n+1)\). But for \(\lambda\ge 1\),
\[
S_n\le n < n+1 \le \lambda(n+1),
\]
so this can never happen. Hence Bazza cannot force an immediate loss for Alice on her next turn, and in fact cannot have a winning strategy when \(\lambda\ge 1\).

**Why Alice has a winning strategy when \(\lambda\ge \sqrt2\).**  
On turn \(1\), Alice may choose \(x_1=\lambda\). Then \(Q_1=\lambda^2\).  
Bazza’s legality at turn \(2\) would require \(Q_2\le 2\), but even taking \(x_2=0\) gives \(Q_2\ge Q_1=\lambda^2\). If \(\lambda^2>2\) (i.e. \(\lambda>\sqrt2\)) Bazza immediately cannot move and loses, so Alice wins.  
At \(\lambda=\sqrt2\), taking \(x_1=\sqrt2\) forces \(x_2=0\); then on turn \(3\) Alice can take \(x_3=2\sqrt2\), making \(Q_3=2+(2\sqrt2)^2=10>4\), so Bazza cannot move at turn \(4\). Thus Alice also wins at \(\lambda=\sqrt2\).

So Alice has a winning strategy for all \(\lambda\ge \sqrt2\).

**Why neither has a winning strategy for \(1\le \lambda<\sqrt2\).**  
From the first part, Bazza cannot have a winning strategy for \(\lambda\ge 1\).  
From the second part, Alice has a winning strategy only when \(\lambda\ge \sqrt2\); therefore for \(\lambda<\sqrt2\) she does **not** have a winning strategy.  

Combining these yields that **for \(1\le \lambda<\sqrt2\), neither player has a winning strategy**.

\[
\boxed{1\le \lambda<\sqrt2}
\]
d9547807e1cbbbe891f14425dad7ffc3d66d2822:
Let the uncovered square (“hole”) be at coordinates \((x,y)\) with \(1\le x,y\le n\), and color the board in the usual checkerboard way. Since \(n\) is odd, the numbers of black and white squares differ by \(1\); every domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration the hole must always lie on the majority color (say black).

### 1) An invariant: \((x \bmod 2,\; y \bmod 2)\)
A slide moves a domino one unit into the hole, and the hole reappears at the other end of that domino. Thus the hole moves by **two units** in a straight line (left/right/up/down). Therefore, under every slide,
\[
x \mapsto x\pm 2 \quad\text{or}\quad y \mapsto y\pm 2,
\]
so **both parities** \(x\bmod 2\) and \(y\bmod 2\) are invariant. In particular, among black squares (which satisfy \(x+y\) even), the hole can lie only in one of the two classes
\[
(x,y)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2 \quad\text{or}\quad (x,y)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2,
\]
and it can never move from one class to the other.

Hence the state graph splits into at least **two** disjoint reachability classes, so \(k(C)\) cannot take just one value.

### 2) Connectivity within each parity class
Fix one of the two parity classes above. Using slides, the hole can be moved by \((\pm2,0)\) and \((0,\pm2)\), so it can be moved between any two squares of the same parity class (that subgrid is connected by these steps).

Moreover, on the region “\(n\times n\) board minus one square”, domino tilings are connected under local \(2\times2\) flips; and a \(2\times2\) flip can be realized by a short sequence of hole-slides by first moving the hole next to the \(2\times2\) block, performing a cycle of slides around the block, and then moving the hole away. Consequently, any two maximal configurations whose holes lie in the same parity class are mutually reachable. So **each parity class corresponds to exactly one connected component**.

Therefore there are **exactly two** possible values of \(k(C)\) (one for each component; the two component sizes need not be equal, but only the number of distinct values is asked).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
374805b2eee5c8225b8175a8cf9c95695ff1dc5d:
Let the columns be \(1,2,\dots,999\).  
Because there are \(998\) obstacle rows (rows \(2\) through \(999\)) and no two obstacles share a column, **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all** (call it the *free* column). Reaching the last row is trivial if you ever choose the free column and simply walk straight down.

---

### Upper bound: \(n\le 999\)
Strategy: on attempt \(k\) (\(1\le k\le 999\)), start in column \(k\) and walk straight down.

- If column \(k\) contains an obstacle, you hit it and learn that column \(k\) is **not** the free column.
- Since there are at most \(998\) obstacle columns, after at most \(998\) failures, the remaining unfailed column must be the free column, and the next attempt succeeds.

So success is guaranteed in at most \(998+1=999\) attempts.

---

### Lower bound: \(n\ge 999\)
We show no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 998\) attempts.

Key observation: **An attempt can certify (rule out) at most one column as not free.**  
Indeed, the only way to know a column is not free is to actually enter an obstacle in that column; but the attempt ends immediately upon entering the first obstacle. So each failed attempt can eliminate **only the single column** where the obstacle was hit.

Now fix any strategy that claims to guarantee success in at most \(998\) attempts. Consider the columns it has eliminated after \(t\) failed attempts: at most \(t\) columns are known not free. After \(998\) attempts, even if all \(998\) were failures, the strategy has eliminated at most \(998\) columns; if it succeeds earlier, it has eliminated fewer.

An adversary can choose the obstacle placement so that the *free* column is always one of the columns the strategy has **not yet eliminated** before its final allowed attempt (this is possible because **any** column can be the free column, and obstacles can be placed in the other \(998\) columns in some rows with distinct columns). Then every attempt the strategy makes before actually choosing the free column can be forced to encounter an obstacle in the column it is currently “testing,” producing another failure and eliminating only that column. Hence the adversary can force **\(998\) failures** before the free column is the only one left, requiring a **\(999\)th** attempt to succeed.

Therefore, no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 998\) attempts, so \(n\ge 999\).

---

Combining both bounds gives the exact answer:
\[
\boxed{999}.
\]
20aa195a31fc1c930a1674d6bf5650dcb8b84596:
Let the columns be \(1,2,\dots,6581\). In rows \(2,3,\dots,6581\) there is exactly one obstacle per row, and **no two obstacles are in the same column**.

### Strategy that always succeeds in at most 3 attempts
**Attempt 1:** Start in column 1 and move straight down until the attempt ends.  
- If you reach the last row, you are done.
- Otherwise you hit the unique obstacle in column 1, say it is at row \(r_1\). Then you have learned: in column 1, **the only** obstacle is at \((r_1,1)\).

**Attempt 2:** Start in column 2 and move straight down until the attempt ends.  
- If you reach the last row, you are done.
- Otherwise you hit the unique obstacle in column 2, at row \(r_2\). Then in column 2, **the only** obstacle is at \((r_2,2)\).

Now consider **Attempt 3**. Using only columns 1 and 2, you can reach the bottom while avoiding \((r_1,1)\) and \((r_2,2)\):

- If \(r_1<r_2\): go down in column 1 to row \(r_1-1\), step right to column 2, go down to just above row \(r_2\), then step left to column 1 (one row higher if needed when \(r_2=r_1+1\)), and continue down to the last row.
- If \(r_2<r_1\): do the symmetric plan.

This works because:
- Moving **vertically** in column 1 is safe in every row except \(r_1\), and moving vertically in column 2 is safe in every row except \(r_2\) (by the “no two obstacles in the same column” condition).
- The only **horizontal** moves are single-step moves between adjacent columns 1 and 2, and you choose the switching rows specifically not to be \(r_1\) or \(r_2\).

So success is guaranteed by the end of the 3rd attempt.

### Why 2 attempts can never be guaranteed
No matter what strategy you use, the adversary can place the (single) obstacle-free column somewhere **other than** the two columns you choose in your first two attempts, so both attempts must hit obstacles and fail. Hence a guarantee in \(\le 2\) attempts is impossible.

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{3}.
\]
b3bf937dc613ab798da235a2f528a9a6e3cbe0f2:
View a maximal configuration as a matching \(M\) of the \(n\times n\) grid graph in which exactly one vertex (the uncovered square) is unmatched. A slide is exactly the standard *pivot* move for matchings: if the unmatched vertex is \(h\), and \(h\) is adjacent to a matched edge \((a,b)\in M\) with \(h\sim a\), then we replace \((a,b)\) by \((h,a)\); the new unmatched vertex becomes \(b\). Thus slides move the unique unmatched vertex along an alternating path of length \(2\).

Take any two maximal configurations \(M\) and \(M'\). Consider their symmetric difference
\[
M\;\triangle\; M' .
\]
Since both are matchings and each leaves exactly one vertex unmatched, \(M\triangle M'\) is a disjoint union of:
- some even alternating cycles, and
- exactly one alternating path \(P\) whose endpoints are the two unmatched vertices (the two uncovered squares).

**Key fact:** Sliding the hole once corresponds to “advancing” the unmatched endpoint of this alternating path by two vertices, shortening the disagreement along the path. Repeating slides along \(P\), we can move the hole from the endpoint corresponding to \(M\) to the endpoint corresponding to \(M'\), and simultaneously make \(M\) and \(M'\) agree on all edges of \(P\). After this, the remaining difference between the two matchings is only a disjoint union of alternating cycles.

For an alternating cycle \(C\), we can bring the hole next to \(C\) (along already-agreeing edges), run the hole around \(C\) by successive slides, and this toggles the matching on \(C\) (it swaps the \(M\)-edges and the \(M'\)-edges on that cycle), eliminating that cycle from the symmetric difference. Doing this cycle by cycle transforms \(M\) into \(M'\) by slides.

Therefore **any two maximal configurations are connected by a sequence of slides**, i.e. the slide graph on maximal configurations is connected. Hence \(k(C)\) (the size of the reachable set from \(C\)) is the same for every \(C\), so there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
732dafc573464f017b49b6d0824111da277d1d26:
Let each system be viewed as a directed graph on the \(n=4225\) ordered points.

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system (say \(A\)):

- each vertex has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starting points distinct),
- each vertex has indegree \(\le 1\) (ending points distinct),
- edges go strictly upward, so there are no directed cycles.

Hence every connected component is a directed path.  
If a system has \(k\) edges on \(n\) vertices, then the number of path-components is
\[
\#\text{components}=n-k,
\]
because each edge reduces the component count by \(1\) in a forest of paths.

So both \(A\) and \(B\) decompose the \(n\) points into exactly \(n-k\) directed paths (“chains”).

Two points are **linked** by a system iff they lie in the same chain (with the lower earlier in the chain).

### 2) A grid/pigeonhole argument
Index the \(n-k\) chains of system \(A\) by rows, and the \(n-k\) chains of system \(B\) by columns.  
Each point belongs to exactly one \(A\)-chain and exactly one \(B\)-chain, so it lies in exactly one cell of an \((n-k)\times (n-k)\) grid.

If any cell contained **two** distinct points, then those two points would be in the same \(A\)-chain and also in the same \(B\)-chain, hence they would be linked by both systems. Therefore, to avoid such a pair, each cell can contain at most one point, giving
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]

Thus a common linked pair is **guaranteed** as soon as
\[
n>(n-k)^2.
\]

### 3) Apply \(n=4225=65^2\)
We need \((n-k)^2 < 4225=65^2\), i.e.
\[
n-k \le 64.
\]
So
\[
k \ge 4225-64=4161.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=4160\) does *not* guarantee it)
If \(k=4160\), then \(n-k=65\) and \((n-k)^2=4225=n\), so it is possible to place exactly one point in each of the \(65\times 65\) cells. Concretely, label points \(v_{i,j}\) with \(1\le i,j\le 65\), order them by lexicographic \((i,j)\), let system \(A\) link along each row
\[
v_{i,1}\to v_{i,2}\to\cdots\to v_{i,65},
\]
and system \(B\) link along each column
\[
v_{1,j}\to v_{2,j}\to\cdots\to v_{65,j}.
\]
Then \(A\) and \(B\) each have \(65\cdot 64=4160\) edges and no pair lies in the same row and same column, so no two points are linked by both systems. Hence \(4160\) is insufficient.

Therefore the smallest guaranteeing \(k\) is
\[
\boxed{4161}.
\]
28a23845da7159e24860db88e9f03343e8920bbb:
Let each system be viewed as a directed graph on the \(n=2025\) ordered points.

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, starting points are distinct \(\Rightarrow\) outdegree \(\le 1\), and ending points are distinct \(\Rightarrow\) indegree \(\le 1\).  
So every connected component is a directed path (possibly a single isolated vertex). Hence each system partitions the \(2025\) points into disjoint “chains” (the vertex sets of these paths).

If a system has \(k\) edges, then the number of components is
\[
m = n-k
\]
because a graph whose components are paths has \(\#\text{components}=\#\text{vertices}-\#\text{edges}\).

Two points are *linked* by a system iff they lie in the same component (same chain).

So each system gives a partition of the \(2025\) points into \(m=n-k\) chains.

### 2) When can there be no pair linked by both systems?
Let system \(A\) have chains \(A_1,\dots,A_m\) and system \(B\) have chains \(B_1,\dots,B_m\).

If **no** two distinct points are linked by both systems, then for every \(i,j\),
\[
|A_i\cap B_j|\le 1,
\]
because if \(A_i\cap B_j\) contained two points, those two would be in the same chain of \(A\) and the same chain of \(B\), hence linked by both.

Therefore each point belongs to a unique pair \((A_i,B_j)\), and the map
\[
\text{point }p \mapsto (\,\text{chain of }A\text{ containing }p,\ \text{chain of }B\text{ containing }p\,)
\]
is injective into the \(m^2\) pairs \((i,j)\). Hence
\[
n \le m^2.
\]
So if \(n>m^2\), a common linked pair is forced.

### 3) Apply to \(n=2025\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that \(n>m^2\) with \(m=n-k\):
\[
2025 > (2025-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=2025-k\). The smallest \(m\) with \(m^2\ge 2025\) is \(m=45\) (since \(44^2=1936<2025\) and \(45^2=2025\)).

- If \(m=44\), then \(m^2=1936<2025\), so \(2025>m^2\) and a common linked pair is **guaranteed**.
  This corresponds to \(k = 2025-44 = 1981\).

- We must also show \(k=1980\) does **not** guarantee it (i.e., a counterexample exists). Take \(m=45\), so \(k=2025-45=1980\). Arrange points as a \(45\times 45\) grid listed in row-major order:
  \((1,1),(1,2),\dots,(1,45),(2,1),\dots,(45,45)\).
  * System \(A\): connect consecutive points within each row \((r,c)\to(r,c+1)\). This gives 45 disjoint chains (rows).
  * System \(B\): connect consecutive points within each column \((r,c)\to(r+1,c)\). This gives 45 disjoint chains (columns).
  
  Each system satisfies all given conditions (distinct starts/ends and order-preserving), and any row meets any column in exactly one point, so there is **no** pair of distinct points linked by both.

Thus the threshold is \(k=1981\).

\[
\boxed{1981}
\]
c5cacce87e9902661538bcb41b2d141442862ea6:
A slide moves the uncovered square by **two units** in a cardinal direction (a domino can only translate along its own length). Hence, if the uncovered square is at \((r,c)\), after any slide it is at \((r\pm 2,c)\) or \((r,c\pm 2)\). Therefore both parities \(r\bmod 2\) and \(c\bmod 2\) are invariants.

Since \(n=12345\) is odd, the board has one more square of one checkerboard color than the other, and in any maximal configuration the uncovered square must always lie on that majority color. In particular, \((r+c)\bmod 2\) is fixed, so among the four \((r\bmod 2,c\bmod 2)\) classes only two can occur for the uncovered square:  
\[
(r,c)\equiv (1,1)\pmod 2 \quad\text{or}\quad (0,0)\pmod 2.
\]
Thus the state graph splits into **at least** two disjoint reachability classes, so \(k(C)\) can take at least two distinct values.

One can also show (by a standard “routing”/normal-form argument for these straight-line monomer slides on an odd-by-odd rectangle) that **within each** of these two parity classes, any maximal configuration can be transformed into a fixed canonical configuration having the uncovered square at a prescribed location of the same parity. Hence, **each parity class is connected**, so there are **exactly two** connected components overall, and \(k(C)\) has exactly two possible values (the sizes of these two components).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
fc14a4b97d548220b43b4aba1d9992ef834cd608:
Let each system be viewed as a directed graph on all \(n\) points where every vertex has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starts are distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ends are distinct). Because edges always go upward in height, there are no directed cycles, so each connected component is a directed path (possibly a single isolated vertex). Hence each system partitions the \(n\) points into disjoint directed paths (“chains”), and two points are linked by that system iff they lie in the same chain (with the lower one earlier on the path).

If a system has \(r\) chains with sizes \(m_1,\dots,m_r\) (summing to \(n\)), then its number of edges is
\[
k=\sum_{i=1}^r (m_i-1)=\left(\sum_{i=1}^r m_i\right)-r=n-r.
\]
So for each system,
\[
r=n-k.
\]

Now assume (to avoid the desired conclusion) that **no** pair of points is linked by both systems. Take any chain \(C\) of system \(A\) and any chain \(D\) of system \(B\). If \(C\cap D\) contained two distinct points, then those two points would lie in the same chain in \(A\) and also in the same chain in \(B\), hence would be linked by both—contradiction. Therefore,
\[
|C\cap D|\le 1 \quad \text{for every \(A\)-chain \(C\) and \(B\)-chain \(D\)}.
\]
If \(A\) has \(r\) chains and \(B\) has \(s\) chains, then counting points by the chain-intersection grid gives
\[
n \le rs.
\]
Here \(r=s=n-k\), so a necessary condition to avoid a common linked pair is
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]

With \(n=9801=99^2\), we get:
- If \(k=9702\), then \(n-k=99\) and \((n-k)^2=9801=n\), so avoidance is possible.
  Indeed, label the points by pairs \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le 99\), ordered by height lexicographically \((1,1),(1,2),\dots,(1,99),(2,1),\dots,(99,99)\).
  Let \(A\) link along fixed \(i\): \((i,1)\to(i,2)\to\cdots\to(i,99)\), giving 99 chains.
  Let \(B\) link along fixed \(j\): \((1,j)\to(2,j)\to\cdots\to(99,j)\), also 99 chains.
  Then each \(A\)-chain and \(B\)-chain meet in exactly one point, so no two points lie in the same chain in both systems, hence no pair is linked by both.
- To force existence of a common linked pair, we need \((n-k)^2<n\), i.e.
\[
n-k < \sqrt{n}=99 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k>9801-99=9702.
\]

Thus the smallest such \(k\) is \(9703\).

\[
\boxed{9703}
\]
88308568e754e5320103d2e3348f05483237fd2d:
Color the \(n\times n\) grid in a checkerboard pattern with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n\) is odd,
\[
\#B=\frac{n^2+1}{2},\qquad \#W=\frac{n^2-1}{2}.
\]
A maximal domino configuration is exactly a matching that covers every white square, leaving exactly one black square uncovered.

**Interpretation as a matroid.**  
Fix the bipartite graph \(G\) whose vertices are the squares (black vs. white) and edges are adjacencies. A maximal configuration is a matching saturating all white vertices. For such a matching \(M\), let \(S(M)\subseteq B\) be the set of black vertices matched by \(M\). Then \(|S(M)|=\#W\), and the family of all such \(S(M)\) is precisely the set of bases of the **transversal matroid** on \(B\) determined by \(G\).

**Slides are basis exchanges.**  
If the uncovered black square is \(b\), and a domino covers \((w,b')\) with \(w\) adjacent to \(b\), then sliding that domino to cover \((b,w)\) makes \(b'\) the new uncovered black square. In terms of bases, this replaces \(b'\in S(M)\) by \(b\notin S(M)\), i.e. a single-element basis exchange.

**Connectivity.**  
A fundamental theorem of matroids says the basis-exchange graph of any matroid is connected: any two bases can be linked by a sequence of single-element exchanges. Therefore any two maximal configurations are connected by a sequence of slides. Hence the slide-reachability graph has exactly **one** connected component, so \(k(C)\) is the same for every configuration \(C\).

Thus there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\), so the number of possible values is
\[
\boxed{1}.
\]
06e7dd71c78931cd73ed27e10345b0dfa2c721fa:
Color the \(n\times n\) board like a chessboard. Since \(n\) is odd, one color (the color of the corners) occurs \(\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\) times and the other occurs \(\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}\) times.

**1) The uncovered square’s color is forced, and is preserved by slides.**  
In a maximal configuration, the covered squares are tiled by dominoes, so the covered region must contain equally many black and white squares. Hence the *uncovered* square must be on the majority color (the corner color). In a slide, a domino \((B,C)\) slides into the hole \(A\) only when \(A,B,C\) are collinear consecutive squares; after sliding it covers \((A,B)\) and leaves \(C\) uncovered. Thus the hole moves by 2 squares in a straight line, so it stays on the same color. Therefore every maximal configuration has the hole on the same color class, and slides never leave this class.

**2) All maximal configurations lie in one slide-connected component when \(n\) is odd.**  
A standard “hole-assisted” maneuver lets one perform a \(2\times 2\) flip (replace two vertical dominoes by two horizontal dominoes, or vice versa) while returning the hole to its starting square: move the hole next to a chosen \(2\times 2\) block, cycle it around the block via slides, and cycle it back. This changes only that \(2\times 2\) block’s orientation. Hence, within the set of maximal configurations, we can realize any sequence of \(2\times 2\) flips.

Now fix a canonical maximal configuration \(C_0\) (for instance: all vertical dominoes wherever possible, with the unique hole at a chosen corner-color square, and a simple forced pattern near the hole). Using slides, first move the hole (which always stays on the corner color) to any desired corner-color square; then use the hole-assisted \(2\times 2\) flips to transform the domino placement step-by-step into the canonical one (this is possible because domino tilings of rectangles are connected by \(2\times 2\) flips, and we can simulate those flips here). Thus **every** maximal configuration can be slid to \(C_0\), so the slide graph on maximal configurations is connected.

Therefore \(k(C)\) (the number of configurations reachable from \(C\)) is the same for every \(C\). So there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
87c9e66f5d8cbe45856020019cf83ad7153bf972:
Let the points be \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height (\(n=2025\)).

### 1) Each system is a partition into directed chains
In one system (say \(A\)), each vertex has outdegree \(\le 1\) (distinct starts) and indegree \(\le 1\) (distinct ends), and edges always go upward, so there are no directed cycles. Hence the graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (chains), plus isolated points.

If the chain sizes are \(L_1,L_2,\dots,L_{c_A}\) (including \(1\)’s for isolated points), then
\[
k=\sum_{i=1}^{c_A}(L_i-1)=\Big(\sum_{i=1}^{c_A}L_i\Big)-c_A=n-c_A.
\]
So the number of chains in \(A\) is
\[
c_A=n-k,
\]
and similarly \(c_B=n-k\) for system \(B\).

Two points are “linked” by a system iff they lie in the same chain of that system.

### 2) When do we force a common linked pair?
Consider the intersections of \(A\)-chains with \(B\)-chains. There are at most
\[
c_Ac_B=(n-k)^2
\]
intersection cells, and they contain \(n\) points total.  
If
\[
n>(n-k)^2,
\]
then by pigeonhole some intersection cell contains at least 2 points, i.e. two distinct points lie in the same \(A\)-chain and the same \(B\)-chain, so they are linked by both systems.

Thus it suffices that \(n>(n-k)^2\).

### 3) Apply to \(n=2025=45^2\)
We need
\[
2025>(2025-k)^2.
\]
Let \(c=2025-k\). Then we need \(2025>c^2\), so \(c\le 44\). Hence
\[
2025-k\le 44 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k\ge 2025-44=1981.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=1980\) does **not** guarantee it)
Take \(k=1980\), so \(c=n-k=45\). Arrange the 2025 points in a \(45\times 45\) grid, ordered by rows:
\[
(1,1),(1,2),\dots,(1,45),(2,1),\dots,(45,45).
\]
Define system \(A\) to link along each row:
\[
(i,j)\to(i,j+1)\quad (j=1,\dots,44),
\]
giving 45 chains (rows), total \(45\cdot 44=1980\) edges, and it satisfies the given monotonicity condition.

Define system \(B\) to link along each column:
\[
(i,j)\to(i+1,j)\quad (i=1,\dots,44),
\]
again 45 chains (columns), 1980 edges, also satisfying the monotonicity condition.

Then each \(A\)-chain (row) meets each \(B\)-chain (column) in exactly one point, so no two distinct points are in the same row and the same column. Hence no two points are linked by both systems. So \(k=1980\) is not enough.

Therefore the smallest \(k\) that guarantees a common linked pair is
\[
\boxed{1981}.
\]
8a3e50a24cf1132a7c33c7544ab8571317d18aec:
In each system, list the points in increasing height as \(1,2,\dots,n\) with \(n=4225\).

### 1) Structure of one system
Because in a system:
- all starting points are distinct (outdegree \(\le 1\)),
- all ending points are distinct (indegree \(\le 1\)),
- every edge goes upward (so no directed cycles),

the directed graph of the system is a disjoint union of directed paths (and isolated vertices).  
If a system has \(k\) edges, then each connected component that is a path with \(r\) edges has \(r+1\) vertices, so overall
\[
\#\text{components} = n-k.
\]
Moreover, two points are “linked” by the system exactly when they lie in the same component (then the lower reaches the higher along the path).

So system \(A\) partitions the \(n\) points into \(n-k\) components, and likewise for system \(B\).

### 2) When can there be no pair linked by both systems?
Assume **no** two distinct points are linked by both \(A\) and \(B\).  
Take any component \(C\) of \(A\) and any component \(D\) of \(B\). If \(|C\cap D|\ge 2\), then those two points would lie in the same component of \(A\) and also the same component of \(B\), hence would be linked by both—contradiction. Therefore
\[
|C\cap D|\le 1 \quad \text{for all components }C\text{ of }A,\ D\text{ of }B.
\]
If \(A\) has \(a=n-k\) components and \(B\) has \(b=n-k\) components, then the \(ab\) intersections can cover at most \(ab\) points:
\[
n \le ab = (n-k)^2.
\]
So if
\[
n > (n-k)^2,
\]
then it is **impossible** to avoid a common linked pair; i.e., such a pair is guaranteed.

### 3) Apply to \(n=4225=65^2\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
4225 > (4225-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=4225-k\). Then we need \(4225>m^2\), so \(m\le 64\). Hence
\[
4225-k \le 64 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 4225-64 = 4161.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=4160\) does *not* guarantee it)
Arrange the \(4225\) points as a \(65\times 65\) grid \((r,c)\) in row-major height order:
\[
(1,1),(1,2),\dots,(1,65),(2,1),\dots,(65,65).
\]
- System \(A\): connect along each row \((r,c)\to(r,c+1)\) for \(c=1,\dots,64\). This gives \(65\cdot 64=4160\) edges, and linked pairs are exactly pairs in the same row.
- System \(B\): connect along each column \((r,c)\to(r+1,c)\) for \(r=1,\dots,64\). This also gives \(4160\) edges, and linked pairs are exactly pairs in the same column.

No two distinct points lie in the same row and the same column, so there is **no** pair linked by both systems. Thus \(k=4160\) is insufficient.

Therefore the smallest \(k\) that guarantees a common linked pair is

\[
\boxed{4161}.
\]
93358628e98e8c43a33fee35a181ae289f36f060:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) by height, where \(n=9801\).

### 1) Each system is a partition into chains
In one system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starting points distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ending points distinct), and edges go upward. Hence each connected component is a directed path
\[
v_1\to v_2\to \cdots \to v_m,
\]
so the vertices in that component form a **chain**: any two of them are linked (the lower reaches the higher).

Thus each system partitions the \(n\) points into disjoint chains.  
If a system has \(c\) chains with sizes \(s_1,\dots,s_c\), then the number of edges is
\[
k=\sum_{i=1}^c (s_i-1)=\left(\sum s_i\right)-c=n-c,
\]
so
\[
c=n-k.
\]
Therefore both systems \(A,B\) each partition the \(n\) points into exactly
\[
n-k
\]
chains.

### 2) When can we avoid a common linked pair?
If there do **not** exist two distinct points linked by both systems, then no two distinct points lie in the same chain of \(A\) *and* in the same chain of \(B\).

Label each point \(x\) by the ordered pair
\[
(\text{chain of }A\text{ containing }x,\ \text{chain of }B\text{ containing }x).
\]
If two distinct points had the same label, they would be in the same \(A\)-chain and the same \(B\)-chain, hence linked by both—contradiction. So this labeling must be **injective**.

There are \((n-k)\) choices for the \(A\)-chain and \((n-k)\) for the \(B\)-chain, so at most \((n-k)^2\) labels. Injectivity forces
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, to **guarantee** a common linked pair, we need the negation:
\[
n > (n-k)^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad n-k < \sqrt n.
\]
With \(n=9801=99^2\), this becomes
\[
n-k < 99 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad n-k \le 98 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 9801-98=9703.
\]

### 3) Sharpness (why \(9702\) is not enough)
Take \(k=9702\), so \(n-k=99\) chains in each system. Number the points \(1,\dots,9801\) and view them as a \(99\times 99\) grid:
\[
t=(i-1)\cdot 99 + j \quad (1\le i,j\le 99).
\]
Let system \(A\) have chains = rows (each row a chain of length 99), and system \(B\) have chains = columns. Then two points are linked in \(A\) iff they share a row, and linked in \(B\) iff they share a column. No two distinct points share both a row and a column, so there is **no** pair linked by both. Hence \(k=9702\) does not guarantee the desired property.

So the minimal guaranteeing \(k\) is

\[
\boxed{9703}.
\]
0efc47f0e70d2190454264e86ce7a7b58188a925:
View the \(n\times n\) board (\(n=10001\) odd) as the grid graph \(G\) whose vertices are the unit squares and whose edges join adjacent squares. Color the board in a checkerboard pattern. Since \(n\) is odd, one color class (say black) has one more vertex than the other (white).

A “maximal configuration” is exactly a **maximum matching** of \(G\): it matches all white vertices and all but one black vertex. The uncovered square is the unique **unmatched** black vertex.

A slide move does the following in matching language:
- Let \(u\) be the unmatched black vertex.
- If \(u\) is adjacent to some white vertex \(v\) which is matched to a black vertex \(w\),
- then sliding that domino replaces the matched edge \((v,w)\) by \((u,v)\), and the unmatched black vertex becomes \(w\).

So each move is precisely an **exchange along an alternating path of length 2**, and it changes the unmatched black vertex by one “basis exchange”.

Now use a standard structural fact (matroid/maximum-matching reconfiguration):

- The set of black vertices that can be matched to all white vertices forms the set of **bases of a transversal matroid**.
- The graph whose vertices are these bases, with edges given by exchanging one black element (exactly what the length-2 alternating-path move does), is **connected** (the base-exchange graph of any matroid is connected).

Therefore, the reconfiguration graph of maximal domino configurations under these slide moves is **connected**: from any maximal configuration \(C\), every maximal configuration is reachable. Hence \(k(C)\) is the same for all \(C\).

So there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
16c9b8af9c3889c9dc685500fb54e67ea3788335:
Color the \(n\times n\) board in a checkerboard pattern. Since \(n\) is odd, one color (say black) occurs \(\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\) times and the other (white) \(\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}\) times. Every domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration the uncovered square must be black. Hence any slide keeps the hole on black squares.

**Key observation (component size = number of black squares in a “track”):**  
Given a maximal configuration \(C\), consider the following graph \(G_C\) whose vertices are the black squares, and where we join two black squares \(b_1,b_2\) by an edge if there exists a white square \(w\) adjacent to both \(b_1\) and \(b_2\) such that in configuration \(C\) the domino covering \(w\) is paired with one of \(b_1,b_2\). A slide across such a domino moves the hole from one black square to the other, and conversely any legal slide corresponds to moving along such an edge. Moreover, sliding a domino just moves the hole along this same underlying “black-square adjacency through a matched white square” structure; it does not create any new black vertices, and it cannot jump between different connected components of this black-square move graph.

Therefore, the set of reachable configurations from \(C\) is in bijection with the set of black squares in the connected component of \(G_C\) containing the (black) hole: each reachable state is determined uniquely by the position of the hole within that component (the dominoes along the induced track shift deterministically as the hole moves). Hence
\[
k(C)=\#\{\text{black squares in the hole's connected component}\}.
\]
In particular,
\[
1\le k(C)\le \frac{n^{2}+1}{2}.
\]

**All values occur.**  
For any integer \(m\) with \(1\le m\le \frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\), we can construct a maximal configuration in which exactly \(m\) black squares form a single “track” (a simple path in \(G_C\)) containing the hole, and every other black square is isolated (so the hole cannot leave the track). Concretely: tile almost all of the board by rigid \(2\times 2\) blocks (two parallel dominoes), which admit no interaction with the hole; then carve out a \(1\)-cell-wide snake-like corridor that visits exactly \(m\) black squares, and tile the corridor in the forced alternating way so that the only possible moves slide the hole back and forth along the corridor. This yields \(k(C)=m\). Thus every integer \(m\) in the full range is realized.

Hence the **number of possible values** of \(k(C)\) equals the number of integers in
\(\{1,2,\dots,\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\}\), namely \(\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\).

With \(n=12345\),
\[
\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}=\frac{12345^{2}+1}{2}=\frac{152399025+1}{2}=76199513.
\]

\[
\boxed{76199513}
\]
e7ee294407062c74511d4c6736007c634eb4c8a5:
Let each system be a directed graph on the \(n=4225\) points, with all edges directed upward.  
Because in each system:

- starting points are distinct \(\Rightarrow\) outdegree \(\le 1\),
- ending points are distinct \(\Rightarrow\) indegree \(\le 1\),
- all edges go upward \(\Rightarrow\) no directed cycles,

each connected component is a directed path (possibly a single isolated vertex). Thus each system partitions the \(n\) points into “chains” (the path components), and two points are *linked* by the system iff they lie in the same chain.

### Step 1: Number of chains
Start with \(n\) isolated vertices (so \(n\) chains). Adding one edge always merges two chains into one (it cannot create a cycle), so \(k\) edges reduce the number of chains by \(k\).  
Hence each system partitions the points into exactly
\[
n-k
\]
chains.

### Step 2: When can we avoid a common linked pair?
Assume there is **no** pair of points linked by both systems. Then no chain from system \(A\) can share two vertices with any chain from system \(B\); otherwise those two vertices would be linked in both.

So each intersection \((\text{an }A\text{-chain})\cap(\text{a }B\text{-chain})\) has size at most \(1\).  
There are \((n-k)\) chains in \(A\) and \((n-k)\) in \(B\), so at most \((n-k)^2\) points can exist under this restriction. Therefore, avoiding a common linked pair forces
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]

Thus, to **guarantee** a pair linked by both, we need
\[
n > (n-k)^2.
\]

### Step 3: Plug in \(n=4225=65^2\)
We need
\[
4225 > (4225-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=4225-k\). Then this is
\[
4225 > m^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad m \le 64.
\]
So
\[
4225-k \le 64 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 4225-64=4161.
\]

### Step 4: Sharpness (construction when \(k=4160\))
Take \(m=65\) and label points as \((i,j)\) for \(1\le i,j\le 65\), ordered lexicographically by \(i\) then \(j\).

- In system \(A\): make chains with fixed \(i\):
  \((i,1)\to(i,2)\to\cdots\to(i,65)\).  
  This uses \(65\cdot 64=4160\) edges.
- In system \(B\): make chains with fixed \(j\):
  \((1,j)\to(2,j)\to\cdots\to(65,j)\).  
  This also uses \(4160\) edges.

These obey the monotonicity condition (“higher start implies higher end”), and any \(A\)-chain meets any \(B\)-chain in exactly one point \((i,j)\). Hence no two distinct points are linked by both systems. So \(k=4160\) does **not** guarantee a common linked pair.

Therefore the smallest guaranteeing \(k\) is

\[
\boxed{4161}.
\]
423635f660f083f16719e559092d983dffc36c59:
Let the columns be \(1,2,\dots,999\). There are \(998\) obstacles, one in each of rows \(2,3,\dots,999\), and no two obstacles share a column, so **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all** (in rows \(2\) through \(999\)).

---

### Upper bound: \(n\le 999\)

Use the following strategy:

For attempt \(k=1,2,\dots,999\), choose a column not previously ruled out and walk **straight down** that column from row \(1\) toward row \(1000\).

- If you ever hit an obstacle, then that column is **not** the obstacle-free column, so discard it.
- If you reach row \(1000\), you have succeeded.

Since there are \(999\) columns and exactly \(998\) of them contain an obstacle somewhere in rows \(2\)–\(999\), after at most \(998\) failures you will have eliminated \(998\) columns, and the remaining column must be obstacle-free; the next attempt succeeds.

So success is guaranteed in at most
\[
998+1=999
\]
attempts.

---

### Lower bound: \(n\ge 999\)

Consider any strategy that claims to guarantee success in at most \(998\) attempts.

An adversary can place the obstacles so that **every one of the first \(998\) columns you ever “commit to” as a full downward route** (i.e., a column you try to use to pass from row \(1\) down through all middle rows without already knowing it is safe) contains an obstacle somewhere below your current knowledge, causing failure. This is possible because:

- There are \(998\) obstacles available in distinct columns.
- Until you have identified the unique obstacle-free column, there remains at least one column consistent with everything you’ve seen that still could contain an obstacle in some unvisited row, so no strategy can certify success sooner in the worst case.

Thus, in the worst case, an adversary can force \(998\) failures before the obstacle-free column is the only remaining possibility, requiring one more attempt to actually traverse it.

Hence any guaranteed bound must satisfy \(n\ge 999\).

---

Combining both bounds gives the minimal such integer:
\[
\boxed{999}.
\]
dc32685212e2c5a93802033090cd37b617107996:
Let the columns be \(1,2,\dots,1999\). There are \(1998\) obstacles (one in each of rows \(2,3,\dots,1999\)), and no two obstacles share a column, so **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all** among rows \(2\) through \(1999\). Call such a column *free*.

---

### Upper bound: \(1999\) attempts suffice
Use the strategy:

- Attempt 1: start in column 1 and move straight down (only vertical moves) until either you reach the last row or you hit an obstacle.
- Attempt 2: do the same in column 2.
- \(\dots\)
- Attempt 1999: do the same in column 1999.

If a column is not free, it contains an obstacle in exactly one of rows \(2\)–\(1999\), so a straight-down attempt in that column must eventually hit it and fail, revealing that this column is not free.

Since there are \(1998\) non-free columns and \(1\) free column, after at most \(1998\) failures you must try the free column, and then the straight-down path reaches the last row.

So success is guaranteed in at most \(1999\) attempts.

---

### Lower bound: fewer than \(1999\) attempts cannot be guaranteed
Consider any strategy. In any failed attempt, the token hits some obstacle in some column \(c\). From that failure you can conclude only one decisive global fact:

- **Column \(c\) is not free.**

Crucially, a failed attempt cannot rule out *any other* column from being free, because obstacles are hidden and fixed, and the absence of an obstacle on the cells you happened to visit in other columns does not prevent those columns from containing an obstacle in some unvisited row.

Thus, after \(t\) failed attempts you can have certified at most \(t\) columns as “not free.” If \(t\le 1997\), then at least \(1999-t \ge 2\) columns are still consistent with being the free column.

Therefore, if a strategy claimed to guarantee success within \(1998\) attempts, then before the final attempt it would have experienced at most \(1997\) failures and hence could not have uniquely identified the free column; an adversary could choose the free column to be a different still-possible column, and place obstacles consistently so that the strategy’s final chosen column is not free, forcing failure. Hence no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 1998\) attempts.

So at least \(1999\) attempts are sometimes necessary.

---

Combining both bounds, the smallest such \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{1999}.
\]
0fafa011bf40c5ff62248c21dcbf95a9825f2c66:
Let each system be viewed as a directed graph on the \(n=9801\) linearly ordered points.

### 1) Each system is a disjoint union of directed paths
In a system, all starts are distinct \(\Rightarrow\) outdegree \(\le 1\), and all ends are distinct \(\Rightarrow\) indegree \(\le 1\). Since every edge goes upward, there are no cycles. Hence each connected component is a directed path (possibly a single isolated vertex).

Two points are “linked” by a system **iff** they lie on the same directed path (with the lower earlier on that path).

If a system has \(k\) edges and its path components have sizes \(l_1,l_2,\dots,l_c\) (including isolated vertices as \(l_i=1\)), then
\[
k=\sum_{i=1}^c (l_i-1)=\left(\sum_{i=1}^c l_i\right)-c = n-c.
\]
So the number of path components is
\[
c=n-k.
\]

### 2) When can two systems avoid having a common linked pair?
Let system \(A\) have \(c\) paths \(A_1,\dots,A_c\) and system \(B\) have \(c\) paths \(B_1,\dots,B_c\), where \(c=n-k\).

If there exist two distinct points lying in the same \(A_i\) and the same \(B_j\), then those two points are linked in both systems. Therefore, to avoid any common linked pair, we must have:

- Each intersection \(A_i\cap B_j\) contains **at most one** point.

But there are only \(c^2\) pairs \((i,j)\), so this implies
\[
n \le c^2 = (n-k)^2.
\]
Hence, if
\[
n > (n-k)^2,
\]
then avoiding a common linked pair is impossible, so a common linked pair is guaranteed.

### 3) Apply to \(n=9801=99^2\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
9801 > (9801-k)^2.
\]
Let \(c=9801-k\). Then we need \(c^2 < 9801 = 99^2\), i.e. \(c \le 98\).

Thus
\[
9801-k \le 98 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 9801-98 = 9703.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=9702\) is not enough)
If \(k=9702\), then \(c=9801-k=99\), and the inequality becomes \(9801 \le 99^2\), which allows avoidance.

A construction exists: label the points by pairs \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le 99\), ordered lexicographically by \(i\) then \(j\).  
- Let \(A\) consist of 99 paths with fixed \(i\): \((i,1)\to(i,2)\to\cdots\to(i,99)\).
- Let \(B\) consist of 99 paths with fixed \(j\): \((1,j)\to(2,j)\to\cdots\to(99,j)\).

Both systems satisfy the given “order-preserving” edge condition, and each intersection \(A_i\cap B_j\) is exactly one point \((i,j)\), so no two distinct points lie in the same path of both systems. Hence no common linked pair occurs.

Therefore \(k=9703\) is minimal.

\[
\boxed{9703}
\]
cf42b8dbbf7cb14c2e6c86356db6f10052127ed0:
\[
n=9099\ \text{(odd)}.
\]
Color the board in a checkerboard pattern with \((1,1)\) black. Every domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration the unique uncovered square must be **black**.

---

### 1) A basic invariant: the uncovered square stays in one of two black sublattices
Write the uncovered square’s coordinates as \((r,c)\). A legal slide moves the hole by two units horizontally or vertically (because a domino \((\text{white},\text{black})\) slides one step, vacating the other black square). Hence each move changes \((r,c)\) by \((\pm2,0)\) or \((0,\pm2)\). Therefore
\[
r \bmod 2\ \text{and}\ c \bmod 2
\]
are invariants of the motion. Since the hole is always on a black square, we have \(r\equiv c\pmod 2\), so there are exactly two possible parity types for the hole:
\[
(r,c)\equiv(0,0)\pmod2\quad\text{or}\quad(r,c)\equiv(1,1)\pmod2.
\]
So the state graph of maximal configurations splits into (at least) two disjoint parts according to this parity type.

---

### 2) There exist configurations with \(k(C)=1\) (jammed configurations)
It is possible to place the hole so that **no adjacent domino is aligned to slide into it** (all dominoes touching the hole are perpendicular to the direction needed). Such a configuration has no legal moves at all, so it is an isolated vertex of the state graph:
\[
k(C)=1.
\]
So \(1\) is one possible value of \(k(C)\).

---

### 3) All non-jammed configurations of the same parity type are mutually reachable
Fix one parity type, say \((r,c)\equiv(0,0)\pmod2\). If a configuration is not jammed, at least one slide is possible, so the hole can be moved. Using standard “corridor” maneuvers (pushing the hole along a path of black squares in steps of 2 and using local rearrangements in small rectangles), one can:

- move the hole to any other black square of the **same** parity type, and
- from there, transform the surrounding domino placement to a fixed canonical form,

all while staying within the same parity type. (The key point is that the allowed slide is exactly the length-2 alternating-path pivot in the matching; on a large rectangular grid this generates a single connected component among all matchings with a mobile hole of that parity.)

Thus, for each of the two parity types, **all non-jammed configurations lie in one single connected component**, hence share the same value of \(k(C)\). Let these two component sizes be \(K_{00}\) and \(K_{11}\). They are different because the two parity classes contain different numbers of black squares when \(n\) is odd (here the counts differ by \(n=9099\)), so the corresponding reachability sets cannot have equal size.

So, besides \(1\), there are exactly two more possible values of \(k(C)\): one for each parity type of the hole among the non-jammed configurations.

---

### 4) Conclusion: exactly three possible values
The set of possible values of \(k(C)\) is:
- \(1\) (jammed/isolated configurations), and
- \(K_{00}\), \(K_{11}\) (the two “giant” components corresponding to the two hole parity types).

Therefore, the **number** of possible values of \(k(C)\) is
\[
\boxed{3}.
\]
78dc405c323dc1b1a0f3b64f52bdeca9b8633453:
Color the board in a checkerboard pattern with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n=10001\) is odd, the number of black squares is \(\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\) and the number of white squares is \(\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}\). Any domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration (a maximum matching) the unique uncovered square must be black.

### 1) A slide preserves \((\text{row}\bmod 2,\ \text{col}\bmod 2)\) of the uncovered square
A slide moves a domino one unit without rotation into the hole, so the hole moves to the domino’s other end. That other end is two units away in a straight line (e.g. \((i,j)\mapsto(i,j\pm2)\) or \((i\pm2,j)\)), hence both the row parity and column parity of the hole are preserved. Therefore the pair
\[
(i \bmod 2,\ j \bmod 2)
\]
is an invariant of reachability.

Among black squares (where \(i+j\) is even), there are exactly two possibilities:
- \((i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\) (odd,odd),
- \((i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2\) (even,even).

So the state graph splits into **at least two** disjoint reachability classes, hence \(k(C)\) cannot take just one value.

### 2) Within each parity class, everything is reachable
For \(n\ge 3\) (certainly for \(10001\)), one can perform standard local “hole-walking” maneuvers inside \(2\times 3\) and \(3\times 2\) rectangles to:
- move the hole to any other black square with the same \((i\bmod 2,\ j\bmod 2)\), and
- realize the usual \(2\times 2\) domino flip (swap two vertical dominos with two horizontal dominos) while keeping the hole’s parity class fixed.

Since domino tilings are connected under \(2\times2\) flips (and here the hole provides the needed freedom to execute these flips while transporting the hole as required), it follows that **any two maximal configurations whose uncovered squares have the same \((i\bmod2,j\bmod2)\)** lie in the same reachability component. Thus there are **exactly two** components: one for hole at (odd,odd) black squares and one for hole at (even,even) black squares.

Therefore \(k(C)\) takes exactly one value on each component, so there are exactly **two** possible values of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
2dcf46296bef9d33f507c8511d9132f50542c41e:
\[
n=4225=65^{2}.
\]

### 1) Reformulation in terms of components
In one system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starting points distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ending points distinct). Hence the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths and isolated vertices (no branching, no cycles since edges go strictly upward).

Two points are “linked” by that system iff they lie in the same (undirected) connected component (a path), because along a directed path every lower vertex can reach every higher vertex.

Let a system have \(k\) edges. Starting from \(n\) isolated vertices, each added edge merges two components, so the number of components is
\[
c = n-k.
\]

Thus system \(A\) partitions the \(n\) points into \(c_A=n-k\) components; similarly \(B\) partitions into \(c_B=n-k\) components.

### 2) Pigeonhole bound
Assign to each point \(p\) the pair
\[
(\text{component of }p\text{ in }A,\ \text{component of }p\text{ in }B).
\]
There are at most \(c_Ac_B=(n-k)^2\) such pairs.

- If every such pair occurred **at most once**, then \(n\le (n-k)^2\).
- Therefore, if
\[
n>(n-k)^2,
\]
then two distinct points share the same pair of components, meaning they lie in the same component in \(A\) and also in the same component in \(B\). Taking the lower and higher of the two points gives two points linked by both systems.

So we need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
4225>(4225-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=4225-k\). Then we need \(4225>m^2\), i.e. \(m\le 64\) (since \(65^2=4225\)). Hence
\[
4225-k \le 64 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k\ge 4225-64=4161.
\]

So \(k=4161\) is sufficient.

### 3) Sharpness (a construction for \(k=4160\))
We show \(k=4160\) does **not** guarantee a common linked pair.

Number the points by height as \(1,2,\dots,4225\).

- **System \(A\)**: make 65 disjoint directed paths, each on 65 consecutive points:
  \[
  1\to2\to\cdots\to65,\quad
  66\to67\to\cdots\to130,\ \dots
  \]
  This uses \(65\cdot 64=4160\) edges and satisfies the “order-preserving” condition.

- **System \(B\)**: make 65 disjoint directed paths by columns mod 65:
  for each \(c=1,\dots,65\),
  \[
  c\to(c+65)\to(c+130)\to\cdots\to(c+64\cdot 65).
  \]
  Again \(65\cdot64=4160\) edges, and if one edge starts lower than another then their ends are also lower (both ends are start \(+65\)), so the condition holds.

In \(A\), two points are linked iff they are in the same block of 65 consecutive numbers (same “row”).
In \(B\), two points are linked iff they have the same residue mod 65 (same “column”).
No two distinct points can share both the same row and the same column, so there is **no** pair linked by both systems.

Thus \(k=4160\) is not sufficient, and the minimal guaranteeing \(k\) is \(4161\).

\[
\boxed{4161}
\]
742a2e29fca27ebe2c0206d404c93a2b4c7e28c8:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) in increasing height, with \(n=2025\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system (say \(A\)) we have \(k\) directed edges, each from a lower point to a higher point, with

- all start points distinct (outdegree \(\le 1\)),
- all end points distinct (indegree \(\le 1\)),
- and the monotonicity condition: if one edge starts higher than another, then it also ends higher.

Because every edge goes upward, there are no directed cycles. With indegree \(\le 1\) and outdegree \(\le 1\), the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (including isolated vertices). Hence, if \(A\) has \(k\) edges on \(n\) vertices, then the number of (undirected) connected components in \(A\) is
\[
c_A = n-k
\]
(since a forest with \(n\) vertices and \(k\) edges has \(n-k\) components).

Within a component (a directed path), any two vertices are linked by \(A\): the lower reaches the higher.

So: **two points are linked by \(A\) iff they lie in the same \(A\)-component** (and similarly for \(B\)).

---

### 2) A pigeonhole lower bound for guaranteeing a common linked pair
Assume, for contradiction, that **no** pair of distinct points is linked by both \(A\) and \(B\).

Let the \(A\)-components be \(A_1,\dots,A_{c_A}\) and the \(B\)-components be \(B_1,\dots,B_{c_B}\), where
\[
c_A=c_B=n-k.
\]

Consider the label of a point \(v\):
\[
\big(\text{index of the }A\text{-component containing }v,\ \text{index of the }B\text{-component containing }v\big).
\]
If two distinct points had the same label \((i,j)\), then they would lie in \(A_i\cap B_j\), hence be in the same component of both \(A\) and \(B\), hence linked by both—contradiction.

Therefore all \(n\) points must have **distinct** labels, so
\[
n \le c_A c_B = (n-k)^2.
\]
Thus, to *guarantee* a common linked pair, we need
\[
(n-k)^2 < n.
\]
Let \(m=n-k\). Then we need \(m^2<n\), i.e. \(m\le \lfloor \sqrt{n-1}\rfloor\). Hence
\[
k \ge n-\lfloor \sqrt{n-1}\rfloor.
\]

For \(n=2025\),
\[
\lfloor \sqrt{2024}\rfloor = 44
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
k \ge 2025-44 = 1981.
\]

So \(k=1981\) is sufficient.

---

### 3) Sharpness: a construction with \(k=1980\) and no common linked pair
Now show \(k=1980\) does **not** guarantee it.

Since \(2025=45^2\), arrange the points in a \(45\times 45\) grid and label them
\[
(r,c),\quad 1\le r,c\le 45,
\]
ordered by height in *row-major* order:
\[
(1,1)<(1,2)<\cdots<(1,45)<(2,1)<\cdots<(45,45).
\]

**System \(A\) (rows):** for each fixed row \(r\), add edges
\[
(r,1)\to(r,2)\to\cdots\to(r,45).
\]
This gives \(45\) disjoint directed paths, so \(k_A=45\cdot 44=1980\) edges.

**System \(B\) (columns):** for each fixed column \(c\), add edges
\[
(1,c)\to(2,c)\to\cdots\to(45,c),
\]
again \(45\) disjoint directed paths, so \(k_B=1980\).

Both systems satisfy the required monotonicity condition (when edges are listed by increasing start height, their endpoints also increase, because moving right stays in the same row and moving down goes to a later row in the global order).

Now:
- Two points are linked by \(A\) iff they are in the same row.
- Two points are linked by \(B\) iff they are in the same column.

A pair linked by both would have to lie in the same row and the same column, hence be the **same point**, impossible for two distinct points. So with \(k=1980\), it is possible to avoid any common linked pair.

Therefore the minimal \(k\) is \(1981\).

\[
\boxed{1981}
\]
ceb4ce115250b420797d1ef441058d5acb015941:
\[
\boxed{6581}
\]

**Why \(6581\) attempts suffice.**  
There are \(6581\) columns and \(6580\) obstacles, with no two obstacles in the same column, so **exactly one column is obstacle–free**. A strategy is:

- Attempt \(1\): choose column \(1\), walk straight down until either you reach the last row (success) or you hit an obstacle (then column \(1\) is not the free column).
- Attempt \(2\): do the same in column \(2\).
- Continue similarly.

In the worst case, the free column is the last one you try, so you may fail \(6580\) times and succeed on attempt \(6581\).

Hence \(n \le 6581\).

**Why \(6580\) attempts cannot be guaranteed.**  
Consider any strategy that claims to guarantee success in at most \(6580\) attempts. An adversary can choose the obstacle placement (still respecting “one obstacle in each of rows \(2\) through \(6581\)” and “distinct columns”) so that **each of the first \(6580\) attempts hits an obstacle**:

- As long as you have not yet reached the last row, during an attempt you must at some moment step from row \(k\) into row \(k+1\) for some \(k\le 6581\).
- The adversary ensures that, for the first \(6580\) attempts, the first time your path enters some as-yet-unforced intermediate row, it does so in a column that has not yet been used by an obstacle in earlier forced failures, and the adversary places that row’s obstacle exactly there.
- This is always possible for \(6580\) failures because there are \(6580\) obstacle-rows available and \(6581\) columns, so the adversary can keep using fresh columns to maintain the “distinct columns” condition.

Thus no strategy can **force** success within \(6580\) attempts in the worst case, so \(n \ge 6581\).

Combining both parts gives \(n=6581\).
fd8c76fe287cba7585c08d9168f922c0c5fdde49:
Let the \(n=9801\) points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) by height.

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each point is the start of at most one edge (starts distinct) and the end of at most one edge (ends distinct). Since every edge goes upward, there are no directed cycles. Hence every connected component is a directed path (possibly a single isolated vertex).

If a component has \(t\) vertices, it has exactly \(t-1\) edges, so summing over components gives
\[
k \;=\; n - c,
\]
where \(c\) is the number of components (paths) in the system. Thus for either system,
\[
c_A=c_B=n-k.
\]

Two points are “linked” by a system iff they lie in the same path component.

### 2) When can we avoid a pair linked by both systems?
Label each point \(x\) by the pair
\[
(\alpha(x),\beta(x)),
\]
where \(\alpha(x)\) is the component of \(x\) in system \(A\), and \(\beta(x)\) is the component of \(x\) in system \(B\).

If two distinct points \(x\neq y\) had the same pair \((\alpha,\beta)\), then they would lie in the same component of \(A\) and also in the same component of \(B\), hence they would be linked by both systems. Therefore, **to avoid** such a pair, all \((\alpha(x),\beta(x))\) must be distinct, implying
\[
n \le c_A c_B = (n-k)^2.
\]
So a common linked pair is **guaranteed** whenever
\[
n > (n-k)^2.
\]

### 3) Apply \(n=9801=99^2\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
9801 > (9801-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=9801-k\). Then we need \(9801>m^2\), i.e. \(m\le 98\). Hence
\[
9801-k \le 98 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 9801-98=9703.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=9702\) does *not* guarantee)
If \(k=9702\), then \(n-k=99\) and \((n-k)^2=9801=n\), so the inequality fails and avoidance should be possible.

Indeed, arrange the points as a \(99\times 99\) grid \((i,j)\) ordered by increasing \(j\), then increasing \(i\) (column-major order).  
- Let system \(A\) connect \((i,j)\to (i,j+1)\) for \(j=1,\dots,98\) (99 horizontal paths, one per row).
- Let system \(B\) connect \((i,j)\to (i+1,j)\) for \(i=1,\dots,98\) (99 vertical paths, one per column).

Both systems satisfy the required “order-preserving” condition, each has \(99\cdot 98=9702\) edges, and each intersection of an \(A\)-component (row) with a \(B\)-component (column) is a single point. Thus no two distinct points are linked by both.

So \(k=9702\) is not enough, but \(k=9703\) is.

\[
\boxed{9703}
\]
794098d586c6a5f443fadb7fb29ce7664a1a4352:
Label the points \(1<2<\cdots<n\) by increasing height (\(n=2025\)).

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each point is the start of **at most one** edge and the end of **at most one** edge (starts are distinct, ends are distinct). Also edges go upward, so there are no directed cycles. Hence each connected component (ignoring direction) is a **directed path**.

Therefore, in one system:
- Any two points in the same component are linked (from the lower one to the higher one along the path).
- If the system has \(k\) edges, then the underlying undirected graph is a forest of paths, so
\[
\#\text{components} \;=\; n-k.
\]
(Reason: in a forest, \(\#\text{components}=n-\#\text{edges}\).)

So each system partitions the \(n\) points into \(n-k\) “linkage classes”.

### 2) When can two systems avoid a common linked pair?
Let system \(A\) have classes \(A_1,\dots,A_{n-k}\) and system \(B\) have classes \(B_1,\dots,B_{n-k}\).

If there is **no** pair of distinct points linked by both systems, then no intersection \(A_i\cap B_j\) can contain 2 points; i.e.
\[
|A_i\cap B_j|\le 1\quad \text{for all }i,j.
\]
But the \( (n-k)^2 \) intersections \(A_i\cap B_j\) partition all \(n\) points, so this would force
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]
Hence, to **guarantee** a common linked pair, it suffices (and is necessary by this counting obstruction) that
\[
n > (n-k)^2.
\]

### 3) Apply to \(n=2025=45^2\)
We need \((n-k)^2 < 2025\), i.e. \(n-k \le 44\). Thus
\[
k \ge 2025-44 = 1981.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (show \(k=1980\) does not guarantee)
Let \(k=1980\), so \(n-k=45\).

- System \(A\): edges \(i\to i+45\) for \(i=1,2,\dots,1980\).  
  This makes 45 chains by residue mod 45: \(\{r,r+45,\dots,r+44\cdot45\}\).

- System \(B\): split into 45 consecutive blocks of size 45, and within each block use edges \(t\to t+1\) (44 edges per block, total \(45\cdot44=1980\)).  
  This makes 45 chains \(\{1,\dots,45\},\{46,\dots,90\},\dots\).

Each residue class meets each block in exactly one point, so no two distinct points lie in the same component in both systems; hence no pair is linked by both.

Thus \(k=1980\) fails, but \(k=1981\) succeeds.

\[
\boxed{1981}
\]
a5721d339078739789b715d23c11836d5e767f42:
Color the \(n\times n\) board in a checkerboard pattern. Since \(n\) is odd, one color (say black) occurs \(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\) times and the other (white) occurs \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) times.

**1) The uncovered square must be black.**  
Every domino covers one black and one white square. If the uncovered square were white, the remaining numbers of black and white squares would differ by \(2\), so they could not be perfectly covered by dominoes. Hence in every maximal configuration the unique hole is on a black square.

**2) Sliding preserves the “black-hole” condition and is the only global constraint.**  
A legal slide moves a domino into the hole; the hole moves to the domino’s vacated square. Since the domino always covers one black and one white, the hole always moves from black to black. Thus all configurations reachable from any start configuration have their hole on a black square.

**3) There are exactly two types of configurations.**
- **Frozen configurations:** the hole has no adjacent domino that can slide into it, so no move is possible. Then \(k(C)=1\).
- **Non-frozen configurations:** at least one slide is possible. For an odd \(n\times n\) board, the standard “hole-walking” argument shows that once movement is possible, the hole can be routed (via successive slides) to arbitrarily chosen black squares and used to “transport” local adjustments; consequently all non-frozen maximal configurations lie in one connected component of the slide graph. Therefore every non-frozen configuration has the same value of \(k(C)\) (namely, the size of that unique giant component).

So \(k(C)\) takes only:
- the value \(1\) (frozen component), and
- one other value (the unique nontrivial component).

Hence the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is

\[
\boxed{2}.
\]
ead6d71a0a85a479679324221f4a7abe5d627e2a:
In each system, every point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starting points are distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ending points are distinct), and all edges go upward, so the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths.

If a system has \(c\) connected components (paths, including isolated points), then in that system
\[
k=\sum(\text{edges in a path})=\sum(\text{vertices in a path}-1)=n-c,
\]
so
\[
c=n-k.
\]

Now consider two systems \(A\) and \(B\), each with \(c=n-k\) components.  Each point \(p\) belongs to exactly one component of \(A\) and one component of \(B\), so it determines an ordered pair
\[
(\,C_A(p),\,C_B(p)\,) \in \{1,\dots,c\}\times\{1,\dots,c\}.
\]
If two distinct points \(p\neq q\) have the same ordered pair, then they lie in the same component of \(A\) and the same component of \(B\), hence are linked by both systems.

So, to avoid having any pair linked by both systems, every one of the \(n\) points must have a distinct ordered pair \((C_A,C_B)\). There are only \(c^2\) such pairs, hence avoidance is possible only if
\[
n \le c^2=(n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, a guarantee occurs as soon as
\[
n>(n-k)^2.
\]

Here \(n=4225=65^2\). Thus we need
\[
4225>(4225-k)^2 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 4225-k \le 64 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad k\ge 4161.
\]

It remains to show \(k=4160\) does **not** guarantee it.  Take \(c=n-k=65\) and label the \(4225\) points as \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le 65\), ordered by increasing \(j\) then increasing \(i\).
- System \(A\): edges \((i,j)\to(i,j+1)\) for \(j=1,\dots,64\) (65 disjoint “rows”), giving \(65\cdot 64=4160\) edges.
- System \(B\): edges \((i,j)\to(i+1,j)\) for \(i=1,\dots,64\) (65 disjoint “columns”), also \(4160\) edges.

Both satisfy the monotonicity condition, and in \(A\) only points in the same row are linked, while in \(B\) only points in the same column are linked; a row and a column meet in exactly one point, so no two distinct points are linked by both systems. Hence \(4160\) is insufficient.

Therefore the smallest such \(k\) is \(\boxed{4161}\).
50c784fdcffb9e24be9c0a58267423afaba63a14:
\[
n=9801.
\]

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system, each point has **outdegree \(\le 1\)** (starting points are distinct) and **indegree \(\le 1\)** (ending points are distinct). Since every edge goes strictly upward, there are no directed cycles. Hence each connected component is a **directed path** (possibly a single isolated vertex).

If a component has \(m\) vertices, it has exactly \(m-1\) edges. Summing over components gives
\[
k=\sum (m_i-1)=\left(\sum m_i\right)-(\#\text{components})=n-(\#\text{components}).
\]
So if a system has \(k\) edges, it has exactly
\[
a=n-k
\]
components (and similarly \(b=n-k\) for the other system).

Also, in such a path component, **any two vertices in it are linked** (you can follow edges along the path).

---

### 2) When can we avoid a pair linked by both systems?
Let the components of system \(A\) be \(A_1,\dots,A_a\) and of system \(B\) be \(B_1,\dots,B_b\).

If there is **no** pair of points linked by both systems, then no two points can lie in the same component of \(A\) *and* the same component of \(B\). Equivalently,
\[
|A_i\cap B_j|\le 1\quad \text{for all }i,j.
\]
