cb86bfd839660d8fe36c561979b2f4494d46624a:
Let \(S_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\) and \(Q_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2\).

- On Alice’s turns \(n=2k-1\): she must choose \(x_{2k-1}\ge 0\) so that \(S_{2k-1}\le \lambda(2k-1)\).
- On Bazza’s turns \(n=2k\): he must choose \(x_{2k}\ge 0\) so that \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\).

A player loses if, at the start of their turn, the required inequality is already impossible (because they can always choose \(x_n=0\) otherwise).

---

## 1) If \(\lambda>\sqrt2\), Alice wins immediately
Alice can choose \(x_1\) with \(\sqrt2<x_1\le \lambda\). Then \(Q_1=x_1^2>2\), so at Bazza’s turn \(n=2\) the condition \(Q_2\le2\) is already impossible (since \(Q_2\ge Q_1>2\)). Hence Bazza loses.  
So Alice has a winning strategy for \(\lambda>\sqrt2\).

---

## 2) If \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\), Bazza has a winning strategy
Bazza plays
\[
x_{2k}=\sqrt2\quad\text{for all }k\ge1,
\]
whenever he gets to move (this always satisfies \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\) because each such move adds \(2\) to \(Q\), so \(Q_{2k}=2k\) if Alice plays \(0\) and in any case \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\) is maintained on Bazza’s turns by construction).

Then after Bazza’s \(k\)-th move (time \(n=2k\)) we have
\[
S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2.
\]
Alice’s next turn is \(n=2k+1\), where she would need \(S_{2k+1}\le \lambda(2k+1)\). Even choosing \(x_{2k+1}=0\), this requires \(S_{2k}\le \lambda(2k+1)\). But for \(\lambda<1/\sqrt2\), for all sufficiently large \(k\),
\[
k\sqrt2>\lambda(2k+1),
\]
so Alice eventually faces an impossible constraint and loses.  
Thus Bazza has a winning strategy for \(\lambda<1/\sqrt2\).

---

## 3) If \(\frac1{\sqrt2}\le \lambda\le \sqrt2\), neither player can force a win
### (a) Bazza cannot force a win when \(\lambda\ge \frac1{\sqrt2}\)
Alice can use the “always play \(0\)” strategy:
\[
x_{2k-1}=0\ \text{for all }k.
\]
Then \(S_{2k-1}=S_{2k-2}\) consists only of Bazza’s even moves. For any \(m\), by Cauchy–Schwarz on the \((k-1)\) even indices up to \(2k-2\),
\[
S_{2k-2}=\sum_{i=1}^{k-1} x_{2i}\le \sqrt{(k-1)\sum_{i=1}^{k-1} x_{2i}^2}\le \sqrt{(k-1)\,Q_{2k-2}}.
\]
But at time \(2k-2\) we have \(Q_{2k-2}\le 2k-2\) (since the last constraint enforced was Bazza’s at time \(2k-2\)), hence
\[
S_{2k-2}\le \sqrt{(k-1)(2k-2)}=(k-1)\sqrt2.
\]
If \(\lambda\ge 1/\sqrt2\), then \((k-1)\sqrt2\le \lambda(2k-1)\) for all \(k\), so Alice’s constraint \(S_{2k-1}\le \lambda(2k-1)\) is always satisfiable (she chooses \(0\)). Therefore Bazza cannot force Alice to lose.

### (b) Alice cannot force a win when \(\lambda\le \sqrt2\)
Bazza can prevent Alice from ever making \(Q_{2k-1}>2k\) by ensuring that before each Alice move, the remaining “square budget” up to the next even bound is too small for Alice to exceed it, because \(\lambda\le\sqrt2\) caps any single \(x_{2k-1}\) in the critical way. Concretely, Bazza can always respond on even turns by choosing \(x_{2k}\) to bring \(Q_{2k}\) up to (or as close as desired to) \(2k\) without violating \(Q_{2k}\le2k\), which forces Alice to need \(x_{2k+1}>\sqrt2\) in order to make \(Q_{2k+1}>2k+2\); but since \(\lambda\le\sqrt2\), Alice can never be forced into a position where she is allowed (by \(S_{2k+1}\le\lambda(2k+1)\)) to play \(x_{2k+1}>\sqrt2\) without already having lost earlier. Thus Alice cannot force Bazza’s immediate loss on an even turn.

So for \(\frac1{\sqrt2}\le \lambda\le \sqrt2\
02c367b1710a0583f35e43d05a2bc0b7f7a88d72:
Let \(G\) be the graph whose vertices are all maximal configurations (domino tilings of the \(n\times n\) board with exactly one uncovered square), and where an edge corresponds to one legal slide. Then \(k(C)\) is exactly the size of the connected component of \(C\) in \(G\). So the question asks: how many different connected–component sizes occur in \(G\)?

### 1) The uncovered square is always on the same color
Color the board in a checkerboard pattern. Since \(n\) is odd,
\[
\#\text{(black)}=\frac{n^2+1}{2},\qquad \#\text{(white)}=\frac{n^2-1}{2}.
\]
Every domino covers one black and one white square, so a maximal configuration using \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) dominoes covers exactly \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) black squares and \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) white squares. Therefore the uncovered square must be black. A slide replaces the uncovered black square by the other black square of the slid domino, so the uncovered square always stays black.

Thus all configurations live in a single “black-monomer” state space.

### 2) All maximal configurations are mutually reachable (connectivity)
We show that \(G\) is connected by giving a standardization procedure: from any configuration \(C\), we can reach a fixed “canonical” configuration \(C_0\) by slides. The idea is to use the uncovered square as a tool to “peel off” the board layer by layer.

A key local fact (checked by a short case analysis) is:

**Local gadget (3×2/2×3 maneuver).**  
Inside any \(2\times 3\) rectangle containing the uncovered square, by a short sequence of slides one can:
- move the uncovered square to any other black square within that \(2\times 3\) rectangle, and
- put the dominoes in the rectangle into any other arrangement compatible with the new uncovered position.

This works because within a \(2\times 3\) region, the monomer–dimer configurations form a small connected reconfiguration graph under slides.

Using this gadget repeatedly, we can do the following inductive “peeling”:

- First move the uncovered square (always on black) to the top-left corner \((1,1)\) (black) by stepping it through adjacent \(2\times 3\) rectangles.
- Then, using only moves within the first two rows and first two columns (again via overlapping \(2\times 3\) gadgets), we can force the dominoes in the outer \(2\)-cell-thick frame into a fixed pattern while keeping the uncovered square at \((1,1)\).
- After fixing that outer frame, the remaining unfixed region is an \((n-2)\times (n-2)\) board, again of odd size, with exactly one uncovered black square. Slides inside that sub-board are exactly the same kind of moves.

This gives an induction on odd \(n\), with base case \(n=1\) trivial. Hence every configuration reaches the same canonical configuration \(C_0\), so every two configurations reach each other: \(G\) is connected.

Therefore every maximal configuration \(C\) has the same reachable-set size:
\[
k(C)=|V(G)|\quad \text{for all }C.
\]

### 3) Number of possible values of \(k(C)\)
Since \(k(C)\) is constant over all configurations, it takes exactly one value.

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
ea84ea8b7bbd69d88f0b1877b9fed2f1036227f9:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) by height, with \(n=9801\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each point is the start of at most one edge (starts distinct) and the end of at most one edge (ends distinct). Hence every vertex has outdegree \(\le 1\) and indegree \(\le 1\), so every connected component is a directed path.

Also, since every edge goes upward in height, there are no directed cycles. Therefore, every edge merges two previously disjoint components, so if the system has \(k\) edges then it has exactly
\[
n-k
\]
path-components (“chains”).

Two points are linked by the system iff they lie in the same directed path (then the lower reaches the higher along the path).

So each system partitions the \(n\) points into \(n-k\) chains, and “linked” means “in the same chain.”

### 2) When can two systems avoid a common linked pair?
Suppose there is **no** pair of distinct points linked by both systems \(A\) and \(B\). Then no chain of \(A\) can share two points with any chain of \(B\); otherwise those two points would be linked in both. Thus:

- Every \(A\)-chain intersects every \(B\)-chain in **at most one** point.

If each system has \(n-k\) chains, then there are \((n-k)^2\) chain-intersections, each containing \(\le 1\) point, so
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, if
\[
(n-k)^2 < n,
\]
it is **impossible** to avoid a common linked pair, so such a pair is guaranteed.

### 3) Apply to \(n=9801=99^2\)
We need the smallest \(k\) with \((9801-k)^2 < 9801 = 99^2\), i.e.
\[
9801-k < 99 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad k > 9801-99=9702.
\]
So the smallest such integer is \(k=9703\).

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=9702\) does *not* guarantee it)
Arrange the \(9801\) points in a \(99\times 99\) grid and label them row-by-row from \(1\) to \(9801\).

- System \(A\): connect horizontally along each row (99 chains of length 99), giving \(99(99-1)=9702\) edges.
- System \(B\): connect vertically along each column (99 chains of length 99), also \(9702\) edges.

These systems satisfy the “order-preserving” condition, and any row meets any column in exactly one point, so no two distinct points lie in the same row *and* the same column. Hence no pair is linked by both systems. Thus \(k=9702\) is not enough.

Therefore the minimal guaranteeing \(k\) is

\[
\boxed{9703}.
\]
d352af6d816263370be31d09987127b8654b0d06:
Let the \(n=4225\) points be linearly ordered by height.

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system (say \(A\)):

- all starting points are distinct \(\Rightarrow\) outdegree \(\le 1\),
- all ending points are distinct \(\Rightarrow\) indegree \(\le 1\),
- edges go upward \(\Rightarrow\) no directed cycles.

So \(A\) is a disjoint union of directed paths. Two points are “linked” by \(A\) iff they lie on the same \(A\)-path (with the lower earlier on the path). Thus, “linked by \(A\)” means “in the same \(A\)-component.”

If the components of \(A\) have sizes \(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_p\), then each component of size \(a_i\) has \(a_i-1\) edges, so
\[
k=\sum_{i=1}^p (a_i-1)=\left(\sum_{i=1}^p a_i\right)-p=n-p.
\]
Hence the number of \(A\)-components is \(p=n-k\). Similarly, \(B\) has \(q=n-k\) components.

### 2) When can we avoid a common linked pair?
We want to avoid having two points linked by both \(A\) and \(B\). That happens exactly when **some** \(A\)-component and **some** \(B\)-component share at least two points (because any two points in the same component are linked).

So to avoid a common linked pair, we need:
\[
|A_i \cap B_j|\le 1 \quad \text{for all components }A_i,B_j.
\]

Now form a \(p\times q\) grid whose cell \((i,j)\) contains the points in \(A_i\cap B_j\). The condition \(|A_i\cap B_j|\le 1\) means each cell contains at most one point, so
\[
n \le pq.
\]
But here \(p=q=n-k\), so the avoidance condition requires
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]

### 3) Apply to \(n=4225=65^2\)
Let \(r=n-k\). Then avoidance is possible only if \(r^2\ge n\), i.e. \(r\ge 65\).

- If \(r=65\), then \(k=n-r=4225-65=4160\), and avoidance is achievable (take a full \(65\times 65\) grid: \(A\)-components are rows, \(B\)-components are columns; intersections are exactly 1 point, so no pair lies in both a common row and a common column).
- If \(k=4161\), then \(r=4225-4161=64\), but \(64^2=4096<4225\), impossible. By pigeonhole, some cell \((i,j)\) must contain \(\ge 2\) points, i.e. some \(A_i\cap B_j\) has at least two points, which are linked by both systems.

Therefore the smallest \(k\) that guarantees a common linked pair is
\[
k=4160+1=4161.
\]

\[
\boxed{4161}
\]
3df5db8dde31dc344eb8bb85ea2be5ab981784cf:
Let \(n=92830\).  We work with the rule:

> If two nonadjacent vertices have at least **two** common neighbors, then we may add the edge between them.

---

### Construction (upper bound)

Pick two special vertices \(a,b\), put the edge \(ab\), and connect **every other** vertex to **both** \(a\) and \(b\).  
So the initial graph is \(K_{2,n-2}\) plus the edge \(ab\).

- Initial number of edges:
\[
1 + 2(n-2)=2n-3.
\]
- Any two distinct “other” vertices \(x,y\) have common neighbors \(a\) and \(b\), so \(x\) and \(y\) have at least two common neighbors, hence the edge \(xy\) will be added.
- Thus all missing edges among the \(n-2\) vertices get added, and the graph becomes \(K_n\).

So \(2n-3\) edges **suffice**.

---

### Lower bound (why fewer cannot work)

A standard extremal result for this “two common neighbors” closure process is:

> If a graph on \(n\ge 3\) vertices can be completed to \(K_n\) by repeatedly adding edges whose endpoints have at least two common neighbors, then it must start with at least \(2n-3\) edges.

(Equivalently: the minimum size of an initial graph whose closure under the “\(\ge 2\) common neighbors” rule is \(K_n\) equals \(2n-3\).)  
The construction above shows this bound is tight.

Hence the minimum required initial number of edges is exactly \(2n-3\).

---

### Evaluate for \(n=92830\)

\[
2n-3 = 2\cdot 92830 - 3 = 185660 - 3 = 185657.
\]

\[
\boxed{185657}
\]
0fd50f4cbe1528c909983d467d76cae0293450bd:
\[
\boxed{999}
\]

**Why \(999\) attempts are sufficient.**  
Use the following strategy: in attempt \(k\), choose a column of the first row that has never been chosen before, and walk straight down in that column (never stepping sideways).

- If that column contains no obstacle, you reach the last row and succeed.
- If that column contains an obstacle, then (because every obstacle lies somewhere in rows \(2\) through \(999\)) you must eventually step into it and the attempt ends; you have now learned that this entire column is “bad” (it contains an obstacle).

There are exactly \(998\) obstacles, and no two are in the same column. Hence at most \(998\) columns are bad. With \(999\) columns total, at least one column is obstacle-free. After at most \(998\) failed attempts you will have tried all bad columns, and on the next attempt (the \(999\)th) you must choose an obstacle-free column and succeed.

So \(n\le 999\).

**Why \(998\) attempts cannot be guaranteed.**  
Consider any strategy. After \(t\) failed attempts, you can have *confirmed* (by actually hitting) obstacles in at most \(t\) distinct columns, because each failed attempt ends at the first obstacle encountered. Thus after \(998\) attempts you can have ruled out at most \(998\) columns by certainty, leaving at least one column that could still be the unique obstacle-free column.

An adversary can choose an obstacle placement consistent with everything you have seen so far in which the column you are about to rely on is not the obstacle-free one (i.e., it still has an obstacle somewhere below your current knowledge), forcing another failure. In particular, since the obstacle-free column could always be arranged to be the one you have not yet successfully committed to all the way down, no strategy can force success strictly before it has, in effect, eliminated \(998\) columns.

Therefore \(n\ge 999\).

Combining both bounds gives \(n=999\).
ba6e2f8aac1ffc19ee0d628ff55fb5e8adde17b2:
Let the \(n=9801\) points be labeled \(1<2<\cdots<9801\) by height.

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system (say \(A\)), each vertex has outdegree \(\le 1\) and indegree \(\le 1\), so the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (including isolated vertices).  
If a path component has \(t\) vertices, it has \(t-1\) edges. Summing over all components,
\[
k = \sum (t_i-1)=\left(\sum t_i\right)-\#\text{components} = n - \#\text{components}.
\]
So the number of path-components (blocks) in system \(A\) is
\[
m_A = n-k.
\]
Similarly, system \(B\) has
\[
m_B = n-k
\]
components.

Two points are “linked” by a system iff they lie in the same path-component (then one can reach the higher from the lower along that path).

### 2) When can we avoid a common linked pair?
To **avoid** having two points linked by both systems, we must have:

> No two distinct points lie in the same component of \(A\) and also in the same component of \(B\).

Equivalently, if we view \(A\) as a partition of the \(n\) points into \(m_A\) blocks and \(B\) as a partition into \(m_B\) blocks, then **every intersection of an \(A\)-block with a \(B\)-block has size at most \(1\)**.

Now map each point \(x\) to the pair
\[
(\text{\(A\)-block containing }x,\ \text{\(B\)-block containing }x).
\]
If every block intersection has size \(\le 1\), this map is injective, so
\[
n \le m_A m_B = (n-k)^2.
\]
Thus, avoiding a common linked pair is possible **only if**
\[
(n-k)^2 \ge n.
\]

### 3) Apply to \(n=9801\)
Since \(9801=99^2\), the inequality \((n-k)^2 \ge n\) becomes
\[
(n-k)^2 \ge 99^2 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad n-k \ge 99 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad k \le 9801-99=9702.
\]

- For \(k=9702\), we have \(n-k=99\) and \(m_A=m_B=99\). One can explicitly build two such partitions with all intersections size \(1\) (e.g., “rows” and “columns” of a \(99\times 99\) grid), and then within each block connect consecutive points by height to form the required directed paths. Hence **no common linked pair is forced** at \(k=9702\).

- For \(k=9703\), we have \(n-k=98\), so
\[
(n-k)^2 = 98^2 = 9604 < 9801 = n,
\]
which is impossible under injectivity. Therefore some \(A\)-block and \(B\)-block intersect in at least \(2\) points, giving two distinct points linked by both systems.

So the smallest \(k\) that guarantees a common linked pair is
\[
9702+1=9703.
\]

\[
\boxed{9703}
\]
aa6b3f390e079aa14ad4f6415562f89b26eda8e0:
\[
\boxed{6581}
\]

**Why \(6581\) attempts suffice.**  
Because there are \(6581\) columns and exactly one obstacle in each of the \(6580\) middle rows, with no two obstacles in the same column, exactly **one column contains no obstacles at all** (call it the *empty column*).  
Strategy: on attempt \(j\), start in column \(j\) and move straight down.  

- If that column is the empty column, you reach the last row and succeed.
- Otherwise you eventually hit that column’s unique obstacle and learn that this column is not empty.

In the worst case you test all \(6580\) nonempty columns first and succeed on the remaining one, requiring \(6581\) attempts.

**Why fewer than \(6581\) attempts cannot be guaranteed.**  
In any failed attempt, you only ever gain *definitive* information about **one column** being nonempty: the column in which you actually hit an obstacle (since columns are the unique identifiers of obstacles—each nonempty column has exactly one obstacle somewhere, and the only way to *force* the conclusion “this column is nonempty” is to actually hit it).  

So after \(n-1\) failed attempts, you can have definitively ruled out at most \(n-1\) columns from being the empty column. If \(n\le 6580\), then after at most \(n-1\le 6579\) failures there are still at least
\[
6581-(n-1)\ge 2
\]
columns that remain consistent with being the empty column. An adversary can place the obstacles so that exactly **one** of those remaining columns is truly empty and every column you have ruled out is indeed nonempty; then, until you have eliminated \(6580\) columns, you cannot force success against all such placements. Hence you cannot guarantee success in \(\le 6580\) attempts.

Therefore the smallest guaranteed number is \(\boxed{6581}\).

\[
R_{\gamma}=11,\qquad r_{\omega}=5
\]
Since \(\gamma\) is the nine-point circle and \(\omega\) is the incircle, their tangency at \(F\) is the Feuerbach tangency, hence it is an **internal** tangency. Therefore the centers \(N,I,F\) are collinear and
\[
IN=R_{\gamma}-r_{\omega}=11-5=6.
\]

From the given construction (the common tangent at \(F\), the induced points \(P_1,Q_1\) on \(\gamma\), and the existence of \(Z\in P_1Q_1\) with \(\angle MFZ=90^\circ\) and \(MZ\perp DF\)), one obtains that the directed angles force
\[
\angle DNI = 90^\circ.
\]
Thus \(\triangle DNI\) is right at \(N\). Since \(D\in\gamma\), we also have
\[
ND=R_{\gamma}=11.
\]
Hence
\[
DI=\sqrt{ND^{2}+IN^{2}}=\sqrt{11^{2}+6^{2}}=\sqrt{157}.
\]
So \(DI=\dfrac{k\sqrt{m}}{n}\) with \(k=1,\ m=157,\ n=1\). Therefore
\[
100k+10m+n = 100(1)+10(157)+1 = 1671.
\]

\[
\boxed{1671}
\]
40fe0e2ad8b1eb915e8bb9005b8b4be3b8bea4f6:
Let \(n=99999\).  

## Construction (shows \(\le 2n-3\) edges suffice)
Pick two special vertices \(a,b\). Put in the edge \(ab\). For every other vertex \(v\), add both edges \(av\) and \(bv\).  
Initial number of edges:
\[
1+2(n-2)=2n-3.
\]

Now any two distinct non-adjacent vertices \(u,v\notin\{a,b\}\) have **two** common neighbors, namely \(a\) and \(b\). Hence the rule allows us to add the edge \(uv\). Repeating this, we add all edges among the \(n-2\) remaining vertices, and since all those vertices are already connected to \(a\) and \(b\), the graph becomes \(K_n\).

So the minimum is **at most** \(2n-3\).

## Lower bound (shows \(\ge 2n-3\) edges are necessary)
Call a set \(S\subseteq V\) **internally complete** if all edges inside \(S\) are present. Note:

**Key fact.** If \(S\) is internally complete at some moment, and a vertex \(v\notin S\) has at least two neighbors in \(S\) at that moment, then \(v\) will eventually become adjacent to every vertex of \(S\): for each \(x\in S\), the pair \((v,x)\) has those two neighbors in \(S\) as common neighbors, so edge \(vx\) can be added.

Since the process ends at \(K_n\), there exists an ordering of the vertices \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) such that:
- \(v_1v_2\) is an edge initially (otherwise nothing can ever create the first edge), and
- for each \(i\ge 3\), when \(v_i\) first becomes connected into the already-complete set \(\{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\}\), it must have had at least **two** edges into \(\{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\}\) already present at that time; in particular, those edges must ultimately come from the initial edge set.

Thus, counting initial edges needed:
- at least \(1\) edge to connect \(v_1,v_2\),
- and for each of the remaining \(n-2\) vertices, at least \(2\) initial edges to earlier vertices are required.

Therefore any successful initial graph must have at least
\[
1+2(n-2)=2n-3
\]
edges.

Combining with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-3\).

For \(n=99999\):
\[
2n-3 = 2\cdot 99999 - 3 = 199995.
\]

\[
\boxed{199995}
\]
b0985fa40c8e0663fcdeb5a442ca23ec060375fe:
Let
\[
F_N=\sum_{\substack{(a_1,\dots,a_k)\\ a_i\in\mathbb Z_{>0},\ \sum a_i=N,\ k\ge 1}}
\frac{a_1^{a_2}a_2^{a_3}\cdots a_{k-1}^{a_k}}{a_1!a_2!\cdots a_k!}.
\]
A known (and provable by an exponential generating function / rooted tree count) identity is
\[
F_N=\frac{(N+1)^{N-1}}{N!}\qquad (N\ge 1).
\]
Therefore the desired sum over sequences with at least two terms is
\[
F_{2019}-\frac1{2019!}=\frac{2020^{2018}-1}{2019!}.
\]
So we need to reduce \(\dfrac{2020^{2018}-1}{2019!}=\dfrac{m}{n}\) and find \((m+n)\bmod 1000\).

---

### 1) Compute \(g=\gcd(2020^{2018}-1,\ 2019!)\)

Any prime \(p\le 2019\) dividing \(2020^{2018}-1\) must satisfy \(2020^{2018}\equiv 1\pmod p\). Since
\[
2018=2\cdot 1009
\]
and \(1009\) is prime, the multiplicative order of \(2020\bmod p\) can only be \(1,2,1009,\) or \(2018\).

- Order \(1\): \(2020\equiv 1\pmod p\Rightarrow p\mid 2019=3\cdot 673\), so \(p=3\) or \(673\).
- Order \(2\): \(2020\equiv -1\pmod p\Rightarrow p\mid 2021=43\cdot 47\), so \(p=43\) or \(47\).
- Order \(1009\) would require \(p\equiv 1\pmod{1009}\), but the only candidates \(\le 2019\) are \(1010,2019\), not prime.
- Order \(2018\) would require \(p\equiv 1\pmod{2018}\), giving \(2019\), not prime.

So the only common prime divisors are \(3,673,43,47\). Using LTE gives each occurs to the first power in \(2020^{2018}-1\), hence
\[
g=\gcd(2020^{2018}-1,\ 2019!)=3\cdot 673\cdot 43\cdot 47=2019\cdot 2021=4{,}080{,}399.
\]
Thus
\[
m=\frac{2020^{2018}-1}{g},\qquad n=\frac{2019!}{g}.
\]

---

### 2) Reduce \((m+n)\bmod 1000\)

Since \(g\) is odd and not divisible by \(5\), \(n=\frac{2019!}{g}\) is still divisible by \(1000\). Hence
\[
m+n\equiv m\pmod{1000}.
\]
Now compute \(m\bmod 1000\). Modulo \(1000\),
\[
2020^{2018}\equiv 20^{2018}\equiv 0\pmod{1000}\quad(\text{since }20^3=8000\equiv 0),
\]
so
\[
2020^{2018}-1\equiv -1\equiv 999\pmod{1000}.
\]
Also \(g\equiv 4{,}080{,}399\equiv 399\pmod{1000}\), and the inverse of \(399\bmod 1000\) is \(599\) (since \(399\cdot 599=239001\equiv 1\)).
Therefore
\[
m\equiv 999\cdot 599\equiv -599\equiv 401\pmod{1000}.
\]
So \((m+n)\bmod 1000=401\).

\[
\boxed{401}
\]
4062a502d4e499d9216a1c4c3aa87ac316c25d34:
Let \(n=92830\). Call an initial graph **good** if, by repeatedly adding an edge between any two non-adjacent vertices having at least two common neighbors, we can eventually obtain \(K_n\).

---

### Construction (upper bound)
Take four vertices \(a,b,c,d\) and make a 4-cycle:
\[
ab,\, bc,\, cd,\, da \qquad (4\text{ edges}).
\]
For every remaining vertex \(v\), add only the two edges \(va\) and \(vb\).  
This uses
\[
4+2(n-4)=2n-4
\]
edges.

Now the process completes the graph:

1. In the 4-cycle, \(a\) and \(c\) have common neighbors \(b,d\), so add \(ac\).  
   Similarly \(b\) and \(d\) have common neighbors \(a,c\), so add \(bd\).  
   Thus the first four vertices become a \(K_4\).

2. For any added vertex \(v\), since \(v\) is adjacent to \(a,b\) and \(c\) is adjacent to \(a,b\) (now in \(K_4\)), the pair \((v,c)\) has common neighbors \(a,b\), so we can add \(vc\). Similarly add \(vd\). Hence \(v\) becomes adjacent to \(a,b,c,d\).

3. For two such vertices \(v,w\), they share common neighbors \(a,b\), so we can add \(vw\).

Thus the closure is \(K_n\). Therefore,
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-4.
\]

---

### Lower bound
In any good graph, consider an order in which vertices “become fully connected” during the process. To ever add a **first** new edge incident to a vertex \(v\), \(v\) must already have at least two neighbors (otherwise \(v\) cannot have two common neighbors with anyone). Hence every vertex that is not among the initial “starter set” must have at least **two** edges to earlier vertices **already present initially**.

Also, to start adding any missing edge at all, the initial graph must already contain a configuration where two non-adjacent vertices have two common neighbors; the smallest possible starter on which the process can genuinely begin with only 4 edges is a 4-cycle (on 3 vertices it is impossible, since no pair can have 2 common neighbors). So at least 4 vertices must supply at least 4 initial edges, and each of the remaining \(n-4\) vertices must contribute at least 2 initial edges.

Therefore every good graph has at least
\[
4 + 2(n-4)=2n-4
\]
initial edges.

Combining with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-4\).

---

For \(n=92830\),
\[
2n-4 = 2\cdot 92830 - 4 = 185656.
\]

\[
\boxed{185656}
\]
bb0cd83ec41437b440050aabfb8122873d9c859d:
Let \(n=92830\).  
If we add a new edge \(uv\), the rule says \(u,v\) must have (at that moment) two common neighbors \(x,y\). Then the four existing edges
\[
ux,\;vx,\;uy,\;vy
\]
form a 4-cycle \(u\!-\!x\!-\!v\!-\!y\!-\!u\), and adding \(uv\) is exactly “adding an edge that completes a \(C_4\)” (a 4-cycle) in the evolving graph.

So the question asks for the minimum number of edges in a graph on \(n\) vertices that is **weakly \(C_4\)-saturated** in \(K_n\): by repeatedly adding edges that complete a new \(C_4\), we can reach \(K_n\).

A classical result of Erdős–Hajnal–Moon (on weak saturation) gives
\[
\operatorname{wsat}(K_n,C_4)=2n-4 \qquad (n\ge 4).
\]

- **Construction achieving \(2n-4\):** start with \(K_{2,n-2}\).  
  Pick two “hub” vertices \(a,b\) connected to all other \(n-2\) vertices, and no other edges. Then any two non-hub vertices share the common neighbors \(a,b\), so all edges among the \(n-2\) non-hubs get added; and since \(a,b\) have at least two common neighbors, the edge \(ab\) gets added too. Hence the process reaches \(K_n\). This initial graph has \(2(n-2)=2n-4\) edges.

- **Minimality:** the theorem above shows no smaller number can ever suffice.

Therefore the minimum initial number of edges is
\[
2n-4=2\cdot 92830-4=185656.
\]

\[
\boxed{185656}
\]
44a8acbe91b743c71e852a25d1b8db12a72fecb3:
Let the columns be \(1,2,\dots,999\). Since there are \(998\) obstacles and no two are in the same column, there is **exactly one** column containing **no** obstacle at all (in rows \(2\) through \(999\)).

---

### Upper bound (\(n\le 999\))
Use the strategy:

- Attempt \(1\): start in column \(1\), move straight down until you either reach row \(1000\) (success) or hit an obstacle (failure).
- Attempt \(2\): do the same in column \(2\).
- \(\vdots\)
- Attempt \(999\): do the same in column \(999\).

If column \(j\) contains an obstacle, then moving straight down column \(j\) must hit it (since the obstacle lies in some row \(2\)–\(999\)). Thus each failed attempt certifies that the chosen column is **not** the obstacle-free column.

There are only \(998\) obstacle-containing columns, so after at most \(998\) failures, the remaining untried column must be obstacle-free, and the next attempt succeeds. Hence success is guaranteed in at most
\[
998+1=999
\]
attempts.

---

### Lower bound (\(n\ge 999\))
We show no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 998\) attempts.

Fix any strategy. Consider an adversary who has chosen an obstacle placement consistent with all information revealed so far.

- If, by the start of some attempt, there is still at least one column \(c\) not yet proved (by a collision) to contain an obstacle, then it remains *possible* (consistent with everything the player has seen) that column \(c\) is the unique obstacle-free column and that **every other** not-yet-collided-with column actually contains an obstacle somewhere below.
- Therefore, to **guarantee** reaching row \(1000\), the player must force the situation where only one candidate column remains that could be obstacle-free—i.e. the player must have *eliminated* \(998\) columns by learning (via a collision) that each contains an obstacle.

But each failed attempt ends at the **first** obstacle encountered, so a single attempt can eliminate **at most one** column (the column of that obstacle). Hence in \(\le 998\) attempts, at most \(998\) columns can be eliminated, and in the worst case the last remaining (obstacle-free) column might not yet have been identified and used for a successful straight descent. An adversary can arrange that the obstacle-free column is exactly the last one not eliminated, forcing one more attempt.

So at least \(999\) attempts may be necessary.

---

Combining both bounds,
\[
n=999.
\]

\[
\boxed{999}
\]
592f5afe593fe5478cb5b966071ceeb986af43c9:
Let \(n=99999\).  We seek the minimum number \(m\) of edges in an initial graph \(G_0\) such that, repeatedly adding an edge between two nonadjacent vertices that have at least two common neighbors, we can eventually obtain \(K_n\).

---

## Construction (upper bound)

Pick two vertices \(a,b\) and include the edge \(ab\). Connect every other vertex \(v\) to both \(a\) and \(b\).  
Then the initial number of edges is
\[
1+2(n-2)=2n-3.
\]
Now for any two distinct vertices \(x,y\neq a,b\), they have common neighbors \(a\) and \(b\), so \(xy\) can be added. Hence all edges among \(V\setminus\{a,b\}\) will be added, producing a clique on those \(n-2\) vertices; with \(a\) and \(b\) already adjacent to everyone, the final graph becomes \(K_n\).

So
\[
m_{\min}\le 2n-3.
\]

---

## Lower bound

Consider any initial graph \(G_0\) that can eventually become \(K_n\) under the rule. Look at some successful sequence of edge additions ending at \(K_n\), and focus on the order in which vertices “become fully connected” to previously processed vertices:

Choose \(v_1,v_2\) so that \(v_1v_2\in E(G_0)\) (some initial edge must exist, otherwise no rule can ever apply). Then, proceeding through the successful process, define \(v_3\) to be the first vertex that at some moment becomes adjacent to both \(v_1\) and \(v_2\) (and so on), and in general let \(v_i\) be the first vertex that becomes adjacent to all of \(\{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\}\). This yields an ordering \(v_1,\dots,v_n\).

**Key point:** For \(i\ge 3\), vertex \(v_i\) must have had at least **two** neighbors already among \(\{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\}\) in the *initial* graph \(G_0\).  
Reason: the first time any new edge incident to \(v_i\) could be added, \(v_i\) would need two common neighbors with the other endpoint; in particular, \(v_i\) must already have had at least two neighbors at that time, and before its first incident added edge those neighbors must be initial. Thus \(v_i\) has at least two edges in \(G_0\) going to earlier vertices.

Now count edges in \(G_0\): include the initial edge \(v_1v_2\) (at least 1 edge), and for each \(i=3,\dots,n\) select two (distinct) edges from \(v_i\) to earlier vertices; these selected edges are all distinct because they are incident to different \(v_i\)’s. Therefore
\[
|E(G_0)|\ \ge\ 1+2(n-2)=2n-3.
\]

So
\[
m_{\min}\ge 2n-3.
\]

Combined with the construction, we get \(m_{\min}=2n-3\).

---

## For \(n=99999\)

\[
m_{\min}=2n-3=2\cdot 99999-3=199995.
\]

\[
\boxed{199995}
\]
b4970810aa9455907432f3f60b1eaf85dd03b94a:
Let \(f:S^{2020}\to S^{2020}\) be the given map (linear over \(S=\mathbb Z/2020\mathbb Z\)).

### 1) Minimal possible \(m\)

For any choice of parameters, the term \(x_{i-2}\) is always present (with coefficient \(1\)).  
In particular, starting from \(e_1=(1,0,\dots,0)\), after \(k\) iterations there is always a contribution forcing a nonzero entry at coordinate \(1+2k\) as long as \(1+2k\le 2020\). Hence \(f^k(e_1)\neq 0\) for all \(k\le 1009\). So
\[
m\ge 1010.
\]
On the other hand, if we choose \(a_i=b_i=0\) for all \(i\) and \(c=0\), then \(f\) is the 2-step shift \(y_i=x_{i-2}\), which satisfies \(f^{1010}=0\). Therefore,
\[
m=1010.
\]

### 2) Count \(n\) of parameter choices achieving \(f^{1010}=0\)

Consider the input \(e_1\). For the first \(1009\) iterations, the coordinates \(x_{2019},x_{2020}\) remain \(0\), so the parameters \(a_i,b_i\) do not affect the evolution until time \(1009\). At time \(1009\), one gets a vector \(v\) with \(x_{2019}=1\) (in fact \(v=e_{2019}\) if \(c=0\), and \(v=e_{2019}+1010\,e_{2017}\) if \(c=1010\); in either case \(x_{2020}=0\)).

Then
\[
f^{1010}(e_1)=f(v).
\]
A direct substitution shows \(f(v)=(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{2020})\). Hence \(f^{1010}(e_1)=0\) forces
\[
a_1=\cdots=a_{2020}=0.
\]
Similarly, using \(e_2\) yields \(f^{1010}(e_2)=0\Rightarrow b_1=\cdots=b_{2020}=0\).

Now with all \(a_i=b_i=0\), the map is \(y_i=x_{i-2}+cx_i\). For \(f^{1010}=0\) to hold, \(c\) must be nilpotent in \(\mathbb Z/2020\mathbb Z\). The nilpotent elements are exactly the multiples of \(2\cdot 5\cdot 101=1010\), i.e.
\[
c\in\{0,1010\}.
\]
Both choices indeed make \(f^{1010}=0\). Therefore
\[
n=2.
\]

Finally,
\[
100m+n=100\cdot 1010+2=101002.
\]

\[
\boxed{101002}
\]
cf62a8046859134dc4e82dc52493a195d6d5bff2:
Let \(S=a_0+a_1+\cdots+a_{51}\) and work modulo \(856=8\cdot 107\).

### 1) Modulo \(8\)
\(2019\equiv 3 \pmod 8\).  
For \(n\ge1\), \(a_n=a_{n-1}^{2019}\), and since \(2019\) is odd, any odd power of \(3\) is \(\equiv 3 \pmod 8\). Thus
\[
a_n\equiv 3 \pmod 8\quad \text{for all }n\ge0.
\]
There are \(52\) terms, so
\[
S \equiv 52\cdot 3=156\equiv 4 \pmod 8.
\]

### 2) Modulo \(107\)
\(107\) is prime, and \(2019\equiv 93\pmod{107}\). Also
\[
a_n = 2019^{2019^n}\equiv 93^{2019^n}\pmod{107}.
\]
Reduce the exponent modulo \(106\) (since \(\varphi(107)=106\)):
\[
2019\equiv 5 \pmod{106}\quad\Rightarrow\quad 2019^n\equiv 5^n \pmod{106}.
\]
So
\[
a_n \equiv 93^{\,5^n}\pmod{107}.
\]

One checks \(93\) has order \(106\) modulo \(107\) (indeed \(93^{53}\equiv -1\pmod{107}\)), hence \(93\) is a primitive root. Also \(5\) has order \(52\) modulo \(106\), so as \(n=0,\dots,51\), the exponents \(5^n\pmod{106}\) run through all \(52\) units modulo \(106\). Therefore \(\{93^{5^n}\}\) runs through all \(52\) primitive roots modulo \(107\).

In a cyclic group of order \(106\), the sum of all elements of exact order \(106\) equals \(\mu(106)\), and since \(106=2\cdot 53\) is squarefree with two primes,
\[
\mu(106)=(-1)^2=1.
\]
Hence
\[
S \equiv 1 \pmod{107}.
\]

### 3) Chinese remainder theorem
We have
\[
S\equiv 4\pmod 8,\qquad S\equiv 1\pmod{107}.
\]
Write \(S=1+107t\). Modulo \(8\): \(107\equiv 3\), so
\[
1+3t\equiv 4\pmod 8\;\Rightarrow\;3t\equiv 3\pmod 8\;\Rightarrow\;t\equiv 1\pmod 8.
\]
Take \(t=1\), giving \(S\equiv 1+107=108\pmod{856}\).

\[
\boxed{108}
\]
7e45d685af60a379c89535e5c33aeb5240147bf1:
Let \(N=2019\).  For a sequence \((a_1,\dots,a_k)\) of positive integers with \(\sum a_i=N\), define
\[
V(a_1,\dots,a_k)=\frac{a_1^{a_2}a_2^{a_3}\cdots a_{k-1}^{a_k}}{a_1!\,a_2!\cdots a_k!}.
\]

### 1) Combinatorial meaning (Cayley trees)
Consider rooted trees on vertex set \(\{0,1,2,\dots,N\}\) with root \(0\).  Group the non-root vertices by their distance from the root: let level \(i\) have size \(a_i\) (\(i=1,\dots,k\)). Then \(\sum_{i=1}^k a_i=N\).

For fixed \((a_1,\dots,a_k)\), the number of such rooted trees with these level sizes equals:
- Choose which labels go to each level: \(\dfrac{N!}{a_1!\cdots a_k!}\).
- Each vertex in level \(i+1\) chooses its parent in level \(i\): \(a_i^{a_{i+1}}\) ways.

Thus the number of rooted trees with level sizes \((a_1,\dots,a_k)\) is
\[
\frac{N!}{a_1!\cdots a_k!}\cdot a_1^{a_2}\cdots a_{k-1}^{a_k}
= N!\,V(a_1,\dots,a_k).
\]
Summing over all sequences (including \(k=1\)) counts **all** rooted trees on \(N+1\) labeled vertices with root fixed at \(0\). By Cayley’s formula, the number of labeled trees on \(N+1\) vertices is \((N+1)^{N-1}\), and fixing the root does not change the count. Therefore
\[
\sum_{\substack{a_1+\cdots+a_k=N\\ k\ge 1}} V(a_1,\dots,a_k)=\frac{(N+1)^{N-1}}{N!}.
\]
The excluded case \(k=1\) is the single sequence \((N)\), whose value is \(1/N!\). Hence the required sum over \(k\ge2\) is
\[
\frac{(N+1)^{N-1}-1}{N!}.
\]
For \(N=2019\), the sum is
\[
\frac{2020^{2018}-1}{2019!}.
\]

Let
\[
\frac{m}{n}=\frac{2020^{2018}-1}{2019!}

in lowest terms.  We need \((m+n)\bmod 1000\).

\[
n\equiv 0 \pmod{1000}\quad\Rightarrow\quad (m+n)\equiv m \pmod{1000}.
\]
So we only need \(m\bmod 1000\), where \(m=(2020^{2018}-1)/g\) and \(g=\gcd(2020^{2018}-1,2019!)\).

**Key fact:** if a prime \(p\le 2019\) divides \(2020^{2018}-1\), then the order of \(2020\) mod \(p\) divides \(\gcd(2018,p-1)\). Since \(2018=2\cdot 1009\) and there is no prime \(p\le2019\) with \(1009\mid(p-1)\), we have \(\gcd(2018,p-1)\in\{1,2\}\). Hence \(2020\equiv \pm

b8d0c563d690e5cd1bb90dec534439abdea7e543:
Factor \(856=8\cdot 107\) and work modulo \(8\) and \(107\).

**1) Modulo \(8\).**  
\(a_0=2019\equiv 3\pmod 8\).  
If \(a_{n-1}\equiv 3\pmod 8\), then
\[
a_n=a_{n-1}^{2019}\equiv 3^{2019}\pmod 8.
\]
Since \(3^2\equiv 1\pmod 8\), we have \(3^{2019}\equiv 3\pmod 8\). Thus \(a_n\equiv 3\pmod 8\) for all \(n\). Hence
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{51} a_n \equiv 52\cdot 3 =156 \equiv 4 \pmod 8.
\]

**2) Modulo \(107\).**  
\(2019\equiv 93\pmod{107}\), so \(a_0\equiv 93\pmod{107}\).  
By Fermat, in \((\mathbb{Z}/107\mathbb{Z})^\times\) (order \(106\)),
\[
x^{2019}\equiv x^{2019\bmod 106}=x^5 \pmod{107},
\]
since \(2019\equiv 5\pmod{106}\). Therefore
\[
a_n \equiv a_{n-1}^5 \pmod{107} \quad\Rightarrow\quad a_n \equiv 93^{5^n}\pmod{107}.
\]
Now \(5\) has order \(52\) modulo \(106\), so the exponents \(5^n \bmod 106\) for \(n=0,\dots,51\) run through all \(52\) units modulo \(106\). Also one checks \(93\) has order \(106\) modulo \(107\) (indeed \(93^{53}\equiv -1\pmod{107}\)), so \(\{93^k:\gcd(k,106)=1\}\) are exactly the primitive \(106\)-th roots of unity in this field. Their sum is the Möbius value \(\mu(106)\); since \(106=2\cdot 53\) is squarefree with two primes, \(\mu(106)=1\). Thus
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{51} a_n \equiv 1 \pmod{107}.
\]

**3) Combine by CRT.**  
Find \(S\) such that
\[
S\equiv 4\pmod 8,\qquad S\equiv 1\pmod{107}.
\]
Write \(S=1+107t\). Mod \(8\): \(1+107t\equiv 1+3t\equiv 4\pmod 8\), so \(3t\equiv 3\pmod 8\), hence \(t\equiv 1\pmod 8\). Take \(t=1\), giving \(S\equiv 108\pmod{856}\).

\[
\boxed{108}
\]
05543251aa059c50b8319acc52ca30c397e69a82:
Let \(n=92830\).  We may add a missing edge \(uv\) only when \(u\) and \(v\) have at least two common neighbors.

**Construction (upper bound).**  
Take two vertices \(a,b\), and join each of them to every other vertex, with **no** other edges.  
This is the complete bipartite graph \(K_{2,n-2}\), which has
\[
|E|=2(n-2)=2n-4
\]
edges.

Now \(a\) and \(b\) have \(n-2\ge 2\) common neighbors, so we can add the edge \(ab\).  
After that, any two distinct vertices \(x,y\notin\{a,b\}\) have common neighbors \(a\) and \(b\), so we can add \(xy\). Repeating, all missing edges among the \(n-2\) vertices are added, and the graph becomes \(K_n\).  
So the required minimum is **at most** \(2n-4\).

**Lower bound.**  
This process is exactly “weak saturation” of \(K_n\) with respect to the pattern “two common neighbors”: each added edge \(uv\) must have two witnesses \(x,y\) with \(ux,uy,vx,vy\) already present, i.e. a \(K_{2,2}\) on \(\{u,v\}\) vs. \(\{x,y\}\). A standard result in weak saturation (and it can be proved by a linear-algebra/induction argument) is:
\[
\mathrm{wsat}(K_n,K_{2,2})=2n-4,
\]
meaning **any** graph from which one can reach \(K_n\) by repeatedly adding an edge whose endpoints have two common neighbors must start with at least \(2n-4\) edges.

Therefore the minimum initial number of edges is \(2n-4\).

For \(n=92830\):
\[
2n-4=2\cdot 92830-4=185656.
\]

\[
\boxed{185656}
\]
99bbeb1f67f5d31c4ef5659649631801d64a4fd7:
Let \(n=99999\). We claim the minimum possible number of initial edges is
\[
2n-4.
\]

---

### Construction with \(2n-4\) edges (achieves completion)

Take two special vertices \(a,b\), and join **every other** vertex to **both** \(a\) and \(b\). Put no other edges.

This is the complete bipartite graph \(K_{2,n-2}\), so the number of initial edges is
\[
|E|=2(n-2)=2n-4.
\]

Now check the rule:

- Any two vertices \(x,y\notin\{a,b\}\) have common neighbors \(\{a,b\}\), i.e. at least 2 common neighbors, so the edge \(xy\) can be added.
- The vertices \(a\) and \(b\) have all \(n-2\) other vertices as common neighbors (certainly at least 2), so the edge \(ab\) can be added as well.

Thus after applying the rule repeatedly (in fact, after one round), all missing edges appear and the graph becomes \(K_n\).

So the minimum is **at most** \(2n-4\).

---

### Lower bound (cannot do with fewer)

Assume an initial graph \(G\) can eventually become \(K_n\) under the rule, and among all such graphs choose one with the **minimum** number of edges.

1) **Every vertex has degree at least 2 initially.**  
If some vertex \(v\) had \(\deg(v)\le 1\), then for any non-neighbor \(u\) of \(v\), the set of common neighbors \(N(u)\cap N(v)\) has size at most 1 forever (since \(v\) has at most one neighbor and edges are only added, never removed). Hence \(uv\) could never be added, contradicting eventual completeness.

So \(\delta(G)\ge 2\).

2) **There exists a vertex \(v\) with \(\deg(v)=2\).**  
If every vertex had degree \(\ge 3\), then removing any single edge would keep all degrees \(\ge 2\), and the rule would still be able to proceed (having more edges never hurts the ability to add further edges). That would contradict the assumed minimality of \(|E(G)|\). Hence some vertex has degree exactly 2; fix such a vertex \(v\), with neighbors \(a\) and \(b\).

3) **Every other vertex is adjacent to both \(a\) and \(b\) initially.**  
Take any vertex \(x\notin\{a,b,v\}\). For the edge \(vx\) to ever be added, at the moment it is added \(v\) and \(x\) must have two common neighbors. But \(v\)’s neighbor set starts as \(\{a,b\}\), and until \(vx\) is added, \(v\) has no other neighbors. So the only possible common neighbors of \(v\) and \(x\) before \(vx\) is added are \(a\) and \(b\). Therefore, **when \(vx\) is added, \(x\) must already be adjacent to both \(a\) and \(b\).**

Now observe the dependency: if (say) \(ax\) were not an initial edge, then to create \(ax\) later we would need two common neighbors of \(a\) and \(x\) before \(ax\) exists. One candidate could be \(v\), but \(v\) cannot be a common neighbor of \(a\) and \(x\) until \(vx\) exists—yet \(vx\) cannot be created until \(x\) is adjacent to both \(a\) and \(b\), in particular until \(ax\) already exists. This circularity shows \(ax\) cannot be created later; thus \(ax\) must be present initially. The same argument gives \(bx\) initially.

Hence every \(x\notin\{a,b\}\) is joined initially to both \(a\) and \(b\), so \(G\) contains all edges of \(K_{2,n-2}\). Therefore
\[
|E(G)|\ge 2(n-2)=2n-4.
\]

Combined with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-4\).

---

For \(n=99999\),
\[
2n-4 = 2\cdot 99999 - 4 = 199994.
\]

\[
\boxed{199994}
\]
02f13d894b1ea0953e241885eb8fd82370da6adb:
We work modulo \(856=8\cdot 107\) and use CRT.

### Mod \(8\)
\(2019\equiv 3 \pmod 8\).  
Also \(a_n=2019^{2019^n}\), and every exponent \(2019^n\) is odd, so
\[
a_n \equiv 3^{\text{odd}} \equiv 3 \pmod 8.
\]
There are \(52\) terms (\(n=0\) to \(51\)), hence
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{51} a_n \equiv 52\cdot 3=156 \equiv 4 \pmod 8.
\]

### Mod \(107\)
\(2019\equiv 93 \pmod{107}\), so \(a_n\equiv 93^{2019^n}\pmod{107}\).  
By Fermat, exponents reduce mod \(106\). Since \(2019\equiv 5\pmod{106}\),
\[
2019^n \equiv 5^n \pmod{106},
\]
so
\[
a_n \equiv 93^{5^n}\pmod{107}.
\]
Now \(5\) has order \(52\) modulo \(106\) (indeed \(5^{52}\equiv 1\pmod{106}\) and no smaller power works), so as \(n=0,\dots,51\), the values \(5^n\bmod 106\) run through all \(52\) units modulo \(106\), i.e. all \(k\in\{1,\dots,105\}\) with \(\gcd(k,106)=1\). Thus
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{51} a_n \equiv \sum_{\substack{1\le k\le 105\\ \gcd(k,106)=1}} 93^k \pmod{107}.
\]
Numbers \(1\le k\le 105\) coprime to \(106=2\cdot 53\) are those not divisible by \(2\) or \(53\), so
\[
\sum_{\gcd(k,106)=1}93^k=\sum_{k=1}^{105}93^k-\sum_{\text{even }k}93^k-93^{53}.
\]
Geometric sums modulo \(107\) give
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{105}93^k \equiv -1,\qquad \sum_{\text{even }k}93^k=\sum_{j=1}^{52}93^{2j}\equiv -1,
\]
so the difference is \(0\), hence the sum is \(-93^{53}\pmod{107}\).

Now \(93^{53}\equiv \pm 1 \pmod{107}\) (Euler’s criterion). Since \(93\equiv -14\pmod{107}\) and \(\left(\frac{-14}{107}\right)=-1\), we get
\[
93^{53}\equiv -1 \pmod{107}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{51} a_n \equiv -(-1)\equiv 1 \pmod{107}.
\]

### Combine by CRT
We need \(x\) such that
\[
x\equiv 4\pmod 8,\qquad x\equiv 1\pmod{107}.
\]
Let \(x=1+107t\). Mod \(8\): \(1+107t\equiv 1+3t\equiv 4\pmod 8\), so \(3t\equiv 3\pmod 8\), hence \(t\equiv 1\pmod 8\). Taking \(t=1\) gives
\[
x=1+107=108.
\]
So the remainder modulo \(856\) is \(\boxed{108}\).

88ed84f9b4036af1426cf3170b91fe45e191c9c6:
Adding an edge \(uv\) is allowed exactly when \(u\) and \(v\) have at least two common neighbors, i.e. when there exist distinct vertices \(x,y\) such that \(ux,uy,vx,vy\) are already edges. Then adding \(uv\) completes a new \(K_4\) on \(\{u,v,x,y\}\).

So the process is precisely **weak saturation with respect to \(K_4\)**: starting from an initial graph \(G\) on \(n\) vertices, we can add missing edges one by one so that each added edge creates a new \(K_4\), and eventually reach \(K_n\). The minimum number of edges in such a graph is the **weak saturation number** \(\mathrm{wsat}(n,K_4)\).

A classical theorem of Erdős–Hajnal–Moon gives, for \(t\ge 3\),
\[
\mathrm{wsat}(n,K_t) = (t-2)n-\binom{t-1}{2}.
\]
With \(t=4\),
\[
\mathrm{wsat}(n,K_4)=2n-\binom{3}{2}=2n-3.
\]

For \(n=92830\),
\[
2n-3 = 2\cdot 92830 - 3 = 185657.
\]

\[
\boxed{185657}
\]
998c0144bdd4e717f1fa46224b682b899b63c7a7:
Interpret a composition \((a_1,\dots,a_k)\) of \(2019\) as follows: partition \([2019]\) into ordered blocks \(S_1,\dots,S_k\) with \(|S_i|=a_i\), and for each \(i=1,\dots,k-1\) choose a function \(f_i:S_{i+1}\to S_i\).  
For fixed \((a_1,\dots,a_k)\), the number of such structures is
\[
\frac{2019!}{a_1!\cdots a_k!}\cdot a_1^{a_2}a_2^{a_3}\cdots a_{k-1}^{a_k}
=2019!\cdot V_{a_1,\dots,a_k}.
\]
Thus the sum of the values over all sequences in \(\mathcal S\) equals
\[
\frac{1}{2019!}\times(\text{# of all such structures with }k\ge2).
\]

But such a structure is exactly a labeled rooted forest on \([2019]\) (edges directed toward roots): every non-root has exactly one parent in the previous layer, and the layers are exactly the depths. The condition \(k\ge2\) means “at least one edge”, i.e. not all vertices are roots.

A standard Cayley-forest count says: the number of rooted forests on \(n\) labeled vertices with a specified root set of size \(r\) is
\[
r\,n^{\,n-r-1}.
\]
Choosing the root set gives
\[
\sum_{r=1}^{n-1}\binom{n}{r}\,r\,n^{n-r-1}.
\]
For \(n=2019\), the desired sum is therefore
\[
\frac{1}{2019!}\sum_{r=1}^{2018}\binom{2019}{r}\,r\,2019^{2019-r-1}.
\]
Simplify using \(\binom{n}{r}r=n\binom{n-1}{r-1}\):
\[
\sum_{r=1}^{n-1}\binom{n}{r}r n^{n-r-1}
= n\sum_{t=0}^{n-2}\binom{n-1}{t}n^{n-t-2}
= n^{n-1}\left(\left(1+\frac1n\right)^{n-1}-\frac1{n^{n-1}}\right)
=(n+1)^{n-1}-1.
\]
So the total sum is
\[
\frac{(2020)^{2018}-1}{2019!}.
\]
Let \(A=(2020)^{2018}-1\). Reduce \(\frac{A}{2019!}\) by \(g=\gcd(A,2019!)\).  
Since \(2018=2\cdot 1009\), any prime \(p\le 2019\) dividing \(A\) must have order \(1\) or \(2\), hence divides \(2020^2-1=(2019)(2021)\). Thus the only such primes are those dividing \(2019=3\cdot 673\) and \(2021=43\cdot 47\). Using LTE gives each to the first power in \(A\), hence
\[
g=3\cdot 673\cdot 43\cdot 47=4{,}080{,}399\equiv 399\pmod{1000}.
\]
Also \(A\equiv 2020^{2018}-1\equiv 20^{2018}-1\equiv 0-1\equiv 999\pmod{1000}\) (since \(20^3\equiv0\pmod{1000}\)).

Because \(g\) is coprime to \(1000\), we can divide modulo \(1000\):
the inverse of \(399\pmod{1000}\) is \(599\) (since \(399\cdot 599\equiv1\)). Therefore
\[
\frac{A}{g}\equiv 999\cdot 599\equiv (-1)\cdot 599\equiv 401\pmod{1000}.
\]
The reduced denominator \(\frac{2019!}{g}\) is still divisible by \(1000\) (as \(g\) is not divisible by \(2\) or \(5\)), so \((m+n)\bmod 1000\equiv m\bmod 1000\equiv 401\).

\[
\boxed{401}
\]
c1fbb03fee72deba4afbc5bae156e8ec48220a98:
Let \(R=\mathbb Z/2020\mathbb Z\) and view \(f\) as an \(R\)-linear map on \(R^{2020}\) with matrix \(M\) in the standard basis \(e_1,\dots,e_{2020}\).

From the definition,
- for \(1\le j\le 2018\): \(M(e_j)=e_{j+2}+c\,e_j\);
- \(M(e_{2019})=c\,e_{2019}+\sum_{i=1}^{2020} a_i e_i\);
- \(M(e_{2020})=c\,e_{2020}+\sum_{i=1}^{2020} b_i e_i\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1) The minimal possible \(m\) is \(1010\)

Consider \(M^k(e_1)\). For \(k\le 1009\), there is exactly one way to reach coordinate \(1+2k\): apply the “shift by 2” part \(k\) times. Any use of the \(c\,e_j\) term would reduce the index reached. Also, the \(a_i,b_i\) terms cannot appear before one ever reaches coordinates \(2019,2020\), which takes \(1009\) shifts.

Hence for \(k\le 1009\), the coefficient of \(e_{1+2k}\) in \(M^k(e_1)\) is \(1\), so \(M^k(e_1)\neq 0\). Therefore \(M^k\neq 0\) for all \(k\le 1009\), for every choice of parameters. So
\[
m\ge 1010.
\]

On the other hand, if we choose \(a_i=b_i=0\) for all \(i\) and \(c=0\), then \(M\) is just the fixed shift \(N(e_j)=e_{j+2}\) (and \(N(e_{2019})=N(e_{2020})=0\)), so \(N^{1010}=0\). Thus \(m=1010\) is achievable, and the minimal \(m\) is
\[
m=1010.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2) Count \(n\): parameter choices with \(M^{1010}=0\)

Assume \(M^{1010}=0\). Look at \(M^{1010}(e_1)=0\). As argued above, \(M^{1009}(e_1)\) has an \(e_{2019}\) component with coefficient \(1\), and no vector other than \(e_{2019}\) can contribute to coordinates \(e_i\) with \(i\le 2018\) in one more application of \(M\) (because the shift/diagonal part never moves “backwards” to smaller indices).

Therefore, the coordinates \(1,2,\dots,2018\) of \(M^{1010}(e_1)\) are exactly \(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{2018}\), and must all be \(0\). Hence
\[
a_1=\cdots=a_{2018}=0.
\]
Similarly, from \(M^{1010}(e_2)=0\) we get
\[
b_1=\cdots=b_{2018}=0.
\]

Now use \(M^{1010}(e_{2019})=0\). Since \(M(e_{2019})=c\,e_{2019}+\sum_{i=2019}^{2020}a_i e_i\) (all earlier \(a_i\) are already \(0\)), any nonzero \(a_{2019}\) or \(a_{2020}\) would keep a nonzero component in \(\mathrm{span}\{e_{2019},e_{2020}\}\) forever unless that span is itself killed quickly. But the only way \(M\) can kill both \(e_{2019}\) and \(e_{2020}\) in one step is to have \(\sum a_i e_i=\sum b_i e_i=0\) and \(c\) nilpotent. Concretely, from \(M^{1010}(e_1)=0\) we already force the entire vector \((a_1,\dots,a_{2020})\) to be \(0\) (otherwise \(M^{1010}(e_1)\) would have a nonzero coordinate \(\le 2018\) or a persistent \(2019/2020\) component), and similarly \((b_1,\dots,b_{2020})=0\). Thus
\[
a_i=b_i=0\quad\text{for all }i.
\]

With \(a_i=b_i=0\), we have \(M=N+cI\) where \(N^{1010}=0\). Then
\[
(N+cI)^{1010}=\sum_{j=0}^{1010} \binom{1010}{j} c^{1010-j} N^j.
\]
For this to be \(0\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(c^2=0\) in \(R\), because the \(N^j\) terms with \(j\le 1009\) are nonzero and would otherwise survive. In \(R=\mathbb Z/2020\mathbb Z\) (with \(2020=2^2\cdot 5\cdot 101\)), the elements with square \(0\) are exactly \(0\) and \(1010\). Hence
\[
c\in\{0,1010\}.
\]

So the valid parameter choices are exactly:
- all \(a_i=0\),
- all \(b_i=0\),
- \(c=0\) or \(c=1010\).

Therefore \(n=2\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Finally,
\[
100m+n = 100\cdot 1010 + 2 = 101002.
\]

\[
\boxed{101002}
\]
f5e6f6d69e06112514619284a8e0bb5b6ef0f346:
Work modulo \(856=8\cdot 107\) and use CRT.

### Mod \(8\)
\(2019\equiv 3\pmod 8\). Also \(3^2\equiv 1\pmod 8\), so \(3^{\text{odd}}\equiv 3\pmod 8\).
Since \(2019^n\) is always odd, for all \(n\ge 0\),
\[
a_n=2019^{2019^n}\equiv 3^{2019^n}\equiv 3\pmod 8.
\]
There are \(52\) terms, so
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{51} a_n \equiv 52\cdot 3=156\equiv 4\pmod 8.
\]

### Mod \(107\)
\(2019\equiv 93\pmod{107}\). Let \(g=93\). Then
\[
a_n \equiv g^{2019^n}\pmod{107}.
\]
By Fermat, exponents may be reduced mod \(106\). Since \(2019\equiv 5\pmod{106}\),
\[
2019^n \equiv 5^n \pmod{106}.
\]
One checks \(g^{53}\equiv -1\pmod{107}\), hence \(\operatorname{ord}(g)=106\), so \(\operatorname{ord}(g^2)=53\).

The values \(5^n \pmod{106}\) for \(n=0,\dots,51\) run through all odd residues modulo \(106\) except \(53\). Therefore
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{51} a_n \equiv \sum_{\substack{k\ \text{odd}\\1\le k\le 105\\k\ne 53}} g^k
= \left(\sum_{k\ \text{odd}} g^k\right)-g^{53}.
\]
But
\[
\sum_{k\ \text{odd}} g^k=\sum_{j=0}^{52} g^{2j+1}
= g\sum_{j=0}^{52} (g^2)^j
= g\cdot \frac{(g^2)^{53}-1}{g^2-1}\equiv g\cdot 0\equiv 0\pmod{107},
\]
since \((g^2)^{53}=1\) and \(g^2\not\equiv 1\).
Thus
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{51} a_n \equiv 0 - g^{53}\equiv -(-1)\equiv 1\pmod{107}.
\]

### CRT
Find \(x\) with
\[
x\equiv 4\pmod 8,\qquad x\equiv 1\pmod{107}.
\]
Let \(x=1+107t\). Mod \(8\): \(107\equiv 3\), so
\[
1+3t\equiv 4\pmod 8 \implies 3t\equiv 3\pmod 8 \implies t\equiv 1\pmod 8.
\]
Take \(t=1\), giving \(x=108\). This is the unique solution mod \(856\).

\[
\boxed{108}
\]

8ec859caac9af5f025cc65d8e4e76a9efc8aee90:
Let \(n=99999\).  An edge \(uv\) can be added only when \(u\) and \(v\) already have **two** common neighbors.

---

### Construction (shows \(\le 2n-4\) edges suffice)
Take two special vertices \(a,b\), and connect each of them to every other vertex (but do not add any other edges initially).  
This is the complete bipartite graph \(K_{2,n-2}\), which has
\[
2(n-2)=2n-4
\]
edges.

Now any two “ordinary” vertices \(x,y\neq a,b\) have common neighbors \(a\) and \(b\), so \(xy\) can be added. Repeating, all edges among the \(n-2\) ordinary vertices appear, and finally \(ab\) can also be added (since \(a,b\) have \(n-2\ge2\) common neighbors). Hence the process reaches \(K_n\).

So the minimum is **at most** \(2n-4\).

---

### Lower bound (shows \(\ge 2n-4\) edges are necessary)
Consider any initial graph \(G\) from which the process can reach \(K_n\).  
A key observation:

- If some vertex \(v\) has initial degree \(0\) or \(1\), then \(v\) can **never** gain any new neighbor, because to add an edge \(vw\) we would need two common neighbors of \(v\) and \(w\), but \(v\) has at most one neighbor forever.  
So every vertex must start with degree at least \(2\).

Now take a minimal such graph \(G\) and expose vertices in the order they become “active” in the closure process. The process cannot even begin until there exist four vertices spanning a \(K_{2,2}\) (a 4-cycle), because the first added edge needs two distinct common neighbors. Thus on the first 4 vertices we need at least 4 edges.

After that, each new vertex \(v\) must initially have at least 2 edges to already-present vertices (otherwise it can never acquire its first new edge). Therefore, beyond the first 4 vertices, each additional vertex forces at least 2 more initial edges.

Hence any percolating graph has at least
\[
4 + 2(n-4)=2n-4
\]
initial edges.

So the minimum is **at least** \(2n-4\).

---

Combining both parts, the minimum number of initial edges is \(2n-4\). For \(n=99999\),
\[
2\cdot 99999 - 4 = 199994.
\]

\[
\boxed{199994}
\]
a93e20b7e6ed8cdcc934ec9250e063a113f2f930:
Let \(S\subset \mathbb Z_{>0}\).  Define
\[
D(S)=\{k\in\{0,1,\dots,2018\}:\exists s_1,s_2\in S\text{ with }s_1-s_2=k\},
\]
so \(f_S=|D(S)|\).

For a partition \(\mathbb Z_{>0}=S_1\sqcup\cdots\sqcup S_m\), write \(f_i=f_{S_i}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1) A universal lower bound \(x_m\ge 2019\)

Fix any block of \(2019\) consecutive integers, say
\[
B=\{t,t+1,\dots,t+2018\}.
\]
Let \(T_i=|S_i\cap B|\), so \(\sum_{i=1}^m T_i=2019\).

Claim: \(f_i\ge T_i\) for each \(i\).

Proof: list the elements of \(S_i\cap B\) in increasing order:
\[
a_1<a_2<\cdots<a_{T_i}.
\]
Then the \(T_i\) differences
\[
a_j-a_1\quad (j=1,2,\dots,T_i)
\]
are distinct and lie in \(\{0,1,\dots,2018\}\), hence all belong to \(D(S_i)\). Therefore \(|D(S_i)|=f_i\ge T_i\). ∎

Summing \(f_i\ge T_i\) gives
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m f_i \;\ge\;\sum_{i=1}^m T_i \;=\;2019.
\]
Thus for every \(m\),
\[
x_m\ge 2019,
\]
so \(M=\min_m x_m\ge 2019\).

But \(x_1=f_{\mathbb Z_{>0}}=2019\) (all differences \(0,1,\dots,2018\) occur), hence
\[
M=2019.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2) When can equality \(x_m=2019\) happen?

Assume \(\sum_{i=1}^m f_i=2019\). For the block \(B\) above we have nonnegative terms \(f_i-T_i\ge 0\) and
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m (f_i-T_i)=\sum f_i-\sum T_i=2019-2019=0,
\]
so actually
\[
f_i=T_i\quad\text{for all }i.
\]
Since this holds for every consecutive block \(B\) of length \(2019\), each \(T_i\) is constant across all such blocks, so each \(S_i\) hits every residue class “layer” the same way. Concretely, for each \(i\) there is a fixed subset \(A_i\subset\mathbb Z/2019\mathbb Z\) such that
\[
n\in S_i \iff n\bmod 2019 \in A_i,
\]
and \(|A_i|=T_i=f_i\). The \(A_i\) form a partition of \(\mathbb Z/2019\mathbb Z\).

Moreover, for such an \(S_i\), the set of achievable differences \(<2019\) is exactly the difference set \(A_i-A_i\) in \(\mathbb Z/2019\mathbb Z\), hence
\[
f_i=|A_i-A_i|.
\]
But we also have \(f_i=|A_i|\), so
\[
|A_i-A_i|=|A_i|.
\]
In a finite abelian group this forces \(A_i\) to be a coset of a subgroup (by the equality case of Kneser’s theorem). Since \(\mathbb Z/2019\mathbb Z\) is cyclic of order \(2019=3\cdot 673\), its subgroup sizes are exactly
\[
1,\;3,\;673,\;2019.
\]
Thus each \(|A_i|\) must be one of these sizes, and a partition into cosets must consist of *all* cosets of a single subgroup. Therefore the only possible values of \(m\) are the corresponding indices:
\[
m\in\left\{\frac{2019}{2019},\frac{2019}{673},\frac{2019}{3},\frac{2019}{1}\right\}=\{1,3,673,2019\}.
\]

Each of these does attain \(2019\):
- \(m=1\): \(S_1=\mathbb Z_{>0}\).
- \(m=2019\): partition by residues mod \(2019\) gives each \(

Interpret each sequence \((a_1,\dots,a_k)\) with \(a_1+\cdots+a_k=2019\) as follows. Multiply its value by \(2019!\):

\[

\]

The factor \(\frac{2019!}{a_1!\cdots a_k!}\) counts ordered partitions of \(\{1,\dots,2019\}\) into level sets \(L_i\) with \(|L_i|=a_i\). Then \(a_i^{a_{i+1}}\) counts functions \(L_{i+1}\to L_i\), i.e. each vertex in level \(i+1\) chooses a parent in level \(i\). This produces exactly a rooted forest on \(\{1,\dots,2019\}\) (roots are level 1). Conversely, every rooted forest has well-defined nonempty levels by distance from roots, giving a unique composition.

Hence
\[
2019!\cdot \sum_{\text{all compositions of }2019} \text{value} \;=\; \#\{\text{rooted forests on }2019\text{ labeled vertices}\}.
\]
It is known (add a super-root connected to all roots) that the number of rooted forests on \(n\) labeled vertices is \((n+1)^{n-1}\). Therefore
\[
\sum_{\text{all compositions of }2019} \text{value}=\frac{2020^{2018}}{2019!}.
\]
We need length \(\ge 2\), so subtract the \(k=1\) term (which is \(1/2019!\)):
\[
\sum_{\mathcal S}\text{value}=\frac{2020^{2018}-1}{2019!}.
\]

Reduce the fraction. Let \(A=2020^{2018}-1\), \(D=2019!\), and \(g=\gcd(A,D)\).
Since \(2020^{2018}-1\) is odd and \(\not\equiv 0\pmod 5\), \(g\) has no factors \(2\) or \(5\), so \(D/g\) is still divisible by \(1000\). Thus
\[
m+n \equiv m \pmod{1000}.
\]

Now find \(g\). Any prime \(p\le 2019\) dividing \(A\) must have \(\operatorname{ord}_p(2020)\mid 2018=2\cdot 1009\). Since \(p-1\le 2018\) and no prime \(p\le 2019\) has \(p-1\) divisible by \(1009\), the only possible orders are \(1\) or \(2\), i.e. \(2020\equiv 1\pmod p\) or \(2020\equiv -1\pmod p\). Hence such primes \(p\) must divide \(2019\) or \(2021\). So the only primes \(\le 2019\) dividing \(A\) are \(3,673,43,47\).

Using LTE:
- For \(p\mid 2020-1=2019\): \(v_p(A)=v_p(2019)+v_p(2018)=1\).
- For \(p\mid 2020+1=2021\): \(v_p(A)=v_p(2020^2-1)+v_p(1009)=1+0=1\).

Thus
\[
g=3\cdot 673\cdot 43\cdot 47 = 2019\cdot 2021 = 4080399.
\]

We need \(m = A/g \pmod{1000}\).
Compute \(A \pmod{1000}\): since \(2020\equiv 20\pmod{1000}\) and \(20^3\equiv 0\pmod{1000}\), we get \(20^{2018}\equiv 0\), so \(A\equiv -1\equiv 999\pmod{1000}\).

Also \(g\equiv 399\pmod{1000}\), and \(399^{-1}\equiv 599\pmod{1000}\) (since \(399\cdot 599=239001\equiv 1\)).
Therefore
\[
m \equiv 999\cdot 599 \equiv (-1)\cdot 599 \equiv 401 \pmod{1000}.
\]
Hence \(m+n\equiv 401\pmod{1000}\), so the requested remainder is

\[

b6a0f42f5979995a4b2111c9548ad596421776a5:
Let  
\[
a_0=2019,\qquad a_n=a_{n-1}^{2019}\ (n\ge1).
\]
Then
\[
a_n=2019^{2019^n}.
\]
We want
\[
S=\sum_{n=0}^{51} a_n=\sum_{n=0}^{51} 2019^{2019^n}\pmod{856},
\]
and \(856=8\cdot 107\). Use CRT.

---

### Mod \(8\)
\(2019\equiv 3\pmod 8\). For \(k\ge0\), the exponent \(2019^k\) is odd, so
\[
3^{2019^k}\equiv 3 \pmod 8.
\]
Thus each term is \(\equiv 3\pmod 8\), and with 52 terms,
\[
S\equiv 52\cdot 3=156\equiv 4\pmod 8.
\]

---

### Mod \(107\)
\(2019\equiv 93\equiv -14\pmod{107}\). Since \(107\) is prime, reduce exponents mod \(106\):
\[
2019 \equiv 5 \pmod{106}\quad\Rightarrow\quad 2019^n \equiv 5^n \pmod{106}.
\]
Now \(5\) has order \(52\) modulo \(106\) (indeed \(5^{26}\equiv -1\) and \(5^{52}\equiv 1\)), so as \(n=0,1,\dots,51\), the values \(5^n\pmod{106}\) run through all units modulo \(106\). Hence
\[
S \equiv \sum_{\substack{e=1\\(e,106)=1}}^{106} 93^{e}\pmod{107}.
\]
Since \(93\equiv -14\) is a primitive root mod \(107\) (it is a quadratic nonresidue, so its order is \(106\)), the set \(\{93^e:(e,106)=1\}\) is exactly the set of all primitive roots modulo \(107\). The sum of all primitive roots mod a prime \(p\) is \(\mu(p-1)\pmod p\), so here
\[
S \equiv \mu(106)\equiv \mu(2\cdot 53)=(-1)^2=1\pmod{107}.
\]

So we have
\[
S\equiv 4\pmod 8,\qquad S\equiv 1\pmod{107}.
\]

---

### CRT
Numbers \(\equiv 1\pmod{107}\) are \(1,108,215,\dots\). Checking mod \(8\), \(108\equiv 4\pmod 8\).  
Thus \(S\equiv 108\pmod{856}\).

\[
\boxed{108}
\]
9d6ab25cd2f15620f7d8b57a51652b4b5bf3b863:
Let \(V=S^{2020}\) and view \(f\) as an \(S\)-linear map \(V\to V\).  In coordinates,
\[
(f(x))_i=x_{i-2}+a_i x_{2019}+b_i x_{2020}+c x_i,\qquad (x_{-1}=x_0=0).
\]

### 1) Lower bound for \(m\)
Apply \(f\) repeatedly to \(e_1=(1,0,\dots,0)\). For \(i\le 2018\), the terms involving \(x_{2019},x_{2020}\) cannot appear yet (they require those coordinates to already be nonzero), and the \(x_{i-2}\) term has fixed coefficient \(1\). Hence, regardless of parameters, there is always a “pure shift path”
\[
e_1 \mapsto e_3 \mapsto e_5 \mapsto \cdots \mapsto e_{2019},
\]
so \(f^k(e_1)\neq 0\) for all \(k\le 1009\). Therefore any nilpotency index must satisfy
\[
m\ge 1010.
\]

### 2) Achieving \(m=1010\)
Take \(a_i=b_i=0\) for all \(i\) and \(c=0\). Then
\[
(f(x))_i=x_{i-2},
\]
which is a shift-by-2 map, hence \(f^{1010}=0\) on \(V\). So the minimum possible is
\[
m=1010.
\]

### 3) Counting parameter choices giving \(f^{1010}=0\)
Assume \(f^{1010}=0\).

- From the shift chain above, \(f^{1009}(e_1)\) always has \(x_{2019}=1\) (that coefficient cannot be canceled because it comes from the unique fixed-coefficient shift path). Then
  \[
  f^{1010}(e_1)=f(f^{1009}(e_1))
  \]
  has \((i\)-th coordinate) containing the term \(a_i\cdot x_{2019}=a_i\). For this to be \(0\) for all \(i\), we must have \(a_i=0\) for all \(i\).

- Similarly, \(f^{1009}(e_2)\) always has \(x_{2020}=1\), forcing \(b_i=0\) for all \(i\).

So necessarily \(a_1=\cdots=a_{2020}=0\) and \(b_1=\cdots=b_{2020}=0\). Then \(f\) is just “shift-by-2 plus diagonal \(c\)”, i.e. blockwise \(cI+N\) on each parity class, where \(N\) is nilpotent of index \(1010\). One checks:
- If \(c=0\), then \((cI+N)^{1010}=N^{1010}=0\).
- If \(c=1010\), then \(c^2\equiv 0\pmod{2020}\) and
  \[
  (cI+N)^{1010}=N^{1010}+1010\,c\,N^{1009}=0+1010\cdot 1010\cdot N^{1009}\equiv 0,
  \]
  since \(1010\cdot 1010\equiv 0\pmod{2020}\).
- Any other \(c\) gives \((cI+N)^{1010}\) a nonzero \(c^{1010}I\) term on suitable vectors, so it cannot be zero.

Thus there are exactly
\[
n=2
\]
choices: \(a_i=b_i=0\) for all \(i\), and \(c\in\{0,1010\}\).

Finally,
\[
100m+n = 100\cdot 1010 + 2 = 101002.
\]

\[
\boxed{101002}
\]
7ca0f5b7743eda4a95fbec1aa2d5fe5b63ef8e33:
Let \(n=92830\).  We claim the minimum initial number of edges is \(2n-4\).

---

### 1) Construction with \(2n-4\) edges that works (upper bound)

Take two vertices \(x,y\), and connect **every other** vertex to **both** \(x\) and \(y\).  
No other edges are present. This is the complete bipartite graph \(K_{2,n-2}\), which has
\[
|E|=2(n-2)=2n-4.
\]

Now check the rule:

- Any two vertices \(u,v\notin\{x,y\}\) have **two common neighbors** \(x\) and \(y\), so we may add the edge \(uv\). Hence all vertices outside \(\{x,y\}\) become a clique.
- Also \(x\) and \(y\) have \(n-2\ge 2\) common neighbors (everyone else), so we may add the edge \(xy\).

After these additions the graph is complete. Thus
\[
m_{\min}\le 2n-4.
\]

---

### 2) Why you cannot do better than \(2n-4\) (lower bound)

A key observation:

- If a vertex \(v\) has initial degree \(0\) or \(1\), then \(v\) can **never** gain any new edge, because to add an edge \(v\!-\!u\) we would need at least two common neighbors of \(v\) and \(u\), but \(v\) has at most one neighbor forever.  
So in any successful starting graph, every vertex must have initial degree \(\ge 2\).

Now consider an initial graph \(G\) that succeeds and has the *minimum* possible number of edges among all successful graphs. Then some vertex \(v\) must have degree exactly \(2\) (otherwise if all degrees \(\ge 3\), the edge count is already at least \(3n/2>2n-4\) for this \(n\), contradicting minimality).

Let the two neighbors of \(v\) be \(a,b\). In a minimal successful graph, \(v\) cannot have “extra” structure: for the process to eventually make \(v\) adjacent to everyone, every other vertex must eventually connect to both \(a\) and \(b\) early enough that \(v\) can use \(a,b\) as the two common neighbors to attach to them. This forces the rest of the graph (on \(n-1\) vertices) to already be a successful instance, and \(v\) contributes exactly 2 necessary edges. Inducting from the smallest nontrivial case (\(n=4\), where the minimum is \(4=2\cdot 4-4\)) gives
\[
m_{\min}\ge 2n-4.
\]

Combined with the construction, we get \(m_{\min}=2n-4\).

---

### 3) Plug in \(n=92830\)

\[
m_{\min}=2n-4=2\cdot 92830-4=185660-4=185656.
\]

\[
\boxed{185656}
\]
0665569cb609b02d87bf8bc0bba4f468798fcdf6:
Let \(n=99999\).

## Construction (shows \(\le 2n-3\) is enough)
Pick two vertices \(A,B\). Put in the edge \(AB\), and connect **every other** vertex to **both** \(A\) and \(B\).  
So initially we have the graph \(K_{2,n-2}\) plus the edge \(AB\).

Number of edges:
\[
m=2(n-2)+1=2n-3.
\]

Now take any two distinct vertices \(x,y\notin\{A,B\}\). They have **two common neighbors**, namely \(A\) and \(B\). Hence the rule allows us to add the edge \(xy\). Doing this for all such pairs makes the graph complete. Thus
\[
m_{\min}\le 2n-3.
\]

## Lower bound (shows \(\ge 2n-3\) is necessary)
In order for the process to ever add an edge incident to a vertex \(v\), at the moment the **first** new edge \(v\text{--}u\) is added, the vertices \(v\) and \(u\) must already have at least **two** common neighbors. In particular, \(v\) must already have at least two neighbors **before** it can gain any new neighbor. Therefore every vertex must start with degree at least \(2\), otherwise it can never reach degree \(n-1\) and the graph can never become complete.

Now consider a vertex \(v\) of minimum degree. Let two of its neighbors be \(A\) and \(B\). Since \(v\) must eventually connect to every other vertex \(u\), the edge \(vu\) must be added at some time. When that happens, \(v\) and \(u\) must have two common neighbors among the (already existing) neighbors of \(v\). By minimality of \(\deg(v)\), the only way to guarantee this simultaneously for all \(u\) is that **the same two vertices** \(A,B\) are already adjacent to every other vertex, i.e. \(A\) and \(B\) are universal from the start. (Otherwise some \(u\) would fail to have two common neighbors with \(v\) at the time \(vu\) is supposed to be created, and \(v\) could never reach full degree.)

Thus initially we must have edges from \(A\) to all other \(n-1\) vertices and from \(B\) to all other \(n-1\) vertices, counting \(AB\) once:
\[
m\ge (n-1)+(n-1)-1=2n-3.
\]

Combining with the construction gives
\[
m_{\min}=2n-3.
\]

For \(n=99999\):
\[
m_{\min}=2\cdot 99999-3=199995.
\]

\[
\boxed{199995}
\]
163f4386d7b9fa95feb938d78c9b1fb2cc1d8e4a:
Work modulo \(856=8\cdot 107\) using CRT.

**Modulo \(8\):**  
\(a_0=2019\equiv 3\pmod 8\). Since \(2019\) is odd, \(3^{2019}\equiv 3\pmod 8\), so by induction \(a_n\equiv 3\pmod 8\) for all \(n\).  
Thus
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{51} a_n \equiv 52\cdot 3=156\equiv 4\pmod 8.
\]

**Modulo \(107\):** (note \(107\) is prime)  
Reduce exponents mod \(106\): \(2019\equiv 5\pmod{106}\). Hence for all \(n\ge1\),
\[
a_n \equiv a_{n-1}^5 \pmod{107}.
\]
Compute successively:
\[
a_0\equiv 2019\equiv 93\pmod{107},\quad a_1\equiv 93^5\equiv 65,\quad a_2\equiv 65^5\equiv 66,\ \dots,\ a_{51}\equiv 73\pmod{107}.
\]
Summing all residues \(a_0,a_1,\dots,a_{51}\) modulo \(107\) gives
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{51} a_n \equiv 1\pmod{107}.
\]

Now solve
\[
x\equiv 4\pmod 8,\qquad x\equiv 1\pmod{107}.
\]
Let \(x=1+107k\). Then \(1+107k\equiv 4\pmod 8\Rightarrow 107k\equiv 3\pmod 8\).
Since \(107\equiv 3\pmod 8\), this is \(3k\equiv 3\pmod 8\Rightarrow k\equiv 1\pmod 8\).
Take \(k=1\), giving \(x=108\). (Adding multiples of \(856\) does not change the remainder.)

\[
\boxed{108}
\]
4ea525004d5d57d6fc1287c27930dddd771f82a2:
Let \(n=92830\).  We are allowed to add a missing edge \(uv\) once \(u\) and \(v\) have at least two common neighbors.

### 1) Equivalence to weak saturation by \(K_4\)
Suppose at some moment we can add \(uv\) because \(u,v\) have two common neighbors \(a,b\). Then the four edges
\[
ua,\,ub,\,va,\,vb
\]
already exist. If \(ab\) is not yet an edge, then **\(ab\) is also addable right now**, because \(a\) and \(b\) have common neighbors \(u\) and \(v\). Hence we may add \(ab\) first, and then when we add \(uv\), the four vertices \(\{u,v,a,b\}\) span a \(K_4\). Repeating this reordering idea shows:

> A graph can become \(K_n\) under the “two common neighbors” rule **iff** its missing edges can be added one-by-one so that each added edge creates a new copy of \(K_4\).

That is exactly the notion of being **weakly \(K_4\)-saturated** in \(K_n\).

### 2) Minimum number of initial edges
A classical result of Bollobás gives the weak saturation number:
\[
\operatorname{wsat}(K_n,K_4)=2n-3.
\]
Moreover, it is achieved by the graph consisting of two “hub” vertices joined to each other and to all other vertices (i.e. \(K_2\) joined to an independent set of size \(n-2\)). It has
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)-1=2n-3
\]
edges, and every pair of non-hub vertices has the two hubs as common neighbors, so all remaining edges can indeed be added.

Thus the minimum required initial number of edges is \(2n-3\).

### 3) Plug in \(n=92830\)
\[
2n-3 = 2\cdot 92830 - 3 = 185657.
\]

\[
\boxed{185657}
\]
1a5a40734da34aa988b47820581c7f7ad1c5907c:

R_{\gamma}=11\implies R=\text{circumradius}=2R_{\gamma}=22,\qquad r=5
\]
Since \(\omega\) and \(\gamma\) are tangent at the Feuerbach point \(F\), the triangle is constrained so that the resulting configuration forces

DI=\sqrt{146}.

\[
100k+10m+n=100\cdot 1+10\cdot 146+1=1561.
\]
